topics.title: Need and Role of Energy Storage Systems in Power System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET521 - ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Need and Role of Energy Storage Systems in Power System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Energy Storage System (ESS)** in a power system is a dedicated assembly of apparatus, subsystems, and power-electronic interfaces that captures electrical energy produced at one instant, retains it in a usable form (mechanical, chemical, electrochemical, thermal, or electromagnetic), and releases it as electrical energy at a different instant to satisfy operational, economic, or reliability objectives of the grid.

Mathematically, an ESS decouples the instantaneous power balance

$$P_{gen}(t) = P_{load}(t) + P_{loss}(t)$$

by introducing a buffering term $P_{ess}(t)$ such that

$$P_{gen}(t) + P_{ess}(t) = P_{load}(t) + P_{loss}(t)$$

where $P_{ess}(t)$ may be positive (discharging) or negative (charging).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-Standard):** An *Energy Storage System* is any device, combination of devices, or installation that is capable of **storing energy and delivering it back as usable electrical energy** for performing one or more grid services, including time-shifting, power quality improvement, and ancillary support.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of a power grid as a large water-supply network. A dam reservoir is the perfect analogy to a grid-scale battery:

- Water (electricity) is generated at a hydroelectric plant.
- Demand (cities, farms) fluctuates — mornings and evenings need more water, nights need less.
- The **dam reservoir stores excess water** when inflow exceeds demand, and **releases it** when demand peaks or inflow drops.

Similarly, an ESS:
- **Stores energy** when generation exceeds demand (or when prices are low).
- **Releases energy** when demand exceeds generation (or when prices are high).
- **Smooths fluctuations** just as a dam smooths river flow variations.

A small-scale analogy: the battery inside a **smartphone**. The phone may be plugged in at night, but the battery stores energy and releases it when you unplug — decoupling charging time from usage time. The grid ESS does the same, but on a **megawatt-to-gigawatt** scale.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Board-Exam Phrase to Memorize:** *"Energy storage introduces temporal flexibility into the otherwise instantaneous generation-load balance of the power system."*

## 1.3 The Fundamental Problem That Storage Solves

Electrical energy, unlike most commodities, is **difficult to stockpile** in its native alternating-current (AC) form. Historically, electricity had to be **produced at the exact moment it was consumed**. Storage breaks this rigid coupling, enabling four paradigm shifts:

1. **Temporal decoupling** — generate and consume at different times.
2. **Spatial decoupling** — energy can be transported and used at different locations.
3. **Buffering of intermittency** — compensates for variable renewable sources.
4. **Dynamic grid support** — provides fast-acting power for stability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction for Examiners:**
> *Capacitor* → stores charge briefly (electrical field, seconds to minutes).
> *Battery / Pumped hydro* → stores energy in a transformed state (chemical or gravitational potential, hours to months).
> The **Role of ESS in Power System** focuses on the latter — long-duration, grid-relevant storage.

## 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics (KTU 2024 Highlighted)

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Energy Capacity | $E$ | **kWh** or **MWh** | Total storable energy |
| Rated Power | $P$ | **kW** or **MW** | Max charge/discharge rate |
| State of Charge | $SOC$ | **%** | Current stored energy fraction |
| Depth of Discharge | $DOD$ | **%** | Fraction of $E$ that can be used |
| Round-Trip Efficiency | $\eta_{RT}$ | **%** | Output energy ÷ Input energy |
| Energy Density | $\rho_E$ | **Wh/kg** | Energy per unit mass |
| Power Density | $\rho_P$ | **W/kg** | Power per unit mass |
| Cycle Life | $N_{cyc}$ | **cycles** | Usable lifetime cycles |
| Response Time | $t_{resp}$ | **ms / s** | Speed of power delivery |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Energy Storage Is Needed — The Six Driving Forces

### 2.1.1 The Time-Mismatch Problem

The grid has a **load duration curve** where peak demand may be 1.5–2× the average demand. Generators are dimensioned to meet peak load, leading to poor capacity utilization (typically 40–60% on average). ESS shifts the peak, allowing base-load plants to operate at higher load factors.

### 2.1.2 Intermittency of Renewables

Wind and solar PV have variable, non-dispatchable output. Without storage, the grid must:
- Curtail (waste) surplus renewable energy, or
- Rely on fossil-fuel spinning reserves, defeating the clean-energy goal.

ESS absorbs the surplus and releases during low-wind / low-solar periods.

### 2.1.3 Frequency and Voltage Stability

The grid must maintain frequency (e.g., **50 Hz** in India) within tight limits (typically $\pm 0.5\%$). Any imbalance between generation and load causes frequency deviation. ESS provides **fast frequency response (FFR)** within milliseconds — far faster than conventional generators.

### 2.1.4 Power Quality Issues

Loads like arc furnaces, welding machines, and large VFDs cause:
- Voltage sags and swells
- Harmonics
- Flicker

Power-electronic interfaced ESS provides **dynamic reactive power support** and ride-through capability.

### 2.1.5 Economic Arbitrage

Electricity prices vary by time-of-use (ToU). ESS charges when price is low and discharges when price is high:

$$\text{Revenue} = \eta_{RT} \cdot E_{disch} \cdot P_{peak} - E_{ch} \cdot P_{off-peak}$$

### 2.1.6 Reliability and Resilience

Critical loads (hospitals, data centers) require uninterrupted supply. ESS provides ride-through during outages and can support **black start** — the ability to restart a grid after a total blackout.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Key-Point (must appear in any 14-mark answer):**
> Storage is needed for **technical** reasons (stability, quality, intermittency), **economic** reasons (arbitrage, deferral of T&D upgrades), and **environmental** reasons (renewable integration, decarbonization).

## 2.2 The Role of Energy Storage — Functional Taxonomy

The role of ESS in a modern power system can be classified into **three time scales**:

| Time Scale | Function | Typical Power | Typical Duration | KTU Board Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Seconds–Minutes** | Frequency regulation, voltage support, ride-through, power-quality | 1–100 MW | 1 s – 15 min | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Minutes–Hours** | Peak shaving, load leveling, spinning reserve, renewable smoothing | 10–500 MW | 15 min – 6 h | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Hours–Days** | Energy arbitrage, seasonal storage, black start, microgrid autonomy | 1 MW – 1 GW | 6 h – 100 h+ | ⭐⭐ |

### 2.2.1 Detailed Role Definitions

**1. Load Leveling (Energy Time-Shift)**
- Charge during off-peak, discharge during peak.
- Reduces fuel cost, defers generation capacity expansion.

**2. Peak Shaving**
- Reduces the maximum demand drawn from the grid.
- Saves demand charges; defers transmission upgrades.

**3. Frequency Regulation (Primary Response)**
- ESS absorbs/injects active power to counter frequency deviations.
- Faster than conventional governor action.

**4. Voltage Support / Reactive Power Compensation**
- Power-electronic inverter supplies/absorbs reactive power (STATCOM-like behavior).
- Maintains voltage within prescribed limits.

**5. Spinning and Non-Spinning Reserve**
- Stored energy acts as instantaneous reserve, freeing up conventional generators.
- A **100 MW battery** can replace several hundred MW of thermal spinning reserve.

**6. Renewable Energy Integration**
- Smooths PV/wind output variability.
- Enables firming of renewable dispatch.

**7. Black Start Capability**
- After a blackout, ESS energizes auxiliary loads of thermal plants to restart the grid.
- A critical role in microgrids and island systems.

**8. Microgrid and Island Operation**
- ESS enables autonomous operation disconnected from main grid.
- Maintains frequency and voltage in islanded mode.

**9. Electric Vehicle (EV) Integration — V2G**
- EV batteries can act as distributed ESS via Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G).
- Aggregator coordinates thousands of EVs for grid support.

**10. Power Quality Improvement**
- Mitigates sags, swells, flicker, and harmonics via fast power-electronic action.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Table)

| Concept | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| Energy Stored | $E = P \cdot t$ | Energy = Power × Duration (in hours) |
| Usable Energy | $E_{use} = E_{rated} \cdot DOD$ | Energy accessible per cycle |
| SOC Definition | $SOC(t) = \dfrac{E_{stored}(t)}{E_{rated}} \times 100\%$ | Instantaneous state of charge |
| Round-Trip Efficiency | $\eta_{RT} = \dfrac{E_{discharged}}{E_{charged}} \times 100\%$ | Energy lost in one full cycle |
| Discharge Energy | $E_{disch} = \eta_{RT} \cdot E_{ch}$ | Energy out for given energy in |
| Peak Shaving Savings | $S = \Delta P \cdot C_{demand}$ | Demand-charge reduction |
| Energy Arbitrage Revenue | $R = \eta_{RT} \cdot E_{cycle} \cdot (P_{peak} - P_{offpeak})$ | Daily arbitrage profit |
| Battery Capacity | $C = \dfrac{E_{use}}{V_{nominal}}$ | Ampere-hour rating |
| Number of Cells in Series | $N_{series} = \dfrac{V_{stack}}{V_{cell}}$ | For battery packs |
| Energy Density (mass) | $\rho_E = \dfrac{E}{m}$ | Wh/kg |
| Power Density (mass) | $\rho_P = \dfrac{P}{m}$ | W/kg |
| Specific Energy | $\rho_{sp} = \dfrac{E_{use}}{E_{rated}} \cdot 100\%$ | (Same as DOD) |
| Cycle Throughput | $Q_{thrpt} = E_{rated} \cdot N_{cyc} \cdot DOD$ | Lifetime energy delivered |
| Self-Discharge Loss | $E_{loss} = E_{stored} \cdot (1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ | Idle loss over time $t$ |
| C-Rate | $C_{rate} = \dfrac{P}{E_{rated}}$ | Inverse of discharge time in hours |
| Investment Payback | $T_{pb} = \dfrac{C_{capital}}{R_{annual}}$ | Years to recover capital cost |

> [!NOTE]
> All percentages above are unitless. **Energy and Power must be in consistent units**: use kW and kWh (or MW and MWh) — never mix kW with MWh.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Energy Stored in an ESS Given Power Profile

**Problem:** An ESS charges at a constant power of $P_{ch}$ for a duration $t_{ch}$ and discharges at $P_{dch}$ for $t_{dch}$. Determine the **net energy delivered to the load**, accounting for round-trip efficiency.

**Step 1 — Energy absorbed during charging**

$$E_{charged} = P_{ch} \cdot t_{ch}$$

**Step 2 — Energy lost during one full cycle (charge + discharge)**

$$E_{loss} = (1 - \eta_{RT}) \cdot E_{charged}$$

**Step 3 — Energy available at the discharge terminals**

$$E_{discharged} = E_{charged} - E_{loss} = E_{charged} \cdot \eta_{RT}$$

**Step 4 — Net energy delivered to the load**

$$E_{load} = P_{dch} \cdot t_{dch} = \eta_{RT} \cdot P_{ch} \cdot t_{ch}$$

**Step 5 — Solving for discharge duration**

$$t_{dch} = \frac{\eta_{RT} \cdot P_{ch} \cdot t_{ch}}{P_{dch}}$$

**Example Numerical Evaluation**

Given: $P_{ch} = 50 \text{ MW}$, $t_{ch} = 4 \text{ h}$, $\eta_{RT} = 0.85$, $P_{dch} = 40 \text{ MW}$.

$$E_{charged} = 50 \times 4 = 200 \text{ MWh}$$

$$E_{discharged} = 0.85 \times 200 = 170 \text{ MWh}$$

$$t_{dch} = \frac{170}{40} = 4.25 \text{ h}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** Even with a 15 % round-trip loss, the **effective discharge time is longer** (4.25 h vs 4 h) because the discharge power is lower (40 MW vs 50 MW). This is why ESS sizing always references *delivered* energy, not *absorbed* energy.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Sizing an ESS for Peak Shaving

**Problem:** A commercial facility has a daily load profile with a peak of $P_{peak} = 5 \text{ MW}$ for $t_{peak} = 3 \text{ hours}$. The utility sets a target demand cap of $P_{cap} = 4 \text{ MW}$. Find the required ESS energy capacity and rated power.

**Step 1 — Peak demand to be shaved**

$$\Delta P = P_{peak} - P_{cap} = 5 - 4 = 1 \text{ MW}$$

This is the **rated discharge power** of the ESS:

$$P_{ess} = 1 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 2 — Energy that must be discharged over the peak window**

$$E_{disch} = \Delta P \cdot t_{peak} = 1 \text{ MW} \times 3 \text{ h} = 3 \text{ MWh}$$

**Step 3 — Account for round-trip efficiency**

The ESS must store more energy than it discharges:

$$E_{charge} = \frac{E_{disch}}{\eta_{RT}}$$

Assuming $\eta_{RT} = 0.90$:

$$E_{charge} = \frac{3}{0.90} = 3.333 \text{ MWh}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Depth of Discharge (DOD)**

For Li-ion, typical $DOD = 80\%$, so the *rated* (nameplate) capacity is:

$$E_{rated} = \frac{E_{charge}}{DOD} = \frac{3.333}{0.80} = 4.167 \text{ MWh}$$

**Step 5 — Final specification**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Rated Power | **1 MW** |
| Energy Capacity | **4.17 MWh** |
| Discharge Window | 3 h |
| Charge Window (off-peak, e.g., 9 h) | Charge at $P_{ch} = 0.46 \text{ MW}$ |

> **Engineering Insight:** The ESS rating must be **oversized** in energy to account for (a) round-trip losses and (b) cycle-life-preserving DOD limits. A 5 MW / 3 MWh load requires a 1 MW / 4.17 MWh ESS.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: State-of-Charge Dynamics

**Problem:** Derive the SOC trajectory of an ESS over a charge-discharge cycle, accounting for self-discharge and Coulombic losses.

**Step 1 — Define the continuous-time SOC equation**

$$\frac{d(SOC)}{dt} = \frac{1}{E_{rated}} \left( \eta_{ch} \cdot P_{ch} - \frac{P_{dch}}{\eta_{dch}} \right) - k_{sd} \cdot SOC$$

where:
- $\eta_{ch}$ = charging efficiency (typically 0.95–0.99)
- $\eta_{dch}$ = discharging efficiency
- $k_{sd}$ = self-discharge constant (per hour)

**Step 2 — Solve for constant-power operation**

For a charging interval with $P_{dch} = 0$:

$$\frac{d(SOC)}{dt} = \frac{\eta_{ch} \cdot P_{ch}}{E_{rated}} - k_{sd} \cdot SOC$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from $t = 0$ to $t = T$**

The general solution is:

$$SOC(t) = SOC(\infty) + \left[ SOC(0) - SOC(\infty) \right] \cdot e^{-k_{sd} \cdot t}$$

where the steady-state charging SOC is:

$$SOC(\infty) = \frac{\eta_{ch} \cdot P_{ch}}{k_{sd} \cdot E_{rated}}$$

**Step 4 — Neglecting self-discharge** ($k_{sd} \to 0$)

The exponential term $\to 1$, and the SOC rises linearly:

$$SOC(t) = SOC(0) + \frac{\eta_{ch} \cdot P_{ch} \cdot t}{E_{rated}}$$

**Example Numerical Evaluation**

Given: $E_{rated} = 10 \text{ MWh}$, $P_{ch} = 2 \text{ MW}$, $\eta_{ch} = 0.95$, $SOC(0) = 20\%$, find SOC after $t = 3$ h, ignoring self-discharge.

$$SOC(3) = 20 + \frac{0.95 \times 2 \times 3}{10} \times 100 = 20 + 57 = 77\%$$

> **Engineering Insight:** The SOC should never exceed 100 % or fall below $100\% - DOD$ to avoid damage. The Energy Management System (EMS) enforces these limits in real time.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Energy Arbitrage Economics

**Problem:** A 1 MWh ESS operates in a ToU tariff with off-peak price $\pi_{op} = \text{₹}3/\text{kWh}$ for 9 hours and peak price $\pi_{pk} = \text{₹}8/\text{kWh}$ for 5 hours. Compute daily arbitrage revenue.

**Step 1 — Energy purchased during off-peak (charging)**

$$E_{ch} = 0.9 \times 1 \text{ MWh} = 0.9 \text{ MWh}$$

(Charging to 90 % of capacity to protect battery health.)

**Step 2 — Charging cost**

$$C_{ch} = E_{ch} \times \pi_{op} = 900 \times 3 = \text{₹}2{,}700$$

**Step 3 — Energy sold during peak (after round-trip losses)**

$$E_{dch} = \eta_{RT} \times E_{ch} = 0.85 \times 0.9 = 0.765 \text{ MWh}$$

**Step 4 — Discharge revenue**

$$R_{dch} = E_{dch} \times \pi_{pk} = 765 \times 8 = \text{₹}6{,}120$$

**Step 5 — Daily net revenue**

$$R_{net} = R_{dch} - C_{ch} = 6{,}120 - 2{,}700 = \text{₹}3{,}420$$

**Step 6 — Annual revenue and payback**

$$R_{annual} = 3{,}420 \times 365 = \text{₹}12{,}48{,}300 \approx \text{₹}12.48 \text{ lakh}$$

If capital cost = ₹1 crore for 1 MWh battery system, payback $\approx 8$ years.

> **Engineering Insight:** Pure arbitrage rarely justifies ESS investment; **stacked services** (frequency regulation + capacity deferral + arbitrage) are essential for project economics.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Matching Roles to Time Scales

| Role | Time Scale | Power Magnitude | Energy Magnitude | Speed Requirement | KTU 2024 Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Frequency Regulation (PFR) | 1–10 s | 1–100 MW | 0.1–10 MWh | < 1 s | Critical |
| Voltage / Reactive Support | 0.1–5 s | 1–50 MVAR | Very low | < 100 ms | High |
| Peak Shaving | 1–6 h | 1–50 MW | 1–300 MWh | < 1 min | High |
| Load Leveling | 4–12 h | 10–500 MW | 40–6000 MWh | < 1 min | High |
| Renewable Smoothing | 1–60 min | 1–100 MW | 0.1–100 MWh | < 1 s | Critical |
| Black Start | 1–4 h | 5–50 MW | 5–200 MWh | < 10 s | Mission-critical |
| Energy Arbitrage | 4–10 h | 1–100 MW | 1–1000 MWh | < 15 min | Economic |
| Microgrid Islanding | 4–24 h | 0.1–10 MW | 0.4–240 MWh | < 1 s | Critical |

> [!NOTE]
> Different roles demand **different response times**, hence different storage technologies. No single ESS is optimal for all roles — this is a *fundamental board-exam takeaway*.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: Role of ESS in Modern Power System — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SourceA[CONVENTIONAL GENERATION]:::gen
    SourceB[PV AND WIND RENEWABLES]:::ren
    SourceC[TRANSMISSION GRID]:::grid

    Bus1[POINT OF COMMON COUPLATION]:::bus
    ESS[ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEM]:::ess
    PCS[POWER CONVERSION SYSTEM]:::pcs
    EMS[ENERGY MANAGEMENT SYSTEM]:::ems

    LoadA[CRITICAL LOADS]:::load
    LoadB[NON CRITICAL LOADS]:::load
    LoadC[MICROGRID CLUSTER]:::load

    SourceA --> Bus1
    SourceB --> Bus1
    SourceC --> Bus1
    Bus1 <--> PCS
    PCS <--> ESS
    EMS -.controls.-> PCS
    EMS -.monitors.-> ESS
    Bus1 --> LoadA
    Bus1 --> LoadB
    Bus1 --> LoadC

    classDef gen fill:#FFD580,stroke:#A06000,color:#000
    classDef ren fill:#A8E6A1,stroke:#1F6B1F,color:#000
    classDef grid fill:#B8C6E6,stroke:#1A3C7A,color:#000
    classDef bus fill:#F5F5F5,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef ess fill:#FF9AA2,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    classDef pcs fill:#C7CEEA,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef ems fill:#FFEBA1,stroke:#806000,color:#000
    classDef load fill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#1F6B1F,color:#000
```

**Reading Guide:** The ESS sits at the Point of Common Coupling (PCC), interfacing with all sources and loads through a bidirectional Power Conversion System (PCS). The EMS (Energy Management System) acts as the brain, dispatching the ESS in real time to maintain balance.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Block-Level Functional Architecture — Sizing and Dispatch

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Input1[LOAD PROFILE PL T]:::in
    Input2[PV WIND PROFILE PT]:::in
    Input3[TARIFF SCHEDULE]:::in

    Forecast[LOAD AND GENERATION FORECAST]:::proc
    OptModel[OPTIMIZATION ENGINE]:::proc
    Dispatch[SOC AND POWER DISPATCHER]:::proc

    Batt[BATTERY BANK]:::dev
    Inv[BIDIRECTIONAL INVERTER]:::dev
    Meter[DC AND AC METERS]:::dev
    Out[GRID PLUS LOAD]:::out

    Input1 --> Forecast
    Input2 --> Forecast
    Input3 --> OptModel
    Forecast --> OptModel
    OptModel --> Dispatch
    Dispatch --> Inv
    Inv --> Batt
    Inv --> Meter
    Meter --> Out
    Dispatch -.feedback SOC.-> OptModel

    classDef in fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef proc fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef dev fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000
```

**Reading Guide:** The sizing process starts with the load and renewable profiles, runs through a forecasting and optimization engine, and dispatches charge/discharge commands to the bidirectional inverter. The closed feedback loop (dashed arrow) maintains SOC within safe limits.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define an Energy Storage System. List any four key roles it plays in a power system.**

**Model Answer (10–12 lines for 3 marks):**

An **Energy Storage System (ESS)** is an assembly of devices and power-electronic interfaces that captures electrical energy, retains it in a transformed form (chemical, mechanical, thermal, or electromagnetic), and releases it as electrical energy at a different time to provide grid services.

**Key roles in the power system:**

1. **Peak shaving / load leveling** — discharges during high demand to reduce the peak and defers generation capacity expansion.
2. **Frequency regulation** — provides fast active power support to maintain nominal grid frequency (e.g., 50 Hz in India).
3. **Renewable energy integration** — smooths the variability of PV and wind, allowing higher renewable penetration.
4. **Power-quality improvement** — compensates voltage sags, swells, flicker, and harmonics.

> *Allocation cue:* Definition (1 mark) + 4 roles (0.5 each) = 3 marks.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2023]
**Differentiate between energy density and power density. Why is it important in the context of selecting an energy storage system for a power system application?**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Energy Density ($\rho_E$) | Power Density ($\rho_P$) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Energy stored per unit mass (Wh/kg) | Power deliverable per unit mass (W/kg) |
| Determines | *How long* the device can run | *How fast* the device can deliver energy |
| Application fit | Long-duration discharge (e.g., pumped hydro for load leveling) | Short-duration, high-power (e.g., supercapacitor for frequency regulation) |

**Importance for ESS selection:**

- **High energy density** is needed for applications requiring long discharge (e.g., 4–6 h peak shaving).
- **High power density** is needed for fast-response services (e.g., frequency regulation in < 1 s).
- No single storage technology excels in both — **batteries balance both**, while **supercapacitors have high power density but low energy density**, and **pumped hydro has high energy density but low power density**.

> *Allocation cue:* Tabular difference (1.5 marks) + selection logic (1.5 marks) = 3 marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 (Expected Pattern)]

#### **Part (a)** — 7 Marks
**Explain the various needs for energy storage in a modern power system. Discuss the technical, economic, and environmental drivers in detail.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Technical Drivers**

a) **Time Mismatch Between Generation and Demand**
The instantaneous balance $P_{gen}(t) = P_{load}(t)$ is rigid. The **load duration curve** shows peak demand that may be 1.5–2× the average. Without storage, generators must be sized to peak load, resulting in poor capacity utilization (40–60%). ESS stores surplus and releases during peaks, improving load factor.

b) **Intermittency of Renewable Sources**
Wind and PV output is variable and non-dispatchable. ESS smooths the output (PV smoothing, wind firming) and enables dispatchability, raising the effective renewable penetration from 20–30% (without storage) to 80%+ (with storage).

c) **Frequency and Voltage Stability**
Grid frequency must remain at 50 Hz $\pm 0.5\%$. Any generation–load imbalance causes deviation. ESS provides **fast frequency response (FFR)** within milliseconds — much faster than conventional generator governor action (seconds). Through power-electronic inverters, ESS also supplies reactive power (STATCOM-like) for voltage control.

d) **Power Quality and Ride-Through**
Loads like arc furnaces, large drives, and welding machines cause sags, swells, flicker, and harmonics. ESS with bidirectional inverters (e.g., Dynamic Voltage Restorer topology) mitigates these within sub-cycle times.

**2. Economic Drivers**

a) **Energy Arbitrage**: Charges when ToU price is low (e.g., night), discharges when high (e.g., evening peak).

b) **Demand-Charge Reduction**: Peak shaving lowers maximum demand, reducing monthly demand charges.

c) **Deferral of T&D Upgrades**: A substation transformer upgrade needed in 5 years can be deferred by installing a 5 MW / 20 MWh battery at a fraction of the upgrade cost.

d) **Reduction in Spinning Reserve Cost**: A 100 MW battery can replace several hundred MW of thermal spinning reserve, saving fuel and maintenance.

**3. Environmental Drivers**

a) **Higher Renewable Penetration** — directly reduces fossil-fuel use and CO₂ emissions.

b) **Reduced Curtailment** — surplus renewable energy is captured rather than wasted.

c) **Electrification of Transport (EVs)** — V2G turns millions of EV batteries into distributed storage, further decarbonizing the system.

> *Allocation cue:* 3 technical + 2 economic + 2 environmental = 7 marks.

---

#### **Part (b)** — 7 Marks
**A factory has a peak demand of 8 MW occurring for 4 hours. The utility imposes a demand cap of 6 MW. Compute the ESS power rating, energy capacity (assuming $\eta_{RT} = 90\%$ and $DOD = 80\%$), and the cost savings if the demand charge is ₹800/kW/month. Assume 30 days in a month.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the peak demand to be shaved**

$$\Delta P = P_{peak} - P_{cap} = 8 \text{ MW} - 6 \text{ MW} = 2 \text{ MW}$$

ESS **rated power** = **2 MW**

**Step 2 — Energy that must be discharged per day over the 4-hour peak**

$$E_{disch} = 2 \text{ MW} \times 4 \text{ h} = 8 \text{ MWh/day}$$

**Step 3 — Required energy absorbed (charging), accounting for round-trip efficiency**

$$E_{charge} = \frac{E_{disch}}{\eta_{RT}} = \frac{8}{0.90} = 8.889 \text{ MWh}$$

**Step 4 — Apply depth of discharge to obtain nameplate capacity**

$$E_{rated} = \frac{E_{charge}}{DOD} = \frac{8.889}{0.80} = 11.11 \text{ MWh}$$

**Step 5 — Compute monthly cost savings from demand-charge reduction**

Without ESS, demand = 8 MW. With ESS, capped at 6 MW. Saved demand = 2 MW = **2000 kW**.

$$\text{Monthly Savings} = 2000 \text{ kW} \times \text{₹}800/\text{kW} = \text{₹}16{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 6 — Annual savings**

$$\text{Annual Savings} = 16{,}00{,}000 \times 12 = \text{₹}1{,}92{,}00{,}000 = ₹1.92 \text{ crore}$$

**Final Specifications Table**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| ESS Rated Power | **2 MW** |
| ESS Energy Capacity | **11.11 MWh** |
| Peak Shaved | 2 MW |
| Monthly Demand-Charge Savings | **₹16 lakh** |
| Annual Savings | **₹1.92 crore** |

> *Allocation cue:* Sizing (4 marks) + cost computation (3 marks) = 7 marks.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Expected Pattern)]

#### **Part (a)** — 7 Marks
**Discuss the role of energy storage in renewable energy integration. How does storage address the variability and uncertainty of PV and wind generation? Explain with suitable waveforms and quantitative reasoning.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. The Variability Problem**
PV output follows solar irradiance (zero at night, peaking at noon). Wind output follows cubic wind-speed dependence. Both are **non-dispatchable** and **uncertain** (forecast error 5–20%). A power system can tolerate up to ~20–30% renewable penetration without storage; beyond this, the variability causes frequency excursions, voltage flicker, and curtailment.

**2. Roles of ESS in Renewable Integration**

a) **Short-Term Smoothing (seconds to minutes):** Supercapacitors or fast batteries absorb ramps in PV output caused by passing clouds. Without storage, irradiance drop of 70% in 30 s causes a frequency dip of > 0.5 Hz in weak grids.

b) **Diurnal Time-Shift:** A 100 MW PV plant + 400 MWh battery can deliver 100 MW for 4 hours into the evening peak, transforming intermittent PV into a *dispatchable* resource — equivalent to a peaking plant.

c) **Firming Capacity:** Storage guarantees a contracted output level (e.g., 50 MW round-the-clock) regardless of wind speed, making the project bankable.

d) **Curtailment Reduction:** In grids with high renewable penetration (e.g., California, Germany, Tamil Nadu), 5–15% of renewable energy is curtailed. Storage captures and time-shifts this energy.

e) **Capacity Credit:** With storage, renewables contribute to *firm capacity*, displacing conventional plants in resource adequacy planning.

**3. Quantitative Reasoning**

Consider a 50 MW PV plant. Daily output (typical clear-sky day):

| Time | 06–10 | 10–14 | 14–18 | 18–22 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Output (MW) | 5–30 | 35–50 | 30–10 | 0 |

Without storage: Plant supplies variable output. With **100 MWh battery** (charged 09:00–14:00, discharged 17:00–21:00), output during peak hours becomes:

$$P_{disch} = \frac{100 \text{ MWh}}{5 \text{ h}} = 20 \text{ MW dispatched for 5 h}$$

This converts a midday-only resource into a *peak-hour dispatchable* resource — exactly aligned with residential air-conditioning load peaks in India.

**4. Combined PV + Wind + Storage**

Adding wind diversifies input; storage covers both. A hybrid plant (PV 30 MW + Wind 30 MW + Battery 60 MWh) provides 40 MW firm for 6 hours — sufficient to bid into capacity markets.

> *Allocation cue:* Variability problem (2 marks) + 5 roles (2 marks) + quantitative example (3 marks) = 7 marks.

---

#### **Part (b)** — 7 Marks
**A grid has a 200 MW pumped hydro storage plant operating with two reservoirs. The upper reservoir holds $1.2 \times 10^6$ m³ of water at an effective head of 300 m. Compute:**
**(i) the maximum gravitational potential energy stored,**
**(ii) the electrical energy that can be delivered if the turbine–generator efficiency is 90% and the pump efficiency is 87%,**
**(iii) the round-trip efficiency.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Volume $V = 1.2 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^3$
- Head $h = 300 \text{ m}$
- Density of water $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Acceleration due to gravity $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- Turbine efficiency $\eta_{turb} = 0.90$
- Pump efficiency $\eta_{pump} = 0.87$

**Step 1 — Compute the mass of water**

$$m = \rho \times V = 1000 \times 1.2 \times 10^6 = 1.2 \times 10^9 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Gravitational Potential Energy (GPE)**

$$E_{GPE} = m \cdot g \cdot h$$

$$E_{GPE} = 1.2 \times 10^9 \times 9.81 \times 300$$

$$E_{GPE} = 3.5316 \times 10^{12} \text{ J}$$

Converting to MWh (1 MWh = $3.6 \times 10^9$ J):

$$E_{GPE} = \frac{3.5316 \times 10^{12}}{3.6 \times 10^9} = 981 \text{ MWh}$$

**Step 3 — Electrical energy delivered during generation**

$$E_{el,\ gen} = E_{GPE} \times \eta_{turb} = 981 \times 0.90 = 882.9 \text{ MWh}$$

**Step 4 — Electrical energy consumed during pumping**

$$E_{el,\ pump} = \frac{E_{GPE}}{\eta_{pump}} = \frac{981}{0.87} = 1127.6 \text{ MWh}$$

**Step 5 — Round-Trip Efficiency**

$$\eta_{RT} = \frac{E_{el,\ gen}}{E_{el,\ pump}} \times 100\% = \frac{882.9}{1127.6} \times 100\% = 78.3\%$$

> **Note:** The product of the two stage efficiencies $\eta_{turb} \times \eta_{pump} = 0.90 \times 0.87 = 0.783$, which is the round-trip efficiency.

**Final Results Table**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Mass of water | $1.2 \times 10^9$ kg |
| GPE stored | **981 MWh** |
| Electrical energy out | **882.9 MWh** |
| Electrical energy in | **1127.6 MWh** |
| Round-Trip Efficiency | **78.3 %** |

> *Allocation cue:* GPE (2) + electrical in/out (3) + round-trip (2) = 7 marks.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost 2–4 Marks per Question:**
> 1. **Mixing units** — writing kW for power and Wh for energy without conversion. Always use consistent units (kW–kWh or MW–MWh).
> 2. **Forgetting to apply DOD** — students often state $E = P \times t$ directly without accounting for the depth-of-discharge limit. A 10 MWh battery with 80% DOD only delivers 8 MWh per cycle.
> 3. **Omitting round-trip efficiency** — when sizing an ESS, multiply by $1/\eta_{RT}$ on the charging side, never on the discharge side.
> 4. **Confusing $\eta_{ch}$, $\eta_{dch}$, and $\eta_{RT}$** — the round-trip efficiency is the *product* of charge and discharge efficiencies: $\eta_{RT} = \eta_{ch} \times \eta_{dch}$.
> 5. **Forgetting the self-discharge term** in long-duration storage (PHS, CAES) — examiners specifically test this.
> 6. **Writing vague roles** like *"storage helps the grid"* — always name the *specific service* (e.g., frequency regulation, peak shaving, black start).
> 7. **Skipping the SOC boundary check** — always state that $0 \leq SOC \leq 100\%$ and $SOC \geq (100\% - DOD_{max})$ during discharge.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Top-Priority Concepts (must memorize for KTU 2024 ESE):**
>
> ☐ **Definition** of ESS and its role as a *temporal decoupler* between generation and demand.
>
> ☐ **Three driving forces** for ESS adoption: Technical, Economic, Environmental.
>
> ☐ **Three time-scale categories** of ESS roles: Seconds (power quality, frequency), Minutes (smoothing, reserves), Hours (arbitrage, load leveling).
>
> ☐ **Ten core roles**: load leveling, peak shaving, frequency regulation, voltage support, spinning reserve, renewable integration, black start, microgrid, V2G, power quality.
>
> ☐ **Master formulas**: $E = P \cdot t$, $SOC = E_{stored}/E_{rated}$, $\eta_{RT} = E_{disch}/E_{ch}$, $E_{use} = E_{rated} \cdot DOD$.
>
> ☐ **Sizing procedure** for peak shaving: Compute $\Delta P$, then $E_{disch} = \Delta P \cdot t$, then apply $1/\eta_{RT}$ and $1/DOD$ corrections.
>
> ☐ **State-of-Charge dynamics**: $SOC(t) = SOC(0) + (\eta_{ch} P_{ch} t)/E_{rated}$ for constant charging.
>
> ☐ **Arbitrage revenue**: $R = \eta_{RT} \cdot E_{cycle} \cdot (P_{peak} - P_{off-peak})$.
>
> ☐ **Pumped-hydro GPE**: $E = \rho \cdot V \cdot g \cdot h$; round-trip efficiency = product of turbine and pump efficiencies.
>
> ☐ **Key performance metrics**: $\eta_{RT}$ (60–95%), energy density, power density, cycle life, response time (ms–h).
>
> ☐ **No single storage technology fits all roles** — Supercapacitors (fast, low energy), Batteries (balanced), PHS / CAES (slow, high energy).
>
> ☐ **Modern relevance**: India targets **500 GWh** of energy storage by 2030 (Ministry of Power, 2023) — a key current-affairs datapoint for KTU 2024.
>
> ☐ **Why storage ≠ capacitor**: A capacitor stores charge briefly; an ESS stores *energy* (transformed form) for grid-relevant durations.
>
> ☐ **ESS is the cornerstone** of: net-zero grids, microgrids, EV ecosystems, and grid resilience.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: General Considerations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET521 - ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** General Considerations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Energy Storage System (ESS)** is an integrated electromechanical, electrochemical, thermal, or mechanical assembly designed to capture energy produced at one moment in time for use at a later period, and to perform the critical functions of **energy management**, **power conditioning**, and **demand-supply balancing** across a wide spectrum of stationary, mobile, and portable applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Energy storage refers to the process of capturing energy produced at one time for use at a later time. A device that stores energy is generally called an **accumulator** or **battery** (in the broader engineering sense, not restricted to electrochemistry alone).

### 1.2 Need for Energy Storage — The Five Engineering Pillars

The integration of energy storage into modern power and energy systems is driven by **five core engineering and economic pillars**:

1. **Temporal Bridging** — Decoupling of energy generation from its consumption (e.g., solar PV generating at noon but demand peaks at 7 PM).
2. **Power Quality Enhancement** — Mitigation of voltage sags, frequency deviations, and harmonic distortions.
3. **Peak Shaving & Load Leveling** — Reduction of demand charges by supplying stored energy during peak tariffs.
4. **Grid Stability & Ancillary Services** — Provision of spinning reserve, frequency regulation, and black-start capability.
5. **Electrification of Transport** — Enabling electric vehicles (EVs), hybrid electric vehicles (HEVs), and aviation propulsion.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tank" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of an Energy Storage System as a **water tank** connected to a variable-pressure water supply.
> - The **tank capacity** (in litres) represents the **Energy Capacity (Wh)** of the ESS.
> - The **pipe diameter** (allowing water in/out) represents the **Power Rating (W)** of the ESS.
> - **Leakage** from the tank represents **Self-Discharge Losses**.
> - **Sludge buildup at the bottom** represents **Capacity Fade** over cycles.
> - The **efficiency of the pump** (energy in vs. energy out) is analogous to **Round-Trip Efficiency (η_RT)**.
> - A **large, thin tank** = high energy, low power (like a dam reservoir). A **small, fat tank** = low energy, high power (like a pressure accumulator). This is the essence of the **Ragone Plot** concept!

### 1.4 Standard Physical Constants & Reference Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Gravitational acceleration | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Faraday's constant | $F$ | **96,485 C/mol** |
| Avogadro's number | $N_A$ | **6.022 × 10²³ /mol** |
| Universal gas constant | $R$ | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** |
| Standard reference temperature | $T_{ref}$ | **298.15 K (25 °C)** |
| Standard atmospheric pressure | $P_{atm}$ | **101.325 kPa** |
| Faraday efficiency reference | — | **1 F = 96,485 Ah/mol** (since 1 Ah = 3600 C) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Performance Metrics of an ESS

The complete characterization of any energy storage technology (whether battery, supercapacitor, flywheel, or pumped hydro) is governed by a finite set of **performance vectors**. A student is expected to master every parameter below for KTU examinations.

#### 2.1.1 Energy Capacity (E)

The total quantity of storable energy in a system, expressed in **Watt-hours (Wh)** or **Joules (J)** where $1 \text{ Wh} = 3600 \text{ J}$.

> **Why it matters:** Determines the **runtime** of the application. An EV battery of 60 kWh can theoretically deliver 60 kW for 1 hour, or 30 kW for 2 hours.

#### 2.1.2 Power Rating (P)

The maximum instantaneous rate at which energy can be delivered or absorbed, expressed in **Watts (W)** or **kW**. Determined by internal resistance, cell architecture, and thermal limits.

#### 2.1.3 Specific Energy (Gravimetric Energy Density)

Energy stored **per unit mass** — critical for **weight-sensitive** applications like aerospace and EVs.

#### 2.1.4 Energy Density (Volumetric Energy Density)

Energy stored **per unit volume** — critical for **space-constrained** applications like smartphones and laptops.

#### 2.1.5 Specific Power & Power Density

Power delivered per unit mass (W/kg) and per unit volume (W/L). High power density enables **burst applications** like EV acceleration or grid frequency regulation.

#### 2.1.6 State of Charge (SOC)

The instantaneous available capacity expressed as a **percentage** of the maximum rated capacity.

#### 2.1.7 Depth of Discharge (DOD)

The fraction of battery capacity that has been **withdrawn**, expressed in percentage. It is the **complement** of SOC.

#### 2.1.8 Round-Trip Efficiency (η_RT)

The ratio of energy **delivered** to energy **input** during one complete charge-discharge cycle. A critical economic metric.

#### 2.1.9 Cycle Life (N_cycles)

The number of complete charge-discharge cycles a system can sustain before its capacity fades to a defined **end-of-life (EOL) threshold** — typically **80%** of original capacity.

#### 2.1.10 Calendar Life (t_cal)

The total operational time (in years) before capacity fade, irrespective of cycling usage.

#### 2.1.11 C-Rate

A normalized discharge/charge current rate that describes how fast a system can deliver its rated capacity. **1C = full discharge in 1 hour.**

#### 2.1.12 Self-Discharge Rate

The percentage of stored energy lost per unit time (per day or per month) when the system is **idle** (no external load).

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM CHEAT SHEET — Master every equation in this table. Direct derivations from these formulas are asked repeatedly in KTU Module 1 questions.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Critical Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Energy stored (basic) | $E = P \times t$ | Wh | Foundation of all storage sizing |
| 2 | Energy from current integration | $E = \int V(t) \cdot I(t) \, dt$ | Wh | $V$ and $I$ may be time-dependent |
| 3 | Energy from capacity | $E = V_{nom} \cdot Q$ | Wh | $Q$ in Ah, $V_{nom}$ in V |
| 4 | Specific energy | $E_{sp} = \dfrac{E}{m_{sys}}$ | Wh/kg | $m_{sys}$ = total system mass |
| 5 | Energy density (volumetric) | $E_{vol} = \dfrac{E}{V_{sys}}$ | Wh/L | $V_{sys}$ = total system volume |
| 6 | Specific power | $P_{sp} = \dfrac{P_{max}}{m_{sys}}$ | W/kg | Critical for EV acceleration |
| 7 | State of Charge | $SOC(t) = \dfrac{Q_{remain}(t)}{Q_{rated}} \times 100\%$ | % | $Q_{remain} = Q_{rated} - Q_{used}$ |
| 8 | Depth of Discharge | $DOD = 1 - SOC$ (fraction) | dimensionless | Often expressed as % |
| 9 | Round-trip efficiency | $\eta_{RT} = \dfrac{E_{discharge}}{E_{charge}} \times 100\%$ | % | Always $\leq 100\%$ |
| 10 | C-rate (discharge) | $C = \dfrac{I_{discharge}}{Q_{rated}}$ | $h^{-1}$ | $2C$ = full discharge in 30 min |
| 11 | Discharge time from C-rate | $t = \dfrac{1}{C}$ | hours | Inversely related to C |
| 12 | Self-discharge | $Q(t) = Q_0 \cdot e^{-k t}$ | Ah | $k$ = self-discharge constant (per day) |
| 13 | Capacity fade (cyclical) | $C_{fade}(N) = C_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda N}$ | Ah | $\lambda$ = cycle decay constant |
| 14 | Capacity fade (calendar) | $C_{fade}(t) = C_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha \sqrt{t}}$ | Ah | Arrhenius-type calendar aging |
| 15 | Energy of capacitor | $E_C = \dfrac{1}{2} C \cdot V^2$ | J | Supercapacitor fundamental equation |
| 16 | Energy of flywheel | $E_{FW} = \dfrac{1}{2} I \cdot \omega^2$ | J | $I$ = moment of inertia, $\omega$ = rad/s |
| 17 | Pumped hydro potential energy | $E_{PH} = m \cdot g \cdot h$ | J | $h$ = head height (m) |
| 18 | Peukert's law | $t = \dfrac{H}{I^n}$ | hours | $n$ = Peukert exponent ($n > 1$) |
| 19 | Peukert capacity | $Q_P = I^n \cdot t$ | — | Used for lead-acid batteries |
| 20 | Energy throughput (lifetime) | $E_{life} = N_{cycles} \times E_{cycle} \times DOD$ | Wh | Total energy over lifetime |

### 2.3 The Ragone Plot — A Critical Visualization Tool

> [!NOTE]
> **Concept Highlight:** A **Ragone Plot** is a log-log scatter chart of **Specific Energy (Wh/kg)** on the X-axis versus **Specific Power (W/kg)** on the Y-axis. It enables a single-glance comparison of *all* storage technologies.

Key observations from a typical Ragone Plot:
- **Batteries** (Li-ion, NiMH) cluster in the **high energy / moderate power** region.
- **Supercapacitors** (EDLCs) cluster in the **high power / low energy** region.
- **Flywheels** occupy the **high power / moderate energy** region.
- **Pumped Hydro & CAES** dominate the **very high energy / low power** region.

A useful empirical upper bound relationship on a Ragone plot is:

$$P_{sp} \cdot E_{sp} \approx \text{const} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \log(P_{sp}) + \log(E_{sp}) \approx \text{const}$$

This **hyperbolic trade-off** indicates that no storage technology can simultaneously achieve maximum energy AND maximum power — a fundamental physical limitation.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **EVs:** ESS enables 400+ km range while delivering 150 kW peak for highway acceleration.
- **Grid:** Tesla Megapack (3.9 MWh) provides 4-hour discharge for load-shifting.
- **Aerospace:** Li-S cells target **500 Wh/kg** for electric aviation.
- **Telecom towers:** VRLA batteries provide **8-hour backup** at remote cell sites.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Relationship Between Energy, Capacity, and Voltage

> [!NOTE]
> **Derivation Objective:** Establish the fundamental energy equation $E = V \cdot Q$ from first principles.

**Step 1 — Define electrical power.** Power is the rate of energy transfer:

$$P(t) = V(t) \cdot I(t)$$

where $V(t)$ is the instantaneous terminal voltage in Volts and $I(t)$ is the instantaneous current in Amperes.

**Step 2 — Integrate power over time to obtain total energy delivered.** Energy is the time integral of power:

$$E = \int_0^{t_f} P(t) \, dt = \int_0^{t_f} V(t) \cdot I(t) \, dt$$

**Step 3 — Apply the constant-voltage approximation.** For engineering analysis of a battery over a discharge cycle, we assume $V(t) \approx V_{nom}$ (nominal voltage) as a first-order approximation:

$$E \approx V_{nom} \cdot \int_0^{t_f} I(t) \, dt$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the integral of current as charge (capacity).** By definition, the charge transferred is:

$$Q = \int_0^{t_f} I(t) \, dt \quad \text{(in Ampere-seconds = Coulombs)}$$

**Step 5 — Express capacity in Ampere-hours.** Since $1 \text{ Ah} = 3600 \text{ C}$, we convert:

$$Q \text{ (in Ah)} = \frac{Q \text{ (in C)}}{3600}$$

**Step 6 — Combine Steps 3 and 4.** The final compact form is:

$$E = V_{nom} \cdot Q \quad \text{(in Wh when $V$ is in V and $Q$ is in Ah)}$$

> **Conclusion:** This 6-step derivation shows that stored energy in a battery is the simple product of nominal voltage and rated capacity. It forms the foundation of every battery-sizing calculation in KTU problems.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — C-Rate and Discharge Time Relationship

> [!NOTE]
> **Derivation Objective:** Show mathematically that the C-rate is the inverse of the discharge time in hours.

**Step 1 — Define C-rate formally.** C-rate is the discharge current normalized by the rated capacity:

$$C = \frac{I_{discharge}}{Q_{rated}}$$

where $Q_{rated}$ is in Ah and $I_{discharge}$ is in A. The units of C are $\text{h}^{-1}$.

**Step 2 — Express discharge time.** The time to fully discharge a battery at constant current $I$ is:

$$t_{discharge} = \frac{Q_{rated}}{I_{discharge}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the C-rate definition into the discharge-time equation.** Replace $I_{discharge} = C \cdot Q_{rated}$:

$$t_{discharge} = \frac{Q_{rated}}{C \cdot Q_{rated}} = \frac{1}{C}$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the result.** If $C = 1 \text{ h}^{-1}$, then $t = 1$ hour. If $C = 2 \text{ h}^{-1}$, then $t = 0.5$ hour (30 minutes). If $C = 0.5 \text{ h}^{-1}$, then $t = 2$ hours.

> **Conclusion:** $t = \dfrac{1}{C}$ — a deceptively simple but *high-yield* KTU formula. Always remember to invert the C-rate to get discharge duration.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Round-Trip Efficiency from Energy In and Out

> [!NOTE]
> **Derivation Objective:** Define and derive the round-trip efficiency under non-ideal losses.

**Step 1 — Define input (charge) energy.** Let $E_{in}$ be the electrical energy supplied to the ESS during charging:

$$E_{in} = \int_0^{t_c} V_c(t) \cdot I_c(t) \, dt$$

**Step 2 — Define output (discharge) energy.** Let $E_{out}$ be the electrical energy extracted from the ESS during discharging:

$$E_{out} = \int_0^{t_d} V_d(t) \cdot I_d(t) \, dt$$

**Step 3 — Define total energy loss.** The energy lost in one complete cycle is:

$$E_{loss} = E_{in} - E_{out}$$

This loss encompasses **Joule heating ($I^2R$)**, **overpotential losses**, **parasitic losses** (pumps, fans, BMS), and **side-reaction losses** (e.g., gas evolution in electrolyzers).

**Step 4 — Formulate round-trip efficiency.** By definition:

$$\eta_{RT} = \frac{E_{out}}{E_{in}} = 1 - \frac{E_{loss}}{E_{in}}$$

**Step 5 — Apply coulombic and voltage efficiencies separately (advanced form).** In batteries:

$$\eta_{RT} = \eta_C \cdot \eta_V = \left( \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} \right) \cdot \left( \frac{V_{avg,discharge}}{V_{avg,charge}} \right)$$

where $\eta_C$ is the **Coulombic Efficiency** and $\eta_V$ is the **Voltage Efficiency**.

> **Conclusion:** Round-trip efficiency is always less than 100% due to irreversible losses. For Li-ion: $\eta_{RT} \approx 85\text{–}95\%$; for lead-acid: $\eta_{RT} \approx 70\text{–}85\%$; for supercapacitors: $\eta_{RT} \approx 95\text{–}98\%$.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Energy Stored in Different ESS Topologies (Comparative)

> [!NOTE]
> **Derivation Objective:** Express the energy stored in a **supercapacitor**, a **flywheel**, and a **pumped hydro** system from their physical first principles to highlight the diversity of storage mechanisms.

**Step 4a — Electrochemical Capacitor (Supercapacitor).** Energy stored in a capacitor is:

$$E_C = \frac{1}{2} C \cdot V^2$$

where $C$ is the capacitance in Farads and $V$ is the voltage in Volts. The factor of $\frac{1}{2}$ arises from the linear relationship $q = CV$ and integration of $V \, dq$.

**Step 4b — Mechanical Flywheel.** Energy stored in a rotating mass is pure rotational kinetic energy:

$$E_{FW} = \frac{1}{2} I \cdot \omega^2$$

where $I$ is the moment of inertia (kg·m²) and $\omega$ is the angular velocity (rad/s). For a solid cylindrical rotor:

$$I = \frac{1}{2} m \cdot r^2$$

Substituting:

$$E_{FW} = \frac{1}{2} \left( \frac{1}{2} m r^2 \right) \omega^2 = \frac{1}{4} m r^2 \omega^2$$

**Step 4c — Pumped Hydroelectric Storage (PHES).** Energy stored is gravitational potential energy:

$$E_{PH} = m \cdot g \cdot h$$

where $m$ is the mass of water lifted, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ is gravitational acceleration, and $h$ is the elevation head in meters. For volumetric storage with water density $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$:

$$E_{PH} = \rho \cdot V_{water} \cdot g \cdot h$$

**Step 4d — Compressed Air Energy Storage (CAES).** Energy stored in compressed gas (isothermal approximation):

$$E_{CAES} = P_1 V_1 \ln \left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} \right)$$

where $P_1$ and $P_2$ are initial and final pressures, $V_1$ is the initial gas volume.

> **Conclusion:** This derivation reveals that ESS design is fundamentally a **transduction problem** — converting electrical energy into chemical, mechanical, thermal, or electromagnetic potential energy for later retrieval.

---

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example — KTU-Style Problem

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example:** A **48 V, 100 Ah** lithium-ion battery pack has a measured discharge voltage of **46 V** and charge voltage of **50 V**. The coulombic efficiency is **99%**. Calculate (a) the nominal energy stored, (b) the round-trip efficiency, and (c) the discharge time at a constant current of **25 A**.

**Part (a) — Nominal energy:**

$$E_{nom} = V_{nom} \cdot Q_{rated} = 48 \text{ V} \times 100 \text{ Ah} = 4800 \text{ Wh} = 4.8 \text{ kWh}$$

**Part (b) — Round-trip efficiency:**

$$\eta_V = \frac{V_{discharge}}{V_{charge}} = \frac{46}{50} = 0.92$$

$$\eta_C = 0.99$$

$$\eta_{RT} = \eta_C \times \eta_V = 0.99 \times 0.92 = 0.9108 = 91.08\%$$

**Part (c) — Discharge time at 25 A:**

$$t_{discharge} = \frac{Q_{rated}}{I} = \frac{100 \text{ Ah}}{25 \text{ A}} = 4 \text{ hours}$$

Equivalently, $C = \frac{25}{100} = 0.25 \text{C}$, and $t = \frac{1}{0.25} = 4$ hours. ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
