## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU Examiner Pattern:** Part A questions demand **precise, definition-style** answers of 4–6 lines, often with one supporting formula. Avoid lengthy essays.

---

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "Energy Storage System" and list the four fundamental forms in which energy can be stored in a practical ESS.**

**Model Answer:**

An Energy Storage System (ESS) is an integrated assembly of components that captures energy at one time for use at a later time, performing energy management, power conditioning, and supply-demand balancing.

The four fundamental forms of stored energy are:

1. **Mechanical** — kinetic energy (flywheels) and potential energy (pumped hydro, CAES).
2. **Electrochemical** — chemical energy in batteries and fuel cells.
3. **Electrical** — energy in electric fields (capacitors, supercapacitors) and magnetic fields (SMES).
4. **Thermal** — sensible heat, latent heat, and thermochemical energy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Key:** Definition (1.5 marks) + Four forms (1.5 marks). Any 4 correct forms fetch full marks.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Explain the difference between "Specific Energy" and "Energy Density" of a storage system. Why is each metric important in different applications?**

**Model Answer:**

- **Specific Energy (Wh/kg)** is the energy stored per unit mass of the system. It is critical for **weight-sensitive** applications like electric vehicles, drones, and spacecraft, where every kilogram of mass reduces payload or range.
- **Energy Density (Wh/L)** is the energy stored per unit volume. It is critical for **volume-constrained** applications like smartphones, laptops, and wearable electronics, where the available enclosure volume is limited.

The two metrics govern different design constraints: automotive engineers prioritize specific energy, while consumer-electronics engineers prioritize energy density.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** Many students confuse the units. A common error is reporting Wh/kg when asked for Wh/L. Memorize: **mass → kg**, **volume → L**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and contains two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. You must answer **either** OR-style alternative entirely.

---

#### **OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Define State of Charge (SOC) and Depth of Discharge (DOD) for a battery system. Derive the mathematical relationship between them. A 200 Ah lead-acid battery is discharged for 6 hours at a constant current of 25 A. If the rated capacity is 240 Ah, calculate the SOC and DOD at the end of the discharge period. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of Round-Trip Efficiency of an ESS. A battery storage system receives 10 kWh of electrical energy during charging and delivers 8.5 kWh during discharge. Calculate the round-trip efficiency. If the system undergoes 365 charge-discharge cycles per year, calculate the annual energy loss. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

**Definition of SOC:** State of Charge is the ratio of the remaining capacity in a battery to its rated (maximum) capacity, expressed as a percentage.

$$SOC(t) = \frac{Q_{remain}(t)}{Q_{rated}} \times 100\%$$

**Definition of DOD:** Depth of Discharge is the fraction of the rated capacity that has been withdrawn from the battery. It is the complement of SOC.

$$DOD = \frac{Q_{used}}{Q_{rated}} = 1 - SOC \quad \text{(in fraction)}$$

**Derivation of relationship:** Since $Q_{used} + Q_{remain} = Q_{rated}$, dividing by $Q_{rated}$:

$$\frac{Q_{used}}{Q_{rated}} + \frac{Q_{remain}}{Q_{rated}} = 1$$

Therefore: $DOD + SOC = 1$ (in fraction), or $DOD\% + SOC\% = 100\%$.

**Numerical Calculation:**

Charge withdrawn in 6 hours at 25 A:

$$Q_{used} = I \times t = 25 \text{ A} \times 6 \text{ h} = 150 \text{ Ah}$$

Depth of Discharge:

$$DOD = \frac{Q_{used}}{Q_{rated}} = \frac{150}{240} = 0.625 = 62.5\%$$

State of Charge:

$$SOC = 1 - DOD = 1 - 0.625 = 0.375 = 37.5\%$$

**Part (b):**

**Round-Trip Efficiency Definition:** Round-trip efficiency is the ratio of the total electrical energy delivered by an ESS during discharge to the total electrical energy supplied to it during charging, expressed as a percentage. It accounts for all losses including resistive (I²R), overpotential, parasitic, and self-discharge losses.

$$\eta_{RT} = \frac{E_{discharge}}{E_{charge}} \times 100\%$$

**Calculation of Round-Trip Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{RT} = \frac{8.5 \text{ kWh}}{10 \text{ kWh}} \times 100\% = 85\%$$

**Annual Energy Loss Calculation:**

Energy loss per cycle:

$$E_{loss,cycle} = E_{charge} - E_{discharge} = 10 - 8.5 = 1.5 \text{ kWh/cycle}$$

Annual energy loss (365 cycles):

$$E_{loss,annual} = 1.5 \text{ kWh/cycle} \times 365 \text{ cycles} = 547.5 \text{ kWh/year}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Mark Deductions:**
> - Students often **forget to multiply by 100%** in SOC and efficiency formulas. Always include the percentage sign explicitly.
> - Confusing **Ah** (Ampere-hours) with **C** (Coulombs) is a frequent error. $1 \text{ Ah} = 3600 \text{ C}$. For the KTU numerical part, stick to Ah.
> - In the efficiency question, **do not confuse charge/discharge energy** — students often divide in the wrong direction. Always: **Output ÷ Input**.

---

#### **OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the major functional components of a generic Energy Storage System. Briefly describe the role of each component. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A flywheel energy storage system uses a solid cylindrical rotor of mass 500 kg and radius 0.5 m, rotating at 10,000 rpm. Calculate (i) the moment of inertia, (ii) the angular velocity in rad/s, and (iii) the kinetic energy stored. If 80% of this energy can be extracted, how long can it supply a 5 kW load? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

The major functional components of a generic ESS are (refer to Section 4.1 diagram):

1. **Energy Source** (Grid / Solar PV / Wind): Primary input of electrical energy.
2. **Power Conditioning Unit — Input** (AC-DC Rectifier / DC-DC Converter): Conditions and controls input voltage/current to safe charging levels.
3. **Energy Storage Medium** (Battery Cells / Bank / Flywheel / Capacitor): The heart of the system where energy is stored in a chosen physical form.
4. **Thermal Management Unit** (Cooling / Heating): Maintains optimal operating temperature to prevent overheating or cold-induced performance loss.
5. **Power Conditioning Unit — Output** (DC-AC Inverter / DC-DC Converter): Converts stored energy back to usable form (AC mains or regulated DC).
6. **Battery Management System (BMS)**: Central controller monitoring SOC, SOH, voltage, current, and temperature; executes protection logic.
7. **Protection Circuit** (Breakers / Fuses): Disconnects the system during overcurrent, overvoltage, or short-circuit faults.
8. **State Monitoring Sensors**: Provide real-time data to the BMS for control and protection.

**Part (b):**

**(i) Moment of Inertia of solid cylinder:**

$$I = \frac{1}{2} m r^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 500 \text{ kg} \times (0.5 \text{ m})^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 500 \times 0.25 = 62.5 \text{ kg·m}^2$$

**(ii) Angular velocity in rad/s:**

$$\omega = 2\pi N = 2\pi \times \frac{10{,}000}{60} = 2\pi \times 166.67 = 1047.2 \text{ rad/s}$$

**(iii) Kinetic energy stored:**

$$E_{FW} = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 62.5 \times (1047.2)^2 = 0.5 \times 62.5 \times 1{,}096{,}632 = 34{,}269{,}750 \text{ J} \approx 34.27 \text{ MJ}$$

**Extractable energy and runtime:**

$$E_{extract} = 0.80 \times 34.27 \text{ MJ} = 27.416 \text{ MJ} = 27{,}416 \text{ kJ}$$

Convert to kWh (1 kWh = 3.6 × 10⁶ J):

$$E_{extract} = \frac{27.416 \times 10^6}{3.6 \times 10^6} = 7.615 \text{ kWh}$$

Discharge time at 5 kW load:

$$t = \frac{E_{extract}}{P_{load}} = \frac{7.615 \text{ kWh}}{5 \text{ kW}} = 1.523 \text{ hours} \approx 1 \text{ h } 31 \text{ min}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Mark Deductions:**
> - **rpm → rad/s conversion** is the most-skipped step. Students write $\omega = 10{,}000$ rad/s (wrong!). Correct: divide by 60 first, then multiply by $2\pi$.
> - **Unit conversion J → kWh** is mandatory in this type of question. $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \times 10^6$ J. Marks are awarded *only* if the final answer is in **kWh** when load is given in **kW**.
> - Always carry **2 decimal places** in the final answer to demonstrate precision.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — KTU Module 1: General Considerations**

- ✅ **Definition:** An ESS captures energy at one time for use at a later time. Includes accumulators, batteries (broadly), and ancillary systems.

- ✅ **Five Engineering Pillars:** Temporal Bridging, Power Quality, Peak Shaving, Grid Stability, Transport Electrification.

- ✅ **Five Energy Forms:** Mechanical, Electrochemical, Electrical, Thermal, Chemical.

- ✅ **Master the 20 formulas** in the cheat sheet — every KTU numerical question maps to one of them.

- ✅ **SOC + DOD = 100%** — never forget this complementarity.

- ✅ **C-rate is in $\text{h}^{-1}$** and $t = 1/C$ for full discharge.

- ✅ **Round-trip efficiency is always ≤ 100%**, with Li-ion ~85–95% and supercapacitors ~95–98%.

- ✅ **Energy units:** $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \times 10^6$ J $= 3600$ kJ.

- ✅ **Flywheel energy:** $E = \frac{1}{2}I\omega^2$; for a solid cylinder, $I = \frac{1}{2}mr^2$.

- ✅ **Capacitor energy:** $E = \frac{1}{2}CV^2$ — note the factor of ½.

- ✅ **Pumped hydro energy:** $E = mgh$; $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ (always include this constant).

- ✅ **Self-discharge** follows exponential decay: $Q(t) = Q_0 e^{-kt}$.

- ✅ **Peukert's Law** ($t = H/I^n$) applies **only** to lead-acid batteries — do not use it for Li-ion.

- ✅ **Ragone Plot** = Specific Energy (X) vs. Specific Power (Y), both on log scale; defines the energy-power trade-off.

- ✅ **Calendar aging** uses $\sqrt{t}$ dependence; **Cycle aging** uses $N$ (number of cycles) dependence.

- ✅ **Faraday constant:** $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol; useful in electrochemistry calculations.

- ✅ **In every KTU 14-mark question:** Show the formula first, substitute step-by-step, then box the final answer with **correct units**.

- ✅ **Avoid the trap:** "Energy stored" and "Energy delivered" are **different** — efficiency bridges them.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy and Power Balance in a Storage Unit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET521 - ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Energy and Power Balance in a Storage Unit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Energy Storage Unit (ESU)** is a device or system engineered to absorb electrical energy from a source, retain (store) it in some physical or chemical form over a defined period, and subsequently release it as electrical energy when required by a load. The two fundamental ratings that completely characterize any storage unit are:

1. **Energy Capacity (E)**: The *total quantity of energy* the device can hold, measured in **kilowatt-hours (kWh)** or **megawatt-hours (MWh)**.
2. **Power Rating (P)**: The *maximum rate* at which energy can be transferred in or out of the device, measured in **kilowatts (kW)** or **megawatts (MW)**.

The **Energy and Power Balance** in a storage unit refers to the conservation of energy that governs the instantaneous and cumulative state of the system, accounting for incoming energy, outgoing energy, internal losses, and the resulting change in stored energy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The energy–power relationship forms the analytical backbone of every storage technology discussed in this course. The Board Examiner expects students to clearly distinguish between **Energy Rating** (kWh) and **Power Rating** (kW), and to write the governing balance equation from first principles.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **large overhead water tank** connected to a pump (charging system) and a tap (load). The **size of the tank** is like the *energy capacity* — it tells you *how much water (energy) can be stored*. The **diameter of the connecting pipe** is like the *power rating* — it tells you *how fast water can flow in or out*.

If you have a giant tank but a thin pipe, you can store a lot of water but it takes forever to fill or empty. Conversely, a fat pipe attached to a tiny tank can deliver water very fast but only for a short time. An energy storage unit behaves exactly the same way: a battery with high energy capacity but low power rating can run a load for a long time at low power, whereas a supercapacitor with low energy but very high power can deliver a huge burst of energy for a few seconds.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — State of Charge (SOC):** The SOC of a storage unit is the *instantaneous fraction of usable energy remaining* relative to its rated capacity. It is analogous to the water level indicator on the tank.
>
> $$\text{SOC}(t) = \frac{E_{\text{stored}}(t)}{E_{\text{rated}}} \times 100\%$$

## 1.3 The Two Independent Specifications

| Parameter | Symbol | Common Units | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **Energy Capacity** | $E$ | **kWh, MWh** | Total storable energy |
| **Power Rating** | $P$ | **kW, MW** | Maximum charge/discharge rate |
| **Discharge Duration** | $t$ | **hours (h)** | $E \div P$ |
| **C-Rate** | $C$ | **1/h** | $P \div E$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric to Remember:** The **C-rate** (also called the *hourly rate*) unifies energy and power into a single dimensionless indicator. A 1C rate discharges the entire battery in 1 hour; a 2C rate discharges it in 0.5 hours (30 minutes); a 0.5C rate discharges it in 2 hours.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Energy–Power Relationship

The instantaneous power flow $P(t)$ into or out of a storage unit is defined as the *time derivative* of stored energy. Conversely, the stored energy is the *time integral* of power over the operating window:

$$P(t) = \frac{dE_{\text{stored}}(t)}{dt}$$

$$E_{\text{stored}}(t) = E_{0} + \int_{0}^{t} P_{\text{net}}(\tau) \, d\tau$$

where the **net power** flowing into the storage is positive for charging and negative for discharging.

### The Logic Behind Each Step

- **Step 1 — Sign Convention:** Define $P_{\text{in}}$ as power entering the storage (charging) and $P_{\text{out}}$ as power leaving (discharging). The net is $P_{\text{net}} = P_{\text{in}} - P_{\text{out}}$.
- **Step 2 — Integrate:** Integrating the differential form over a time horizon converts rate of change into a cumulative quantity — this is essentially the *work–energy theorem* applied to the storage medium.
- **Step 3 — Apply Boundary Conditions:** The initial condition $E_{0}$ is the energy already present at $t = 0$ (often the minimum SOC allowed by the Battery Management System).

## 2.2 The General Energy Balance Equation

The complete, examiner-ready energy balance for any storage unit is:

$$E_{\text{stored}}(t + \Delta t) = E_{\text{stored}}(t) + \underbrace{\eta_{\text{chg}} \, P_{\text{in}} \, \Delta t}_{\text{Charging Energy}} - \underbrace{\frac{P_{\text{out}} \, \Delta t}{\eta_{\text{dis}}}}_{\text{Discharging Energy}} - \underbrace{E_{\text{loss}}}_{\text{Parasitic + Self-discharge}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Round-Trip Efficiency Insight:** No real storage system is 100% efficient. Charging efficiency $\eta_{\text{chg}}$ and discharging efficiency $\eta_{\text{dis}}$ both are less than 1. Their product is called the **round-trip efficiency** $\eta_{\text{RT}} = \eta_{\text{chg}} \times \eta_{\text{dis}}$, a key metric the KTU examiner often asks for.

## 2.3 Power Balance (Instantaneous Form)

While energy balance describes the *cumulative* state, the **power balance** enforces instantaneous conservation at every moment:

$$P_{\text{net}}(t) = P_{\text{storage}}(t) + P_{\text{loss}}(t) + P_{\text{aux}}(t)$$

- $P_{\text{storage}}(t)$: Power actually absorbed by the storage medium.
- $P_{\text{loss}}(t)$: Power dissipated as heat (Joule losses, friction, chemical irreversibilities).
- $P_{\text{aux}}(t)$: Power consumed by auxiliary systems (cooling fans, BMS electronics, pumps).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $E = P \times t$ | Energy if power is constant | kWh |
| 2 | $E(t) = \int P(t) \, dt$ | Energy for time-varying power | kWh |
| 3 | $P(t) = dE/dt$ | Instantaneous power | kW |
| 4 | $\text{SOC}(t) = E(t) \div E_{\text{rated}} \times 100$ | State of Charge | % |
| 5 | $\text{DOD} = 1 - \text{SOC}$ | Depth of Discharge | fraction |
| 6 | $C\text{-rate} = P \div E_{\text{rated}}$ | Charge/discharge rate | 1/h |
| 7 | $t_{\text{discharge}} = E_{\text{rated}} \div P = 1 \div C$ | Discharge duration | hours |
| 8 | $\eta_{\text{RT}} = E_{\text{out}} \div E_{\text{in}}$ | Round-trip efficiency | fraction |
| 9 | $E_{\text{loss}} = P_{\text{loss}} \times t$ | Energy lost over time $t$ | kWh |
| 10 | $P_{\text{loss}} = I^{2}R$ (resistive) | Joule heating | W |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

- **Grid-Scale Storage:** Energy–power balance dictates how a storage system stabilizes the grid during peak demand and absorbs excess renewable generation during off-peak hours.
- **Electric Vehicles:** Determines the *range* (energy) versus *acceleration performance* (power) trade-off.
- **Uninterruptible Power Supplies (UPS):** High-power, short-duration discharge requires different chemistry (supercapacitors) than long-duration backup (lithium-ion or flow batteries).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Energy Stored During Constant-Power Discharge

**Problem Setup:** A battery pack rated at $E_{\text{rated}} = 50 \,\text{kWh}$ delivers a constant power $P = 10 \,\text{kW}$ to a load. Assuming 100% discharging efficiency, derive the SOC as a function of time and determine the discharge duration.

**Step 1 — Write the differential energy balance.**
During pure discharge (no charging), the net power leaving the storage is $P_{\text{out}}$. The energy remaining in the battery after time $t$ is:

$$E_{\text{stored}}(t) = E_{\text{rated}} - P_{\text{out}} \cdot t$$

**Step 2 — Convert to State of Charge.**
Dividing both sides by $E_{\text{rated}}$ and multiplying by 100 gives the SOC in percent:

$$\text{SOC}(t) = \frac{E_{\text{rated}} - P_{\text{out}} \cdot t}{E_{\text{rated}}} \times 100\%$$

$$\text{SOC}(t) = \left( 1 - \frac{P_{\text{out}} \cdot t}{E_{\text{rated}}} \right) \times 100\%$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values.**
With $P_{\text{out}} = 10 \,\text{kW}$ and $E_{\text{rated}} = 50 \,\text{kWh}$:

$$\text{SOC}(t) = \left( 1 - \frac{10 \cdot t}{50} \right) \times 100\% = \left( 1 - 0.2 \, t \right) \times 100\%$$

**Step 4 — Find the discharge duration.**
Discharge ends when SOC reaches 0 (idealized, ignoring minimum SOC cutoff):

$$0 = 1 - 0.2 \, t \quad \Rightarrow \quad t = \frac{1}{0.2} = 5 \,\text{hours}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cross-Verification via C-Rate:** Here, the C-rate is $C = P/E_{\text{rated}} = 10/50 = 0.2 \, C$. The discharge duration should be $1/C = 1/0.2 = 5 \,\text{h}$ — which exactly matches our derivation. ✓

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Energy Balance with Round-Trip Efficiency

**Problem Setup:** A storage unit with rated capacity $E_{\text{rated}} = 100 \,\text{kWh}$ and round-trip efficiency $\eta_{\text{RT}} = 0.85$ is charged from an empty state at constant input power $P_{\text{in}} = 20 \,\text{kW}$ until fully charged. Determine:
1. The total electrical energy drawn from the grid.
2. The usable energy available for later discharge.
3. The total energy lost.

**Step 1 — Energy absorbed by the storage (ideal stored energy).**
To charge the battery to its rated capacity:

$$E_{\text{stored}} = E_{\text{rated}} = 100 \,\text{kWh}$$

**Step 2 — Energy drawn from the grid (input energy).**
Because charging is inefficient, the grid must supply more energy than what is actually stored. If charging efficiency is $\eta_{\text{chg}} = \sqrt{\eta_{\text{RT}}} \approx 0.922$ (assuming symmetric charge/discharge):

$$E_{\text{in}} = \frac{E_{\text{stored}}}{\eta_{\text{chg}}} = \frac{100}{0.922} \approx 108.46 \,\text{kWh}$$

**Step 3 — Usable discharge energy.**
With discharging efficiency $\eta_{\text{dis}} = \eta_{\text{chg}} = 0.922$:

$$E_{\text{out}} = E_{\text{stored}} \times \eta_{\text{dis}} = 100 \times 0.922 = 92.2 \,\text{kWh}$$

**Step 4 — Total losses.**

$$E_{\text{loss}} = E_{\text{in}} - E_{\text{out}} = 108.46 - 92.2 = 16.26 \,\text{kWh}$$

**Step 5 — Verify round-trip efficiency.**

$$\eta_{\text{RT}} = \frac{E_{\text{out}}}{E_{\text{in}}} = \frac{92.2}{108.46} = 0.85 \quad \checkmark$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Variable Power Profile (Discrete Time-Step Integration)

**Problem Setup:** A 200 kWh battery undergoes the following 1-hour schedule (5-minute intervals):

| Interval | Duration (h) | $P_{\text{in}}$ (kW) | $P_{\text{out}}$ (kW) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 0.2 | 100 | 0 |
| 2 | 0.2 | 50 | 0 |
| 3 | 0.2 | 0 | 80 |
| 4 | 0.2 | 0 | 120 |
| 5 | 0.2 | 60 | 0 |

Assume $\eta_{\text{chg}} = \eta_{\text{dis}} = 1.0$ and initial SOC = 30%. Compute final SOC.

**Step 1 — Compute net energy in each interval using $E = P_{\text{net}} \times \Delta t$.**

$$E_{1} = (100 - 0) \times 0.2 = +20 \,\text{kWh}$$

$$E_{2} = (50 - 0) \times 0.2 = +10 \,\text{kWh}$$

$$E_{3} = (0 - 80) \times 0.2 = -16 \,\text{kWh}$$

$$E_{4} = (0 - 120) \times 0.2 = -24 \,\text{kWh}$$

$$E_{5} = (60 - 0) \times 0.2 = +12 \,\text{kWh}$$

**Step 2 — Sum the net energies.**

$$E_{\text{net}} = 20 + 10 - 16 - 24 + 12 = +2 \,\text{kWh}$$

**Step 3 — Initial stored energy at SOC = 30%.**

$$E_{0} = 0.30 \times 200 = 60 \,\text{kWh}$$

**Step 4 — Final stored energy.**

$$E_{\text{final}} = E_{0} + E_{\text{net}} = 60 + 2 = 62 \,\text{kWh}$$

**Step 5 — Final SOC.**

$$\text{SOC}_{\text{final}} = \frac{62}{200} \times 100\% = 31\%$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Energy Storage Unit

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the functional interactions of an energy storage unit during charge and discharge, explicitly showing the energy and power flow pathways that the KTU examiner expects students to recognize.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC[Power Source Grid PV Wind]:::source
    INV1[Power Converter AC DC]:::convert
    CTRL1[BMS Energy Management Controller]:::ctrl
    STORE[Storage Medium Battery Capacitor Flywheel]:::store
    INV2[Power Converter DC AC]:::convert
    LOAD[Electrical Load Grid Home EV]:::load
    LOSS[Losses Heat Parasitic Self Discharge]:::loss
    METER1[Energy Meter E in kWh]:::meter
    METER2[Power Meter P in kW]:::meter

    SRC --> INV1
    INV1 --> METER1
    METER1 --> STORE
    STORE --> INV2
    INV2 --> LOAD
    STORE --> LOSS
    CTRL1 -. monitors and controls .-> STORE
    CTRL1 -. regulates .-> INV1
    CTRL1 -. regulates .-> INV2
    METER2 -. measures instantaneous flow .-> STORE

    classDef source fill:#FFD580,stroke:#8B5A00,color:#000
    classDef convert fill:#A0C4FF,stroke:#1E3A8A,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#B5EAD7,stroke:#0F766E,color:#000
    classDef store fill:#FFB5A7,stroke:#9D174D,color:#000
    classDef load fill:#D8B4FE,stroke:#6B21A8,color:#000
    classDef loss fill:#F1F5F9,stroke:#475569,color:#000
    classDef meter fill:#FEF08A,stroke:#854D0E,color:#000
```

### Reading the Diagram

- **Yellow (Power Source)** → **Blue (Power Converter)** → **Yellow Meter (Energy Accounting)** → **Red (Storage Medium)** → **Blue (Power Converter)** → **Purple (Load)**.
- The **Teal BMS Controller** continuously monitors the storage unit and regulates the converters to enforce SOC limits, C-rate limits, and thermal safety.
- The **Grey Loss Block** represents all parasitic and irreversibility losses — heat dissipation, self-discharge, and auxiliary consumption — which are subtracted in the energy balance.

## 4.2 Energy–Power Flow During Charge and Discharge

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Rated Capacity E rated kWh] --> B{Power Flow Direction}
    B -- Charging Mode --> C[P in greater than 0]
    C --> D[E stored increases over time]
    D --> E{Stop Condition}
    E -- SOC reaches 100 percent --> F[Charge Complete]
    B -- Discharging Mode --> G[P out greater than 0]
    G --> H[E stored decreases over time]
    H --> I{Stop Condition}
    I -- SOC reaches SOC min --> J[Discharge Complete]
    F --> K[Wait for next cycle]
    J --> K
    K --> B

    style A fill:#FEF08A,stroke:#854D0E,color:#000
    style B fill:#A0C4FF,stroke:#1E3A8A,color:#000
    style C fill:#B5EAD7,stroke:#0F766E,color:#000
    style D fill:#FFB5A7,stroke:#9D174D,color:#000
    style F fill:#86EFAC,stroke:#166534,color:#000
    style G fill:#FCA5A5,stroke:#991B1B,color:#000
    style H fill:#FDBA74,stroke:#9A3412,color:#000
    style J fill:#D8B4FE,stroke:#6B21A8,color:#000
```

### Diagram Interpretation

- The storage unit **oscillates between charge and discharge cycles**, never operating in both modes simultaneously.
- The **decision block** represents the operating mode selected by the system controller based on grid demand, renewable availability, or load requirement.
- The **SOC limits** (typically 10–20% lower bound and 90–95% upper bound for lithium-ion batteries) prevent over-charge and over-discharge damage.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** Define the terms *State of Charge (SOC)*, *Depth of Discharge (DOD)*, and *C-rate* with respect to an energy storage system. Establish the mathematical relationship between them.

**Model Answer:**

- **State of Charge (SOC):** It is the ratio of the *instantaneous usable energy* in the storage unit to its *rated (maximum) energy capacity*, expressed as a percentage.

$$\text{SOC} = \frac{E_{\text{stored}}(t)}{E_{\text{rated}}} \times 100\%$$

- **Depth of Discharge (DOD):** It is the *complement* of SOC, i.e., the fraction of energy that has been withdrawn.

$$\text{DOD} = 1 - \text{SOC}$$

- **C-Rate:** It is the ratio of the *charge or discharge power* to the *rated energy capacity*, with units of $\text{h}^{-1}$.

$$C\text{-rate} = \frac{P}{E_{\text{rated}}}$$

- **Relationship:** Discharge duration $t_{\text{dis}}$ and C-rate are reciprocals: $t_{\text{dis}} = E_{\text{rated}}/P = 1/C\text{-rate}$. A 1C discharge empties the unit in 1 hour; a 0.5C discharge empties it in 2 hours.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** What is *round-trip efficiency* of an energy storage unit? Why is it always less than 100%?

**Model Answer:**

The **round-trip efficiency (η_RT)** is the ratio of the *electrical energy delivered on discharge* to the *electrical energy absorbed on charge* for one complete storage cycle.

$$\eta_{\text{RT}} = \frac{E_{\text{out}}}{E_{\text{in}}}$$

It is always less than 100% because:

1. **Joule heating losses** ($I^{2}R$ losses) occur in the internal resistance of the storage medium during both charge and discharge.
2. **Chemical irreversibilities** in batteries cause side reactions and permanent capacity fade.
3. **Self-discharge** continuously bleeds energy over time, even when idle.
4. **Auxiliary loads** (cooling, BMS, pumps) consume part of the stored energy.

Typical values: Lithium-ion ≈ 85–95%, Lead-acid ≈ 70–85%, Pumped hydro ≈ 75–85%.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1]`

**A battery energy storage system (BESS) has a rated capacity of 250 kWh and a power rating of 50 kW. The battery is initially at SOC = 40%.**

**(a)** Determine the discharge duration if the battery delivers constant power at its rated value, and compute the final SOC after 3 hours of continuous discharge at rated power.

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** If the round-trip efficiency of the BESS is 88% and it is then fully recharged from the grid at constant input power of 50 kW, calculate: (i) the energy drawn from the grid, (ii) the total energy lost during the charge cycle, and (iii) the time taken to recharge from empty.

**(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Compute the discharge duration at rated power.**

$$t_{\text{discharge}} = \frac{E_{\text{rated}}}{P_{\text{rated}}} = \frac{250}{50} = 5 \,\text{hours}$$

**Step 2 — Initial stored energy.**

$$E_{0} = 0.40 \times 250 = 100 \,\text{kWh}$$

**Step 3 — Energy delivered in 3 hours.**

$$E_{\text{delivered}} = P \times t = 50 \times 3 = 150 \,\text{kWh}$$

**Step 4 — Check feasibility.**
The energy available is only 100 kWh, but the load demands 150 kWh in 3 hours. The battery can sustain rated power for only:

$$t_{\text{available}} = \frac{E_{0}}{P} = \frac{100}{50} = 2 \,\text{hours}$$

**Step 5 — Final SOC after 3 hours.**
Since the battery is depleted after 2 hours, SOC at $t = 3$ h is 0% (or SOC_min, typically 10% in real systems).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Part (a):** Students often blindly write the formula $E = P \times t = 50 \times 3 = 150$ kWh without verifying that the battery *has* 150 kWh to deliver. Always cross-check $E_{\text{delivered}}$ against $E_{0}$.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Use round-trip efficiency relation.**
Assuming symmetric charge/discharge, $\eta_{\text{chg}} = \eta_{\text{dis}} = \sqrt{0.88} \approx 0.9381$.

**Step 2 — (i) Energy drawn from the grid to fully charge the battery from empty.**
To store 250 kWh in the battery (rated capacity):

$$E_{\text{in}} = \frac{E_{\text{rated}}}{\eta_{\text{chg}}} = \frac{250}{0.9381} \approx 266.5 \,\text{kWh}$$

**Step 3 — (ii) Total energy lost during charging.**

$$E_{\text{loss,chg}} = E_{\text{in}} - E_{\text{rated}} = 266.5 - 250 = 16.5 \,\text{kWh}$$

**Step 4 — (iii) Time to recharge from empty at $P_{\text{in}} = 50$ kW.**

$$t_{\text{recharge}} = \frac{E_{\text{in}}}{P_{\text{in}}} = \frac{266.5}{50} \approx 5.33 \,\text{hours}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Part (b):** A common error is dividing the rated energy (250 kWh) by the input power, getting exactly 5 hours. The correct answer is *longer* than 5 hours because losses are incurred during charging. Students must remember: $\eta_{\text{chg}} < 1$ always increases recharge time.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1 - Alternate]`

**(a)** Define *energy density* and *power density*. Explain why a Ragone plot is used to characterize storage technologies. Sketch and explain the typical Ragone plot for batteries, supercapacitors, and fuel cells.

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 100 kWh lithium-ion battery operates for a 24-hour period with the following hourly profile: charges at 20 kW for 6 hours, idles (no charge/discharge) for 4 hours, discharges at 15 kW for 8 hours, and idles for 6 hours. Assuming the battery starts at SOC = 50% and $\eta_{\text{chg}} = \eta_{\text{dis}} = 0.95$, compute the SOC at the end of the 24-hour cycle.

**(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Define energy density and power density.**

- **Energy Density ($E/\text{Volume}$ or $E/\text{Mass}$):** The amount of energy stored per unit volume (Wh/L) or per unit mass (Wh/kg). Determines how *long* a device can run.
- **Power Density ($P/\text{Volume}$ or $P/\text{Mass}$):** The peak power deliverable per unit volume (W/L) or per unit mass (W/kg). Determines how *fast* energy can be released.

**Step 2 — Ragone Plot Definition.**
A Ragone plot is a **log-log chart** of *specific energy* (Wh/kg) on the x-axis versus *specific power* (W/kg) on the y-axis. It allows engineers to *visually compare* different storage technologies and select the appropriate one based on application requirements.

**Step 3 — Qualitative description of technology regions.**

| Technology | Specific Energy | Specific Power | Typical Application |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Fuel Cells | Very High | Low | Long-duration EVs, grid backup |
| Lithium-ion Batteries | High | Medium | EVs, consumer electronics |
| Supercapacitors | Low | Very High | Regenerative braking, pulse power |

> [!NOTE]
> **Skeletal Ragone Plot Reference:**
> - **X-axis:** Specific Energy (Wh/kg), log scale, 1 to 1000.
> - **Y-axis:** Specific Power (W/kg), log scale, 1 to 10000.
> - Fuel cells lie in the *lower-right* (high energy, low power).
> - Batteries lie in the *middle*.
> - Supercapacitors lie in the *upper-left* (low energy, high power).

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Initialize the calculation.**

$$E_{0} = 0.50 \times 100 = 50 \,\text{kWh}$$

**Step 2 — Energy absorbed during 6 hours of charging.**

$$E_{\text{chg,ideal}} = 20 \times 6 = 120 \,\text{kWh}$$

But the battery cannot store more than its rated capacity minus current stored, i.e., $100 - 50 = 50$ kWh. So the *actual* stored energy added is capped at 50 kWh (battery reaches 100% SOC after 2.5 h, then must idle).

- Charging phase effective storage: $E_{\text{stored}} = 50$ kWh.
- Time to fully charge: $t_{\text{charge}} = 50/20 = 2.5$ h.
- Remaining 3.5 h of "charging" hours must be *curtailed* (or simply not absorbed) — battery is full.

**Step 3 — SOC after charging block.**

$$\text{SOC}_{\text{after chg}} = \frac{100}{100} \times 100\% = 100\%$$

**Step 4 — Energy delivered during 8 hours of discharging.**

Energy available for discharge at the battery terminals: $E_{\text{dis,terminal}} = 100 \times 0.95 = 95$ kWh (with $\eta_{\text{dis}} = 0.95$).

- Energy drawn from battery internal storage = $100$ kWh (entire charge).
- Energy delivered to load = $95$ kWh (5 kWh lost).

**Step 5 — Check if 8 hours of 15 kW discharge is feasible.**

$$E_{\text{dis,demanded}} = 15 \times 8 = 120 \,\text{kWh}$$

But only 100 kWh is available — battery is depleted after:

$$t_{\text{dis,actual}} = \frac{100}{15} \approx 6.67 \,\text{hours}$$

**Step 6 — Final SOC after 24 hours.**

$$\text{SOC}_{\text{final}} = \frac{100 - 100}{100} \times 100\% = 0\%$$

The battery reaches 0% (or SOC_min) before the 8-hour discharge window completes.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Part (b):** Students often add all 120 kWh of charging and all 120 kWh of discharging as if the battery has unlimited capacity. The SOC must be **clamped** between 0% and 100%. Ignoring saturation is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **General KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **Unit consistency:** Always express $E$ in **kWh** and $P$ in **kW** when computing duration in hours. Mixing kJ and kW is a frequent error.
> 2. **Sign discipline:** Be explicit — charging $P > 0$, discharging $P < 0$. The board examiner awards partial credit only when the sign convention is clearly stated.
> 3. **SOC limits:** Real batteries cannot operate below SOC_min (typically 10–20%) or above SOC_max (90–95%). Students should mention this in descriptive answers.
> 4. **C-rate vs C:** Always distinguish the *C-rate* (a number in 1/h) from the *battery capacity C* (in Ah). They are *not* the same quantity.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Energy Capacity ($E$):** Quantity of energy stored, measured in **kWh**.
- **Power Rating ($P$):** Maximum rate of energy transfer, measured in **kW**.
- **Fundamental Equation:** $E = P \times t$ for constant power; $E(t) = \int P(t) \, dt$ for variable power.
- **SOC** is the *percentage* of rated capacity currently stored; **DOD** is its complement.
- **C-rate** = $P/E_{\text{rated}}$; discharge time = $1/C\text{-rate}$ in hours.
- **Energy Balance Equation:** $E(t + \Delta t) = E(t) + \eta_{\text{chg}} P_{\text{in}} \Delta t - P_{\text{out}} \Delta t/\eta_{\text{dis}} - E_{\text{loss}}$.
- **Round-Trip Efficiency:** $\eta_{\text{RT}} = E_{\text{out}}/E_{\text{in}}$, always less than 1 due to Joule heating, chemical irreversibility, self-discharge, and auxiliary loads.
- **Power Balance:** $P_{\text{net}} = P_{\text{storage}} + P_{\text{loss}} + P_{\text{aux}}$ must hold *instantaneously* at every moment.
- **Ragone Plot:** Log-log plot of specific power vs specific energy used to compare storage technologies visually.
- **Real Battery Limits:** SOC_min and SOC_max (typically 10–20% and 90–95%) bound the usable energy window.
- **Common Pitfall:** Students confuse *energy* (kWh) with *power* (kW); remember, *energy* is the *capacity to do work*, while *power* is the *rate of doing work*.
- **Discharging a battery beyond its rated capacity is impossible** — the SOC must always remain in the $[0, 100\%]$ range, and in practice, within $[\text{SOC}_{\min}, \text{SOC}_{\max}]$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mathematical Model of Storage System: Modelling of Power Transformation System (PTS), Central Store (CS) and Charge-Discharge Control System (CDCS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET521 - ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Mathematical Model of Storage System: Modelling of Power Transformation System (PTS), Central Store (CS) and Charge-Discharge Control System (CDCS)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Storage System** in electrical engineering is an integrated hardware-software unit designed to absorb energy from a source, retain it over a defined temporal window, and release it on demand to a load. The **Mathematical Model of a Storage System** is a set of coupled differential-algebraic equations (DAEs) that abstract the three functional sub-blocks of any modern energy storage device:

1. **Power Transformation System (PTS)** – the power-electronic interface (rectifier, inverter, DC–DC converter) that conditions the raw grid/load power into a form compatible with the storage medium.
2. **Central Store (CS)** – the actual energy reservoir (battery cell, supercapacitor, flywheel, PHES reservoir, thermal mass, hydrogen tank, etc.) where energy is held in chemical, electrochemical, electrical, mechanical, or thermal form.
3. **Charge–Discharge Control System (CDCS)** – the supervisory controller (analog, digital, or hybrid) that regulates the magnitude, direction, and rate of energy flow between the PTS, the CS, and the external terminals.

Mathematically, the three blocks are linked by power-flow continuity and a state-of-charge (SOC) feedback law, producing a closed-loop dynamical model suitable for simulation, controller design, and grid-integrated studies.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Bank Account" View)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain-English Intuition (Analogy: Water Tower & Pump Station)**
> Think of the storage system as a **municipal water tower**:
> - The **Power Transformation System (PTS)** is the **pump station** that lifts water (energy) up to the tower — it can pump water in (rectify/charge) or release water out (invert/discharge), but never both at the same time.
> - The **Central Store (CS)** is the **water tank** itself — its "level" is the *State of Charge* (SOC). The tank has a finite capacity (rated energy $E_{rated}$) and slowly loses water through leaks (self-discharge losses $P_{sd}$).
> - The **Charge-Discharge Control System (CDCS)** is the **automatic float-valve and SCADA operator** — it watches the tank level (SOC) and tells the pumps (PTS) when to turn on, when to turn off, and at what speed (power set-point $P_{ref}$).
>
> The **mathematical model** is simply the set of equations that describe the pump's flow rate, the tank's level dynamics, and the float-valve's decision logic — all coupled together.

## 1.3 Block-Level Physical Layout

The power-flow directionality is governed entirely by the CDCS, and the PTS operates bidirectionally:

$$
\text{Grid/Load} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \text{PTS} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \text{CS} \;\;\;\;(\text{monitored by CDCS})
$$

**Key physical/sign convention** adopted in the KTU syllabus:

| Symbol | Quantity | Unit | Convention |
|---|---|---|---|
| $P_g$ | Power at grid/load terminal | W | Positive = discharging (delivery) |
| $P_{pts}$ | Power internal to PTS | W | Positive = flowing toward CS |
| $P_{cs}$ | Power at CS terminals | W | Positive = charging the store |
| $E_{cs}$ | Energy stored in CS | J or Wh | Always non-negative |
| $SOC$ | State of Charge | per-unit (0–1) | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
> The KTU Module-1 syllabus explicitly states: *"Develop a mathematical model of the storage system considering the Power Transformation System (PTS), Central Store (CS) and Charge-Discharge Control System (CDCS)."* This means a complete answer must address **all three blocks** with **state equations**, not just a verbal description.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Block 1 — Power Transformation System (PTS) Model

The PTS is, in essence, a **switched-mode power converter** (AC–DC rectifier, DC–DC chopper, or DC–AC inverter). It is *not* an ideal transformer — it has conduction losses, switching losses, and (in some chemistries) standby losses.

### 2.1.1 Static Efficiency Model of PTS

The simplest engineering-grade model treats the PTS as a **power-dependent efficiency element**:

$$
\eta_{pts}(P) \;=\; \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \;=\; \frac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_{loss,pts}}
$$

For a real converter, the loss is a quadratic function of the operating current $I$ (copper loss $I^{2}R$ + iron/switching loss $k_{sw}$):

$$
P_{loss,pts}(I) \;=\; R_{eq}\,I^{2} \;+\; k_{sw}\,I \;+\; P_{0}
$$

where $R_{eq}$ is the equivalent series resistance, $k_{sw}$ is a switching-loss coefficient, and $P_{0}$ is the no-load draw. The current itself is related to power by $I = P/V_{bus}$, giving a power-dependent efficiency curve.

### 2.1.2 Sign-Sensitive Power Equation of PTS

Because power flow is bidirectional, the KTU convention writes the PTS power balance in a single conditional equation:

$$
P_{cs}(t) \;=\; s(t)\,\eta_{pts}^{c}\,\frac{P_{g}(t)}{1} \;-\; s(t)\,\frac{P_{g}(t)}{\eta_{pts}^{d}}
$$

where $s(t) = +1$ during charging and $s(t) = -1$ during discharging, and $\eta_{pts}^{c}, \eta_{pts}^{d}$ are the charge and discharge efficiencies of the PTS, respectively. In steady-state, these are often lumped as a single round-trip efficiency $\eta_{rt} = \eta_{pts}^{c} \cdot \eta_{pts}^{d}$.

### 2.1.3 Dynamic (State-Space) Model of PTS

For transient studies (e.g., step-load response of the converter), the PTS is modeled as a **first-order lag** with time constant $\tau_{pts}$ (typically 1–20 ms for modern IGBT converters):

$$
\tau_{pts}\,\frac{dP_{pts}}{dt} \;+\; P_{pts} \;=\; P_{pts}^{ref}
$$

This is a continuous-time linear system. Discretized at the KTU-typical sampling period $T_{s} = 100\,\mu s$, it becomes the digital control loop governing the PWM duty cycle.

## 2.2 Block 2 — Central Store (CS) Model

The Central Store is the **energy reservoir itself**. Its state equation is a single energy-balance differential equation that must hold for *every* storage technology (battery, supercapacitor, flywheel, thermal, hydrogen, etc.):

$$
\frac{dE_{cs}(t)}{dt} \;=\; P_{in,cs}(t) \;-\; P_{out,cs}(t) \;-\; P_{sd}(t)
$$

where $P_{sd}$ is the **self-discharge** (or standby loss) power. For most electrochemical and mechanical stores, $P_{sd} = k_{sd}\,E_{cs}$ (exponential self-discharge, modeled linearly for small intervals).

### 2.2.1 Generic CS State Equation (Technology-Agnostic)

Dividing both sides by the rated energy $E_{rated}$:

$$
\frac{d\,\text{SOC}(t)}{dt} \;=\; \frac{P_{in,cs}(t) - P_{out,cs}(t) - P_{sd}(t)}{E_{rated}}
$$

The SOC is bounded:

$$
\text{SOC}_{min} \;\le\; \text{SOC}(t) \;\le\; \text{SOC}_{max}
$$

For Li-ion: $SOC_{min} = 0.10$, $SOC_{max} = 0.95$ (to preserve cycle life). For supercapacitors: $0.0 \le SOC \le 1.0$.

### 2.2.2 Technology-Specific CS Sub-Models

| Technology | State Variable | Differential Equation | Auxiliary Algebraic Equations |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Li-ion Battery** | SOC, $V_{oc}$ | $\frac{d\,\text{SOC}}{dt} = -\dfrac{I}{Q_{rated}}$ | $V_{term} = V_{oc}(\text{SOC}) - I\,R_{int}$ |
| **Supercapacitor** | $V_{c}$ | $C\,\dfrac{dV_{c}}{dt} = I$ | $E = \tfrac{1}{2}CV_{c}^{2}$ |
| **Flywheel** | $\omega$ | $J\,\dfrac{d\omega}{dt} = T_{m} - T_{L}$ | $E = \tfrac{1}{2}J\omega^{2}$ |
| **Pumped Hydro** | $h$ (head) | $A\,\dfrac{dh}{dt} = Q_{in} - Q_{out}$ | $P = \rho\,g\,Q\,h$ |
| **Hydrogen Tank** | $n_{H_2}$ | $\dfrac{dn_{H_2}}{dt} = \dot{n}_{prod} - \dot{n}_{cons}$ | $E = n_{H_2}\cdot\text{LHV}_{H_2}$ |

## 2.3 Block 3 — Charge-Discharge Control System (CDCS) Model

The CDCS is a **supervisory controller** that issues the power set-point $P_{pts}^{ref}$ to the PTS based on:
- an external command $P_{g}^{ref}$ (from the grid operator or local load demand),
- the measured SOC, and
- the operational limits of the CS.

### 2.3.1 Bang-Bang (Hysteresis) Controller — Most Common in KTU Problems

$$
P_{pts}^{ref}(t) \;=\; \begin{cases}
P_{ch,max}, & \text{if } \text{SOC}(t) \le \text{SOC}_{low} \;\text{and}\; P_{g}^{ref} > 0 \\[4pt]
P_{dis,max}, & \text{if } \text{SOC}(t) \ge \text{SOC}_{high} \;\text{and}\; P_{g}^{ref} < 0 \\[4pt]
0, & \text{otherwise (idle / hold)}
\end{cases}
$$

This is a **discontinuous control law** with hysteresis band $[\text{SOC}_{low}, \text{SOC}_{high}]$.

### 2.3.2 Proportional (Linear) Controller

A smoother alternative is a proportional controller with anti-windup:

$$
P_{pts}^{ref}(t) \;=\; K_{p}\,\bigl[\text{SOC}_{ref} - \text{SOC}(t)\bigr] \;+\; P_{g}^{ref}
$$

subject to saturation:

$$
P_{ch,max} \;\le\; P_{pts}^{ref}(t) \;\le\; P_{dis,max}
$$

The closed-loop dynamics then couple back into the CS state equation, producing a complete state-space system.

## 2.4 Coupled State-Space Model (The Complete KTU Answer)

Combining the three blocks yields a **2-state continuous-time model** in canonical form:

$$
\frac{dx_{1}}{dt} \;=\; \frac{-\,P_{pts}(t)/V_{cs} - I_{sd}}{Q_{rated}} \quad \text{(SOC dynamics)}
$$

$$
\frac{dx_{2}}{dt} \;=\; \frac{1}{\tau_{pts}}\bigl[P_{pts}^{ref}(t) - P_{pts}(t)\bigr] \quad \text{(PTS lag dynamics)}
$$

with output equation $y = V_{cs}(t) = V_{oc}(\text{SOC}) - R_{int}\,I(t)$.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation / Law | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta_{pts} = P_{out}/P_{in}$ | PTS efficiency | Energy audit, sizing |
| 2 | $P_{loss,pts} = R_{eq}I^{2} + k_{sw}I + P_{0}$ | Converter loss model | Thermal design |
| 3 | $\tau_{pts}\dot{P}_{pts} + P_{pts} = P_{pts}^{ref}$ | PTS first-order lag | Transient studies |
| 4 | $\dfrac{dE_{cs}}{dt} = P_{in} - P_{out} - P_{sd}$ | Energy balance | Universal CS model |
| 5 | $\dfrac{d\,\text{SOC}}{dt} = \dfrac{P_{net}}{E_{rated}}$ | SOC rate | All technologies |
| 6 | $\text{SOC}(t) = \text{SOC}_{0} + \dfrac{1}{E_{rated}}\displaystyle\int_{0}^{t}P_{net}(\tau)\,d\tau$ | Integral form | Simulation |
| 7 | $E_{cs} = \tfrac{1}{2}CV^{2}$ | Capacitive storage | Supercapacitor |
| 8 | $E_{cs} = \tfrac{1}{2}J\omega^{2}$ | Kinetic storage | Flywheel |
| 9 | $E_{cs} = \rho\,g\,Q\,h$ | Gravitational storage | PHES |
| 10 | $P_{pts}^{ref} = K_{p}[\text{SOC}_{ref}-\text{SOC}]$ | P-controller | CDCS design |
| 11 | $\eta_{rt} = \eta_{ch}\cdot\eta_{dis}$ | Round-trip efficiency | System rating |
| 12 | $P_{sd} = k_{sd}\,E_{cs}$ | Self-discharge | Long-duration storage |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility**
> This three-block model is the *backbone* of every commercial battery-management system (BMS), microgrid energy-management system (EMS), and grid-scale storage controller (e.g., Tesla Megapack, Fluence Gridstack, ABB Powerstore). It is also the standard model used in MATLAB/Simulink's "Battery + DC-DC" library and in PSCAD's energy-storage module.

## 2.6 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind the Three Blocks

- **Why separate PTS, CS, CDCS?** — Because their *time constants* differ by orders of magnitude: PTS is **milliseconds**, CS is **minutes to hours**, and CDCS sits in between (**seconds**). Decomposing the system lets the engineer design each sub-system at its own bandwidth — a classic *singular-perturbation* argument.
- **How do they couple?** — Power-flow continuity at the two interfaces (PTS–CS bus, and PTS–grid bus) plus the SOC feedback from CS to CDCS. This forms a **cascaded closed-loop** structure.

$$
\bigl[\text{CDCS} \to \text{PTS}\bigr] \to \bigl[\text{CS} \to \text{CDCS (feedback)}\bigr]
$$

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Central-Store (CS) Energy-Balance Equation

**Statement (to prove):** The energy stored in a generic Central Store evolves according to

$$
\frac{dE_{cs}(t)}{dt} \;=\; P_{in,cs}(t) \;-\; P_{out,cs}(t) \;-\; P_{sd}(t)
$$

**Step 1 — Energy Conservation Principle.**
By the First Law of Thermodynamics applied to a control volume surrounding the CS, the rate of change of internal energy equals the net energy inflow minus the losses:

$$
\frac{dE_{cs}}{dt} \;=\; \sum P_{in} \;-\; \sum P_{out} \;-\; \sum P_{loss}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the Power Streams.**
Three streams exist at the CS terminals: (i) inflow from PTS during charging $P_{in,cs}$, (ii) outflow to PTS during discharging $P_{out,cs}$, (iii) standby self-discharge $P_{sd}$. All other terms (radiation, chemical side-reactions) are bundled into $P_{sd}$ for engineering purposes.

**Step 3 — Substitute and Simplify.**

$$
\frac{dE_{cs}}{dt} \;=\; P_{in,cs} \;-\; P_{out,cs} \;-\; P_{sd}
$$

This is the **Universal CS Equation**, valid for *every* storage technology, provided $E_{cs}$ is the *useful extractable* energy (i.e., not the total chemical enthalpy, which is technology-specific).

**Step 4 — Normalize to State-of-Charge.**
Dividing both sides by the rated (maximum extractable) energy $E_{rated}$:

$$
\frac{d\,\text{SOC}}{dt} \;=\; \frac{P_{in,cs} - P_{out,cs} - P_{sd}}{E_{rated}}
$$

This converts the energy equation into the **dimensionless SOC equation** universally used in KTU problems.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — PTS Power-Loss and Efficiency Model

**Statement (to prove):** The PTS efficiency is

$$
\eta_{pts}(P) \;=\; \frac{P}{P + R_{eq}I^{2} + k_{sw}I + P_{0}}
$$

**Step 1 — Define the Loss Mechanisms.**
For a switched-mode converter, the dominant losses are:
- **Conduction (ohmic) loss:** $P_{cond} = I^{2}R_{eq}$ (IGBT on-state + cable + inductor ESR)
- **Switching loss:** $P_{sw} = k_{sw}\,I$ (proportional to switched current, with $k_{sw} = f_{sw}\,E_{sw}/\pi$)
- **No-load loss:** $P_{0}$ (gate drivers, fans, control electronics)

**Step 2 — Total Loss.**

$$
P_{loss,pts} \;=\; R_{eq}I^{2} \;+\; k_{sw}I \;+\; P_{0}
$$

**Step 3 — Express Current in Terms of Power.**
The current at the CS-side bus of voltage $V_{cs}$ is $I = P_{cs}/V_{cs}$. Substituting:

$$
P_{loss,pts} \;=\; R_{eq}\left(\frac{P_{cs}}{V_{cs}}\right)^{2} \;+\; k_{sw}\left(\frac{P_{cs}}{V_{cs}}\right) \;+\; P_{0}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Efficiency.**
By definition, $\eta = P_{out}/P_{in} = P_{cs}/(P_{cs} + P_{loss,pts})$:

$$
\eta_{pts}(P_{cs}) \;=\; \frac{P_{cs}}{P_{cs} + R_{eq}(P_{cs}/V_{cs})^{2} + k_{sw}(P_{cs}/V_{cs}) + P_{0}}
$$

This is the **KTU-standard static efficiency equation** for the PTS.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Coupled 2-State State-Space Model (PTS + CS + CDCS)

This is the *flagship* derivation expected in 14-mark KTU questions.

**Step 1 — Define the State Vector.**
Choose two states: $x_{1} = \text{SOC}$ (slow) and $x_{2} = P_{pts}$ (fast):

$$
x \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} x_{1} \\ x_{2} \end{bmatrix} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} \text{SOC} \\ P_{pts} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Write the CS State Equation.**
The current into the CS is $I = P_{pts}/V_{cs}$, so:

$$
\dot{x}_{1} \;=\; \frac{d\,\text{SOC}}{dt} \;=\; \frac{P_{pts}}{V_{cs}\cdot Q_{rated}} \;=\; \frac{x_{2}}{V_{cs}\,Q_{rated}}
$$

(sign chosen for charging positive; discharge simply reverses sign)

**Step 3 — Write the PTS State Equation.**
First-order lag from Section 2.1.3:

$$
\dot{x}_{2} \;=\; \frac{dP_{pts}}{dt} \;=\; \frac{1}{\tau_{pts}}\bigl[P_{pts}^{ref} - P_{pts}\bigr] \;=\; \frac{1}{\tau_{pts}}\bigl[P_{pts}^{ref} - x_{2}\bigr]
$$

**Step 4 — Write the CDCS Control Law (Bang-Bang).**

$$
P_{pts}^{ref} \;=\; \begin{cases}
P_{ch,max}, & \text{if } x_{1} \le \text{SOC}_{low} \\
P_{dis,max}, & \text{if } x_{1} \ge \text{SOC}_{high} \\
0, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 5 — Assemble the State-Space System.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{x}_{1} \;&=\; \frac{x_{2}}{V_{cs}\,Q_{rated}} \\[4pt]
\dot{x}_{2} \;&=\; \frac{1}{\tau_{pts}}\bigl[P_{pts}^{ref}(x_{1}) - x_{2}\bigr] \\[4pt]
y \;&=\; V_{cs} \;=\; V_{oc}(x_{1}) \;-\; R_{int}\cdot\frac{x_{2}}{V_{cs}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Identify System Type.**
The CS branch is an **integrator** (energy accumulates), and the PTS branch is a **first-order lag**. Together, they form a *second-order system* with a right-half-plane structure that is **stable only with proper CDCS feedback** (SOC must be bounded by the hysteresis band).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions for Stability**
> For the model to be **physically realizable**, the following KTU-graded constraints must be enforced:
> 1. $\text{SOC}_{min} \le \text{SOC}(t) \le \text{SOC}_{max}$
> 2. $-P_{dis,max} \le P_{pts}(t) \le P_{ch,max}$
> 3. $V_{cs}(t) > 0$ (the CS cannot reverse its terminal voltage polarity without damage for uni-polar stores).

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Standard Style)

**Problem.** A 500 kWh battery storage system has the following parameters:
- $V_{cs,\text{bus}} = 800$ V (DC bus)
- $Q_{rated} = 625$ Ah
- $R_{int} = 0.05\,\Omega$
- $P_{ch,max} = 250$ kW
- $P_{dis,max} = 250$ kW
- $\text{SOC}_{low} = 0.20$, $\text{SOC}_{high} = 0.90$
- Initial SOC = 0.50
- CDCS command: charge at full power for 1 hour.

**Find:** (a) Final SOC, (b) Energy actually delivered to CS, (c) PTS loss (assume $\eta_{pts} = 0.95$ constant).

**Solution.**

(a) Energy delivered to CS bus:
$$
E_{del} \;=\; P_{ch,max}\cdot\Delta t \cdot \eta_{pts} \;=\; 250\,\text{kW}\cdot 1\,\text{h}\cdot 0.95 \;=\; 237.5\,\text{kWh}
$$

(b) Final SOC:
$$
\text{SOC}_{final} \;=\; \text{SOC}_{0} + \frac{E_{del}}{E_{rated}} \;=\; 0.50 + \frac{237.5}{500} \;=\; 0.50 + 0.475 \;=\; 0.975
$$

**Pitfall check:** $\text{SOC}_{final} = 0.975 > \text{SOC}_{high} = 0.90$. The CDCS will **terminate charging** before the full hour, so the actual delivered energy is

$$
E_{del,\text{actual}} \;=\; 0.90 \cdot 500 - 0.50\cdot 500 \;=\; 200\,\text{kWh}
$$

(c) PTS loss:
$$
P_{loss,pts} \;=\; P_{ch,max}(1 - \eta_{pts}) \;=\; 250\,(1 - 0.95) \;=\; 12.5\,\text{kW}
$$
$$
E_{loss,pts} \;=\; 12.5\,\text{kW}\cdot \frac{200}{250}\,\text{h} \;=\; 10\,\text{kWh}
$$

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Mermaid Flow Diagram)

The diagram below maps the **interactions** between the three sub-blocks (PTS, CS, CDCS) and the external grid/load, including feedback signal paths and power flow directions. This is the **canonical schematic** expected in KTU 14-mark answers.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    gridA[Grid or Load Terminal] -- "Pg : power demand" --> ptsA[PTS Block]
    ptsA -- "Ppts : conditioned power" --> csA[CS Block]
    csA -- "Ecs : stored energy" --> ptsA
    csA -- "SOC feedback signal" --> cdcsA[CDCS Block]
    cdcsA -- "Pref : power setpoint" --> ptsA
    ptsA -- "Vbus : bus voltage" --> cdcsA
    gridA -- "Vgrid and fgrid" --> cdcsA
    ptsA -- "Pts loss: heat" --> lossA[Thermal Sink]
    csA -- "Psd : self discharge" --> lossA
    cdcsA -- "Switching commands" --> ptsA
    ptsA -- "Pcs : charge discharge" --> csA
```

## 4.2 Detailed Control-Loop Schematic (Mermaid)

This expanded diagram shows the **internal variables** and the **closed-loop feedback** of the full mathematical model.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    refA[External Reference Pgrid_ref] --> sumA{{Summing Junction}}
    socA[SOC Measurement] --> compA[CDCS Controller]
    refA --> compA
    compA -- "Ppts_ref" --> ptsB[PTS Dynamics: first order lag tau pts]
    ptsB -- "Ppts" --> csB[CS Dynamics: dEcs over dt]
    csB -- "Ecs" --> socB[SOC Calculation: Ecs divide Erated]
    socB -- "SOC" --> socA
    csB -- "Terminal Voltage Vcs" --> outA[Output to Load]
    ptsB -- "Ploss pts" --> thermA[Thermal Loss]
    csB -- "Psd self discharge" --> thermA
    socA -.SOC bounds.-> limA[Limiter SOCmin to SOCmax]
    ptsB -.Power bounds.-> limB[Limiter Pchmax to Pdismax]
```

## 4.3 State-Space Interaction Diagram (Mermaid)

The diagram below illustrates the *mathematical coupling* between the two state variables ($x_{1} = \text{SOC}$ and $x_{2} = P_{pts}$) and the control input $u = P_{pts}^{ref}$, exactly as derived in Section 3.3.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA[Input u = Ppts_ref from CDCS] --> ptsDyn[PTS Dynamics x2 dot equals 1 over tau pts times u minus x2]
    ptsDyn --> x2State[State x2 equals Ppts]
    x2State --> csDyn[CS Dynamics x1 dot equals x2 over Vcs times Qrated]
    csDyn --> x1State[State x1 equals SOC]
    x1State --> ctrlLaw[Control Law: Ppts ref equals f of x1]
    ctrlLaw --> inputA
    x1State --> outV[Output Vcs equals Voc of x1 minus Rint times I]
    x2State --> outV
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams**
> - **Solid arrows** denote *causal/power/signal flow*.
> - **Dotted arrows** denote *constraint or limit enforcement* (anti-windup, saturation).
> - The **closed loop** (Grid $\to$ PTS $\to$ CS $\to$ CDCS $\to$ PTS) is the mathematical heart of every commercial storage controller.
