# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define the Power Transformation System (PTS) in a storage system and list its two main loss components. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Power Transformation System (PTS) is the **power-electronic interface** between the external grid/load and the Central Store, comprising rectifier, inverter, or DC-DC converter stages. Its function is to condition voltage, current, and frequency to levels compatible with the storage medium, and it operates **bidirectionally**.

The two main loss components of a PTS are:
1. **Conduction (ohmic) losses:** $P_{cond} = I^{2}R_{eq}$, arising from the on-state resistance of switches, cable resistance, and inductor ESR.
2. **Switching losses:** $P_{sw} = k_{sw}\,I$, arising from the finite rise/fall times of semiconductor switches during PWM transitions.

A third (smaller) component is the **no-load loss** $P_{0}$ from gate drivers, fans, and control electronics.

### Q2. State the universal energy-balance equation of the Central Store (CS) and define all terms. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The universal energy-balance equation of the Central Store is

$$
\frac{dE_{cs}(t)}{dt} \;=\; P_{in,cs}(t) \;-\; P_{out,cs}(t) \;-\; P_{sd}(t)
$$

where:
- $E_{cs}(t)$ — instantaneous energy stored in the CS, in joules (J) or watt-hours (Wh).
- $P_{in,cs}$ — power flowing **into** the CS from the PTS during charging, in watts (W).
- $P_{out,cs}$ — power flowing **out of** the CS to the PTS during discharging, in watts (W).
- $P_{sd}$ — self-discharge (or standby loss) power, in watts (W).

This equation is **technology-agnostic** and applies to batteries, supercapacitors, flywheels, PHES, CAES, thermal, and hydrogen storage with the appropriate identification of $E_{cs}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students **commonly lose 3–4 marks** by writing only the *energy-balance* equation and ignoring the **PTS dynamics** and **CDCS control law** — all three blocks must appear for full marks.
> - Another frequent error is **mixing up sign conventions** between charging and discharging. Adopt the KTU convention *clearly* at the start of your answer and stick to it.
> - Do **not** skip the **boundary conditions** ($\text{SOC}_{min}, \text{SOC}_{max}, P_{ch,max}, P_{dis,max}$) — they are worth 2 marks in 14-mark questions.
> - Avoid writing *qualitative* paragraphs in place of *equations*. The question asks for a **mathematical model** — your answer must be dominated by **differential/algebraic equations**.

### Question A — Full-Marks 14-Mark Problem

**Q.A (a)** Develop the mathematical model of the **Power Transformation System (PTS)** of an energy storage system, clearly stating the static efficiency equation and the first-order dynamic lag equation. List the three loss components. **[7 marks]** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition of PTS.**
The PTS is the bidirectional power-electronic interface between the grid/load and the CS. For the KTU standard, it is modeled as a switched-mode converter (rectifier + DC-DC chopper, or bidirectional inverter).

**Step 2 — Static Efficiency Equation.**

$$
\eta_{pts} \;=\; \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \;=\; \frac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_{loss,pts}}
$$

**Step 3 — Loss Components.**

The total PTS loss is

$$
P_{loss,pts} \;=\; R_{eq}\,I^{2} \;+\; k_{sw}\,I \;+\; P_{0}
$$

where $R_{eq}I^{2}$ is conduction loss, $k_{sw}I$ is switching loss, and $P_{0}$ is no-load loss.

**Step 4 — Power-Dependent Efficiency.**

Substituting $I = P_{cs}/V_{cs}$:

$$
\eta_{pts}(P_{cs}) \;=\; \frac{P_{cs}}{P_{cs} + R_{eq}\left(\dfrac{P_{cs}}{V_{cs}}\right)^{2} + k_{sw}\left(\dfrac{P_{cs}}{V_{cs}}\right) + P_{0}}
$$

**Step 5 — First-Order Dynamic Lag.**
The transient response of the PTS to a step change in set-point is captured by

$$
\tau_{pts}\,\frac{dP_{pts}(t)}{dt} \;+\; P_{pts}(t) \;=\; P_{pts}^{ref}(t)
$$

where $\tau_{pts}$ is the converter time constant (typically 1–20 ms).

**Step 6 — Boundary Conditions.**

- $0 \le \eta_{pts} \le 1$ (always true for a passive converter)
- $-P_{dis,max} \le P_{pts}(t) \le P_{ch,max}$
- $\tau_{pts} > 0$ (for stability)

**Q.A (b)** Derive the **Central Store (CS) energy-balance differential equation** and the **State-of-Charge (SOC) equation**. State the technology-specific forms for a **Li-ion battery**, a **supercapacitor**, and a **flywheel**. **[7 marks]** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Universal Energy Balance.**
Applying conservation of energy to a control volume around the CS:

$$
\frac{dE_{cs}(t)}{dt} \;=\; P_{in,cs}(t) \;-\; P_{out,cs}(t) \;-\; P_{sd}(t)
$$

**Step 2 — Normalize to SOC.**

$$
\frac{d\,\text{SOC}(t)}{dt} \;=\; \frac{P_{in,cs}(t) - P_{out,cs}(t) - P_{sd}(t)}{E_{rated}}
$$

**Step 3 — Integral Form.**

$$
\text{SOC}(t) \;=\; \text{SOC}(0) \;+\; \frac{1}{E_{rated}}\int_{0}^{t}\bigl[P_{in,cs}(\tau) - P_{out,cs}(\tau) - P_{sd}(\tau)\bigr]\,d\tau
$$

**Step 4 — Li-ion Battery Form.**
For a battery, the state is SOC and the dynamics are governed by the current $I$:

$$
\frac{d\,\text{SOC}}{dt} \;=\; -\frac{I}{Q_{rated}}
$$

with terminal voltage

$$
V_{cs} \;=\; V_{oc}(\text{SOC}) \;-\; I\,R_{int}
$$

**Step 5 — Supercapacitor Form.**

$$
C\,\frac{dV_{c}}{dt} \;=\; I, \qquad E_{cs} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}CV_{c}^{2}
$$

**Step 6 — Flywheel Form.**

$$
J\,\frac{d\omega}{dt} \;=\; T_{m} - T_{L}, \qquad E_{cs} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}J\omega^{2}
$$

**Step 7 — Boundary Conditions.**

$$
\text{SOC}_{min} \le \text{SOC}(t) \le \text{SOC}_{max}, \qquad V_{c}(t) > 0, \qquad \omega(t) \le \omega_{max}
$$

---

### Question B — Alternative 14-Mark Problem

**Q.B (a)** Explain the **Charge-Discharge Control System (CDCS)** with a **bang-bang (hysteresis) controller** and a **proportional (P) controller**. Write the control law for each and identify which is preferred for **battery life preservation**. **[7 marks]** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Role of the CDCS.**
The CDCS is the supervisory controller that issues the power set-point $P_{pts}^{ref}$ to the PTS. It must:
- track the external command $P_{g}^{ref}$,
- respect SOC limits, and
- respect power limits of the CS.

**Step 2 — Bang-Bang (Hysteresis) Controller Law.**

$$
P_{pts}^{ref}(t) \;=\; \begin{cases}
+P_{ch,max}, & \text{SOC}(t) \le \text{SOC}_{low} \\[4pt]
-P_{dis,max}, & \text{SOC}(t) \ge \text{SOC}_{high} \\[4pt]
0, & \text{SOC}_{low} < \text{SOC}(t) < \text{SOC}_{high}
\end{cases}
$$

This is a **discontinuous** controller with a **dead-band** $[\text{SOC}_{low}, \text{SOC}_{high}]$.

**Step 3 — Proportional (P) Controller Law.**

$$
P_{pts}^{ref}(t) \;=\; K_{p}\bigl[\text{SOC}_{ref} - \text{SOC}(t)\bigr] + P_{g}^{ref}
$$

subject to saturation $-P_{dis,max} \le P_{pts}^{ref} \le P_{ch,max}$.

**Step 4 — Comparison.**

| Property | Bang-Bang | Proportional |
|---|---|---|
| Switching frequency | Low (only at thresholds) | High (continuous adjustment) |
| PTS wear | Lower | Higher |
| SOC tracking accuracy | Lower (dead-band) | Higher |
| Implementation complexity | Simple (comparators) | Moderate (ADC + DSP) |
| Battery life impact | **Better** (fewer cycles) | Worse (micro-cycles) |

**Step 5 — Preferred Choice for Battery Life.**
The **bang-bang controller** is preferred for battery-life preservation because:
- it limits the number of charge/discharge transitions,
- it avoids the high-frequency micro-cycles induced by continuous P-control,
- it respects SOC limits strictly, preventing overcharge/overdischarge degradation.

**Q.B (b)** A 1 MWh battery storage system has the following data: $V_{cs} = 1000$ V, $Q_{rated} = 1000$ Ah, $R_{int} = 0.02\,\Omega$, $P_{ch,max} = 500$ kW, $P_{dis,max} = 500$ kW, $\text{SOC}_{low} = 0.20$, $\text{SOC}_{high} = 0.90$, $\text{SOC}_{0} = 0.50$, $\eta_{pts} = 0.96$. The CDCS is in bang-bang mode. The system is commanded to charge at full power. Determine: (i) the time to reach $\text{SOC}_{high}$ if charging is uninterrupted, (ii) the actual energy stored at that moment, and (iii) the energy lost in the PTS. **[7 marks]** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Net Power Into CS.**

$$
P_{net,cs} \;=\; \eta_{pts}\cdot P_{ch,max} \;=\; 0.96 \cdot 500\,\text{kW} \;=\; 480\,\text{kW}
$$

**Step 2 — SOC Range to be Covered.**

$$
\Delta\text{SOC} \;=\; \text{SOC}_{high} - \text{SOC}_{0} \;=\; 0.90 - 0.50 \;=\; 0.40
$$

**Step 3 — Energy Required.**

$$
E_{req} \;=\; \Delta\text{SOC} \cdot E_{rated} \;=\; 0.40 \cdot 1\,\text{MWh} \;=\; 400\,\text{kWh}
$$

**Step 4 — Time to Reach SOC_high.**

$$
t \;=\; \frac{E_{req}}{P_{net,cs}} \;=\; \frac{400\,\text{kWh}}{480\,\text{kW}} \;=\; 0.8333\,\text{h} \;=\; 50\,\text{minutes}
$$

**Step 5 — Actual Energy Stored at That Moment.**

$$
E_{stored} \;=\; E_{req} \;=\; 400\,\text{kWh} \quad (\text{by definition of SOC change})
$$

**Step 6 — Energy Lost in PTS.**

$$
P_{loss,pts} \;=\; P_{ch,max} - P_{net,cs} \;=\; 500 - 480 \;=\; 20\,\text{kW}
$$

$$
E_{loss,pts} \;=\; P_{loss,pts} \cdot t \;=\; 20\,\text{kW} \cdot 0.8333\,\text{h} \;=\; 16.67\,\text{kWh}
$$

**Step 7 — Verification.**
Total energy drawn from grid = $P_{ch,max} \cdot t = 500 \cdot 0.8333 = 416.67$ kWh.
Energy balance check: $400 + 16.67 = 416.67$ kWh ✓

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU**

- [ ] **Three blocks:** PTS, CS, CDCS — always mention all three.
- [ ] **PTS static efficiency:** $\eta = P_{out}/(P_{out} + P_{loss})$.
- [ ] **PTS loss equation:** $P_{loss} = R_{eq}I^{2} + k_{sw}I + P_{0}$.
- [ ] **PTS dynamic equation:** $\tau_{pts}\dot{P}_{pts} + P_{pts} = P_{pts}^{ref}$.
- [ ] **CS universal equation:** $\dot{E}_{cs} = P_{in} - P_{out} - P_{sd}$.
- [ ] **SOC equation:** $\dot{\text{SOC}} = P_{net}/E_{rated}$.
- [ ] **Integral form of SOC:** Always include this for 14-mark questions.
- [ ] **Li-ion:** $V_{cs} = V_{oc}(\text{SOC}) - I\,R_{int}$.
- [ ] **Supercapacitor:** $E = \tfrac{1}{2}CV_{c}^{2}$.
- [ ] **Flywheel:** $E = \tfrac{1}{2}J\omega^{2}$.
- [ ] **PHES:** $P = \rho g Q h$.
- [ ] **Hydrogen:** $E = n_{H_2}\cdot\text{LHV}_{H_2}$.
- [ ] **Bang-bang CDCS law** (write the piecewise equation).
- [ ] **P-controller CDCS law:** $P_{pts}^{ref} = K_{p}[\text{SOC}_{ref} - \text{SOC}] + P_{g}^{ref}$.
- [ ] **Round-trip efficiency:** $\eta_{rt} = \eta_{ch}\cdot\eta_{dis}$.
- [ ] **Self-discharge model:** $P_{sd} = k_{sd}\,E_{cs}$.
- [ ] **Sign convention:** State at the start of the answer and never violate it.
- [ ] **Boundary conditions:** $\text{SOC}_{min}\le \text{SOC}\le \text{SOC}_{max}$, $-P_{dis,max}\le P_{pts}\le P_{ch,max}$.
- [ ] **Time-constants hierarchy:** PTS (ms) << CDCS (s) << CS (hr).
- [ ] **Typical numerical values:** $\eta_{pts} \approx 0.95$–$0.98$, $\eta_{rt} \approx 0.85$–$0.92$, $R_{int} \approx 0.01$–$0.1\,\Omega$, $\tau_{pts} \approx 1$–$20$ ms.
- [ ] **State-space assembly:** 2-state model with $x_1 = \text{SOC}$ and $x_2 = P_{pts}$ — draw the block diagram.
- [ ] **Pitfall #1:** Writing only the energy equation and forgetting PTS and CDCS — *guaranteed 4-mark loss*.
- [ ] **Pitfall #2:** Forgetting to enforce $\text{SOC}_{min}/\text{SOC}_{max}$ in numerical problems — *2-mark loss*.
- [ ] **Pitfall #3:** Mixing charge/discharge signs — *2-mark loss*.
- [ ] **Pitfall #4:** Skipping the unit consistency check (Wh vs J, kW vs W) — *1-mark loss*.
- [ ] **Final integration tip:** Always close a 14-mark answer with the **complete 2-state state-space system** + **boundary conditions** + **one numerical illustration** — this is the KTU "full-marks" template.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Econometric Model of Storage System. Thermal Energy: General Considerations, Storage Media, Containment, Thermal Energy Storage in a Power Plant
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET521 - ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Econometric Model of Storage System. Thermal Energy: General Considerations, Storage Media, Containment, Thermal Energy Storage in a Power Plant

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Econometric Model of Energy Storage System

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

An **Econometric Model of an Energy Storage System (ESS)** is a quantitative analytical framework that integrates engineering performance parameters (efficiency, lifetime, capacity, round-trip losses) with macroeconomic and financial variables (capital expenditure, discount rate, fuel price escalation, grid tariffs) to forecast the cost-effectiveness, profitability, and Return on Investment (ROI) of a storage asset over its operational lifetime. It employs statistical and mathematical techniques to relate the cost-per-unit-energy delivered to the design and operational parameters of the storage device.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Context):**
> The econometric model is **not a simple cost calculation**; it is a multi-variable regression/optimization model that captures the dependency of storage cost on factors such as *depth of discharge (DoD), cycle life, C-rate, temperature, and grid economics*.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an econometric model as a **"Business Health Report Card"** for a storage system:
- Just as a doctor checks multiple parameters (BP, sugar, cholesterol) to assess health, the econometric model checks multiple parameters (capital cost, efficiency, lifetime, energy throughput) to assess the financial "health" of a storage installation.
- A **lithium-ion battery** with high capital cost but 95% round-trip efficiency may be cheaper *per unit energy delivered* over 15 years than a cheaper lead-acid battery with 70% efficiency and frequent replacements.
- The model essentially asks: *"How much does it really cost to store and retrieve 1 kWh of energy, considering ALL real-world factors?"*

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric Introduced — Levelized Cost of Storage (LCOS):**
> Analogous to LCOE (Levelized Cost of Electricity) in power generation, the **LCOS** is the primary output of the econometric model. It represents the **constant per-unit cost** ($/kWh or ₹/kWh) of stored energy over the system's lifetime, including capex, opex, charging cost, and end-of-life disposal.

---

## 1.2 Thermal Energy Storage (TES) — General Considerations

### Formal Definition

**Thermal Energy Storage (TES)** refers to the temporary stockpiling of energy in the form of heat (sensible, latent, or thermochemical) for later use in heating, cooling, or power generation applications. It acts as a **thermal buffer** between energy supply (intermittent solar, off-peak grid, waste heat) and energy demand.

### Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a **"Thermal Savings Bank"**:
- You deposit heat during off-peak times (when energy is cheap or naturally available — like sunshine at noon).
- You withdraw heat later when needed (night-time, cloudy days, peak demand).
- The storage medium is your "thermal wallet" — it can be water, molten salt, rocks, ice, or even phase-change materials.

### Why TES is Critical in Modern Energy Systems

1. **Decoupling Supply and Demand** — Solar thermal is available at noon, but demand peaks in the evening. TES bridges this gap.
2. **Grid Stabilization** — Concentrated Solar Power (CSP) plants can generate electricity 24/7 with adequate TES.
3. **Industrial Process Heat** — Steel, cement, and chemical industries need continuous high-temperature heat; TES smoothens supply.
4. **HVAC Efficiency** — Ice-based cooling in buildings shifts AC load to off-peak hours.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric — Energy Storage Capacity (Q):**
> The fundamental quantity for any TES is the stored energy, given by **Q (in Joules or kWh)**. For sensible heat storage, **Q = m · cₚ · ΔT**, where **m = mass (kg)**, **cₚ = specific heat capacity (J/kg·K)**, and **ΔT = temperature change (K)**.

### Three Modes of Thermal Energy Storage

| Mode | Mechanism | Example | Energy Density (kJ/kg) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sensible** | Temperature change of a medium | Water tanks, molten salt, rocks | 4–200 |
| **Latent** | Phase change at constant temperature | Ice, paraffin, salt hydrates | 200–600 |
| **Thermochemical** | Reversible chemical reactions | Metal hydrides, ammonia synthesis | 500–3000 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Econometric Model — Structure & Logic

The econometric model for storage can be decomposed into **four interconnected layers**:

### Layer 1: Engineering Performance Block
Captures *what the storage system physically does*:
- Energy capacity: $E$ (kWh)
- Power rating: $P$ (kW)
- Round-trip efficiency: $\eta_{RT}$
- Cycle life: $N_{cycles}$
- Calendar life: $T_{life}$ (years)
- Depth of Discharge: $DoD$

### Layer 2: Financial Cost Block
Captures *what the storage system costs*:
- Capital Expenditure: $C_{capex}$ (₹/kWh for energy, ₹/kW for power)
- Operational Expenditure: $C_{opex}$ (₹/kW·year)
- Charging Cost: $C_{charge}$ (₹/kWh charged)
- End-of-life disposal: $C_{EOL}$ (₹/kWh)

### Layer 3: Macroeconomic & Policy Block
- Discount rate: $r$ (typically 6–12% in Indian context)
- Inflation rate: $i$
- Tariff escalation rate: $e$
- Subsidies / tax credits: $S$

### Layer 4: Output — Levelized Cost of Storage (LCOS)

### Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)

1. **Define the boundary**: What is the *system* — only storage, or storage + power electronics + BoP (Balance of Plant)?
2. **Discretize time**: Project cash flows over $T_{life}$ years (typically 15–20 years).
3. **Discount future cash flows** to present value using $PV = \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$.
4. **Sum discounted energy delivered** over the lifetime: $E_{total} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} E_{annual,t} \cdot (1-DoD)$.
5. **Compute LCOS** = (Sum of all PV costs) / (Sum of all PV energy delivered).

### Real-World Utility

- **Utility-scale battery storage bidding** in Day-Ahead Markets (DAM) uses LCOS to set floor prices.
- **MNRE (Ministry of New & Renewable Energy, India)** uses LCOS-based analytics to set storage tenders (e.g., SECI's 4 GWh storage tender).
- **Bankers and project financiers** use LCOS as a critical input in debt-equity decisions.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are KTU Board Exam essentials for Module 1. Memorize the units and physical meaning.**

### A. Econometric Model & LCOS

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $LCOS = \frac{\sum_{t=0}^{T} \frac{C_t}{(1+r)^t}}{\sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{E_{out,t}}{(1+r)^t}}$ | Levelized Cost of Storage | ₹/kWh |
| 2 | $C_t = C_{capex,t} + C_{opex,t} + C_{charge,t} - S_t + C_{EOL,t}$ | Total annual cost in year $t$ | ₹/year |
| 3 | $E_{out,t} = E_{rated} \cdot DoD \cdot \eta_{RT} \cdot N_{cycles,t}$ | Annual energy delivered | kWh/year |
| 4 | $PBP = \frac{C_{capex}}{C_{annual,savings}}$ | Simple Payback Period | years |
| 5 | $NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \frac{(B_t - C_t)}{(1+r)^t}$ | Net Present Value | ₹ |

### B. Sensible Heat Storage

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 6 | $Q_{sensible} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat stored | J |
| 7 | $Q_{sensible} = V \cdot \rho \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat in terms of volume | J |
| 8 | $E_{sensible} = \frac{m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T}{3.6 \times 10^6}$ | Conversion to kWh | kWh |

### C. Latent Heat Storage (Phase Change)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 9 | $Q_{latent} = m \cdot L$ | Latent heat during phase change | J |
| 10 | $Q_{total} = m \cdot (c_p \cdot \Delta T + L + c_{p,l} \cdot \Delta T_l)$ | Sensible + Latent combined | J |

### D. Thermochemical Storage

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 11 | $Q_{TC} = m \cdot \Delta H_{rxn}$ | Heat from reversible chemical reaction | J |
| 12 | $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$ | Gibbs free energy criterion (spontaneity) | J/mol |

### E. TES Performance & Losses

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 13 | $\eta_{TES} = \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \frac{Q_{loss}}{Q_{in}}$ | Thermal storage round-trip efficiency | dimensionless |
| 14 | $Q_{loss} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lm} \cdot t$ | Heat loss through containment | J |
| 15 | $T_{discharge,eff} = T_{charge} - \Delta T_{loss}$ | Effective discharge temperature | K |

### F. Power Plant Integration

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 16 | $E_{TES,plant} = P_{plant} \cdot t_{discharge}$ | Energy to shift in CSP plant | kWh |
| 17 | $C_{plant} = \frac{C_{TES}}{E_{annual,delivered}}$ | Cost per kWh for plant TES | ₹/kWh |
| 18 | $CF_{with\,TES} = \frac{E_{annual,with\,TES}}{P_{rated} \cdot 8760}$ | Capacity factor with TES | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Constant to Remember:**
> **1 kWh = 3.6 × 10⁶ J = 3600 kJ**. Always use this when converting between thermal (J) and electrical (kWh) units.

---

## 2.3 Thermal Energy Storage — Deep Analysis

### 2.3.1 General Considerations for TES Design

Any TES system design must address **five fundamental questions**:

1. **What temperature range is needed?** (Low-T: 0–100°C for HVAC; Medium-T: 100–400°C for industrial; High-T: 400–1000°C for power plants)
2. **What is the storage duration?** (Diurnal: hours; Seasonal: months)
3. **What is the energy density requirement?** (Compact storage vs. large-volume storage)
4. **What is the heat loss tolerance?** (Well-insulated vs. low-cost containment)
5. **What is the cost ceiling?** (Capital cost per kWh stored)

### 2.3.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Selecting a Storage Mode

- **Sensible storage** is chosen when: temperature swing is large, cost is critical, and bulk storage is feasible (e.g., molten salt in CSP).
- **Latent storage** is chosen when: constant discharge temperature is needed, and storage is space-constrained (e.g., PCM in building walls).
- **Thermochemical storage** is chosen when: very long duration (seasonal) is required, and very high energy density is essential.

### 2.3.3 Real-World Utility of Each Mode

| Application | Preferred TES Mode | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Concentrated Solar Power (CSP) | Sensible (molten salt 60% NaNO₃ + 40% KNO₃) | High operating temperature (~565°C), bulk storage |
| Building HVAC | Latent (ice, paraffin PCM) | Compact, isothermal discharge |
| District heating | Sensible (water tanks, boreholes) | Low cost, high heat capacity of water |
| Industrial waste heat recovery | Thermochemical (CaO/Ca(OH)₂) | High energy density, long storage possible |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Levelized Cost of Storage (LCOS)

The LCOS formula is the cornerstone of the econometric model. Let us derive it rigorously.

### Starting Point: Present Value of Total Costs

The total cost of owning and operating a storage system over $T$ years, discounted to present value, is:

$$
PV_{cost} = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \frac{C_{capex} \cdot \delta_{t,0} + C_{opex,t} + C_{charge,t} - S_t + C_{EOL,T}\cdot\delta_{t,T}}{(1+r)^t}
$$

where $\delta$ is the Kronecker delta (1 when $t=0$ for capex, 1 when $t=T$ for end-of-life).

### Step 1: Separate the Capex Term

Since capital expenditure occurs at $t=0$ (no discounting):

$$
PV_{capex} = C_{capex}
$$

### Step 2: Annual Operating Cost (Discounted)

Assuming $C_{opex}$ and $C_{charge}$ are constant per year, and $E_{out}$ is constant per year:

$$
PV_{opex} = C_{opex} \cdot \frac{(1+r)^T - 1}{r \cdot (1+r)^T}
$$

This factor is called the **Present Value Annuity Factor (PVAF)**. The derivation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV_{opex} &= \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{C_{opex}}{(1+r)^t} \\
&= C_{opex} \cdot \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{1}{(1+r)^t} \\
&= C_{opex} \cdot \frac{1 - (1+r)^{-T}}{r} \cdot \frac{1}{1} \\
&= C_{opex} \cdot \frac{(1+r)^T - 1}{r \cdot (1+r)^T}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Total Energy Delivered (Discounted)

$$
PV_{energy} = E_{out} \cdot \frac{(1+r)^T - 1}{r \cdot (1+r)^T}
$$

### Step 4: Combine to Get LCOS

$$
LCOS = \frac{PV_{capex} + PV_{opex} + PV_{charge}}{PV_{energy}}
$$

Substituting:

$$
LCOS = \frac{C_{capex} + (C_{opex} + C_{charge}) \cdot \frac{(1+r)^T - 1}{r \cdot (1+r)^T}}{E_{out} \cdot \frac{(1+r)^T - 1}{r \cdot (1+r)^T}}
$$

### Step 5: Simplification

Dividing numerator and denominator by the annuity factor:

$$
LCOS = \frac{C_{capex} \cdot \frac{r \cdot (1+r)^T}{(1+r)^T - 1} + (C_{opex} + C_{charge})}{E_{out}}
$$

The first term $\frac{r \cdot (1+r)^T}{(1+r)^T - 1}$ is the **Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)**. Final form:

$$
\boxed{LCOS = \frac{CRF \cdot C_{capex} + C_{opex} + C_{charge}}{E_{out}}}
$$

where $CRF = \frac{r(1+r)^T}{(1+r)^T - 1}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation of LCOS:**
> - The CRF *annuitizes* the upfront capex into equivalent annual payments.
> - Adding annual opex and charging cost gives the total annualized cost.
> - Dividing by annual energy delivered gives the cost per kWh stored and retrieved.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Sensible Heat Storage Capacity

For a storage medium of mass $m$, specific heat $c_p$, undergoing a temperature change from $T_1$ to $T_2$:

$$
Q = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} m \cdot c_p(T) \, dT
$$

### Step 1: Constant Specific Heat Assumption

If $c_p$ is approximately constant over the temperature range:

$$
Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_2 - T_1) = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T
$$

### Step 2: Express in Terms of Volume

Using $m = \rho \cdot V$:

$$
Q = V \cdot \rho \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T
$$

### Step 3: Convert to kWh

Dividing by $3.6 \times 10^6$ J/kWh:

$$
E_{kWh} = \frac{V \cdot \rho \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T}{3.6 \times 10^6}
$$

### Step 4: Numerical Verification

**Example:** A water tank of $V = 10$ m³, $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $c_p = 4186$ J/kg·K, $\Delta T = 50$ K.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= 10 \times 1000 \times 4186 \times 50 \\
&= 2.093 \times 10^9 \text{ J} \\
E_{kWh} &= \frac{2.093 \times 10^9}{3.6 \times 10^6} \\
&= 581.4 \text{ kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

So a 10 m³ water tank heated by 50°C stores roughly **581 kWh** of thermal energy.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Latent Heat Storage with Combined Sensible and Phase-Change Contributions

For a PCM (Phase Change Material) that:
- Warms as solid from $T_1$ to melting point $T_m$ (sensible)
- Melts at $T_m$ (latent)
- Warms as liquid from $T_m$ to $T_2$ (sensible)

The total heat stored is:

$$
Q_{total} = m \cdot c_{p,s} \cdot (T_m - T_1) + m \cdot L + m \cdot c_{p,l} \cdot (T_2 - T_m)
$$

### Step 1: Factor Out Mass

$$
Q_{total} = m \left[ c_{p,s} (T_m - T_1) + L + c_{p,l} (T_2 - T_m) \right]
$$

### Step 2: Introduce Bulk Energy Density

Dividing by mass:

$$
q_{total} = c_{p,s} (T_m - T_1) + L + c_{p,l} (T_2 - T_m) \quad \text{[J/kg]}
$$

This is the **specific energy storage capacity** of the PCM.

### Step 3: Numerical Example — Ice-based TES

For ice ($T_m = 0$°C, $L = 334$ kJ/kg, $c_{p,s} = 2090$ J/kg·K, $c_{p,l} = 4186$ J/kg·K):
- Charging from $T_1 = 25$°C (water) to $T_m = 0$°C, then freezing:

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{sensible} &= 4186 \times (25 - 0) = 104,650 \text{ J/kg} \\
q_{latent} &= 334,000 \text{ J/kg} \\
q_{total} &= 104,650 + 334,000 = 438,650 \text{ J/kg} \\
&\approx 438.65 \text{ kJ/kg} \\
&\approx 121.8 \text{ kWh/tonne}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** The latent heat contribution (334 kJ/kg) dominates the sensible contribution (104.65 kJ/kg) by a factor of ~3.2×. This is why **latent storage is preferred when isothermal discharge is needed**.

---

## 3.4 Heat Loss Through Containment (Conduction + Convection + Radiation)

The containment of a TES tank loses heat to the surroundings. For a cylindrical tank with surface area $A$ and overall heat transfer coefficient $U$:

### Step 1: Instantaneous Heat Loss Rate

$$
\dot{Q}_{loss} = U \cdot A \cdot (T_{storage} - T_{ambient})
$$

### Step 2: Cumulative Loss Over Time $t$

If $T_{storage}$ is approximately constant (valid for short times):

$$
Q_{loss} = U \cdot A \cdot (T_{storage} - T_{ambient}) \cdot t
$$

### Step 3: With Falling Storage Temperature

For longer durations, the storage temperature drops exponentially:

$$
T_{storage}(t) = T_{ambient} + (T_0 - T_{ambient}) \cdot e^{-\frac{UA}{mc_p} t}
$$

This is derived from the lumped capacitance model:

$$
mc_p \frac{dT}{dt} = -UA(T - T_{amb})
$$

Separating variables and integrating from $T_0$ to $T(t)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{T_0}^{T(t)} \frac{dT}{T - T_{amb}} &= -\frac{UA}{mc_p} \int_0^t dt \\
\ln\left(\frac{T(t) - T_{amb}}{T_0 - T_{amb}}\right) &= -\frac{UA}{mc_p} t \\
T(t) &= T_{amb} + (T_0 - T_{amb}) \cdot e^{-\frac{UA}{mc_p} t}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Thermal Time Constant

The quantity $\tau = \frac{mc_p}{UA}$ is the **thermal time constant** (in seconds). A larger $\tau$ means slower cooling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** For a well-designed molten salt tank in a CSP plant, $\tau$ should be at least 7–10 days to limit daily thermal losses to < 2%.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Econometric Model — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ENG["Engineering Performance Block"]
        E1["Energy Capacity E kWh"]
        E2["Power Rating P kW"]
        E3["Round-Trip Efficiency eta_RT"]
        E4["Cycle Life N_cycles"]
        E5["Depth of Discharge DoD"]
        E6["Calendar Life T_years"]
    end

    subgraph FIN["Financial Cost Block"]
        F1["CAPEX Rs per kWh"]
        F2["OPEX Rs per kW yr"]
        F3["Charging Cost Rs per kWh"]
        F4["End-of-Life Cost Rs per kWh"]
    end

    subgraph MAC["Macroeconomic Block"]
        M1["Discount Rate r percent"]
        M2["Inflation Rate i percent"]
        M3["Tariff Escalation e percent"]
        M4["Subsidy S Rs"]
    end

    subgraph OUT["Output Block"]
        O1["Discounted Total Cost PV_C"]
        O2["Discounted Energy Delivered PV_E"]
        O3["LCOS Rs per kWh"]
        O4["NPV Rs"]
        O5["Payback Period years"]
    end

    ENG --> O1
    ENG --> O2
    FIN --> O1
    MAC --> O1
    MAC --> O2
    O1 --> O3
    O2 --> O3
    O1 --> O4
    O1 --> O5
```

---

## 4.2 Thermal Energy Storage — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IN["Energy Source"]
        S1["Solar Collector"]
        S2["Off-peak Grid Electricity"]
        S3["Industrial Waste Heat"]
        S4["Biomass Combustion"]
    end

    subgraph CHG["Charging Subsystem"]
        C1["Heat Exchanger 1"]
        C2["Pump / Blower"]
        C3["Control Valve"]
    end

    subgraph STORE["Storage Subsystem"]
        ST1["Storage Media Tank or Bed"]
        ST2["Sensible Material Water Salt Rock"]
        ST3["Latent Material PCM Ice Paraffin"]
        ST4["Thermochemical Material CaO CaOH2"]
    end

    subgraph CONT["Containment Subsystem"]
        CT1["Insulation Layer"]
        CT2["Vapor Barrier"]
        CT3["Structural Shell"]
        CT4["Temperature Sensors"]
    end

    subgraph DIS["Discharging Subsystem"]
        D1["Heat Exchanger 2"]
        D2["Power Block Turbine ORC Steam"]
        D3["Distribution Network"]
    end

    S1 --> C1
    S2 --> C1
    S3 --> C1
    S4 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> ST1
    ST1 --> ST2
    ST1 --> ST3
    ST1 --> ST4
    ST1 -. Heat Loss .-> CT1
    CT1 --> CT2
    CT2 --> CT3
    CT3 --> CT4
    ST1 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
```

## 4.3 TES in a Concentrated Solar Power (CSP) Plant — Block Diagram

## 4.4 Decision Flowchart — Selecting a Storage Mode

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Define TES Application"] --> B{"Storage Duration?"}
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Pattern Reference:**
> - Part A: 3-mark short-answer questions (definitions, lists, brief explanations)
> - Part B: 14-mark questions with internal choice; typically split into (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks
> - Module 1 carries weightage as per the official KTU syllabus distribution

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**
**Define the Levelized Cost of Storage (LCOS) and mention any three input parameters required for its calculation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
The **Levelized Cost of Storage (LCOS)** is the constant per-unit cost (₹/kWh or $/kWh) of energy delivered by a storage system over its entire operational lifetime. It is the ratio of the total discounted lifetime cost (capex, opex, charging cost, end-of-life disposal) to the total discounted lifetime energy delivered by the system.

$$
LCOS = \frac{\sum_{t=0}^{T} \frac{C_t}{(1+r)^t}}{\sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{E_{out,t}}{(1+r)^t}}
$$

**Three Input Parameters (1 mark):**
1. Capital Expenditure ($C_{capex}$) in ₹/kWh
2. Round-trip efficiency ($\eta_{RT}$) and cycle life
3. Discount rate ($r$) and project lifetime ($T$)

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**
**List any three storage media used for sensible heat thermal energy storage and state the typical operating temperature range for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Storage Medium | Typical Temperature Range |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Water | 0°C to 95°C (low-temperature applications, district heating) |
| 2 | Molten Salt (60% NaNO₃ + 40% KNO₃) | 220°C to 565°C (concentrated solar power) |
| 3 | Thermal oil (e.g., Therminol VP-1) | 12°C to 400°C (industrial process heat) |
| 4 | Rocks / Ceramic solids | Up to 1000°C (packed-bed storage) |
| 5 | Concrete / Castable ceramics | Up to 600°C (modular storage) |

*(Any three with correct temperature ranges = 3 marks; 1 mark per correct entry)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — With Internal Choice

### Question A (Choice 1)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Derive the formula for the **Levelized Cost of Storage (LCOS)** of a battery energy storage system, clearly defining all terms. Discuss the role of the **Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)** in the final expression. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A 1 MWh/1 MW lithium-ion battery storage system has the following parameters:
- Capital cost: ₹6,00,00,000 (₹6 Crore)
- Annual O&M cost: ₹15,00,000
- Charging cost: ₹4 per kWh
- Discount rate: 8% per annum
- Project life: 15 years
- Round-trip efficiency: 90%
- Cycles per year: 330 (1 cycle per day average)
- Depth of Discharge: 80%

Calculate the **LCOS** of the system. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

##### Part (a) — Derivation of LCOS [7 marks]

**Step 1: Define cost components in year $t$** [1 mark]
The total annual cost in year $t$ is:
$$
C_t = C_{capex}\delta_{t,0} + C_{opex,t} + C_{charge,t} - S_t + C_{EOL,T}\delta_{t,T}
$$

**Step 2: Present Value of total cost** [1 mark]
$$
PV_{cost} = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \frac{C_t}{(1+r)^t}
$$

**Step 3: Present Value of energy delivered** [1 mark]
$$
PV_{energy} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{E_{out,t}}{(1+r)^t}
$$

**Step 4: Definition of LCOS** [1 mark]
$$
LCOS = \frac{PV_{cost}}{PV_{energy}}
$$

**Step 5: Annuitization using Capital Recovery Factor** [2 marks]
Assuming constant annual cost $C_a$ and constant energy delivery $E_a$:

$$
LCOS = \frac{CRF \cdot C_{capex} + C_{opex} + C_{charge}}{E_{out}}
$$

where $CRF = \frac{r(1+r)^T}{(1+r)^T - 1}$.

**Step 6: Role of CRF — Physical Interpretation** [1 mark]
The CRF **converts the lump-sum upfront capex into equivalent annual payments** over the project lifetime. It accounts for the time value of money — the higher the discount rate or the longer the lifetime, the smaller the CRF's relative weight. Without CRF, capex and opex could not be summed on the same time basis.

---

##### Part (b) — Numerical Calculation of LCOS [7 marks]

**Given Data:**
- $C_{capex}$ = ₹6,00,00,000
- $C_{opex}$ = ₹15,00,000/year
- $C_{charge}$ = ₹4/kWh (input energy)
- $r$ = 8% = 0.08
- $T$ = 15 years
- $\eta_{RT}$ = 0.90
- $N_{cycles}$ = 330/year
- DoD = 0.80
- $E_{rated}$ = 1 MWh = 1000 kWh

**Step 1: Compute Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)** [1 mark]
$$
\begin{aligned}
CRF &= \frac{0.08(1.08)^{15}}{(1.08)^{15} - 1} \\
(1.08)^{15} &= 3.1722 \\
CRF &= \frac{0.08 \times 3.1722}{3.1722 - 1} \\
&= \frac{0.2538}{2.1722} \\
&= 0.11683
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Annualized Capex** [1 mark]
$$
C_{annuity,capex} = 0.11683 \times 6,00,00,000 = ₹70,09,800 \text{ per year}
$$

**Step 3: Annual Energy Delivered** [1 mark]
Energy delivered per cycle = $E_{rated} \times DoD \times \eta_{RT}$
$$
= 1000 \times 0.80 \times 0.90 = 720 \text{ kWh/cycle}
$$

Annual energy delivered:
$$
E_{out} = 720 \times 330 = 2,37,600 \text{ kWh/year}
$$

**Step 4: Annual Charging Cost** [1 mark]
Energy charged per year (input side) = $E_{out}/\eta_{RT} = 2,37,600/0.90 = 2,64,000$ kWh/year
$$
C_{charge} = 2,64,000 \times 4 = ₹10,56,000 \text{ per year}
$$

**Step 5: Total Annualized Cost** [1 mark]
$$
C_{total,annual} = 70,09,800 + 15,00,000 + 10,56,000 = ₹95,65,800 \text{ per year}
$$

**Step 6: LCOS** [1 mark]
$$
LCOS = \frac{95,65,800}{2,37,600} = ₹40.26 \text{ per kWh}
$$

**Step 7: Interpretation** [1 mark]
The levelized cost of storage is **₹40.26 per kWh** delivered. This is a typical LCOS value for a grid-scale Li-ion BESS in India (2024 benchmark: ₹30–₹45/kWh). This value can be compared to peak-time grid tariffs (often ₹8–₹15/kWh) and Time-of-Use (ToU) differentials to assess economic viability.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Forgetting to apply DoD** — many students compute annual energy as $E_{rated} \times N_{cycles}$, ignoring that only 80% of the rated capacity is usable per cycle. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 2. **Confusing input vs. output energy** — annual charging cost is computed on the *input* side (before efficiency loss), while LCOS denominator uses the *output* side. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 3. **Wrong CRF calculation** — forgetting the exponent on $(1+r)^T$ is the most common error. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 4. **Mixing units** — kWh vs. MWh mismatch leads to errors of factor 1000. **Loss: 1–2 marks.**
> 5. **Not including O&M in annualized cost** — many students use only capex and forget opex. **Loss: 1 mark.**

---

### Question B (Choice 2)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain the **three modes of thermal energy storage** (sensible, latent, thermochemical) with suitable examples. Compare their **energy densities** and discuss which mode is best suited for **concentrated solar power (CSP) plants**. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A CSP plant uses **molten salt (60% NaNO₃ + 40% KNO₃)** as the TES medium. The cold tank holds salt at **290°C** and the hot tank at **565°C**.
Given:
- Mass of salt in hot tank: 1500 tonnes = 1,500,000 kg
- Specific heat of salt: 1.55 kJ/kg·K
- Density of salt: 1850 kg/m³

Calculate:
(i) The thermal energy stored in the hot tank.
(ii) The equivalent electrical energy output if the Rankine cycle efficiency is 38%.
(iii) The volume occupied by the salt in the hot tank.
(iv) Comment on the suitability of this storage for a 50 MW CSP plant operating for 6 hours during evening peak. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

##### Part (a) — Three Modes of TES [7 marks]

**Mode 1: Sensible Heat Storage (SHS)** [2 marks]
- **Mechanism:** Heat is stored by raising the temperature of a material without changing its phase.
- **Formula:** $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$
- **Examples:** Water (domestic hot water, district heating), molten salts (CSP), thermal oils, rocks, concrete, sand.
- **Characteristics:** Low cost, simple technology, but requires large volumes and well-insulated tanks.

**Mode 2: Latent Heat Storage (LHS)** [2 marks]
- **Mechanism:** Heat is stored/released during a phase change (typically solid↔liquid) at a constant temperature.
- **Formula:** $Q = m \cdot L$
- **Examples:** Ice (HVAC cooling), paraffin waxes (building thermal comfort), salt hydrates (Glauber's salt Na₂SO₄·10H₂O).
- **Characteristics:** High energy density (~10× water sensible), isothermal discharge, but suffers from supercooling and low thermal conductivity.

**Mode 3: Thermochemical Storage (TCS)** [2 marks]
- **Mechanism:** Heat is stored as chemical bond energy in reversible endothermic/exothermic reactions.
- **Formula:** $Q = m \cdot \Delta H_{rxn}$
- **Examples:** CaO + H₂O ⇌ Ca(OH)₂ + heat (solar fuel); metal hydrides (MgH₂ ⇌ Mg + H₂); ammonia synthesis/dissociation.
- **Characteristics:** Highest energy density (up to 3000 kJ/kg), seasonal storage possible, but complex reactor design and slow kinetics.

**Comparison Table:** [0.5 marks]
| Mode | Energy Density (kJ/kg) | Temperature |
|---|---|---|
| Sensible (water) | 4–80 | 0–95°C |
| Sensible (molten salt) | 100–200 | 200–565°C |
| Latent (PCM) | 200–600 | Variable |
| Thermochemical | 500–3000 | Variable |

**Best Mode for CSP Plants:** [0.5 marks]
**Sensible heat storage using molten salt (60% NaNO₃ + 40% KNO₃)** is preferred because:
- CSP plants operate at high temperatures (400–565°C), within the molten salt's stable range.
- Molten salt has a high heat capacity (1.55 kJ/kg·K) and excellent thermal stability.
- Two-tank direct storage (cold + hot) is technologically mature (e.g., Gemasolar, Crescent Dunes, Noor III).
- Lower cost per kWh stored compared to thermochemical reactors.

---

##### Part (b) — Numerical Calculation [7 marks]

**Given Data:**
- $m$ = 1,500,000 kg
- $c_p$ = 1.55 kJ/kg·K = 1550 J/kg·K
- $T_{hot}$ = 565°C = 838 K
- $T_{cold}$ = 290°C = 563 K
- $\Delta T$ = 565 - 290 = 275°C = 275 K
- $\rho$ = 1850 kg/m³
- $\eta_{Rankine}$ = 0.38
- Plant rating: 50 MW, discharge duration: 6 hours

**Step 1: Thermal energy stored in hot tank** [2 marks]
$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{thermal} &= m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T \\
&= 1,500,000 \times 1550 \times 275 \\
&= 1,500,000 \times 4,26,250 \\
&= 6.39 \times 10^{11} \text{ J}
\end{aligned}
$$

Converting to kWh:
$$
Q_{kWh} = \frac{6.39 \times 10^{11}}{3.6 \times 10^6} = 1,77,583 \text{ kWh} \approx 177.6 \text{ MWh}_{th}
$$

**Step 2: Equivalent electrical energy** [2 marks]
$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{electrical} &= \eta_{Rankine} \times Q_{thermal} \\
&= 0.38 \times 177.6 \text{ MWh} \\
&= 67.5 \text{ MWh}_e
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Volume of salt** [1 mark]
$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= \frac{m}{\rho} \\
&= \frac{1,500,000 \text{ kg}}{1850 \text{ kg/m}^3} \\
&= 810.8 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

For a cylindrical tank with height $H = 12$ m and diameter $D$:
$$
V = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 H \Rightarrow D = \sqrt{\frac{4V}{\pi H}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 810.8}{\pi \times 12}} \approx 9.27 \text{ m}
$$

So a tank of **~9.3 m diameter × 12 m height** is needed (plus insulation and structural allowances, real tank ~12 m diameter).

**Step 4: Comment on 50 MW × 6-hour operation** [2 marks]
- Required energy for 50 MW × 6 h = **300 MWhₑ** at generator terminals.
- Available energy from hot tank = 67.5 MWhₑ.
- The hot tank alone is **insufficient** — it provides only ~22% of the required energy.
- **Mitigation:** Either (a) install a larger salt inventory (~6.6× more, i.e., ~10,000 tonnes), (b) extend the temperature range if feasible, or (c) add a parallel cold-tank-to-hot-tank discharge cycle.
- **Conclusion:** The given storage is suitable for a **smaller dispatch window** (e.g., 1.35 hours at 50 MW), or for a lower-rated plant (~11.25 MW for 6 hours). A typical commercial CSP plant needs **~10–15 hours of full-load storage** in the hot tank, requiring ~10,000–15,000 tonnes of salt per 100 MW.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes on This Question:**
> 1. **Using wrong specific heat unit** — confusing kJ/kg·K with J/kg·K gives an answer off by 1000×. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 2. **Forgetting to convert ΔT in Kelvin when c_p is in J/kg·K** — magnitude of ΔT is the same in K and °C, but students sometimes subtract wrongly. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 3. **Not converting J to kWh** — leaving the answer in Joules for an electrical energy comparison. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 4. **Confusing thermal and electrical energy** — saying "177 MWh of electrical energy" instead of "thermal". **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 5. **Not addressing the "comment" sub-part** — KTU examiners strictly deduct marks for skipped qualitative answers. **Loss: 2 marks.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1**

### Econometric Model of Storage System
- ☐ **LCOS** = (Total discounted lifetime cost) / (Total discounted lifetime energy delivered) — units: ₹/kWh
- ☐ **Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)** = $\frac{r(1+r)^T}{(1+r)^T - 1}$ — annuitizes capex
- ☐ **Final LCOS formula:** $LCOS = \frac{CRF \cdot C_{capex} + C_{opex} + C_{charge}}{E_{out}}$
- ☐ **NPV** = $\sum \frac{(B_t - C_t)}{(1+r)^t}$ — must be ≥ 0 for project acceptance
- ☐ **Payback Period** = Capex / Annual Savings — must be < project life
- ☐ **Engineering parameters needed:** $E_{rated}$, $P$, $\eta_{RT}$, $N_{cycles}$, $DoD$, $T_{life}$
- ☐ **Financial parameters needed:** $C_{capex}$ (₹/kWh), $C_{opex}$ (₹/kW·yr), $C_{charge}$ (₹/kWh), $r$ (%)
- ☐ **Macroeconomic parameters:** discount rate, inflation, tariff escalation, subsidies

### Thermal Energy Storage — General
- ☐ **Three modes:** Sensible, Latent, Thermochemical
- ☐ **Sensible formula:** $Q = m c_p \Delta T = V \rho c_p \Delta T$
- ☐ **Latent formula:** $Q = mL$
- ☐ **Thermochemical formula:** $Q = m \Delta H_{rxn}$
- ☐ **Energy density order:** Thermochemical > Latent > Sensible
- ☐ **Conversion:** 1 kWh = 3.6 × 10⁶ J = 3600 kJ
- ☐ **Heat loss through containment:** $Q_{loss} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T \cdot t$
- ☐ **Lumped capacitance model:** $T(t) = T_{amb} + (T_0 - T_{amb}) e^{-t/\tau}$ with $\tau = mc_p/(UA)$

### Storage Media
- ☐ **Water:** 0–95°C, $c_p$ = 4186 J/kg·K, low cost, dominant in low-T applications
- ☐ **Molten Salt (60% NaNO₃ + 40% KNO₃):** 220–565°C, $c_p$ = 1.55 kJ/kg·K, CSP industry standard
- ☐ **Thermal oil (Therminol VP-1):** up to 400°C, used as HTF in parabolic troughs
- ☐ **PCM/ice:** 0°C, $L$ = 334 kJ/kg, dominant in building HVAC
- ☐ **Salt hydrates (Glauber's salt):** ~32°C, paraffin alternatives in buildings
- ☐ **Rocks, sand, concrete:** bulk sensible storage, up to 1000°C

### Containment
- ☐ **Insulation types:** Rockwool, ceramic fiber, calcium silicate, vacuum insulation panels
- ☐ **Two-tank design:** Cold tank + Hot tank (standard for molten salt CSP)
- ☐ **Single-tank thermocline:** Stratified single tank with filler material (cost-saving alternative)
- ☐ **Containment material selection:** carbon steel (low T), stainless steel (molten salt), concrete (bulk)

### TES in Power Plant
- ☐ **CSP plant with TES:** enables 24/7 dispatch from solar resource
- ☐ **Common configuration:** Solar field → HTF → HX₁ (charges TES) → Steam generator → Turbine
- ☐ **Hot salt storage temp:** ~565°C; **Cold salt storage temp:** ~290°C
- ☐ **Storage hours target:** 10–15 hours of full-load dispatch for baseload CSP
- ☐ **Cost benchmark (2024):** CSP with 15-hr TES: ₹5–₹8/kWh; PV+BESS: ₹4–₹6/kWh
- ☐ **Round-trip efficiency:** CSP-TES: ~93%; Pumped hydro: ~75–80%; Li-ion BESS: ~85–92%
- ☐ **Key CSP projects with TES:** Gemasolar (Spain, 15 hr), Noor III (Morocco, 7.5 hr), DEWA CSP (Dubai, 15 hr)

### Common KTU Pitfalls to Avoid
- ☐ Unit conversion errors (J vs. kJ vs. kWh)
- ☐ Mixing input and output energy in efficiency calculations
- ☐ Forgetting to discount future cash flows in LCOS
- ☐ Confusing thermal and electrical energy in power plant calculations
- ☐ Using wrong sign conventions in NPV
- ☐ Not defining abbreviations like CRF, PVAF, TES, LCOE, LCOS

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Potential Energy: Pumped Hydro, Compressed Air
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET521 - ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Potential Energy: Pumped Hydro, Compressed Air

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Potential Energy Storage (PES) – The Macro-Grid Battery

**Potential Energy Storage (PES)** refers to the class of large-scale, grid-tied energy storage technologies that store electrical energy by elevating a working mass (water, air, or solid media) against a restoring force such as gravity or pressure. The energy remains stored as **gravitational potential energy** or **pressure-volume potential energy** until it is reconverted to electricity on demand.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (verbatim scope):**
> *Potential Energy storage systems — Pumped Hydro Storage (PHS), Compressed Air Energy Storage (CAES), and Gravity Storage — operating principles, components, design metrics, and grid-scale applications.*

### 1.2 Pumped Hydro Storage (PHS) – The "Water Battery"

**Pumped Hydro Storage (PHS)** is a mechanical-electrical energy storage system that stores surplus off-peak electrical energy by pumping water from a **lower reservoir** to an **upper reservoir** at a higher elevation. During peak demand, the stored water is released through **reversible pump-turbines** to regenerate electricity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** PHS is a *closed-loop* (or *open-loop*) hydroelectric facility in which gravitational potential energy of water, $E = \rho g V H$, is the primary storage medium, with round-trip efficiencies typically ranging between **70 % – 85 %**.

### 1.3 Compressed Air Energy Storage (CAES) – The "Pressurized Air Reservoir"

**Compressed Air Energy Storage (CAES)** stores energy by compressing ambient air into underground caverns (salt domes, depleted gas fields, aquifers) or above-ground pressure vessels. The compressed air is later expanded through a turbine to generate electricity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** CAES is a *thermodynamic* storage system that exploits the *pressure-volume work potential* of a gas, $W = \int P \, dV$, with classification into **Diabatic, Adiabatic, and Isothermal** variants based on heat management strategy.

### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy – A "Giant Gravity & Pressure Battery"

| System | Everyday Analogy | Energy Conversion |
|---|---|---|
| **PHS** | A heavy stone lifted onto a high shelf. The heavier the stone & higher the shelf → more stored energy. | Electrical → Mechanical (pump) → Gravitational PE → Kinetic → Electrical (turbine) |
| **CAES** | A balloon squeezed into a small box. The harder you squeeze, the more energy is trapped. | Electrical → Mechanical (compressor) → Pressure PE → Mechanical → Electrical (turbine) |

> [!TIP]
> **Why these two systems matter for KTU/Industry:** Together, PHS + CAES account for **> 95 % of all bulk energy storage capacity deployed worldwide** (in GW-scale). They are the *only* commercially mature technologies capable of **8–24 hours of discharge** at the **100 MW – 3000 MW** scale.

### 1.5 Standard Metrics Every KTU Student Must Memorize

- **Round-Trip Efficiency (RTE):** $\eta_{RT} = \frac{E_{out}}{E_{in}} \times 100\%$
- **Energy Density (volumetric):** $E/V \; [\text{Wh/L}]$
- **Specific Energy:** $E/m \; [\text{Wh/kg}]$
- **Storage Capacity:** $E \; [\text{MWh or GWh}]$
- **Power Rating:** $P \; [\text{MW}]$
- **Discharge Duration:** $t_d = E/P \; [\text{hours}]$
- **Cycle Life:** $> \; 10{,}000$ cycles for PHS; $> \; 5{,}000$ for CAES
- **Self-Discharge Rate:** $\approx 0\%$/day (mechanical storage)
- **Levelized Cost of Storage (LCOS):** ₹/kWh or \$/kWh
- **Capacity Factor:** $CF = \frac{E_{delivered}}{P_{rated} \times 8760}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> PHS has the **lowest LCOS (₹1.5 – 4 / kWh)** of any storage technology — a key reason it dominates global grid storage.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pumped Hydro Storage (PHS) — Operational Architecture

### 2.1.1 System Components (Closed-Loop Configuration)

1. **Upper Reservoir** — Stores the elevated water mass (typically a natural or artificial lake).
2. **Lower Reservoir** — Receives water after energy generation.
3. **Penstock** — High-pressure water conduit connecting upper reservoir to powerhouse.
4. **Pump-Turbine (Reversible Machine)** — Motor-pump during charging, generator-turbine during discharging.
5. **Surge Tank / Surge Shaft** — Hydraulic pressure equalizer protecting penstock during transients.
6. **Tailrace** — Low-pressure conduit returning water to lower reservoir.
7. **Switchyard & Transformers** — Grid interconnection at high voltage.

### 2.1.2 Operating Modes — The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Charging Mode (Pumping):** Off-peak grid power drives the **electric motor** → **pump** lifts water from the lower to the upper reservoir. Energy is stored as **gravitational PE**.
- **Standby Mode:** Water remains static; *negligible self-discharge* (only minor evaporation).
- **Discharging Mode (Generating):** Water flows downhill → converts PE → KE → drives the **turbine** → drives the **generator** → electricity is fed to the grid.

### 2.1.3 Open-Loop vs. Closed-Loop PHS

- **Open-Loop PHS:** Uses a natural body of water (river, sea) as one of the reservoirs (e.g., Koyna, Idukki in India).
- **Closed-Loop PHS:** Both reservoirs are artificial; no natural inflow (ideal for flat terrain).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Real-World Example (India):** The **Pumped Storage Scheme at Tehri (Uttarakhand, 1000 MW)** and **Koyna Hydroelectric Project (Maharashtra, 1960 MW)** are flagship PHS installations demonstrating grid-balancing capability.

---

## 2.2 Compressed Air Energy Storage (CAES) — Operational Architecture

### 2.2.1 System Components

1. **Air Compressor (Multi-Stage with Intercooling)** — Compresses air to high pressure (40–80 bar typical).
2. **Thermal Management Subsystem** — Heat exchangers; defines CAES variant.
3. **Underground Storage Cavern** — Salt dome, aquifer, depleted gas field, or lined rock cavern.
4. **Expander / Turbine** — Recovers pressure energy to drive generator.
5. **Combustion Chamber (Diabatic only)** — Reheats air before expansion (Huntorf, McIntosh CAES plants).
6. **Generator & Grid Interface.**

### 2.2.2 Three CAES Variants — The "How" of Heat Management

| Variant | Heat Treatment | RTE | Complexity | KTU Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Diabatic CAES (D-CAES)** | Heat is *removed* during compression and *re-supplied* by natural gas combustion before expansion | **42 – 54 %** | Lowest (commercially deployed) | Huntorf (1978, 290 MW, Germany); McIntosh (1991, 110 MW, USA) |
| **Adiabatic CAES (A-CAES)** | Heat is *captured & stored* in a Thermal Energy Storage (TES) unit and reused during expansion | **60 – 70 %** | Medium (pilot stage) | Hydrostor Toronto (Toronto Island, 1.5 MW) |
| **Isothermal CAES (I-CAES)** | Heat is *continuously removed/added* to maintain constant temperature — closest to ideal isothermal process | **70 – 80 %** | High (advanced TES / multi-stage sprays) | SustainX, LightSail Energy (R&D stage) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Distinction:** D-CAES is the *only* commercially-deployed CAES technology today. A-CAES is the *future* of CAES — it eliminates fossil-fuel dependence. I-CAES is *theoretically* the most efficient but technically the most challenging.

### 2.2.3 Operating Modes

- **Charging:** Compressor compresses air → air is cooled (D-CAES) or heat captured (A-CAES) → stored in cavern.
- **Standby:** Air remains under pressure. Slow leakage = small self-discharge (~1–3 %/cycle).
- **Discharging:** Compressed air is expanded through turbine → generator produces electricity (D-CAES: preheated by natural gas; A-CAES: preheated by stored thermal energy).

### 2.2.4 Storage Cavern Geologies

- **Salt Caverns** — Most common, leak-tight, depths 600–1500 m, pressures up to 100 bar.
- **Depleted Gas/Oil Fields** — Large volume, requires existing infrastructure.
- **Aquifer Reservoirs** — Porous rock structures with water cushion.
- **Lined Rock Caverns (LRC)** — Artificial, expensive but location-flexible.

> [!TIP]
> **Geological Constraint:** India's **Thar Desert (Rajasthan)** and **Kutch basin (Gujarat)** contain salt formations suitable for CAES caverns — a major research and deployment opportunity for the Indian grid.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Reference)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **all key equations** required for solving numerical and analytical problems on PHS and CAES in the KTU 2024 ESE. Note: vertical bars use `\vert` to preserve table syntax.

| # | Concept | Equation | Variable Definitions | Typical Units | Validity / Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Gravitational PE stored in PHS** | $E = \rho \, g \, V \, H$ | $\rho$ = water density, $g$ = gravity, $V$ = water volume, $H$ = head (elevation difference) | kWh or MJ | Steady state, incompressible fluid |
| 2 | **PHS Electrical Energy Output (Generation)** | $E_{out} = \eta_{turb-gen} \, \rho \, g \, V \, H$ | $\eta_{turb-gen}$ = turbine-generator efficiency | kWh | Discharging mode |
| 3 | **PHS Electrical Energy Input (Pumping)** | $E_{in} = \dfrac{\rho \, g \, V \, H}{\eta_{pump-mot}}$ | $\eta_{pump-mot}$ = pump-motor efficiency | kWh | Charging mode |
| 4 | **PHS Round-Trip Efficiency** | $\eta_{RT} = \eta_{pump-mot} \times \eta_{turb-gen}$ | Both efficiencies multiplied | dimensionless | Ideal model; real systems include hydraulic losses |
| 5 | **Hydraulic Power (PHS)** | $P_{hyd} = \rho \, g \, Q \, H$ | $Q$ = volumetric flow rate | W or kW | Steady flow |
| 6 | **Pressure-Volume Work (CAES, Isothermal)** | $W = P_1 V_1 \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$ | $P_1, V_1$ = initial pressure, volume; $P_2$ = final pressure | J | Isothermal process, ideal gas |
| 7 | **Pressure-Volume Work (CAES, Adiabatic)** | $W = \dfrac{P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1}{1 - \gamma}$ | $\gamma = C_p / C_v$ for air $\approx 1.4$ | J | Adiabatic, reversible (Polytropic) |
| 8 | **CAES Energy Stored (Isothermal Ideal)** | $E_{store} = nRT \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$ | $n$ = moles, $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K), $T$ = absolute temperature | J | Isothermal compression |
| 9 | **Mass of Air Stored in CAES** | $m = \dfrac{P_1 V_1}{RT}$ | $M$ = molar mass of air (0.029 kg/mol) | kg | Ideal gas law |
| 10 | **CAES Round-Trip Efficiency** | $\eta_{RT} = \eta_{comp} \times \eta_{exp} \times \eta_{TES}$ | Comp/expander/TES efficiencies | dimensionless | Depends on variant |
| 11 | **Adiabatic Temperature Rise (CAES)** | $T_2 = T_1 \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ | $T_1, T_2$ = inlet, outlet temperature (K) | K | Reversible adiabatic |
| 12 | **Discharge Time** | $t_d = \dfrac{E}{P}$ | Energy $E$, rated power $P$ | hours | Steady discharge |
| 13 | **Specific Energy (PHS)** | $E/m = gH$ | Per unit mass of water | Wh/kg | Theoretical |
| 14 | **LCOS (Simplified)** | $LCOS = \dfrac{C_{cap} + C_{O\&M}}{E_{lifetime}}$ | $C_{cap}$ = capital cost, $C_{O\&M}$ = O&M cost, $E_{lifetime}$ = lifetime throughput | ₹/kWh | Project finance metric |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **PHS** is the **backbone of frequency regulation & load-leveling** in national grids (e.g., in India, PHS capacity ≈ 96 GW of planned additions by 2030).
- **CAES** is **geographically flexible** (can be built where salt caverns exist) and **complementary to wind/solar** in regions without mountain terrain.
- Both are **non-degrading**, **long-life**, and **zero self-discharge** technologies — unmatched by batteries in the > 4-hour duration category.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Design Metric – Typical PHS Performance:**
> - $H = 100$–$700$ m, $V = 10^6$–$10^9$ m³, $P = 100$–$3000$ MW, $t_d = 6$–$24$ h, $\eta_{RT} = 75$–$85\%$.
> **Typical CAES Performance:**
> - $P_{storage} = 40$–$80$ bar, $P = 50$–$300$ MW, $t_d = 8$–$26$ h, $\eta_{RT} = 42$–$70\%$.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Guidance:** The KTU board examiner expects a *fully written-out derivation* — every algebraic transition must be shown. Numerical evaluation requires **unit consistency** (SI). I have provided exhaustive working below.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Gravitational Energy Stored in a PHS Upper Reservoir

**Statement:** Derive an expression for the total gravitational potential energy stored when a volume $V$ of water of density $\rho$ is raised through a vertical head $H$ against gravity $g$.

**Setup (Textual):**
We consider a differential slab of water of cross-sectional area $dA$ and thickness $dh$ located at height $h$ above the lower reservoir. The mass of this slab is $dm = \rho \, dA \, dh$. Its gravitational potential energy relative to the lower reservoir is $dE = dm \cdot g \cdot h = \rho \, g \, h \, dA \, dh$.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= \int_{0}^{H} \int_{A} \rho \, g \, h \, dA \, dh \\
  &= \rho \, g \left( \int_{0}^{H} h \, dh \right) \left( \int_{A} dA \right) \\
  &= \rho \, g \left[ \frac{h^2}{2} \right]_{0}^{H} \cdot A \\
  &= \rho \, g \cdot \frac{H^2}{2} \cdot A
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual Explanation of Each Step:**
- Line 1: Sets up the double integral over height ($0 \to H$) and area ($A$).
- Line 2: Since $\rho$ and $g$ are constant, they are factored outside the integral.
- Line 3: Height integration yields $H^2/2$; area integration yields $A$ (the total horizontal cross-section at any level, treated as a constant for simplicity).
- Line 4: Substituting limits $0$ to $H$ and evaluating area integral.

Since the **total volume** of water is $V = A \cdot H$, we substitute $A = V/H$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= \rho \, g \cdot \frac{H^2}{2} \cdot \frac{V}{H} \\
  &= \frac{1}{2} \, \rho \, g \, V \, H
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor $\tfrac{1}{2}$ arises because *not all* water sits at height $H$ — water near the bottom contributes less PE than water at the top. The full reservoir's PE is the average head ($\tfrac{H}{2}$) times the total weight ($\rho g V$).

**Final Form (using full head, accounting for reservoir geometry):** For real reservoirs with arbitrary shape, the engineering approximation uses the **mean head** $\bar{H}$, and the total stored energy simplifies to:

$$
\boxed{E_{PHS} = \rho \, g \, V \, \bar{H} \quad [\text{Joules}]}
$$

**Unit Conversion to kWh (often required in KTU):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E \;[\text{kWh}] &= \frac{\rho \, g \, V \, \bar{H}}{3.6 \times 10^{6}} \\
\text{For water:} \quad E \;[\text{kWh}] &= \frac{1000 \times 9.81 \times V \,[\text{m}^3] \times \bar{H}\,[\text{m}]}{3.6 \times 10^6} \\
&= 2.725 \times 10^{-3} \, V \, \bar{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: PHS Round-Trip Efficiency

**Statement:** Show that the round-trip efficiency of a PHS plant equals the product of pump-motor and turbine-generator efficiencies (neglecting hydraulic & penstock friction losses).

**Charging Energy Input:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{in} &= \frac{\text{Hydraulic energy delivered to upper reservoir}}{\eta_{pump-mot}} \\
       &= \frac{\rho \, g \, V \, \bar{H}}{\eta_{pm}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Discharging Energy Output:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{out} &= \text{Hydraulic energy} \times \eta_{turbine-generator} \\
        &= \rho \, g \, V \, \bar{H} \times \eta_{tg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Round-Trip Efficiency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{RT} &= \frac{E_{out}}{E_{in}} \\
          &= \frac{\rho \, g \, V \, \bar{H} \cdot \eta_{tg}}{\dfrac{\rho \, g \, V \, \bar{H}}{\eta_{pm}}} \\
          &= \eta_{tg} \cdot \eta_{pm}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{RT,PHS} = \eta_{pump-motor} \times \eta_{turbine-generator}}
$$

**Typical numerical values (KTU Board):** $\eta_{pm} = 0.90$, $\eta_{tg} = 0.92$ → $\eta_{RT} = 0.828 = 82.8\%$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Isothermal CAES Energy Stored

**Statement:** Derive the energy stored in compressing an ideal gas isothermally from pressure $P_1$ to $P_2$ in a cavern of volume $V_1$.

**Starting Point — First Law for Closed System (Isothermal, Ideal Gas):**
For an isothermal process, internal energy change $\Delta U = 0$. Therefore, by the first law:

$$
Q = W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV
$$

For an ideal gas, $P V = n R T = \text{constant} = P_1 V_1$. So $P = P_1 V_1 / V$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{P_1 V_1}{V} \, dV \\
  &= P_1 V_1 \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{dV}{V} \\
  &= P_1 V_1 \left[ \ln V \right]_{V_1}^{V_2} \\
  &= P_1 V_1 \ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual Step-by-Step Logic:**
- Line 1: Substitutes ideal gas law to express $P$ as a function of $V$.
- Line 2: Constants ($P_1 V_1$) are pulled out of the integral.
- Line 3: Integral of $1/V$ is $\ln V$.
- Line 4: Evaluates between limits $V_1$ and $V_2$.

Since $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$ (isothermal ideal gas), we can also write:

$$
\boxed{E_{CAES,\text{iso}} = P_1 V_1 \ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = n R T \ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) \quad [\text{Joules}]}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** The energy stored is *linear in* $\ln(P_2/P_1)$ — doubling the pressure ratio does *not* double the energy. This is a key conceptual insight: **diminishing returns at high pressure ratios**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Adiabatic CAES Temperature Rise

**Statement:** A CAES system compresses air adiabatically from $(T_1, P_1)$ to $(T_2, P_2)$. Show that $T_2 = T_1 \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$.

**Starting Point:** For a reversible adiabatic (isentropic) process of an ideal gas:

$$
\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}
$$

This follows from combining $T V^{\gamma - 1} = \text{const}$ and $P V^\gamma = \text{const}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P V^\gamma &= \text{const} \quad \text{(adiabatic relation)} \\
T V^{\gamma - 1} &= \text{const} \quad \text{(derived from ideal gas + adiabatic)} \\
\Rightarrow \quad T &= \text{const} \cdot V^{1 - \gamma} \\
\Rightarrow \quad \frac{T_2}{T_1} &= \left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{\gamma - 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

Eliminating $V$ using $\dfrac{V_1}{V_2} = \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/\gamma}$:

$$
\boxed{T_2 = T_1 \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}
$$

**Numerical Example (KTU):** $T_1 = 300$ K, $P_2/P_1 = 10$, $\gamma = 1.4$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_2 &= 300 \times (10)^{0.4/1.4} \\
    &= 300 \times 10^{0.2857} \\
    &= 300 \times 1.9307 \\
    &\approx 579.2 \; \text{K} \; (\approx 306^\circ C)
\end{aligned}
$$

This extreme temperature justifies why **A-CAES must store the heat** in a TES — otherwise thermal energy is wasted (D-CAES loses it to the environment, requiring natural gas to reheat).

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A PHS plant has an upper reservoir of volume $V = 2 \times 10^6$ m³ at a mean head $\bar{H} = 300$ m above the lower reservoir. Pump-motor efficiency is 88 %, turbine-generator efficiency is 92 %. Calculate:
(a) Total energy stored.
(b) Round-trip efficiency.
(c) Useful electrical energy delivered per cycle.
(d) Hours of discharge at a rated power of 800 MW.

**Solution:**

**(a) Total Energy Stored (Mechanical, water PE):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{PE} &= \rho \, g \, V \, \bar{H} \\
       &= 1000 \times 9.81 \times (2 \times 10^6) \times 300 \\
       &= 5.886 \times 10^{12} \; \text{J}
\end{aligned}
$$

Converting to kWh:

$$
E_{PE} = \frac{5.886 \times 10^{12}}{3.6 \times 10^6} = 1.635 \times 10^6 \; \text{kWh} = 1635 \; \text{MWh}
$$

**(b) Round-Trip Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{RT} = \eta_{pm} \times \eta_{tg} = 0.88 \times 0.92 = 0.8096 \approx 80.96\%
$$

**(c) Useful Electrical Output per Cycle:**

$$
E_{out} = \eta_{RT} \times E_{PE} = 0.8096 \times 1635 = 1323.7 \; \text{MWh}
$$

**(d) Discharge Duration at 800 MW:**

$$
t_d = \frac{E_{out}}{P} = \frac{1323.7 \; \text{MWh}}{800 \; \text{MW}} = 1.655 \; \text{h} \approx 1 \; \text{h} \; 39 \; \text{min}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Board Tip:** Always quote answers in **2–3 significant figures**, include units in *every step*, and convert to kWh/MWh for electrical energy (mandatory in KTU board valuation).

```mermaid
graph TD

```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Top branch: **Charging path** — Grid power → Pump → Lower reservoir → Upper reservoir.
- Bottom branch: **Discharging path** — Upper reservoir → Turbine → Generator → Grid.
- **Surge tank monitor** and **SCADA control** ensure hydraulic and electrical stability.

```mermaid
graph TD

    P --> J
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Dashed line** between TES and combustion chamber represents the *optional* adiabatic upgrade — heat captured at compression is reused at expansion, eliminating natural gas dependency.
- **Multi-stage compression with intercooling** is essential to limit temperature rise and reduce compressor work.

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — CAES Variant Comparison Matrix (Functional View)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph VARIANT_DIABATIC
        A1[Compressor] --> A2[Heat Rejected to Atmosphere]
        A2 --> A3[Cavern Storage]

        A4 --> A5[Expander - Generator]
    end

    subgraph VARIANT_ADIABATIC
        B1[Compressor] --> B2[Heat Captured in TES Unit]
        B2 --> B3[Cavern Storage]
        B3 --> B4[TES Heat Released]
        B4 --> B5[Expander - Generator]
    end

    subgraph VARIANT_ISOTHERMAL

        C2 --> C3[Cavern Storage]
        C3 --> C4[Continuous Heat Addition]
        C4 --> C5[Expander - Generator]
    end
```

**Engineering Interpretation:**
- **Diabatic:** Cheapest, *wastes* compression heat (40–54% RTE).
- **Adiabatic:** Stores compression heat in TES (60–70% RTE).
- **Isothermal:** Maintains constant temperature throughout — closest to ideal reversible (70–80% RTE theoretical, technically hardest).
