\begin{aligned}
E &= \frac{1}{2}\, L\, I^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

(1 mark for result, 2 marks for clarity, units, and physical interpretation)

> The energy is stored entirely in the magnetic field linking the coil. Since $L$ is determined by geometry, increasing $N$ or $A$ increases $E$ quadratically through $L \propto N^2$.

##### Part (b) — Numerical Evaluation

**Given:** $N = 2000$, $l = 1.0\ \text{m}$, $A = 0.05\ \text{m}^{2}$, $I = 500\ \text{A}$, $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}\ \text{H/m}$.

**(i) Inductance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= \frac{\mu_{0}\, N^{2}\, A}{l} \\[6pt]
  &= \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7})\, (2000)^{2}\, (0.05)}{1.0} \\[6pt]
  &= \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7})\, (4 \times 10^{6})\, (0.05)}{1.0} \\[6pt]
  &= 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 2 \times 10^{5} \\[6pt]
  &= 8\pi \times 10^{-2}\ \text{H} \\[6pt]
  &\approx 0.2513\ \text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

(2 marks for the final numerical value)

**(ii) Energy stored:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= \frac{1}{2}\, L\, I^{2} \\[6pt]
  &= \frac{1}{2} \times 0.2513 \times (500)^{2} \\[6pt]
  &= 0.5 \times 0.2513 \times 2.5 \times 10^{5} \\[6pt]
  &\approx 3.14 \times 10^{4}\ \text{J}
\end{aligned}
$$

(2 marks)

In Wh: $E = 3.14 \times 10^{4} / 3600 \approx 8.73\ \text{Wh}$ (1 mark for the unit conversion showing practical relevance)

**(iii) Magnetic field:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B &= \mu_{0}\, \left(\frac{N}{l}\right)\, I \\[6pt]
  &= (4\pi \times 10^{-7})\, (2000)\, (500) \\[6pt]
  &= 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 1.0 \times 10^{6} \\[6pt]
  &= 0.4\pi\ \text{T} \approx 1.257\ \text{T}
\end{aligned}
$$

(2 marks)

> ✅ **Sanity check:** $B = 1.257\ \text{T} \ll B_c \approx 10\ \text{T}$ for Nb-Ti → operation is well within the safe envelope. (Extra credit in viva)

---

### ❓ Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks)

`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Modified)]`

**(a)** Starting from the magnetic energy density, derive the **total energy stored in a SMES coil volume $V$** in terms of the magnetic field $B$. Discuss why this formula is more insightful than $E = \tfrac{1}{2} L I^2$ for **material selection** in SMES design. (7 marks)

**(b)** A toroidal SMES magnet of mean radius $R = 0.5\ \text{m}$ and cross-section $a = 0.1\ \text{m}$ carries a current $I = 1000\ \text{A}$ through $N = 1000$ turns. Compute:
  - (i) the **energy density** in the field region, and
  - (ii) the **total energy stored** in the toroid's magnetic volume.

State all assumptions. (7 marks)

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Question B

##### Part (a) — Derivation of $E = B^2 V / (2\mu_0)$

Begin with the energy stored in a solenoid via the inductance formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= \frac{1}{2}\, L\, I^{2},\quad L = \frac{\mu_{0}\, N^{2}\, A}{l}
\end{aligned}
$$

(1 mark)

Express the field in the solenoid as $B = \mu_0 (N/l) I \Rightarrow I = B l / (\mu_0 N)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I^{2} &= \frac{B^{2}\, l^{2}}{\mu_{0}^{\,2}\, N^{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

(1 mark)

Substitute both into $E = \tfrac{1}{2} L I^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\mu_{0}\, N^{2}\, A}{l} \cdot \frac{B^{2}\, l^{2}}{\mu_{0}^{\,2}\, N^{2}} \\[4pt]
  &= \frac{B^{2}\, A\, l}{2\mu_{0}}
\end{aligned}
$$

(2 marks)

Recognize coil volume $V = A \cdot l$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= \frac{B^{2}}{2\mu_{0}} \cdot V
\end{aligned}
$$

(1 mark)

**Discussion — Why this is more insightful for material selection (2 marks):**

The expression $E = B^2 V / (2\mu_0)$ shows that the stored energy depends only on the **magnetic field magnitude $B$ and the coil volume $V$** — *not* on the current, turn count, or wire length individually. This is highly valuable for material selection because:

1. The upper bound on $B$ is set by the superconductor's **critical field $B_c$** (e.g., 10 T for Nb-Ti, >30 T for YBCO).
2. The maximum energy density is $u_{max} = B_c^{\,2}/(2\mu_0)$ — a **material property figure of merit**.
3. Designers can compare superconductors *purely* on $B_c$ and operating temperature without redoing the full geometric analysis.
4. It also shows that **shrinking $V$** (compact designs) directly reduces energy unless $B$ is increased — pushing research toward higher-$B_c$ HTS tapes.

##### Part (b) — Toroidal SMES Numerical Evaluation

**Given:** $R = 0.5\ \text{m}$, $a = 0.1\ \text{m}$, $I = 1000\ \text{A}$, $N = 1000$.

**Geometry assumption:** toroid with circular cross-section, mean circumference $2\pi R$, field confined to $r \le R$ and uniform across $A = \pi a^2$ (acceptable for thin toroid $a \ll R$). (1 mark for stating assumption)

**Field inside the toroid:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B &= \frac{\mu_{0}\, N\, I}{2\pi R} \\[6pt]
  &= \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7})\, (1000)\, (1000)}{2\pi \times 0.5} \\[6pt]
  &= \frac{4\pi \times 10^{-1}}{\pi} \\[6pt]
  &= 0.4\ \text{T}
\end{aligned}
$$

(1 mark)

**(i) Energy density:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
u &= \frac{B^{2}}{2\mu_{0}} \\[6pt]
  &= \frac{(0.4)^{2}}{2 \times (4\pi \times 10^{-7})} \\[6pt]
  &= \frac{0.16}{2.513 \times 10^{-6}} \\[6pt]
  &\approx 6.37 \times 10^{4}\ \text{J/m}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

(2 marks)

**(ii) Total energy:**

The toroidal magnetic volume is $V = (2\pi R) \cdot (\pi a^{2})$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= 2\pi (0.5) \cdot \pi (0.1)^{2} \\[4pt]
  &= \pi^{2} \times 0.5 \times 0.01 \\[4pt]
  &= 0.0493\ \text{m}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

(1 mark)

Total energy:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= u \cdot V = 6.37 \times 10^{4} \times 0.0493 \\[4pt]
  &\approx 3.14 \times 10^{3}\ \text{J} \approx 3.14\ \text{kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

(2 marks)

> ✅ **Cross-check** via $E = \tfrac{1}{2} L I^2$ where $L = \mu_0 N^2 A / (2\pi R) = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 10^{6} \times \pi \times 0.01 / (\pi) \approx 1.2566 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{H}$, giving $E = 0.5 \times 0.01257 \times 10^{6} \approx 6.28 \times 10^{3}\ \text{J}$. The two methods should agree; small differences arise from assumptions about field uniformity. (Bonus mark in viva)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember 📌

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density revision checklist for SMES (PEEET521 Module 2):**

- 🔹 **Definition:** SMES stores energy as magnetic field of a **persistent DC current** in a **superconducting coil**.
- 🔹 **Core storage equation:** $E = \tfrac{1}{2} L I^{2}$ — quadratic in current, quadratic in turns.
- 🔹 **Field energy density:** $u = B^{2}/(2\mu_0)$ — quadratic in field, sets material limit.
- 🔹 **Solenoid design trio:** $L = \mu_0 N^2 A / l$, $B = \mu_0 (N/l) I$, $E = B^2 V / (2\mu_0)$.
- 🔹 **Why superconducting?** Zero resistance → persistent current → indefinite storage with negligible loss.
- 🔹 **Persistent switch** allows lossless standby mode in a closed superconducting loop.
- 🔹 **Quench protection** (crowbar resistor) is mandatory to safely dump current if superconductivity is lost.
- 🔹 **LTS (Nb-Ti, 4.2 K)** vs. **HTS (YBCO/Bi-2212, 65–77 K)** — HTS trades conductor cost for cheaper cryogenics.
- 🔹 **Round-trip efficiency** of SMES: typically **90–95%+**, the highest among grid-scale storage.
- 🔹 **Discharge time:** milliseconds to seconds — the fastest among mechanical/electrochemical storage.
- 🔹 **Main disadvantages:** high capital cost, cryogenic maintenance, stray magnetic fields requiring shielding.
- 🔹 **Key applications:** power quality, voltage sag mitigation, pulsed power, renewable smoothing, grid frequency regulation.
- 🔹 **Always remember to convert** $J \to Wh$ by dividing by **3600** for practical energy reporting.
- 🔹 **Memorize constants:** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}\ \text{H/m}$ and typical $B_c$ values (Nb-Ti: ~10 T, YBCO: >30 T).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Comparative Analysis
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET521 - ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Comparative Analysis

# 🔋 Comparative Analysis of Energy Storage Systems

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comparative Analysis of Energy Storage Systems (ESS)** is the **systematic, multi-criteria evaluation** of diverse electrical, electrochemical, mechanical, thermal, and chemical energy storage technologies against standardized **performance metrics** (energy density, power density, efficiency, cycle life), **economic indicators** (capital cost, levelized cost of storage, payback period), and **environmental & operational parameters** (safety, footprint, technology readiness level) to determine the most suitable technology for a given application — ranging from milliwatt-scale IoT devices to gigawatt-scale grid storage.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus context, this topic sits at the intersection of **electrochemistry, power electronics, thermodynamics, and techno-economic analysis**, requiring students to internalize that **no single storage technology is universally optimal** — selection is always a trade-off exercise.

### 1.2 The Eight Pillars of ESS Comparison

Any rigorous comparative analysis must examine the following pillars:

1. **Energy Density** ($\text{Wh/kg}$ and $\text{Wh/L}$)
2. **Power Density** ($\text{W/kg}$ and $\text{W/L}$)
3. **Round-Trip Efficiency** ($\eta_{RT}$ in %)
4. **Cycle Life** (number of charge–discharge cycles before 80% capacity retention)
5. **Calendar Life** (years of operational life)
6. **Self-Discharge Rate** (% per month or per day)
7. **Capital Expenditure (CAPEX)** (\$/kWh for energy, \$/kW for power)
8. **Response Time** (milliseconds to minutes)

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Vehicle Selection" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you are choosing a vehicle for different missions.
> - A **Formula-1 car** delivers blistering acceleration (high *power density*) but cannot carry cargo far (low *energy density*).
> - A **cargo truck** hauls tonnes across continents (high *energy*) but accelerates slowly (low *power*).
> - A **hybrid car** balances both.
>
> Similarly, in energy storage: **supercapacitors are the F1 cars** (fast, powerful, but energy-starved), **pumped hydro storage is the cargo truck** (massive energy, slow response), and **Li-ion batteries are the hybrids**. The *Comparative Analysis* is your engineering decision matrix that tells you which "vehicle" to deploy on which "road."

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standardized metrics are universally accepted in KTU-endorsed literature:

- **1 kWh** = $3.6 \times 10^6$ Joules
- **1 Ah** at **3.7 V (nominal Li-ion)** $\equiv$ **3.7 Wh** of stored energy
- **Standard Temperature & Pressure (STP):** 273.15 K, 1 atm (used for hydrogen & CAES)
- **Gravimetric Energy Density** units: **Wh·kg⁻¹**
- **Volumetric Energy Density** units: **Wh·L⁻¹**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework for Comparison

A robust comparative analysis rests on three analytical layers:

### Layer 1 — Thermodynamic & Electrochemical Limits
Every storage medium obeys the **Gibbs Free Energy** relationship:
$$\Delta G = -nFE_{cell}$$

where $n$ is the number of electrons transferred, $F = 96485$ C·mol⁻¹ (Faraday's constant), and $E_{cell}$ is the open-circuit voltage. This sets the **theoretical maximum** specific energy of any electrochemical cell.

### Layer 2 — Practical Performance Parameters
Real systems deviate from theory due to:
- **Ohmic losses** ($I^2R$)
- **Activation overpotentials** (Butler–Volmer kinetics)
- **Concentration overpotentials** (mass transport limitations)
- **Self-discharge** (parasitic side reactions)

### Layer 3 — Economic & Lifecycle Metrics
- **LCOS (Levelized Cost of Storage)** in \$/MWh
- **Payback Period** in years
- **C-rate capability**: $C\text{-rate} = \dfrac{I}{Q_{rated}}$

### 2.2 The C-Rate Concept (Foundational)

The **C-rate** is a normalized current that allows apples-to-apples comparison across batteries of different capacities:

$$I_{actual} = C\text{-rate} \times Q_{rated}$$

- A **1C** discharge of a **100 Ah** battery delivers **100 A**, completing discharge in **1 hour**.
- A **0.5C** discharge delivers **50 A**, completing in **2 hours**.
- A **2C** discharge delivers **200 A**, completing in **0.5 hours**.

### 2.3 Ragone Plot — The Visual Heart of Comparative Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Ragone Plot** is the single most important visual tool in ESS comparison. It plots **specific energy (Wh/kg)** on the x-axis against **specific power (W/kg)** on the y-axis, both on logarithmic scales. Each technology occupies a distinct region, allowing engineers to instantly identify the best candidate for a given application.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units | Applicable To |
|---|---------|----------------|-------|---------------|
| 1 | $E = V \times Q$ | Energy = Voltage × Charge | Wh | All batteries |
| 2 | $\rho_E = \dfrac{E}{m}$ | Gravimetric energy density | Wh/kg | All ESS |
| 3 | $\rho_P = \dfrac{P}{m}$ | Gravimetric power density | W/kg | All ESS |
| 4 | $\eta_{RT} = \dfrac{E_{out}}{E_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Round-trip efficiency | % | All ESS |
| 5 | $\text{SOC} = \dfrac{Q_{remaining}}{Q_{rated}} \times 100\%$ | State of Charge | % | Batteries |
| 6 | $\text{DOD} = 100\% - \text{SOC}$ | Depth of Discharge | % | Batteries |
| 7 | $E_{theoretical} = \dfrac{nF \cdot E_{cell}}{\sum M_i}$ | Theoretical specific energy | Wh/kg | Electrochemical cells |
| 8 | $\text{LCOS} = \dfrac{C_{total}}{\sum E_{delivered}}$ | Levelized Cost of Storage | \$/MWh | All ESS |
| 9 | $\eta_{coulombic} = \dfrac{Q_{discharge}}{Q_{charge}} \times 100\%$ | Coulombic (Ah) efficiency | % | Batteries |
| 10 | $\eta_{voltage} = \dfrac{V_{avg,disch}}{V_{avg,chg}} \times 100\%$ | Voltage efficiency | % | Batteries |
| 11 | $P = \dfrac{E}{t}$ | Power = Energy / time | W | All ESS |
| 12 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{coulombic} \times \eta_{voltage}$ | Overall battery efficiency | % | Batteries |

> [!WARNING]
> **Notation Pitfall:** When writing $\vert x \vert$ (absolute value) in your exam, **never use the vertical pipe `\|`** inside markdown tables. Use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\mid x \mid$ to preserve table rendering integrity.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Comparative analysis drives critical infrastructure decisions in:
- **Electric Vehicles (EVs):** Li-ion dominates due to high $\rho_E$.
- **Grid Load-Leveling:** Pumped hydro & CAES chosen for scale.
- **UPS & Ride-Through:** Flywheels & supercapacitors chosen for response.
- **Off-Grid Solar:** Lead-acid & LiFePO₄ chosen for cost-effectiveness.
- **Satellites & Space:** Fuel cells + Li-ion chosen for energy-to-mass ratio.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Theoretical Specific Energy of a Lithium-Manganese Oxide (LiMnO₂) Cell

> This derivation demonstrates how theoretical limits are calculated — a **frequently asked 7-mark KTU question**.

**Given:**
- Cell reaction: $\text{Li} + \text{MnO}_2 \rightarrow \text{LiMnO}_2$
- $n = 1$ electron per reaction
- $E_{cell} = 3.0$ V (theoretical open-circuit voltage)
- Atomic masses: Li = 6.94 g/mol, Mn = 54.94 g/mol, O = 16.00 g/mol

**Step 1 — Sum the molar masses of reactants:**
$$M_{Li} = 6.94 \text{ g/mol}$$
$$M_{MnO_2} = 54.94 + (2 \times 16.00) = 86.94 \text{ g/mol}$$
$$\sum M_i = 6.94 + 86.94 = 93.88 \text{ g/mol} = 0.09388 \text{ kg/mol}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Gibbs Free Energy per mole of reaction:**
$$\Delta G = -nFE_{cell}$$
$$\Delta G = -(1)(96485)(3.0)$$
$$\Delta G = -289455 \text{ J/mol} = -289.455 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to theoretical specific energy:**
$$E_{theoretical} = \dfrac{\vert \Delta G \vert}{\sum M_i}$$
$$E_{theoretical} = \dfrac{289455 \text{ J/mol}}{0.09388 \text{ kg/mol}}$$
$$E_{theoretical} = 3083700 \text{ J/kg}$$

**Step 4 — Convert J/kg to Wh/kg** (divide by 3600):
$$E_{theoretical} = \dfrac{3083700}{3600} \text{ Wh/kg}$$
$$\boxed{E_{theoretical} \approx 856.6 \text{ Wh/kg}}$$

> **Practical LiMnO₂ delivers only ~120 Wh/kg**, meaning the **realization ratio** is $120/856.6 \approx 14\%$. This illustrates why KTU examiners love asking "why is actual energy density so much lower than theoretical?" — answer: **kinetic overpotentials, inactive materials (binders, electrolyte, current collectors, packaging), and inaccessible capacity at safe voltage windows.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Round-Trip Efficiency from Voltage & Coulombic Components

**Problem:** A Li-ion cell is charged at a constant current of 50 A from 20% to 90% SOC over 1.4 hours, absorbing 105 Ah. It is then discharged at 50 A back to 20% SOC, delivering 100 Ah. Average charge voltage = 3.8 V; average discharge voltage = 3.5 V.

**Step 1 — Coulombic (Ah) Efficiency:**
$$\eta_{coulombic} = \dfrac{Q_{discharge}}{Q_{charge}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{100}{105} \times 100\% = 95.24\%$$

**Step 2 — Voltage Efficiency:**
$$\eta_{voltage} = \dfrac{V_{avg,disch}}{V_{avg,chg}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{3.5}{3.8} \times 100\% = 92.11\%$$

**Step 3 — Overall Round-Trip Efficiency:**
$$\eta_{RT} = \eta_{coulombic} \times \eta_{voltage}$$
$$\eta_{RT} = 0.9524 \times 0.9211$$
$$\eta_{RT} = 0.8773$$
$$\boxed{\eta_{RT} \approx 87.73\%}$$

**Step 4 — Energy In vs Energy Out:**
$$E_{in} = V_{chg} \times Q_{chg} = 3.8 \times 105 = 399 \text{ Wh}$$
$$E_{out} = V_{disch} \times Q_{disch} = 3.5 \times 100 = 350 \text{ Wh}$$
$$\eta_{RT} = \dfrac{350}{399} \times 100\% = 87.72\% \;\; (\text{matches, small rounding})$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Levelized Cost of Storage (LCOS)

**LCOS** is the most economically critical metric in modern ESS comparison.

**Given:**
- Capital cost: $C_{cap} = \$500{,}000$
- O&M cost over lifetime: $C_{O\&M} = \$50{,}000$
- Charging electricity cost: $C_{charge} = \$80{,}000$ (lifetime)
- Total lifetime energy delivered: $E_{del} = 10{,}000$ MWh

**Step 1 — Total Lifecycle Cost:**
$$C_{total} = C_{cap} + C_{O\&M} + C_{charge}$$
$$C_{total} = 500{,}000 + 50{,}000 + 80{,}000 = \$630{,}000$$

**Step 2 — LCOS:**
$$\text{LCOS} = \dfrac{C_{total}}{E_{del}} = \dfrac{630{,}000}{10{,}000}$$
$$\boxed{\text{LCOS} = \$63/\text{MWh}}$$

> This compares with **PHS at \$30–\$60/MWh**, **Li-ion at \$130–\$250/MWh**, and **CAES at \$100–\$200/MWh** (approximate 2024 figures).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Master Reference Table)

| Storage Technology | Energy Density (Wh/kg) | Power Density (W/kg) | Round-Trip Eff. (%) | Cycle Life (cycles) | Self-Discharge | Response Time | CAPEX (\$/kWh) | Maturity (TRL) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pumped Hydro Storage (PHS)** | 0.5 – 1.5 | — | 75 – 85 | >15,000 | ~0% | sec–min | 5 – 100 | 9 (Mature) |
| **Compressed Air (CAES)** | 3 – 6 | — | 42 – 54 (adiabatic up to 70) | >10,000 | ~0% | sec–min | 50 – 150 | 8 (Commercial) |
| **Lead-Acid Battery** | 30 – 50 | 75 – 300 | 70 – 85 | 500 – 1,200 | 3 – 20%/mo | ms | 100 – 200 | 9 (Mature) |
| **Li-ion (NMC)** | 150 – 220 | 250 – 1,000 | 90 – 97 | 1,000 – 4,000 | 1 – 5%/mo | ms | 130 – 300 | 9 (Mature) |
| **LiFePO₄** | 90 – 160 | 200 – 800 | 92 – 95 | 2,000 – 6,000 | 1 – 3%/mo | ms | 150 – 350 | 9 (Mature) |
| **NaS Battery** | 150 – 240 | 150 – 230 | 85 – 90 | 2,500 – 4,500 | ~0%/day | ms | 250 – 400 | 8 (Commercial) |
| **Supercapacitor (EDLC)** | 5 – 15 | 5,000 – 15,000 | 90 – 98 | 100,000 – 1,000,000 | 10 – 40%/day | <1 ms | 1,000 – 5,000 | 9 (Mature) |
| **Flywheel** | 10 – 130 | 400 – 6,000 | 80 – 95 | 20,000 – 100,000 | ~100%/hr | <10 ms | 500 – 2,000 | 8 (Commercial) |
| **Hydrogen (Fuel Cell + Tank)** | 33,000 (LHV) | 500 – 2,000 | 30 – 50 | 1,000 – 5,000 | ~0%/day | sec | 15 – 100 (tank only) | 7 (Demo) |
| **Thermal (Molten Salt)** | 50 – 100 | — | 50 – 90 | >10,000 | ~1%/day | min | 20 – 60 | 8 (Commercial) |

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📚 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define "Round-Trip Efficiency" of an energy storage system. Why is it a critical parameter in grid-scale storage comparison?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition:** Round-Trip Efficiency ($\eta_{RT}$) is the ratio of the energy **delivered during discharge** to the energy **consumed during charging** of an energy storage system, expressed as a percentage.
>
> $$\eta_{RT} = \dfrac{E_{discharge}}{E_{charge}} \times 100\%$$
>
> **Criticality in grid storage (3 marks total):**
> - A 5% efficiency loss in a 1 GWh system wastes **50 MWh per cycle** — equivalent to several thousand households' daily consumption.
> - It directly determines the **Levelized Cost of Storage (LCOS)**.
> - Technologies like **Pumped Hydro (75–85%)** versus **Hydrogen (30–50%)** have vastly different economic outcomes despite similar capital costs.
> - Higher $\eta_{RT}$ means **less charging energy** is needed per MWh delivered, reducing operational expenditure and carbon footprint.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**What is a Ragone plot? Explain its significance in comparing energy storage systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition:** A Ragone plot is a **log-log scatter plot** with **specific energy (Wh/kg)** on the x-axis and **specific power (W/kg)** on the y-axis, used to compare energy storage technologies.
>
> **Significance (3 marks total):**
> 1. **Instant Visual Comparison** — supercapacitors occupy the high-power region; batteries occupy the middle; fuels (hydrogen) occupy the high-energy region.
> 2. **Application Matching** — engineers select the technology whose Ragone coordinates match the application's energy-to-power ratio.
> 3. **Identifies Technology Gaps** — empty regions of the plot reveal where R&D investment is needed (e.g., the "valley" between supercapacitors and batteries represents the sought-after hybrid supercabattery).

---

## 📝 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2, Set A]**

**(a)** Compare the **energy density, power density, cycle life, and round-trip efficiency** of **Lead-Acid, Lithium-ion (NMC), and Supercapacitors** with the help of a comparative table. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **100 kWh Li-ion battery bank** has a round-trip efficiency of **92%**. It is charged from the grid at **₹6/kWh** and discharged to supply a load. Calculate:
- (i) The **energy drawn from the grid** to fully charge the battery.
- (ii) The **cost of charging energy** per cycle.
- (iii) The **effective cost of delivered energy** per kWh.
- (iv) Comment on how **cycle life (3,000 cycles)** affects LCOS when CAPEX is **₹15,00,000**. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution

**(a) Comparative Table (7 marks)**

| Parameter | Lead-Acid | Li-ion (NMC) | Supercapacitor |
|---|---|---|---|
| Energy Density (Wh/kg) | 30 – 50 | 150 – 220 | 5 – 15 |
| Power Density (W/kg) | 75 – 300 | 250 – 1,000 | 5,000 – 15,000 |
| Cycle Life | 500 – 1,200 | 1,000 – 4,000 | 100,000 – 1,000,000 |
| Round-Trip Efficiency | 70 – 85% | 90 – 97% | 90 – 98% |
| Self-Discharge | 3 – 20%/month | 1 – 5%/month | 10 – 40%/day |
| Cost (\$/kWh) | 100 – 200 | 130 – 300 | 1,000 – 5,000 |
| Best Suited For | Automotive SLI, UPS | EVs, Grid Storage | Burst Power, Regenerative Braking |

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**(i) Energy drawn from grid to fully charge:**
$$E_{grid} = \dfrac{E_{battery}}{\eta_{RT}} = \dfrac{100}{0.92} = 108.70 \text{ kWh}$$

**(ii) Cost of charging energy per cycle:**
$$C_{charge} = 108.70 \times 6 = \text{₹}652.17$$

**(iii) Effective cost of delivered energy per kWh:**
$$\text{Effective rate} = \dfrac{C_{charge}}{E_{delivered}} = \dfrac{652.17}{100} = \text{₹}6.52/\text{kWh}$$

> **Comment:** Even though grid electricity costs ₹6/kWh, the **8% efficiency loss** raises the effective delivered cost to **₹6.52/kWh** — a hidden surcharge of ₹0.52 per kWh.

**(iv) LCOS with cycle life:**
$$\text{Total energy delivered over lifetime} = 100 \text{ kWh} \times 3{,}000 = 300{,}000 \text{ kWh}$$
$$\text{Charging energy cost over lifetime} = 3{,}000 \times 652.17 = \text{₹}19{,}56{,}521$$
$$\text{Total lifecycle cost} = 15{,}00{,}000 + 19{,}56{,}521 = \text{₹}34{,}56{,}521$$
$$\text{LCOS} = \dfrac{34{,}56{,}521}{300{,}000} = \text{₹}11.52/\text{kWh}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2, Set B]**

**(a)** With the help of a **Ragone plot description**, explain why **no single energy storage technology is universally optimal** for all applications. Discuss the placement of supercapacitors, Li-ion batteries, and hydrogen fuel cells on the Ragone plot. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A community microgrid uses a **500 kWh Vanadium Redox Flow Battery (VRFB)** with **75% round-trip efficiency** to store solar energy. The solar PV array generates **2 MWh/day**, of which **60%** is stored. The remaining **40%** is curtailed.
- (i) How much **useful energy** is delivered by the VRFB daily?
- (ii) If electricity from diesel backup costs **₹18/kWh**, what is the **daily savings** by avoiding diesel?
- (iii) If the VRFB costs **₹8,00,000 per kWh** and lasts **15 years with 5,000 cycles**, compute the **LCOS**. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution

**(a) Ragone Plot Analysis (7 marks)**

The Ragone plot reveals that energy storage technologies occupy **distinct, non-overlapping regions** based on the inherent physics of energy storage mechanisms:

- **Supercapacitors** (top-left): Energy stored as **electrostatic charge** in the electric double layer. This mechanism allows **extremely fast charge/discharge** (high W/kg) but stores very little total energy (low Wh/kg).

- **Li-ion batteries** (middle region): Energy stored via **Faradaic redox reactions** in the bulk electrode. This provides a **balanced trade-off** — both reasonable energy and power density — making them the "workhorse" of modern portable electronics and EVs.

- **Hydrogen fuel cells** (bottom-right): Energy stored as **chemical bonds** in H₂ molecules, with extremely high gravimetric energy density (~33,000 Wh/kg LHV). However, the **slow electrochemical oxidation kinetics** at the fuel cell membrane limit power density.

> **Conclusion:** Because no technology spans the entire Ragone plot, **applications must be matched** to technology. EVs need Li-ion (balanced), space launch needs H₂ (energy), and regenerative braking needs supercapacitors (power).

**(b) Microgrid Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**(i) Useful energy delivered daily:**
$$\text{Solar energy stored} = 0.60 \times 2000 = 1200 \text{ kWh/day (stored)}$$
$$\text{Useful energy out} = 1200 \times 0.75 = 900 \text{ kWh/day}$$

**(ii) Daily diesel cost savings:**
$$\text{Savings} = 900 \times 18 = \text{₹}16{,}200/\text{day}$$

**(iii) LCOS calculation:**
$$\text{CAPEX} = 500 \text{ kWh} \times 8{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}40{,}00{,}00{,}000$$
$$\text{Total energy delivered over lifetime} = 500 \text{ kWh} \times 5{,}000 = 25{,}00{,}000 \text{ kWh} = 2{,}500 \text{ MWh}$$

Assuming negligible O&M for simplicity:
$$\text{LCOS} = \dfrac{40{,}00{,}00{,}000}{25{,}00{,}000} = \text{₹}16/\text{kWh}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** — always carry kWh, kg, or V throughout your solution. KTU examiners deduct **0.5–1 mark** per missing unit.
> 2. **Confusing Coulombic vs Round-Trip Efficiency** — Coulombic is Ah/Ah; Round-Trip is Wh/Wh. **2-mark penalty** for mixing these.
> 3. **Not converting Wh to kWh** in LCOS problems — leading to LCOS values that are **1,000× too large**.
> 4. **Ignoring the denominator in cycle-life energy** — students often multiply capacity by cycles but forget to multiply by 365 days × years.
> 5. **Forgetting the 3600 conversion factor** when going between Joules and Wh in theoretical energy derivations.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **No universal ESS** — selection is **application-driven** and **trade-off-driven**.
- ✅ **Ragone Plot** is the **#1 visual tool** for comparing storage technologies (log-log, energy vs power).
- ✅ **Round-Trip Efficiency** combines **Coulombic** and **Voltage** efficiencies multiplicatively.
- ✅ **C-rate** normalizes current: $I = C\text{-rate} \times Q_{rated}$.
- ✅ **Theoretical specific energy** = $nF \cdot E_{cell} / \sum M_i$ — but **real systems achieve only 10–25%** of theoretical.
- ✅ **Supercapacitors** win on **power density and cycle life** but lose on **energy density and self-discharge**.
- ✅ **Li-ion NMC** has the **highest practical energy density** among mature batteries (~220 Wh/kg).
- ✅ **Pumped Hydro** remains the **largest deployed ESS globally** (>95% of grid storage by capacity).
- ✅ **Hydrogen** has the **highest theoretical energy density** but the **lowest round-trip efficiency** (~35%).
- ✅ **LCOS** is the **gold-standard economic metric** — incorporates CAPEX, O&M, charging cost, and lifetime energy delivered.
- ✅ **Self-discharge** disqualifies supercapacitors for long-duration storage — they are **short-term buffers only**.
- ✅ **TRL (Technology Readiness Level)** of 9 = fully commercial, deployed at scale; TRL 6–7 = pilot/demonstration stage.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Environmental Impacts of Different Technologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET521 - ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Environmental Impacts of Different Technologies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Environmental Impact of Energy Storage Technologies** refers to the cumulative set of ecological, chemical, and physical effects exerted on the natural environment across the entire **Life Cycle** (Cradle-to-Grave) of an energy storage system (ESS). This encompasses raw material extraction, materials processing, cell/system manufacturing, transportation, operational use-phase, and end-of-life (EOL) management (recycling, repurposing, or disposal).

In the KTU 2024 scheme, this topic is evaluated through the lens of **Life Cycle Assessment (LCA)**, an ISO 14040/14044 standardized methodology that quantifies environmental burdens using categories such as:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key LCA Impact Categories for ESS:**
> - **GWP** – Global Warming Potential (kg CO₂-equivalent / kWh)
> - **AP** – Acidification Potential (kg SO₂-equivalent)
> - **EP** – Eutrophication Potential (kg PO₄-equivalent)
> - **HTP** – Human Toxicity Potential
> - **ADP** – Abiotic Resource Depletion Potential
> - **WDP** – Water Depletion Potential (litres/kWh)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Think of an energy storage device as a **"savings bank account"** for electricity. Just as a real bank has overheads (rent, staff salaries, building materials, paper waste), every ESS has an **environmental overhead** — the energy and pollution consumed to "build" and "dispose" of the storage system itself.

- A **Lithium-ion battery** is like a high-yield but maintenance-heavy bank account — high returns (energy density) but with toxic "paperwork" (cobalt, lithium brine, electrolyte).
- A **Pumped Hydro Storage (PHS)** system is like an ancient, robust vault — built from earth and water, but the construction requires moving **millions of tonnes of rock and concrete**, which itself is a huge initial carbon debt.
- A **Hydrogen tank** is like storing cash in compressed paper notes — the notes themselves (green H₂) are clean, but the vault (tank, compressor) and the "money printing machine" (electrolysis) consume a lot of energy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** No energy storage technology is 100% "green". The environmental burden is always a function of **(Energy Density, Round-Trip Efficiency, Lifetime Cycles, and Recyclability)**.

## 1.3 Standard Environmental Metrics (Bold Constants)

| Metric | Standard Value / Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Avg. Li-ion GWP (NMC) | **120 – 150** | kg CO₂-eq / kWh |
| Avg. Lead-acid GWP | **70 – 100** | kg CO₂-eq / kWh |
| Pumped Hydro GWP | **1 – 10** | kg CO₂-eq / kWh |
| Li-ion Energy Payback Time (EPBT) | **1 – 3** | Years |
| PHS Energy Payback Time (EPBT) | **10 – 30** | Years |
| Critical Raw Materials (CRM) | Li, Co, Ni, Graphite, REE | — |
| Recycling Efficiency (Li-ion) | **50 – 95 %** | % |
| Lead-acid Recyclability | **~ 99 %** (closed loop) | % |

## 1.4 Environmental Impact Classification of ESS

Energy storage systems are broadly classified into **5 families**, each with a distinct ecological fingerprint:

1. **Electrochemical Storage** — Li-ion, Lead-acid, NiMH, NaS, Redox Flow Batteries.
2. **Capacitive Storage** — Supercapacitors (EDLCs), Hybrid Capacitors.
3. **Mechanical Storage** — Pumped Hydro Storage (PHS), Compressed Air (CAES), Flywheels.
4. **Thermal Storage** — Sensible (molten salts, water), Latent (PCM), Thermochemical.
5. **Chemical / Power-to-X** — Hydrogen (H₂), Synthetic Natural Gas (SNG), Ammonia (NH₃).

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Heuristic:** When a question asks "which is the greenest storage?", the answer is **always qualified by application scale, geography, and grid mix**. Do not give a one-word answer in KTU exams.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) Framework

The environmental impact of any ESS is evaluated using the **ISO 14040 Four-Phase LCA Framework**:

1. **Goal & Scope Definition** — Establish functional unit (e.g., 1 kWh delivered over 20 years).
2. **Life Cycle Inventory (LCI)** — Quantify all material/energy inputs and emissions at each stage.
3. **Life Cycle Impact Assessment (LCIA)** — Translate inventory into impact categories (GWP, AP, EP).
4. **Interpretation** — Identify hotspots (e.g., cell manufacturing vs. raw mining).

### Why Each Stage Matters for ESS

| LCA Stage | Typical % of GWP (Li-ion) | KTU High-Yield Insight |
|---|---|---|
| **Raw Material Extraction** | 20 – 30 % | Mining Li-brine (Chile), Spodumene (Australia); cobalt from DRC raises **ethical + carbon** issues. |
| **Material Processing** | 15 – 25 % | Refining Ni, Co, graphite purification is energy-intensive. |
| **Cell/Pack Manufacturing** | 35 – 45 % | Cleanroom + dry-room operations; NMP solvent recovery. |
| **Use Phase (Charging/Discharging)** | 5 – 15 % | Depends on **grid carbon intensity** (coal-heavy India ≈ 0.7 kg CO₂/kWh). |
| **End-of-Life** | 5 – 15 % | Pyrometallurgy vs. hydrometallurgy; transport distance to recycler. |

## 2.2 Core Environmental Formulas (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use the table below as the **only reference** needed for all numerical problems on this topic in KTU ESE.

| # | Formula | Symbol Legend | Boundary Conditions | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **EPBT** = $\dfrac{E_{\text{primary,total}}}{E_{\text{delivered,annual}}}$ | $E_{\text{primary}}$ = cumulative primary energy input (MJ), $E_{\text{delivered}}$ = annual energy output (MJ/yr) | EPBT < Lifetime | Years until the system "repays" its embodied energy debt. |
| 2 | **EROI / ERoEI** = $\dfrac{E_{\text{out,total}}}{E_{\text{in,total}}}$ | Ratio of energy returned to energy invested | EROI > 1 is viable | Energy Return on Investment. |
| 3 | **GWP** = $\sum_{i} m_i \cdot GWP_{100,i}$ | $m_i$ = mass of GHG *i*, $GWP_{100,i}$ = 100-yr GWP factor (CO₂=1, CH₄=28, N₂O=265) | IPCC AR5 values | Total CO₂-equivalent emissions. |
| 4 | **EDP** = $\sum_i \dfrac{m_i}{R_i}$ | $m_i$ = kg of resource *i*, $R_i$ = ultimate reserve of *i* | kg Sb-eq | Abiotic resource depletion. |
| 5 | **C-Rate Impact Factor** $C_f = \dfrac{I_{\text{discharge}}}{E_{\text{nominal}}}$ | Current (A) / Capacity (Ah) | Determines degradation | Higher C → faster aging → more frequent replacement. |
| 6 | **Recycling Rate** $\eta_R = \dfrac{m_{\text{recovered}}}{m_{\text{input}}} \times 100\%$ | Recovered mass / input mass | $0 \le \eta_R \le 100$ | Measures circularity. |
| 7 | **Round-Trip Efficiency** $\eta_{RT} = \dfrac{E_{\text{out}}}{E_{\text{in}}}$ | Energy out / Energy in | $\eta_{RT} \in (0, 1)$ | Determines "energy loss" pollution proxy. |
| 8 | **Specific CO₂ per kWh Delivered** $CO_{2,\text{spec}} = \dfrac{GWP_{\text{total}}}{E_{\text{delivered,total}}}$ | kg CO₂ / kWh | Compare across technologies | Standardized comparison metric. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pipe-Symbol Quirk:** When writing absolute values inside the markdown table above, I have used $\vert$ rendering because raw `|` breaks the column delimiter. In LaTeX answers, you may write $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ freely.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Battery Passport (EU 2023/1542 Regulation):** Mandates LCA disclosure for every EV battery sold in Europe — directly uses the formulas above.
- **Carbon-Accounting Software (SimaPro, GaBi, OpenLCA):** Industry-standard tools that implement the **CML-2001** or **ReCiPe** LCIA methods.
- **Green Bond Certification for BESS Projects:** Investors use EPBT and GWP to evaluate storage project sustainability.
- **E-Waste Management Rules (India, 2022):** Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) directly uses the **Recycling Rate** $\eta_R$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation #1 — Energy Payback Time (EPBT) for a 100 MWh Li-ion BESS

**Problem Statement:**
A grid-scale **100 MWh Li-ion BESS** (NMC chemistry) is used for peak shaving. Calculate the **Energy Payback Time (EPBT)** given:
- Embodied energy in cell manufacturing: **E_manuf = 1.5 × 10⁵ GJ** (typical for 100 MWh NMC).
- Self-discharge + auxiliary loss: **5 % per year** of nominal capacity.
- Annual energy delivered (after losses): **E_del,annual = 87,600 MWh/yr**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the formula.**

$$EPBT = \frac{E_{\text{primary,total}}}{E_{\text{delivered,annual}}}$$

**Step 2 — Convert embodied energy to compatible units.**

$$E_{\text{primary,total}} = 1.5 \times 10^{5} \, \text{GJ} = 1.5 \times 10^{5} \times 0.27778 \, \text{MWh}$$

$$E_{\text{primary,total}} = 41{,}667 \, \text{MWh}$$

The conversion factor is $1 \text{ GJ} = 0.27778 \text{ MWh}$ since $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \text{ MJ} \Rightarrow 1 \text{ GJ} = 277.78 \text{ kWh} = 0.27778 \text{ MWh}$.

**Step 3 — Apply the EPBT formula.**

$$EPBT = \frac{41{,}667 \, \text{MWh}}{87{,}600 \, \text{MWh/yr}}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the quotient.**

$$EPBT \approx 0.4756 \, \text{years}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to days for clarity.**

$$EPBT \approx 0.4756 \times 365 = 173.6 \, \text{days}$$

**Conclusion:** The BESS repays its embodied energy in roughly **6 months**, well within its 15–20 year operational life.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation #2 — Specific CO₂ per kWh (Comparing Li-ion vs. PHS)

**Problem Statement:**
A **Li-ion battery** with a GWP of **130 kg CO₂-eq / kWh** and **Round-Trip Efficiency** $\eta_{RT} = 0.90$ is compared against a **Pumped Hydro Storage (PHS)** system with GWP of **5 kg CO₂-eq / kWh** and $\eta_{RT} = 0.80$. Both systems deliver **10,000 kWh** over their lifetime. Calculate the **CO₂ emitted per kWh of useful energy delivered**, accounting for efficiency losses.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Define the specific GWP formula adjusted for efficiency.**

$$CO_{2,\text{spec}} = \frac{GWP_{\text{embodied}}}{E_{\text{delivered,total}} \times \eta_{RT}}$$

We need to include efficiency because some of the input energy is lost as heat; effectively, more *embodied CO₂* is amortized per useful kWh.

**Step 2 — Compute for the Li-ion system.**

$$E_{\text{delivered,useful}} = 10{,}000 \times 0.90 = 9{,}000 \, \text{kWh}$$

$$CO_{2,\text{spec,Li-ion}} = \frac{130}{9{,}000} = 0.01444 \, \text{kg CO}_2\text{-eq/kWh}$$

**Step 3 — Compute for the PHS system.**

$$E_{\text{delivered,useful}} = 10{,}000 \times 0.80 = 8{,}000 \, \text{kWh}$$

$$CO_{2,\text{spec,PHS}} = \frac{5}{8{,}000} = 0.000625 \, \text{kg CO}_2\text{-eq/kWh}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the ratio (Impact Multiplier).**

$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{0.01444}{0.000625} = 23.1$$

**Conclusion:** Even though Li-ion is "greener" at point of use, when **embodied emissions** are amortized, PHS is **~23× less carbon-intensive per useful kWh**. However, PHS requires specific **geography** (two reservoirs at different elevations) — a constraint that has **no parallel** in battery storage.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Full LCA Snapshot)

| Storage Technology | Embodied GWP (kg CO₂/kWh) | EPBT (years) | Cycle Life | Recyclability | Critical Material Risk | Overall Eco-Score (1–10) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pumped Hydro (PHS)** | 1 – 10 | 10 – 30 | 10,000+ | 95 % (steel/concrete) | Low | 9 |
| **CAES (Diabatic)** | 5 – 30 | 2 – 5 | 5,000+ | 80 % | Low | 7 |
| **Li-ion (NMC)** | 120 – 150 | 1 – 3 | 2,000 – 4,000 | 50 – 70 % | **High (Co, Li, Ni)** | 5 |
| **Li-ion (LFP)** | 60 – 90 | 0.5 – 2 | 4,000 – 8,000 | 60 – 80 % | Medium | 7 |
| **Lead-acid** | 70 – 100 | 1 – 2 | 500 – 1,200 | **99 %** | Medium (Pb toxic) | 6 |
| **Redox Flow (VRFB)** | 30 – 60 | 1 – 2 | 15,000+ | 80 % (vanadium) | Low (V is recoverable) | 8 |
| **Supercapacitors (EDLC)** | 50 – 100 | 0.3 – 1 | 100,000+ | 40 – 60 % | Medium (activated carbon) | 6 |
| **Hydrogen (Green H₂)** | 30 – 80 | 1 – 4 | N/A (cycle-free) | 30 – 50 % | Low (water input) | 7 |
| **Flywheel** | 20 – 50 | 1 – 3 | 100,000+ | 85 % (steel) | Low | 7 |
| **Molten Salt (TES)** | 5 – 20 | 0.5 – 2 | 5,000+ | 90 % | Low | 9 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** LFP (Lithium Iron Phosphate) chemistry is **the dominant choice for grid storage in India and China** because it eliminates cobalt (ethical + supply-chain concern) and offers **2× the cycle life of NMC**, dramatically amortizing the embodied CO₂.

---

## 3.4 End-of-Life Pathways (EOL Sankey Logic)

The end-of-life route chosen for an ESS drastically alters the cumulative GWP. Consider the four standard EOL tiers:

1. **Direct Reuse (Second-Life)** — EV battery → grid storage. Avoids **~70 %** of manufacturing GWP. *(Best option.)*
2. **Repurposing + Refurbishment** — Cell-level testing, reconfiguration.
3. **Material Recycling** — Hydrometallurgy (chemical leaching) vs. Pyrometallurgy (smelting). Hydrometallurgy consumes less energy.
4. **Landfill / Incineration** — Worst case; leachates of Li, Pb, Ni poison groundwater. Legally restricted in EU/India.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Recycling is **not free**. Pyrometallurgical recycling of Li-ion emits **~1.5 kg CO₂ per kg of battery** due to high-temperature smelting. Always include recycling energy in your EPBT calculation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: LCA of a Li-ion BESS

The following Mermaid block represents the **functional flow of environmental impacts** across the life cycle of a generic Li-ion battery energy storage system. It is not a physical schematic — it is a *process flow* mapping inputs, transformations, and outputs at each life-cycle stage.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage 1: Raw Material Mining] --> B[Stage 2: Material Refining]
    B --> C[Stage 3: Cell Manufacturing]
    C --> D[Stage 4: Pack Assembly and BMS]
    D --> E[Stage 5: Use Phase - Grid Services]
    E --> F[Stage 6: End of Life Decision Gate]
    F --> G[Path A: Second Life Reuse]
    F --> H[Path B: Hydrometallurgical Recycling]
    F --> I[Path C: Pyrometallurgical Recycling]
    F --> J[Path D: Landfill Disposal]

    A1[Outputs: Brine waste, CO2, Heavy metals] --> A
    B1[Outputs: SO2, Particulates, Process heat] --> B
    C1[Outputs: NMP solvent, Dry room energy] --> C
    D1[Outputs: Electronics waste, Aluminum scrap] --> D
    E1[Outputs: No direct emissions; only grid CO2 proxy] --> E
    G1[Outputs: Avoided 70 percent manufacturing GWP] --> G
    H1[Outputs: Li2CO3, NiSO4, CoSO4, lower CO2] --> H
    I1[Outputs: Slag, high CO2 per kg] --> I
    J1[Outputs: Leachate, Soil contamination] --> J

    A --> A1
    B --> B1
    C --> C1
    D --> D1
    E --> E1
    G --> G1
    H --> H1
    I --> I1
    J --> J1
```

## 4.2 Comparative Decision Flow — Choosing the "Greenest" Storage

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start: Application Requirements] --> Q1{Short duration high power?}
    Q1 -->|Yes, less than 1 hour| Sup[Supercapacitor or Flywheel]
    Q1 -->|No, 1 to 8 hours| Q2{Grid scale or behind the meter?}
    Q2 -->|Grid scale 100 MWh plus| PHSorFlow[Pumped Hydro or Vanadium Redox Flow]
    Q2 -->|Behind the meter residential| LIB[Li-ion LFP]
    Q2 -->|Industrial peak shaving| Q3{Region has salt caverns?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| CAES[Compressed Air Energy Storage]
    Q3 -->|No| LIB2[Li-ion LFP or NaS]
    Sup --> EPBT{EPBT less than 1 year?}
    PHSorFlow --> EPBT
    LIB --> EPBT
    CAES --> EPBT
    LIB2 --> EPBT
    EPBT -->|Yes| Green[Verified Green Choice]
    EPBT -->|No| Reassess[Reassess with grid carbon intensity]
```

## 4.3 Critical Raw Materials Map (Qualitative Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
```
