```mermaid
    Co[Cobalt] -->|DRC, Russia, Australia| Cell
    Ni[Nickel] -->|Indonesia, Philippines, Russia| Cell
    Gr[Graphite] -->|China, Mozambique, Brazil| Cell
    Cell[Li-ion Cell]
    V[Vanadium] -->|China, Russia, South Africa| Flow
    Flow[Vanadium Redox Flow Battery]
    Pb[Lead] -->|China, Australia, Peru| Lead
    Lead[Lead Acid Battery]
    REE[Rare Earth Elements Nd Pr] -->|China, Australia, USA| PM
    PM[Permanent Magnet Motor]
    PM --> Flywheel[Flywheel Energy Storage]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** A 14-mark question often asks *"Discuss the environmental impact of Li-ion batteries with reference to critical raw materials."* — This map gives you a ready-to-use diagram worth 2–3 marks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "Energy Payback Time (EPBT)" with reference to an energy storage system. Mention its significance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** EPBT is the time required by an energy storage system to generate an amount of usable energy equal to the cumulative primary energy consumed in its entire life cycle (raw material extraction → manufacturing → installation → recycling). Mathematically, $EPBT = \dfrac{E_{\text{primary,total}}}{E_{\text{delivered,annual}}}$.
- **Significance (1 mark):** It is a key sustainability metric — a lower EPBT (typically 0.5–3 years for Li-ion) indicates a faster recovery of the embodied energy debt, making the technology environmentally viable. The system should ideally have EPBT ≪ operational lifetime.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**List any four environmental impact categories used in the Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) of energy storage systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The four LCA impact categories (1/2 mark each) are:
1. **Global Warming Potential (GWP)** — measured in kg CO₂-equivalent.
2. **Acidification Potential (AP)** — kg SO₂-equivalent.
3. **Eutrophication Potential (EP)** — kg PO₄-equivalent.
4. **Human Toxicity Potential (HTP)** — kg DCB-equivalent *(or any other valid category such as Abiotic Depletion Potential, ADP)*.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module 2 Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 / Model Paper 2]**

**(a) Compare the environmental impact of Lithium-ion batteries and Pumped Hydro Storage (PHS) systems across the entire life cycle. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| LCA Stage | Li-ion (NMC) | PHS |
|---|---|---|
| **Raw Material** | High — Li-brine extraction, cobalt mining in DRC, graphite purification. **Ethical concerns** (child labour in Co mining). | Low — Steel, cement, water. |
| **Manufacturing** | Very high GWP (~120–150 kg CO₂/kWh). Cleanroom energy + NMP solvent. | Moderate GWP (~5 kg CO₂/kWh) due to concrete & steel. |
| **Use Phase** | ~5–15 % GWP, dominated by **grid carbon intensity** of charging electricity. | ~10–20 % GWP from pumping electricity. |
| **EOL** | Recycling 50–70 %; pyrometallurgy emits 1.5 kg CO₂/kg. | Steel/concrete repurposed; > 95 % recyclable. |
| **Land Use** | Compact — suitable for urban installations. | Enormous — needs 2 reservoirs; submerges large land. |
| **Water Use** | Low (process water in refining). | High — reservoir evaporation. |
| **Cumulative GWP (kg CO₂/kWh)** | 120 – 150 | 1 – 10 |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** PHS has a **20–30× lower carbon footprint** per kWh, but its applicability is geographically restricted. Li-ion dominates where **land/water** are scarce (urban India, offshore, islands).

---

**(b) Discuss the environmental concerns associated with the extraction of critical raw materials (Li, Co, Ni) for Li-ion battery manufacturing. Suggest two mitigation strategies. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

*Environmental Concerns (4 marks):*
1. **Lithium Extraction:** Brine evaporation in the **Salar de Atacama (Chile)** consumes 65 % of the region's water, affecting indigenous communities and flamingo habitats. Hard-rock spodumene mining (Australia) generates 1.5 tonnes of CO₂ per tonne of Li.
2. **Cobalt Mining:** The **Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC)** supplies 70 % of global cobalt, often via artisanal mines with severe **human toxicity** exposure and child-labour issues. Risk of **blood, lung, and skin disorders** in miners.
3. **Nickel Mining:** Indonesia's laterite nickel causes **deforestation, acid mine drainage, and heavy-metal contamination** of coastal waters.
4. **Graphite Purification:** China dominates, using **hydrofluoric acid (HF)** — high HTP, GWP, and AP impacts.

*Mitigation Strategies (3 marks):*
1. **Chemistry Substitution:** Shift to **LFP (Lithium Iron Phosphate)** batteries, which contain **zero cobalt and minimal nickel**. Already dominant in China and India.
2. **Direct Recycling & Urban Mining:** Develop hydrometallurgical processes that recover **>90 % Li, Co, Ni** from spent batteries, reducing new mining pressure.
3. **Closed-Loop Supply Chain:** Adopt the **EU Battery Passport (2023/1542)** framework mandating traceability, recycled-content thresholds, and ethical sourcing certification.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Paper 1]**

**(a) With a neat flow diagram, describe the Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) methodology for an energy storage system. Explain each stage briefly. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

*Flow Diagram (3 marks):*

```
[Goal & Scope] → [Life Cycle Inventory] → [Life Cycle Impact Assessment] → [Interpretation]
                          ↑                              ↓
                  (data collection)              (impact category calculation)
```

*Stage-wise Description (4 marks):*

1. **Goal & Scope Definition:** Define the functional unit (e.g., 1 kWh of stored electricity delivered over 20 years), system boundaries (cradle-to-grave or cradle-to-gate), and assumptions.
2. **Life Cycle Inventory (LCI):** Quantify all material/energy inputs and emissions at each stage — mining (kg of Li, Co, Ni), manufacturing (kWh of electricity, kg of NMP), use phase (cycles, depth-of-discharge), EOL (recycling yield).
3. **Life Cycle Impact Assessment (LCIA):** Translate the LCI into impact categories using characterization factors (e.g., IPCC AR5 for GWP, CML-2001 for AP). Compute cumulative values such as $\text{GWP} = \sum m_i \cdot GWP_{100,i}$.
4. **Interpretation:** Identify hotspots, perform sensitivity analysis, and recommend improvements (e.g., switching to LFP, increasing recycled content).

---

**(b) Hydrogen-based energy storage is often called "clean fuel". Critically evaluate this statement with reference to (i) production pathway, (ii) round-trip efficiency, and (iii) leakage effects. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Production Pathway (2.5 marks):** Hydrogen's environmental impact is **entirely pathway-dependent**.
- **Grey H₂** (steam methane reforming — SMR): 9–12 kg CO₂/kg H₂. **Not clean.**
- **Blue H₂** (SMR + Carbon Capture & Storage — CCS): 2–4 kg CO₂/kg H₂. **Conditionally clean.**
- **Green H₂** (electrolysis using renewable electricity): 0.5–3 kg CO₂/kg H₂. **Genuinely clean, but tied to renewable grid availability.**

**(ii) Round-Trip Efficiency (2.5 marks):** Hydrogen's RTE is only **35 – 45 %** (electrolysis 65–75 % × fuel cell 40–60 % × compression losses 10–15 %). Compare to Li-ion at **85–95 %**. The "energy lost" implies a much higher **upstream generation requirement**, increasing *indirect* environmental burden.

**(iii) Leakage Effects (2 marks):** Molecular hydrogen is an **indirect greenhouse gas (GHG)** with a **GWP₁₀₀ ≈ 11** (US EPA, 2022). Hydrogen leakage during compression, transport, and refueling can offset up to **9 %** of the climate benefit. Additionally, hydrogen leakage reacts with **tropospheric OH radicals**, potentially increasing methane lifetime and worsening ozone formation.

**Conclusion:** Hydrogen is "clean" **only if green H₂ is used, leakage is controlled (<1 %), and the application justifies the low RTE** (e.g., seasonal storage, aviation, steel-making — not short-term grid balancing, where batteries outperform).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in This Topic:**
> 1. **Conflating "Embodied" and "Operational" emissions** — Students often quote only operational GWP (use-phase), forgetting that **manufacturing GWP of Li-ion (120–150 kg CO₂/kWh) is the dominant contributor**. Always specify the LCA stage.
> 2. **Ignoring Indirect Effects** — Hydrogen leakage, refrigerant gases in thermal storage, and **NMP solvent emissions** from Li-ion coating lines are frequently overlooked.
> 3. **Quoting LCA values without a source timeframe** — GWP₅₀ ≠ GWP₁₀₀ ≠ GWP₂₀. Always mention "100-year GWP (IPCC AR5)".
> 4. **Confusing "Recyclable" with "Recycled"** — 95 % *recyclability* of PHS steel is theoretical; actual *recycled content* in new PHS is ~30–50 %. Differentiate both.
> 5. **Overgeneralizing "Green" Storage** — A KTU examiner will deduct marks if you say "Hydrogen is the greenest fuel" without qualifying the production pathway.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print This Before the Exam):**
> - [ ] EPBT = $E_{\text{primary,total}} / E_{\text{delivered,annual}}$; system is viable only if EPBT ≪ operational life.
> - [ ] LCA has **4 phases**: Goal & Scope → LCI → LCIA → Interpretation (ISO 14040/14044).
> - [ ] **PHS** has the lowest GWP (1–10 kg CO₂/kWh) but is **geography-limited**.
> - [ ] **Li-ion NMC** has the highest manufacturing GWP (120–150 kg CO₂/kWh) due to Co, Ni, and cleanroom energy.
> - [ ] **LFP** is the preferred Li-ion chemistry for India/China because it eliminates cobalt and offers 2× cycle life of NMC.
> - [ ] **Lead-acid** is the most recycled product on Earth (~99 %), but lead toxicity is severe.
> - [ ] **Vanadium Redox Flow Batteries** have a unique advantage — vanadium electrolyte is fully recoverable, no cross-contamination.
> - [ ] **Hydrogen** is "green" only via **green H₂** (renewable-powered electrolysis); blue is a bridge; grey is fossil-based.
> - [ ] **Supercapacitors** are clean in use but have high embodied GWP per kWh because of **activated carbon** production.
> - [ ] **Second-life reuse** of EV batteries can offset up to **70 %** of manufacturing GWP — the single biggest sustainability lever.
> - [ ] Key GWP�₁₀₀ factors to memorize: **CO₂ = 1, CH₄ = 28, N₂O = 265, H₂ = 11 (indirect)**.
> - [ ] **Critical Raw Materials (CRM)** for ESS: Li, Co, Ni, Graphite, REE (Nd, Pr, Dy) — supply-chain risk is a key environmental + geopolitical concern.
> - [ ] **Hydrometallurgy > Pyrometallurgy** for Li-ion recycling — lower CO₂ footprint, higher recovery rates.
> - [ ] Always mention **system boundaries** (cradle-to-grave, cradle-to-gate, well-to-wheel) when stating GWP values.
> - [ ] **Energy Density ≠ Environmental Friendliness** — high energy density does NOT mean low impact; LFP (lower energy density) is greener than NMC.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wave
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET521 - ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Wave

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Wave Energy Storage** refers to the capture, conversion, and storage of kinetic and potential energy contained in ocean surface gravity waves into usable electrical energy, often coupled with secondary storage media (batteries, supercapacitors, pumped hydro, or hydrogen) to overcome the inherent **intermittency** of wave regimes. The fundamental energy source is the **gravitational coupling** between wind shear stress over the ocean surface and the resulting orbital motion of water particles beneath propagating wave fronts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Emphasis):** Wave energy is the transport of energy by ocean surface waves, captured using **Wave Energy Converters (WECs)** and stored using hybrid energy storage systems to ensure dispatchable power delivery.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a stadium of people doing a **Mexican wave**. Each person stands and sits, but the *energy* moves horizontally around the stadium while individual people barely move from their seats. Ocean waves behave identically — **water particles trace small circular orbits**, but the *energy* propagates over thousands of kilometres. A WEC is essentially a device that "taps" this travelling energy wave, much like a surfer catches a Mexican wave and converts its motion into useful work.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Waves are a **concentrated form of wind energy**. Because wind blows more reliably over oceans than on land, waves act as a natural "buffer," storing wind energy and releasing it predictably at the coast.

### 1.3 Core Physical Parameters of an Ocean Wave

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Wave Height (crest to trough) | $H$ | m |
| Wave Period | $T$ | s |
| Wavelength | $L$ | m |
| Wave Celerity (phase velocity) | $c$ | m/s |
| Gravitational Acceleration | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Sea Water Density | $\rho$ | **1025 kg/m³** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU expects students to differentiate between **deep water waves** ($d > L/2$) and **shallow water waves** ($d < L/20$). Most offshore WECs operate in deep-water regime.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The chain of energy conversion in a wave energy storage system follows this hierarchy:

1. **Wind-Sea Coupling:** Pressure fluctuations in moving air transfer momentum to the ocean surface, generating gravity waves.
2. **Wave Propagation:** Energy travels via orbital particle motion; group velocity governs transport speed.
3. **Capture Stage (WEC):** Mechanical structures (OWCs, point absorbers, attenuators) intercept wave motion.
4. **PTO Conversion (Power Take-Off):** Linear or rotational generators convert mechanical motion to electricity.
5. **Power Conditioning:** AC-DC rectification and smoothing capacitor banks stabilise the wild electrical output.
6. **Storage Stage:** Surplus energy charges batteries (Li-ion, flow batteries) or performs **electrolytic hydrogen production** via electrolysers.
7. **Dispatch:** Stored energy feeds the grid on demand, decoupling wave availability from grid load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why storage is mandatory for WECs:** Wave energy density varies by a factor of **10–20×** between calm and storm conditions. Without storage, grid integration is impossible.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Expression | Applicable Condition | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Deep-water dispersion | $\omega^{2} = g k$ | Deep water ($d > L/2$) | rad²/s² |
| 2 | Wave celerity (deep) | $c = \dfrac{gT}{2\pi}$ | Deep water | m/s |
| 3 | Wavelength (deep) | $L = \dfrac{gT^{2}}{2\pi}$ | Deep water | m |
| 4 | Wave energy density | $E = \dfrac{\rho g H^{2}}{16}$ | Sinusoidal wave, per unit surface area | J/m² |
| 5 | Group velocity (deep) | $c_{g} = \dfrac{c}{2} = \dfrac{gT}{4\pi}$ | Deep water | m/s |
| 6 | Wave power per unit crest width | $P = \dfrac{\rho g^{2} H^{2} T}{64\pi}$ | Deep water, regular wave | W/m |
| 7 | Capture width ratio | $\text{CWR} = \dfrac{P_{\text{absorbed}}}{P_{\text{incident}} \cdot D}$ | WEC performance metric | dimensionless |
| 8 | Annual energy yield | $E_{a} = P_{\text{rated}} \cdot \text{CF} \cdot 8760$ | Plant energy estimation | kWh/yr |

### 2.3 Engineering Utility in Modern Industry

- **Remote Island Microgrids:** Wave + battery systems power isolated communities (e.g., **Mutriku OWC Plant**, Spain).
- **Offshore Oil Platforms:** Hybrid wind-wave-battery systems displace diesel gensets, cutting **CO₂ emissions by 30–60%**.
- **Green Hydrogen Production:** Wave-driven electrolysers produce H₂ without grid transmission losses — a flagship KTU 2024 research direction.
- **Desalination Coupling:** Wave-powered RO plants provide freshwater with zero fossil input.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Wave Power Per Unit Crest Width (Deep Water)

This is the **single most important derivation** for KTU Module 3. Examiners typically award 7 marks for the complete working.

**Starting Premises (Airy Linear Wave Theory):**

- Wave surface elevation: $\eta(x,t) = a \cos(kx - \omega t)$
- Amplitude $a = H/2$
- Deep water dispersion: $\omega^{2} = g k$

---

**Step 1 — Total Energy Density per Unit Horizontal Area**

The average energy density (sum of kinetic + potential) for a sinusoidal wave over one period is:

$$E = E_{k} + E_{p} = \frac{1}{2}\rho g a^{2}$$

Substituting $a = H/2 \Rightarrow a^{2} = H^{2}/4$:

$$E = \frac{1}{2}\rho g \cdot \frac{H^{2}}{4} = \frac{\rho g H^{2}}{8}$$

> **Logic Note:** Kinetic and potential energy densities are **equal on average** for a propagating gravity wave (equipartition theorem applied to wave motion).

---

**Step 2 — Group Velocity in Deep Water**

For a non-dispersive wave, energy travels at phase velocity. For dispersive deep-water gravity waves, energy propagates at **half** the phase velocity. Derivation:

$$c = \frac{\omega}{k}, \quad c_{g} = \frac{d\omega}{dk}$$

From dispersion $\omega = \sqrt{gk}$:

$$\frac{d\omega}{dk} = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{g}{k}} = \frac{\omega}{2k} = \frac{c}{2}$$

Therefore:

$$c_{g} = \frac{c}{2} = \frac{gT}{4\pi}$$

---

**Step 3 — Power Transmitted Per Unit Crest Width**

Power = Energy flux = Energy density × Group velocity:

$$P = E \cdot c_{g} = \frac{\rho g H^{2}}{8} \cdot \frac{gT}{4\pi}$$

$$\boxed{P = \frac{\rho g^{2} H^{2} T}{32\pi}}$$

> **Logic Note:** Some textbooks use the relation $P = \rho g^{2}H^{2}T/(64\pi)$ because they define $E = \rho g H^{2}/16$ using a different averaging convention (RMS vs. peak). **KTU 2024 accepts both forms; show your assumed definition clearly.**

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A deep-water WEC site has significant wave height $H_{s} = 2.5$ m and peak period $T_{p} = 8$ s. Sea water density $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³. Calculate (a) wavelength, (b) celerity, (c) energy density, (d) wave power per metre crest width.

**(a) Wavelength**

$$L = \frac{g T^{2}}{2\pi} = \frac{9.81 \times 8^{2}}{2\pi} = \frac{9.81 \times 64}{6.283}$$

$$L = \frac{627.84}{6.283} \approx 99.93 \text{ m} \approx 100 \text{ m}$$

**(b) Celerity**

$$c = \frac{L}{T} = \frac{99.93}{8} \approx 12.49 \text{ m/s}$$

(cross-check: $c = gT/2\pi = 9.81 \times 8 / 6.283 = 12.49$ m/s ✓)

**(c) Energy Density**

$$E = \frac{\rho g H^{2}}{8} = \frac{1025 \times 9.81 \times 2.5^{2}}{8}$$

$$E = \frac{1025 \times 9.81 \times 6.25}{8} = \frac{62846.25}{8} \approx 7855.78 \text{ J/m}^{2}$$

**(d) Wave Power Per Metre Crest**

$$P = \frac{\rho g^{2} H^{2} T}{32\pi} = \frac{1025 \times 9.81^{2} \times 6.25 \times 8}{32\pi}$$

$$P = \frac{1025 \times 96.24 \times 50}{100.53} = \frac{4932150}{100.53} \approx 49{,}057 \text{ W/m}$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 49.06 \text{ kW per metre of crest width}}$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** A 100 m wide WEC array at this site captures the theoretical equivalent of **~4.9 MW** continuous wave power before conversion losses.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Wave vs Other Renewables

| Attribute | Wave | Solar PV | Wind |
|---|---|---|---|
| Energy Density (W/m²) | **~2–3 kW/m²** | ~170 W/m² (peak) | ~0.5–1 kW/m² |
| Capacity Factor | **25–35%** | 15–25% | 25–45% |
| Predictability | High (hours ahead) | Moderate | Low (minutes ahead) |
| Storage Burden | High | Moderate | High |
| LCOE (USD/kWh) | **0.15–0.50** | 0.04–0.10 | 0.03–0.08 |
| Visual Impact | Low (offshore) | Low | Moderate |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Wave Energy Conversion & Storage — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Wind Shear Over Ocean] --> B[Gravity Wave Generation]
    B --> C[Wave Propagation Across Sea]
    C --> D{WEC Type}
    D --> D1[Oscillating Water Column]
    D --> D2[Point Absorber]
    D --> D3[Attenuator Pelamis]
    D --> D4[Overtopping Device]
    D1 --> E[Air Turbine Wells Impulse]
    D2 --> F[Linear PTO Generator]
    D3 --> F
    D4 --> G[Low Head Hydro Turbine]
    E --> H[AC/DC Rectifier Stage]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[DC Bus Stabilisation]
    I --> J{Storage Routing}
    J --> K1[Li Ion Battery Bank]
    J --> K2[Supercapacitor Bank]
    J --> K3[Pumped Hydro Module]
    J --> K4[Electrolyser H2 Tank]
    K1 --> L[DC AC Inverter]
    K2 --> L
    K3 --> L
    K4 --> M[Hydrogen Fuel Cell]
    M --> L
    L --> N[Microgrid / Utility Grid]
    N --> O[End User Load]
```

### 4.2 Oscillating Water Column (OWC) — Internal Functional Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OWC1[Incoming Wave Crest] --> OWC2[Water Column Rises]
    OWC2 --> OWC3[Air Pressurised in Chamber]
    OWC3 --> OWC4[Bidirectional Wells Turbine Spins]
    OWC4 --> OWC5[Generator Produces AC]
    OWC5 --> OWC6[Rectifier Bank]
    OWC6 --> OWC7[Battery Storage Bank]
    OWC7 --> OWC8[Inverter]
    OWC8 --> OWC9[Grid Output]
    OWC1b[Outgoing Wave Trough] --> OWC2b[Water Column Falls]
    OWC2b --> OWC3b[Air Suction In Chamber]
    OWC3b --> OWC4
```

### 4.3 Wave Energy Storage — Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Site Wave Resource Assessment] --> S2{Annual Power > 20 kW per m?}
    S2 -- Yes --> S3[Select WEC Technology]
    S2 -- No --> S4[Reject Site]
    S3 --> S5{PTO Output Variability}
    S5 --> S6[Design Storage Sizing]
    S6 --> S7[Battery Capacity kWh]
    S6 --> S8[Hydrogen Storage kg]
    S6 --> S9[Pumped Hydro Reservoir m3]
    S7 --> S10[Hybrid Energy Management Controller]
    S8 --> S10
    S9 --> S10
    S10 --> S11[Dispatchable Power to Grid]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Notes:** Mermaid syntax uses purely alphanumeric node identifiers prefixed with letters (e.g., `OWC1`, `nodeA`) and raw text labels without markdown formatting to ensure clean rendering.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "Wave Celerity" and state its relationship with wavelength and period for deep-water conditions.*

**Model Answer:**

Wave celerity is the speed at which a wave crest propagates across the ocean surface. For deep-water waves ($d > L/2$), it is given by:

$$c = \frac{gT}{2\pi} = \frac{L}{T}$$

It increases linearly with wave period, meaning longer-period (swell) waves travel faster than short-period (chop) waves — a phenomenon called **wave dispersion**.

*(3 marks: 1 definition + 1 formula + 1 conceptual note)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*List any three types of Wave Energy Converters (WECs) and give one example site for each.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Oscillating Water Column (OWC):** Example — Mutriku Plant, Bay of Biscay, Spain (296 kW).
2. **Point Absorber:** Example — WaveBob project, Ireland.
3. **Attenuator (Pelamis-type):** Example — Aguçadoura Wave Farm, Portugal (2.25 MW).
4. **Overtopping Device:** Example — WaveDragon, Denmark.

*(3 marks: 3 WEC types + 1 site each)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the wave power per unit crest width in deep water conditions, clearly stating the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A deep-water WEC site has $H = 3$ m, $T = 9$ s. Calculate (i) wavelength, (ii) wave celerity, (iii) energy density per unit area, and (iv) wave power per metre crest width. Take $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³ and $g = 9.81$ m/s². **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to A(a):**

Assumptions (Airy/Linear Wave Theory):

- Wave amplitude is small compared to wavelength ($a \ll L$).
- Fluid is incompressible and inviscid.
- Flow is irrotational.
- Deep water regime applies ($d > L/2$).

Step 1 — Energy density per unit surface area:

$$E = \frac{1}{2}\rho g a^{2} = \frac{\rho g H^{2}}{8}$$

Step 2 — Group velocity in deep water:

$$c_{g} = \frac{c}{2} = \frac{gT}{4\pi}$$

Step 3 — Power per unit crest width:

$$P = E \cdot c_{g} = \frac{\rho g H^{2}}{8} \cdot \frac{gT}{4\pi}$$

$$\boxed{P = \frac{\rho g^{2} H^{2} T}{32\pi} \quad \text{W/m}}$$

*(7 marks: 2 for assumptions, 3 for derivation, 2 for final expression)*

---

**Model Solution to A(b):**

(i) Wavelength:

$$L = \frac{g T^{2}}{2\pi} = \frac{9.81 \times 81}{6.283} = \frac{794.61}{6.283} \approx 126.48 \text{ m}$$

(ii) Celerity:

$$c = \frac{L}{T} = \frac{126.48}{9} \approx 14.05 \text{ m/s}$$

(iii) Energy density:

$$E = \frac{1025 \times 9.81 \times 9}{8} = \frac{90490.25}{8} \approx 11311.28 \text{ J/m}^{2}$$

(iv) Wave power per metre crest:

$$P = \frac{1025 \times 9.81^{2} \times 9 \times 9}{32\pi} = \frac{1025 \times 96.24 \times 81}{100.53}$$

$$P = \frac{7991{,}892}{100.53} \approx 79{,}496 \text{ W/m} \approx 79.5 \text{ kW/m}$$

*(7 marks: ~1.5 marks per sub-part, including unit and interpretation)*

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the working principle of an Oscillating Water Column (OWC) wave energy converter. Discuss its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain why energy storage is essential for wave energy systems. Discuss any three storage technologies suitable for hybrid wave-storage plants with their energy densities. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to B(a):**

Working Principle of OWC:

An OWC consists of a partially submerged hollow chamber open to the sea below. As waves approach:

1. **Wave crest enters:** Water rises inside the chamber, compressing the air column above.
2. **Air flows out:** Pressurised air passes through a **bidirectional (self-rectifying) Wells turbine** coupled to a generator, producing electricity.
3. **Wave trough exits:** Water level falls inside the chamber, creating partial vacuum.
4. **Air flows in:** Atmospheric air rushes back through the same Wells turbine, sustaining rotation in the same direction (this is the unique feature of Wells turbines).

Advantages:

- No moving parts in seawater (low corrosion).
- Wells turbine handles bidirectional flow without rectification valves.
- Suitable for shore-based and breakwater installations.

Limitations:

- Low overall efficiency (typically 20–40%).
- Resonance tuning is critical — chamber geometry must match dominant wave period.
- Noise and air-pressure pulsations affect marine life if not damped.

*(7 marks: 3 for working + 2 for diagram explanation + 2 for advantages/limitations)*

---

**Model Solution to B(b):**

Why Storage is Essential:

Wave energy is **intermittent on multiple timescales** — wave groups cause power oscillations every 20–30 s; storms and calms cause variability over days. The grid requires **dispatchable, smooth power**, so storage decouples generation from demand.

Three Storage Technologies:

| Technology | Energy Density | Suitability for Wave |
|---|---|---|
| **Li-ion Battery** | 150–250 Wh/kg | Excellent for short-term smoothing (seconds to hours) |
| **Supercapacitor** | 5–10 Wh/kg | Ideal for very fast power fluctuations (wave-by-wave) |
| **Pumped Hydro Storage** | 0.5–1.5 Wh/kg (system) | Best for seasonal/long-duration storage |
| **Hydrogen (via electrolyser)** | 33,000 Wh/kg (HHV) | Long-duration seasonal storage |

Typical hybrid architecture: **Supercapacitor (seconds) + Li-ion (hours) + H₂ (seasonal).**

*(7 marks: 2 for justification + 5 for technology explanation with data)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to write the dispersion relation** $\omega^{2} = gk$ before deriving celerity — losing 1 mark.
> 2. **Mixing up deep-water vs shallow-water formulas** — confirm $d > L/2$ before applying $c = gT/2\pi$.
> 3. **Not stating assumptions** of Airy linear wave theory in derivations — KTU deducts up to 2 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting units** in numerical answers — always write J/m², W/m, or kW/m explicitly.
> 5. **Confusing energy density (J/m²)** with **power density (W/m)** — these are not interchangeable.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Deep-water dispersion: $\omega^{2} = gk$; Celerity $c = gT/2\pi$; Wavelength $L = gT^{2}/2\pi$
- ✅ Energy density: $E = \rho g H^{2}/8$ (or $\rho g H^{2}/16$ depending on convention — **state which you use**)
- ✅ Group velocity in deep water: $c_{g} = c/2 = gT/4\pi$
- ✅ Wave power per metre crest: $P = \rho g^{2} H^{2} T / (32\pi)$
- ✅ Standard values: $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³ (sea water), $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- ✅ Four WEC categories: **OWC, Point Absorber, Attenuator, Overtopping**
- ✅ **Wells turbine** is bidirectional and self-rectifying — key for OWC
- ✅ Storage hierarchy for wave: **Supercapacitor (seconds) → Battery (hours) → Hydrogen (seasonal)**
- ✅ Wave energy density (~2–3 kW/m²) is **10–20× higher** than wind or solar per unit area
- ✅ Capacity factor for wave plants: **25–35%**, higher than solar PV
- ✅ Wave is a **concentrated, predictable form of wind energy** stored by the ocean
- ✅ Capture Width Ratio (CWR) is the **key WEC performance metric**
- ✅ Always identify regime: deep ($d > L/2$), intermediate, or shallow ($d < L/20$)

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wind
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET521 - ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Wind

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Wind Energy Storage** refers to the integrated system of converting the **kinetic energy of moving air masses** into usable electrical or mechanical energy, and subsequently storing that energy in a suitable medium (electrochemical, mechanical, thermal, or chemical) to overcome the inherent **intermittency and variability** of wind resources. In the context of KTU's Energy Storage Systems framework, the topic "Wind" encompasses:

- The physics of wind energy conversion (WECS – Wind Energy Conversion System)
- Classification of wind turbines and aerodynamic fundamentals
- Standalone, grid-connected, and hybrid wind energy storage architectures
- Storage modalities coupled to wind: **battery banks, pumped hydro, compressed air, hydrogen, and supercapacitor buffering**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 - Wind):**
> The focus of this module is **NOT** to study wind as a generation source alone, but specifically the **storage and dispatchability** of wind-derived energy. Examiners heavily test the *coupling interfaces* and *storage sizing* for wind systems.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a water tank on a rooftop. Water (energy) is pumped up when supply is plenty (windy conditions) and released when needed (calm periods). **Wind energy storage works on the exact same principle**: wind is the "tap" that fills various "tanks" — batteries, reservoirs, gas cylinders, or hydrogen tanks — so that energy remains available even when the wind stops blowing.

Think of the wind itself as a *messy, unreliable friend* who keeps sending you money in unpredictable amounts at random hours. To live comfortably, you need a **bank account** (storage) to deposit and withdraw at your convenience.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Air density (ρ) at standard sea-level conditions:** $\rho = \mathbf{1.225 \; kg/m^3}$
> - **Betz Limit (maximum extractable power fraction):** $C_{p,\max} = \mathbf{16/27 \approx 0.5926 \; (59.26\%)}$
> - **Standard swept-area power rating metric:** $\mathbf{W/m^2}$ (power flux density)
> - **Cut-in, rated, and cut-out wind speeds (typical):** $v_{cut\text{-}in} = \mathbf{3{-}4 \; m/s}$, $v_{rated} = \mathbf{12{-}14 \; m/s}$, $v_{cut\text{-}out} = \mathbf{25 \; m/s}$
> - **Capacity Factor (CF) of onshore wind farms:** $\mathbf{25{-}40\%}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework: From Breeze to Battery

The conversion-storage chain for wind energy follows a six-stage logical pipeline:

1. **Aerodynamic Capture:** Moving air with velocity $v$ carries kinetic energy. A turbine intercepts a swept area $A$ and extracts a fraction of the kinetic power.
2. **Mechanical Transmission:** The rotating shaft drives a gearbox coupling the low-speed rotor to a high-speed generator.
3. **Electrical Generation:** A generator (commonly DFIG or PMSG in modern WECS) produces variable-frequency, variable-voltage AC.
4. **Power Conditioning:** Power electronics (AC–DC–AC converters, MPPT controllers) regulate voltage/frequency to match grid or load specifications.
5. **Storage Interface:** A bidirectional inverter/battery management system (BMS) charges storage when generation > demand, and discharges when demand > generation.
6. **Dispatch / Load Delivery:** Stored energy is reconverted to AC and supplied to the consumer/grid on demand.

### 2.2 Why Storage is Essential for Wind

- **Intermittency:** Wind speed varies on timescales of seconds to seasons.
- **Non-dispatchability:** Without storage, wind cannot guarantee power on demand.
- **Grid stability:** Storage smooths voltage/frequency fluctuations (e.g., a sudden gust may cause over-frequency; lull causes under-frequency).
- **Capacity credit:** Storage increases the *firm capacity* a wind farm can reliably contribute to the grid.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{wind} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3}$ | Power available in the wind stream | W (watts) |
| 2 | $A = \pi R^{2}$ | Swept area of rotor (radius $R$) | $\text{m}^{2}$ |
| 3 | $C_{p} = \dfrac{P_{extracted}}{P_{wind}}$ | Power coefficient (efficiency of capture) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $P_{extracted} = C_{p} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3}$ | Actual mechanical power extracted | W |
| 5 | $C_{p,\max} = \dfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926$ | Betz limit (theoretical max) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\lambda = \dfrac{\omega R}{v}$ | Tip speed ratio (TSR) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $C_{p} = f(\lambda, \beta)$ | $C_{p}$ depends on TSR $\lambda$ and pitch angle $\beta$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | $E_{stored} = P_{gen} \cdot \Delta t \cdot \eta_{charge}$ | Energy stored in time interval $\Delta t$ | Wh or J |
| 9 | $E_{dispatched} = E_{stored} \cdot \eta_{discharge}$ | Energy delivered to load/grid | Wh or J |
| 10 | $\text{DoD} = \dfrac{E_{used}}{E_{rated}} \times 100\%$ | Depth of Discharge of storage | % |
| 11 | $CF = \dfrac{E_{actual}}{P_{rated} \cdot T}$ | Capacity Factor of wind farm | dimensionless |
| 12 | $SOH = \dfrac{E_{current\;capacity}}{E_{initial\;capacity}} \times 100\%$ | State of Health (battery) | % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Boundary Conditions for KTU Problems:**
> - Use $\rho = 1.225 \; \text{kg/m}^{3}$ unless stated otherwise.
> - Betz limit applies only to an *ideal, single, disc-shaped actuator* (no hub, no drag).
> - Real machines achieve $C_{p} \in [0.35, 0.45]$.
> - Storage round-trip efficiency $\eta_{RT} = \eta_{charge} \times \eta_{discharge}$ typically lies in $0.65 - 0.90$ depending on technology.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Onshore/Offshore wind farms** (e.g., India's Muppandal wind farm, 1,500 MW) require storage to firm up capacity.
- **Remote microgrids** (Ladakh, Andaman Islands) use wind–battery–diesel hybrids to displace expensive diesel transport.
- **Green hydrogen production** couples wind turbines to electrolyzers, storing energy as $\text{H}_{2}$ for long-duration use.
- **Pumped hydro storage with wind** (e.g., Switzerland's Nant-de-Drance) provides GW-scale bulk storage.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Power Available in the Wind

**Statement:** A wind stream of velocity $v$ passing through a cross-sectional area $A$ with air density $\rho$ carries kinetic power $P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3}$.

**Derivation (Mass-flow approach):**

Consider a control volume of air moving with uniform velocity $v$ through area $A$.

**Step 1:** The mass of air passing through area $A$ per unit time (mass flow rate) is:

$$
\dot{m} = \rho A v
$$

*Logic:* In time $dt$, a column of air of length $v \cdot dt$ crosses the area. Its mass is $\rho A (v \cdot dt)$, hence rate $= \rho A v$.

**Step 2:** The kinetic energy per unit mass of this moving air stream is:

$$
\text{KE per unit mass} = \frac{1}{2} v^{2}
$$

**Step 3:** Therefore, the kinetic power (energy per unit time) carried by the wind is:

$$
P_{wind} = \dot{m} \times \frac{1}{2} v^{2}
$$

**Step 4:** Substituting $\dot{m} = \rho A v$:

$$
P_{wind} = \rho A v \cdot \frac{1}{2} v^{2}
$$

**Step 5:** Simplifying:

$$
P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3}
$$

Hence proved. The cubic dependence on $v$ is the **single most important reason** wind energy density is highly sensitive to wind-speed variations.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Betz Limit ($C_{p,\max} = 16/27$)

**Statement:** The maximum fraction of kinetic power in a wind stream that an *ideal* turbine can extract is $\frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926$.

**Setup (Actuator Disc Model):** Let the upstream velocity be $v_1$, the velocity at the disc be $v_d$, and the downstream (wake) velocity be $v_2$. By conservation, $v_1 > v_d > v_2$.

**Step 1:** The mass flow rate through the disc is:

$$
\dot{m} = \rho A v_d
$$

**Step 2:** The thrust force on the disc equals the rate of change of momentum:

$$
F = \dot{m} (v_1 - v_2) = \rho A v_d (v_1 - v_2)
$$

**Step 3:** The pressure drop across the disc gives a force:

$$
F = (p_1 - p_2) A
$$

**Step 4:** Apply Bernoulli's equation on the upstream and downstream streamlines (far from the disc):

$$
p_1 + \frac{1}{2} \rho v_1^{2} = p_2 + \frac{1}{2} \rho v_2^{2}
$$

Therefore:

$$
p_1 - p_2 = \frac{1}{2} \rho (v_2^{2} - v_1^{2})
$$

**Step 5:** From the actuator disc continuity and the induction factor $a$ defined by $v_d = v_1(1 - a)$:

$$
v_2 = v_1 (1 - 2a)
$$

**Step 6:** Equating the two expressions for force $F$:

$$
\frac{1}{2} \rho (v_1^{2} - v_2^{2}) = \rho v_d (v_1 - v_2)
$$

Substitute $v_d = v_1(1-a)$ and $v_2 = v_1(1-2a)$:

$$
\frac{1}{2} \rho v_1^{2} [1 - (1-2a)^{2}] = \rho v_1 (1-a) v_1 [1 - (1-2a)]
$$

**Step 7:** Simplifying the bracket terms using $1 - (1-2a)^{2} = 4a(1-a)$:

$$
\frac{1}{2} \rho v_1^{2} \cdot 4a(1-a) = \rho v_1^{2} (1-a) \cdot 2a
$$

Both sides reduce to the same expression — confirming consistency.

**Step 8:** The power extracted is the thrust times the disc velocity:

$$
P_{extracted} = F \cdot v_d = \rho A v_d^{2} (v_1 - v_2)
$$

**Step 9:** Substitute $v_d = v_1(1-a)$ and $v_1 - v_2 = 2 a v_1$:

$$
P_{extracted} = \rho A v_1^{2} (1-a)^{2} \cdot 2 a v_1
$$

$$
P_{extracted} = 2 \rho A v_1^{3} a (1-a)^{2}
$$

**Step 10:** The power coefficient is:

$$
C_p = \frac{P_{extracted}}{P_{wind}} = \frac{2 \rho A v_1^{3} a (1-a)^{2}}{\frac{1}{2} \rho A v_1^{3}}
$$

$$
C_p = 4 a (1-a)^{2}
$$

**Step 11:** To maximize, differentiate with respect to $a$ and set to zero:

$$
\frac{d C_p}{d a} = 4 [(1-a)^{2} + a \cdot 2(1-a)(-1)] = 4 (1-a)[(1-a) - 2a] = 0
$$

Non-trivial solution: $(1 - 3a) = 0 \implies a = \frac{1}{3}$.

**Step 12:** Substitute $a = 1/3$:

$$
C_{p,\max} = 4 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{3}\right)^{2} = \frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{9} = \frac{16}{27}
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{p,\max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926}
$$

**Real-world implication:** A 2 MW turbine *theoretically* cannot extract more than $0.5926 \times 2 = 1.185$ MW from any wind speed — and real machines achieve $C_p \approx 0.4$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Energy Balance of a Wind–Storage System

**Statement:** For a wind-storage system with daily generation $E_g$ and load demand $E_L$, the *required* storage capacity is:

$$
C_{storage} = \frac{E_L - E_g \cdot \eta_{charge}}{\text{DoD} \cdot \eta_{discharge}}
$$

when $E_g \eta_{charge} < E_L$.

**Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Energy available after charging:

$$
E_{stored} = E_g \cdot \eta_{charge}
$$

**Step 2:** Energy deliverable from storage accounting for depth of discharge:

$$
E_{deliverable} = E_{stored} \cdot \eta_{discharge} \cdot \text{DoD}
$$

**Step 3:** Energy balance for deficit period:

$$
E_L = E_g \cdot \eta_{charge} + C_{storage} \cdot \eta_{discharge} \cdot \text{DoD}
$$

**Step 4:** Solving for $C_{storage}$:

$$
C_{storage} = \frac{E_L - E_g \cdot \eta_{charge}}{\eta_{discharge} \cdot \text{DoD}}
$$

This expression is the basis of **battery bank sizing for standalone wind microgrids**, a frequent KTU Part B problem.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Wind–Storage Architecture Comparison

| Storage Type | Response Time | Round-Trip $\eta_{RT}$ | Energy Density | Suitable Duration | KTU Exam Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Li-ion Battery | ms | 85–95% | High | min – 4 h | Frequency regulation, smoothing |
| Lead-acid Battery | ms | 70–80% | Moderate | min – 4 h | Low-cost rural microgrids |
| Supercapacitor | $\mu$s | 90–95% | Low | < 30 s | Short-term power buffering |
| Pumped Hydro | min | 75–85% | Very Low (vol.) | 4 – 24+ h | Bulk grid storage |
| CAES | min | 45–70% | Moderate | 8 – 24+ h | Bulk long-duration |
| Hydrogen ($\text{H}_{2}$) | s – min | 30–45% | Very High (mass) | Days – Months | Seasonal storage |
| Flywheel | ms | 80–90% | Low | s – min | Inertia, ride-through |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Wind-to-Storage Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Wind Resource] --> B[Aerodynamic Rotor Blades]
    B --> C[Gearbox and Shaft]
    C --> D[Generator PMSG or DFIG]
    D --> E[AC to DC Rectifier]
    E --> F{MPPT and Power Conditioning}
    F --> G[Battery Energy Storage System BESS]
    F --> H[Supercapacitor Bank]
    F --> I[Electrolyzer for H2 Production]
    F --> J[Pumped Hydro or CAES]
    G --> K[DC to AC Inverter]
    H --> K
    I --> L[H2 Storage Tank]
    L --> M[Fuel Cell or Turbine]
    M --> K
    J --> N[Grid Pump or Compressor]
    N --> O[Reservoir or Cavern]
    O --> P[Turbine Generator]
    P --> K
    K --> Q[Grid or Local Load]
    G --> R[BMS Battery Management System]
    R --> G
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The central node "MPPT and Power Conditioning" is the *brain* of the system. MPPT (Maximum Power Point Tracking) continuously adjusts the load on the generator to extract peak power at every wind speed. The branching into multiple storage media represents **hybrid storage** — a popular KTU essay topic.

### 4.2 Wind Turbine Classification Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    WT[Wind Turbines] --> HAWT[Horizontal Axis HAWT]
    WT --> VAWT[Vertical Axis VAWT]
    HAWT --> Upwind[Upwind with Yaw]
    HAWT --> Downwind[Downwind Passive Yaw]
    HAWT --> M1[3 Blade Most Common]
    HAWT --> M2[2 Blade Tethered]
    VAWT --> Darrieus[Darrieus Egg Beater]
    VAWT --> Savonius[Savonius Drag Type]
    VAWT --> Hrotor[H Rotor]
    M1 --> Con1[Pitch Controlled Variable Speed]
    M1 --> Con2[Stall Controlled Fixed Speed]
```

### 4.3 Power vs. Wind Speed Curve (Conceptual Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V0[Below Cut in] --> V1[No Power]
    V1 --> V2[Cut in to Rated]
    V2 --> V3[Cubic Increase P=0.5 rho A v cubed]
    V3 --> V4[Rated Region Flat Power]
    V4 --> V5[Cut out Shutdown]
```

**Interpretation of the Block Architecture:**

- **Region V0 → V1:** Wind speed below $v_{cut\text{-}in} \approx 3$ m/s — turbine is idle, generator disconnected, storage in standby.
- **Region V1 → V2 → V3:** Power rises **cubically** with $v$. This is the MPPT zone.
- **Region V3 → V4:** Pitch control limits power to rated $P_{rated}$ to prevent mechanical/electrical overload.
- **Region V4 → V5:** Above $v_{cut\text{-}out}$, turbine feathers blades and shuts down to prevent damage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Tip:** The cubic relationship $P \propto v^{3}$ means that **doubling the wind speed increases power eightfold**. This is why siting (and storage sizing) depends so critically on accurate wind resource assessment (Weibull distribution, $k$-shape factor, $c$-scale parameter).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "Capacity Factor" of a wind energy system. State its typical range for onshore and offshore wind farms.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Capacity Factor (CF)** of a wind energy conversion system is the ratio of the **actual energy generated over a period** to the **maximum possible energy that could have been generated** if the turbine operated at its rated power continuously over the same period.

$$
CF = \frac{E_{actual}}{P_{rated} \times T}
$$

- **Onshore wind farms:** $CF \in [0.25, \; 0.40]$ i.e., **25% – 40%**
- **Offshore wind farms:** $CF \in [0.40, \; 0.60]$ i.e., **40% – 60%**

CF is **not** the same as efficiency — it captures the combined effect of wind variability, turbine availability, and aerodynamic losses.

*(Mark distribution: 1.5 marks for definition + formula, 1.5 marks for typical range and explanation.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*List any three energy storage technologies suitable for wind energy applications and state one key advantage of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Storage Technology | Key Advantage |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Lithium-ion Battery Bank** | High round-trip efficiency ($\approx 90\%$) and fast response (ms), ideal for frequency smoothing. |
| 2 | **Pumped Hydro Storage (PHS)** | Mature, GW-scale, long-duration (4–24 h) bulk storage capability. |
| 3 | **Hydrogen ($\text{H}_{2}$) Storage** | Very high mass-energy density; enables **seasonal** storage (days to months) — unmatched by batteries. |

*(Mark distribution: 1 mark for each correct pair; full credit only if a relevant advantage is given.)*

---

### PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Wind Resource and Power Calculation**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** A wind turbine has a rotor diameter of **80 m**, operating at a location where the average wind speed is **12 m/s**. The air density is **1.225 kg/m³** and the turbine operates at a power coefficient of **0.40**. Calculate:
- (i) The power available in the wind. (3 marks)
- (ii) The actual power extracted by the turbine. (2 marks)
- (iii) Comment on how the extracted power would change if the wind speed drops to **9 m/s**. (2 marks)

**(b)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the various **energy storage options** that can be coupled to a wind farm, and explain why **hybrid energy storage systems (HESS)** are preferred over single-technology storage in modern wind plants. (7 marks)

---

#### **MODEL SOLUTION (Question A)**

**(a)(i) Power available in the wind (3 marks):**

**Step 1:** Compute the swept area $A$:

$$
A = \pi R^{2} = \pi \left(\frac{80}{2}\right)^{2} = \pi \times 40^{2} = 1600 \pi \; \text{m}^{2} \approx 5026.55 \; \text{m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the wind power equation:

$$
P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 5026.55 \times (12)^{3}
$$

$$
P_{wind} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 5026.55 \times 1728
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{wind} \approx 5.318 \; \text{MW}}
$$

**(a)(ii) Actual power extracted (2 marks):**

$$
P_{extracted} = C_p \times P_{wind} = 0.40 \times 5.318 \; \text{MW}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{extracted} \approx 2.127 \; \text{MW}}
$$

**(a)(iii) Effect of wind speed drop to 9 m/s (2 marks):**

The ratio of new power to old power, since $P \propto v^{3}$:

$$
\frac{P_{new}}{P_{old}} = \left(\frac{9}{12}\right)^{3} = \left(\frac{3}{4}\right)^{3} = \frac{27}{64} \approx 0.422
$$

$$
P_{new,\,wind} = 5.318 \times 0.422 \approx 2.244 \; \text{MW}
$$

$$
P_{new,\,extracted} = 0.40 \times 2.244 \approx 0.898 \; \text{MW}
$$

**Comment:** A 25% reduction in wind speed (from 12 to 9 m/s) causes a **~57.8% drop** in extracted power. This vividly demonstrates the **intermittency problem** — the very reason storage is mandatory.

---

**(b) Storage options and HESS justification (7 marks):**

**Block diagram (text representation):**

$$
\text{Wind Farm} \rightarrow \text{AC Bus} \rightarrow
\begin{cases}
\text{Battery Bank (Li-ion)} \rightarrow \text{BMS} \rightarrow \text{Inverter} \\
\text{Supercapacitor Bank} \rightarrow \text{DC-DC Converter} \rightarrow \text{Inverter} \\
\text{Electrolyzer} \rightarrow \text{H}_2 \text{Tank} \rightarrow \text{Fuel Cell} \rightarrow \text{Inverter} \\
\text{Pumped Hydro or CAES} \rightarrow \text{Turbine-Generator} \rightarrow \text{Inverter}
\end{cases}
\rightarrow \text{Grid / Load}
$$

**Six key storage options and their roles:**

1. **Lithium-ion Batteries:** Energy-intensive (Wh/kg), fast response, suited for **minutes-to-hours** smoothing.
2. **Supercapacitors:** Power-intensive (W/kg), ultra-fast response, suited for **seconds-level** power buffering (gust smoothing).
3. **Pumped Hydro Storage (PHS):** Gravitational potential, large-scale, suited for **4–24 h** bulk shifting.
4. **Compressed Air Energy Storage (CAES):** Pressurized underground caverns/tanks, 8–24 h bulk.
5. **Hydrogen Storage:** Electrolysis → compression → fuel cell; suited for **days-to-seasonal** long-duration storage.
6. **Flywheels:** Rotational kinetic energy, suited for inertia emulation and ride-through.

**Why HESS is preferred (4 sub-points):**

- No single storage technology simultaneously satisfies *high energy density*, *high power density*, *long cycle life*, *low cost*, and *fast response*.
- A **HESS combines a high-power-density element (supercapacitor) for transient smoothing** with a **high-energy-density element (battery)** for longer-duration support.
- This **decoupling of power and energy ratings** reduces battery stress, extends cycle life, and lowers levelized cost of storage (LCOS).
- HESS enables **multi-timescale management**: supercapacitor handles ms–s fluctuations; battery handles min–h fluctuations; H₂ or PHS handles day–week fluctuations.

*(Mark distribution for part b: 3 marks for the block diagram and listing 4+ storage types; 4 marks for HESS advantages with valid justification.)*

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice on Betz Limit & Standalone Wind-Battery Sizing**

**(a)** Derive the **Betz limit** expression for an ideal wind turbine, clearly stating the assumptions. Show that the maximum power extractable is **16/27** of the power in the wind. (7 marks)

**(b)** A **standalone 10 kW wind turbine** operates at a site with daily average wind speed of **10 m/s**. The turbine has a rotor diameter of **6 m**, $C_p = 0.35$, and $\rho = 1.225 \; \text{kg/m}^3$. The daily electrical load demand is **80 kWh**. The battery bank has:
- Charging efficiency $\eta_{charge} = 0.90$
- Discharging efficiency $\eta_{discharge} = 0.95$
- Allowable Depth of Discharge (DoD) = **70%**

Determine the **required battery bank capacity (in kWh)** assuming **8 hours of effective generation per day**. Comment on whether this is a feasible standalone system. (7 marks)

---

#### **MODEL SOLUTION (Question B)**

**(a) Derivation of Betz Limit (7 marks):**

**Assumptions (1.5 marks):**
1. Steady, incompressible, non-turbulent wind flow.
2. Uniform velocity across the rotor disc; no hub, no blade drag.
3. Far upstream and downstream, pressure equals atmospheric (Bernoulli applicable).
4. An infinite number of blades (no rotational wake).
5. Single actuator disc — Betz model.

**Derivation steps (5.5 marks):**

*Step 1:* Define induction factor $a$ such that disc velocity $v_d = v_1(1-a)$ and wake velocity $v_2 = v_1(1-2a)$.

*Step 2:* Mass flow rate through disc: $\dot{m} = \rho A v_d = \rho A v_1(1-a)$.

*Step 3:* Thrust force: $F = \dot{m}(v_1 - v_2) = \rho A v_1(1-a)(2a v_1) = 2 \rho A v_1^{2} a(1-a)$.

*Step 4:* Power extracted = thrust × disc velocity:

$$
P_{ext} = F \cdot v_d = 2 \rho A v_1^{2} a(1-a) \cdot v_1(1-a) = 2 \rho A v_1^{3} a(1-a)^{2}
$$

*Step 5:* Power coefficient:

$$
C_p = \frac{P_{ext}}{\frac{1}{2} \rho A v_1^{3}} = 4 a (1-a)^{2}
$$

*Step 6:* Differentiate: $\dfrac{dC_p}{da} = 4(1-a)(1 - 3a) = 0 \Rightarrow a = \dfrac{1}{3}$.

*Step 7:* Substitute:

$$
\boxed{C_{p,\max} = 4 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^{2} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926}
$$

---

**(b) Battery Sizing Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Swept area**

$$
A = \pi R^{2} = \pi (3)^{2} = 9\pi \approx 28.27 \; \text{m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2: Power available in the wind**

$$
P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 28.27 \times (10)^{3} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 28.27 \times 1000
$$

$$
P_{wind} \approx 17{,}316 \; \text{W} \approx 17.32 \; \text{kW}
$$

**Step 3: Actual power extracted**

$$
P_{extracted} = C_p \times P_{wind} = 0.35 \times 17.32 = 6.062 \; \text{kW}
$$

**Step 4: Daily energy generated (8 hours effective)**

$$
E_g = P_{extracted} \times 8 = 6.062 \times 8 = 48.50 \; \text{kWh}
$$

**Step 5: Compare with load demand**

$$
E_L = 80 \; \text{kWh} > E_g = 48.50 \; \text{kWh}
$$

**Deficit exists**; storage must compensate.

**Step 6: Apply the energy balance equation for required battery capacity**

$$
C_{storage} = \frac{E_L - E_g \cdot \eta_{charge}}{\eta_{discharge} \cdot \text{DoD}}
$$

$$
C_{storage} = \frac{80 - 48.50 \times 0.90}{0.95 \times 0.70} = \frac{80 - 43.65}{0.665} = \frac{36.35}{0.665}
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{storage} \approx 54.66 \; \text{kWh}}
$$

**Step 7: Feasibility comment (1.5 marks):**

- The required battery bank (≈ 55 kWh) is **larger than the daily deficit** (≈ 36.35 kWh) due to round-trip losses and DoD limitation.
- With an **8-hour effective generation** at $v = 10 \; \text{m/s}$, the system **cannot fully meet** the 80 kWh daily demand on its own.
- A **hybrid system** (e.g., add solar PV, diesel genset, or a larger wind turbine / higher CF site) is recommended.
- Increasing the rotor diameter or installing a taller tower (to capture higher-altitude winds) would improve the capacity factor.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically LOSE marks in this module:**
> 1. **Forgetting $\rho = 1.225 \; \text{kg/m}^3$** when not explicitly given. Always state it.
> 2. **Confusing $C_p$ with efficiency** — $C_p$ is the *fraction* of wind power extracted, not the overall system efficiency.
> 3. **Not multiplying by 8 hours** in the daily energy calculation for wind — many students use rated kW × 24 h, grossly overestimating generation.
> 4. **Ignoring DoD in battery sizing** — the *usable* capacity is $\text{DoD} \times \text{rated capacity}$. Sizing based on full capacity leads to chronic under-design.
> 5. **Dropping units** in the final answer (e.g., writing "55" instead of "55 kWh"). Examiners in KTU 2024 strictly penalize unit omission.
> 6. **Forgetting to discuss intermittency** — the keyword "intermittency" is a guaranteed *keyword-match* in KTU valuation scripts. Always mention it.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Power in the wind** scales with **$v^{3}$** — a critical concept for siting and storage sizing.
- **Betz limit** = **16/27 ≈ 59.26%** — a hard physical ceiling for an *ideal* turbine.
- **Real-world $C_p$** lies in **[0.35, 0.45]** for commercial turbines.
- **Tip speed ratio $\lambda = \omega R / v$** controls $C_p$; every turbine has an optimal $\lambda^*$.
- **Capacity factor** is NOT efficiency; it captures variability + availability.
- **Cut-in (3–4 m/s), rated (12–14 m/s), cut-out (25 m/s)** wind speeds bracket the operating envelope.
- **Storage coupling** is *the* essence of this module — the topic is incomplete without it.
- **Hybrid Energy Storage Systems (HESS)** combine power-dense (supercapacitor) and energy-dense (battery/H₂) media.
- **Battery sizing formula:**

$$
C_{bat} = \frac{E_L - E_g \cdot \eta_{charge}}{\eta_{discharge} \cdot \text{DoD}}
$$

- **Round-trip efficiency** = $\eta_{charge} \times \eta_{discharge}$ — always quote both.
- **Pumped hydro** for bulk (GW), **Li-ion** for fast/short, **H₂** for long-duration/seasonal.
- **PMSG and DFIG** are the dominant generator types in modern variable-speed WECS.
- **Power electronics (AC–DC–AC)** is the *enabling technology* for variable-speed wind + storage integration.
- **Keyword checklist for KTU answers:** *intermittency, dispatchability, Betz limit, $C_p$, $\lambda$, DoD, round-trip efficiency, MPPT, HESS, MPPT, PMSG, BESS.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 30-Second KTU Mnemonic:** *"Wind Power is Cubic (v³), Betz Caps it at 16/27, Storage Firms it, Hybrid Storage Saves it."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tidal
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET521 - ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Tidal

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tidal Energy** is a form of **renewable ocean energy** that harnesses the kinetic and potential energy of ocean tides — the periodic rise and fall of sea levels caused primarily by the **gravitational interaction** between the Earth, Moon, and Sun, and the **centrifugal force** generated by the Earth–Moon system's rotation around its common center of mass.

In the context of **Energy Storage Systems (PEEET521)**, tidal energy is classified as a **predictable, dispatchable renewable resource** because tides are driven by deterministic celestial mechanics, allowing accurate forecasting hours, days, and even years in advance. This makes tidal energy uniquely suited for **coupling with energy storage systems** such as **pumped hydro, compressed air (CAES), battery banks, and green hydrogen storage** to firm up grid supply.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology — Tidal Range vs. Tidal Stream**
> - **Tidal Range Energy** (potential energy): exploits the vertical head difference between high and low tides. Implemented via **tidal barrages** and **tidal lagoons**.
> - **Tidal Stream Energy** (kinetic energy): exploits the horizontal flow of water during flood and ebb tides. Implemented via **underwater tidal turbines** (similar to submerged wind turbines).

## 1.2 The Three Tidal Patterns (Astronomical Background)

> [!NOTE]
> - **Semidiurnal Tides** — Two high tides and two low tides of approximately equal magnitude every lunar day (period ≈ **12 hours 25 minutes**). Most common worldwide, including the Kerala coast.
> - **Diurnal Tides** — One high and one low tide per lunar day (period ≈ 24 hours 50 minutes). Found in parts of the Gulf of Mexico and Southeast Asia.
> - **Mixed Semidiurnal Tides** — Two high and two low tides per day, but with **unequal magnitudes** (typical of the Pacific coast of North America).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Breathing Ocean"

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Picture:** Imagine the ocean as a giant pair of lungs. As the **Moon orbits the Earth**, it pulls a bulge of water toward itself (and a corresponding bulge on the opposite side due to inertia). Every ≈6 hours and 12 minutes, this bulge passes a coastline, causing the sea to "**inhale**" (flood tide) and "**exhale**" (ebb tide). If we place a turbine at the mouth of a bay, we can capture the air-flow of this breathing cycle — converting it into electricity. A tidal barrage is essentially a "**dam with lungs**" that lets water in, holds it, and releases it through a turbine at controlled moments.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Density of seawater:** $\rho_{sw} = \mathbf{1025 \ kg/m^3}$ (≈ 2.5% denser than fresh water due to dissolved salts).
- **Gravitational acceleration:** $g = \mathbf{9.81 \ m/s^2}$.
- **Earth–Moon distance:** ≈ **384,400 km** (mean).
- **Synodic tidal period (spring–neap cycle):** ≈ **14.77 days** (this determines the "**spring**" and "**neap**" tides).
- **Semidiurnal tidal period:** $T = \mathbf{12.42 \ hours}$ (a quarter of a lunar day).
- **Betz Limit (turbine efficiency ceiling):** $C_{P,max} = \mathbf{16/27 \approx 0.593}$.
- **Global theoretical tidal energy resource:** ≈ **3,000 GW** (continuous equivalent), with the **World Energy Council** estimating a technically harvestable share of ≈ **800 TWh/year**.

## 1.5 Classification of Tidal Energy Conversion Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Principal TEC (Tidal Energy Converter) Architectures:**
>
> 1. **Tidal Barrage** — A dam-like structure built across a bay or estuary with sluice gates and low-head bulb/tubular turbines. Mature technology (e.g., La Rance, France — 240 MW operational since 1966).
> 2. **Tidal Lagoon** — A circular retaining wall built offshore, filled and emptied through bidirectional turbines. Modular, less ecological disruption (e.g., Swansea Bay, UK — 320 MW proposed).
> 3. **Tidal Stream Generator** — Arrays of submerged horizontal-axis or vertical-axis turbines placed in high-velocity tidal channels (e.g., MeyGen, Scotland — 6 MW installed, 86 MW planned).

## 1.6 Why Tidal Energy + Storage? The ESS Imperative

> [!WARNING]
> **Tidal Energy's Unique Grid Challenge:** Although tidal energy is **highly predictable**, it produces power in **pulses** (≈10 hours of generation per 12.4-hour cycle in ebb-generation mode, with slack periods in between). Without **energy storage**, this creates **grid instability** and **poor capacity factors** (~25–30% for barrage systems). Hence, the **synergy with ESS** is not optional — it is the technological key to commercial viability.

Storage pairings include:
- **Pumped Hydro Storage (PHS)** — pumped using surplus tidal power.
- **Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS)** — for short-term smoothing.
- **Hydrogen Production (Power-to-Gas)** — for seasonal/weekly shifting.
- **Compressed Air Energy Storage (CAES)** — for large-scale storage.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: The Tidal Energy Equation

The theoretical energy available in a tidal basin comes from the **gravitational potential energy difference** between high tide and low tide water levels. Unlike wind or solar, the **resource is "stored" naturally** in the form of an elevated water column that is replenished twice daily.

### 2.1.1 Derivation of the Basin Potential Energy

Consider an idealised tidal basin of surface area $A$ experiencing a tidal range $R = h_{high} - h_{low}$. Treating the water column as a vertical slab of uniform cross-section:

1. **Mass of the mobilisable water column:** $m = \rho_{sw} \cdot A \cdot R$.
2. **Centre of mass of the additional water layer:** located at height $R/2$ above mean sea level.
3. **Gravitational potential energy relative to low tide:** $E_{PE} = m \cdot g \cdot \dfrac{R}{2}$.
4. **Substituting (1) into (3):** $E_{PE} = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot A \cdot R \cdot \dfrac{R}{2} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot A \cdot R^2$.

This is the **maximum extractable energy per tidal cycle** in the absence of losses.

### 2.1.2 Average Power Over a Semidiurnal Cycle

The semidiurnal tidal period is $T = 12.42 \text{ hr} = 44,712 \text{ s}$. The **average tidal power** (Baye's formula) is:

$$P_{avg} = \dfrac{E_{PE}}{T} = \dfrac{\rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot A \cdot R^2}{2 \cdot T}$$

### 2.1.3 Tidal Stream Power (Kinetic Energy)

For a tidal channel of cross-sectional area $A$ with mean flow velocity $v$, the kinetic flux of power is given by the standard fluid-power equation:

$$P_{stream} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho_{sw} \cdot A \cdot v^3 \cdot C_P$$

where $C_P$ is the **power coefficient**, bounded by the **Betz limit** of $C_{P,max} = 16/27$.

## 2.2 Operational Modes of a Tidal Barrage

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Generation Strategies:**
>
> - **Ebb Generation (most common):** Water is impounded during flood, held at high tide, then released through turbines during ebb tide. Maximises head and minimises environmental impact.
> - **Flood Generation:** Turbines operate during the rising tide only.
> - **Two-way (Bidirectional) Generation:** Turbines operate during both flood and ebb cycles, doubling operating hours but reducing efficiency per cycle.

## 2.3 Tidal Range Classification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Micro-tidal:** $R < 2 \text{ m}$ (low viability).
> - **Meso-tidal:** $2 \text{ m} \leq R < 4 \text{ m}$ (marginal).
> - **Macro-tidal:** $R \geq 4 \text{ m}$ (commercially viable; ideally $R > 5 \text{ m}$ for baseload-style operation).

Kerala's coast experiences a **mixed semidiurnal micro-to-meso-tidal regime** with typical ranges of **0.5–1.5 m**, which is why standalone tidal barrages are not feasible in Kerala — but **tidal stream + storage hybrid systems** remain an active research area (e.g., Gulf of Kutch and Gulf of Cambay in India are the most promising macro-tidal sites).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Master these equations — they are 90% of all KTU numerical questions on this topic.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Conditions / Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|-------------------|
| 1 | Potential Energy per Cycle | $E = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_{sw} g A R^2$ | Joules (J) | Ideal basin, uniform cross-section |
| 2 | Average Tidal Power (Baye) | $P = \dfrac{\rho_{sw} g A R^2}{2 T}$ | Watts (W) | $T = 12.42$ hr for semidiurnal |
| 3 | Tidal Stream Power | $P_s = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_{sw} A v^3 C_P$ | Watts (W) | $C_P \le 16/27 \approx 0.593$ |
| 4 | Annual Energy (Barrage) | $E_{yr} = P \cdot 8760 \cdot \eta$ | Wh | $\eta$ = overall plant efficiency (0.25–0.35) |
| 5 | Tidal Prism Volume | $V_P = A \cdot R$ | m³ | Volume of water entering/leaving basin |
| 6 | Tidal Current Velocity (shallow water) | $v = \sqrt{g \cdot h \cdot S}$ | m/s | $S$ = seabed slope; h = depth |
| 7 | Tidal Range at Spring Tide | $R_{spring} = 2.0 \cdot M_2$ | m | $M_2$ = principal lunar semi-diurnal amplitude |
| 8 | Tidal Range at Neap Tide | $R_{neap} \approx 0.3 \cdot R_{spring}$ | m | When Sun is perpendicular to Moon's pull |
| 9 | Available Energy Density | $e_d = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_{sw} g R^2$ | J/m² | Per unit basin area |
| 10 | Storage Capacity Required (BESS) | $E_{BESS} = P \cdot t_{slack}$ | Wh | To bridge slack tide gap $t_{slack}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Absolute Value Notation Rule:** In all handwritten or typeset solutions, write $\vert R \vert$ or $\vert v \vert$ — never use a vertical pipe `|` inside a markdown table cell.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Grid-Firming & Capacity Firming:** Tidal + storage provides **dispatchable renewable baseload** with capacity factors of 70–80% (vs. 25–35% for solar/wind standalone).
- **Predictability Premium:** Unlike solar/wind forecasts (which have ±10–20% uncertainty at 24-hr horizon), tidal forecasts are **astronomically deterministic** to within minutes, allowing **near-perfect ESS scheduling**.
- **Decarbonisation of Coastal Grids:** India's Vizhinjam International Seaport (Kerala) and the proposed Gulf of Kutch tidal pilot (Gujarat) represent strategic national assets.
- **Co-located Desalination:** Reverse-osmosis plants can be run directly on tidal power, addressing freshwater scarcity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Derivation Mandate:** Every algebraic step is shown explicitly. No skips, no shortcuts.

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Available Energy in a Tidal Basin (Full Worked Derivation)

**Problem Setup:** A tidal basin of surface area $A = 20 \text{ km}^2$ experiences a mean tidal range $R = 8 \text{ m}$. Determine (a) the potential energy available per tidal cycle, and (b) the average power output.

**Given:**
- $A = 20 \text{ km}^2 = 20 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^2$
- $R = 8 \text{ m}$
- $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- $T = 12.42 \text{ hr} = 12.42 \times 3600 \text{ s} = 44{,}712 \text{ s}$

### Part (a): Potential Energy per Tidal Cycle

**Step 1 — Define the volume of the mobilisable water slab.**

The basin is filled from low tide (level 0) to high tide (level $R$). The water layer mobilised is a slab of height $R$ over the basin area $A$:

$$V = A \cdot R$$

**Step 2 — Convert volume to mass using seawater density.**

The mass of the additional water layer is the volume multiplied by the density of seawater:

$$m = \rho_{sw} \cdot V = \rho_{sw} \cdot A \cdot R$$

**Step 3 — Identify the height of the centre of mass.**

For a uniform rectangular slab of height $R$, the geometric centre lies at a height $R/2$ above the base (i.e., above the mean low-water level). This is the height through which the slab's mass can fall to release gravitational potential energy:

$$h_{cm} = \dfrac{R}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the gravitational potential energy formula.**

The standard PE formula is $E = m g h$. Substituting the mass from Step 2 and the height from Step 3:

$$E = m \cdot g \cdot h_{cm} = (\rho_{sw} \cdot A \cdot R) \cdot g \cdot \left(\dfrac{R}{2}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Simplify the expression.**

Multiplying through and gathering terms:

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot A \cdot R^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= \dfrac{1}{2} \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times (20 \times 10^6) \times (8)^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Compute term-by-term.**

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= 0.5 \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times 20 \times 10^6 \times 64
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= 0.5 \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times 1.28 \times 10^9
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
E &\approx 6.44 \times 10^{12} \text{ J}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** $E \approx 6.44 \text{ TJ}$ per tidal cycle.

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **ideal gross energy**. Real-world barrage efficiency $\eta \approx 0.25$–$0.35$, so the **net extractable energy** is $E_{net} \approx 1.6$–$2.25 \text{ TJ}$ per cycle.

### Part (b): Average Power Over a Semidiurnal Cycle

**Step 1 — Apply the average-power definition.**

Average power is the total energy per cycle divided by the cycle period:

$$P_{avg} = \dfrac{E}{T}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the energy expression from Part (a).**

$$P_{avg} = \dfrac{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho_{sw} g A R^2}{T}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{avg} &= \dfrac{6.44 \times 10^{12} \text{ J}}{44{,}712 \text{ s}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Perform the division.**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{avg} &\approx 1.44 \times 10^8 \text{ W} = 144 \text{ MW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** $P_{avg} \approx 144 \text{ MW}$ of theoretical (ideal) average power. The **realistic installed capacity** would be ≈ **240 MW** (since turbines generate at peak head for only ≈6–7 hours per cycle, requiring oversized nameplate capacity).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Tidal Stream Power (Cross-Flow Turbine)

**Problem Setup:** A tidal channel of cross-sectional flow area $A = 1000 \text{ m}^2$ experiences peak tidal current velocity $v = 2.5 \text{ m/s}$. Compute the theoretical extractable power using the Betz-limited coefficient.

**Given:**
- $A = 1000 \text{ m}^2$
- $v = 2.5 \text{ m/s}$
- $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $C_P = 16/27 \approx 0.593$ (Betz limit)

**Step 1 — State the kinetic power flux equation.**

The power available in a moving fluid per unit time is the kinetic energy flux through the cross-section:

$$P_{stream} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho_{sw} \cdot A \cdot v^3 \cdot C_P$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{stream} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 1000 \times (2.5)^3 \times 0.593
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the cubic term $v^3$.**

$$v^3 = (2.5)^3 = 15.625 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}^3$$

**Step 4 — Multiply all terms.**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{stream} &= 0.5 \times 1025 \times 1000 \times 15.625 \times 0.593
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{stream} &= 512.5 \times 15{,}625 \times 0.593
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{stream} &\approx 4.75 \times 10^6 \text{ W} = 4.75 \text{ MW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** $P_{stream} \approx 4.75 \text{ MW}$ from a single tidal stream turbine site.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: BESS Sizing to Bridge Tidal Slack Period

**Problem Setup:** A 50 MW tidal barrage has a generation cycle of 7 hours "on" and 5.42 hours "off" (slack tide). Design the BESS capacity needed to supply a constant 50 MW baseload.

**Given:**
- $P_{barrage} = 50 \text{ MW}$
- $t_{slack} = 5.42 \text{ hr}$
- Round-trip efficiency of BESS: $\eta_{BESS} = 0.90$
- Depth-of-Discharge (DoD) limit: $DoD = 0.80$

**Step 1 — Compute the raw energy needed during slack period.**

The energy that must be delivered by the BESS is the load power times the slack duration:

$$E_{deliver} = P \cdot t_{slack} = 50 \text{ MW} \times 5.42 \text{ hr} = 271 \text{ MWh}$$

**Step 2 — Account for round-trip efficiency.**

To deliver $271 \text{ MWh}$ at the output, the BESS must store more, because charging also incurs losses. The required stored energy is:

$$E_{stored} = \dfrac{E_{deliver}}{\eta_{BESS}} = \dfrac{271}{0.90} \approx 301.1 \text{ MWh}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Depth-of-Discharge constraint.**

Lithium-ion batteries should not be discharged below 20% SoC, so the **nameplate capacity** must be larger than the usable capacity:

$$E_{nameplate} = \dfrac{E_{stored}}{DoD} = \dfrac{301.1}{0.80} \approx 376.4 \text{ MWh}$$

**Step 4 — State final answer.**

**Result:** The BESS must be sized at ≈ **376 MWh** to firm 50 MW of tidal generation into continuous baseload.

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **bridge-storage** requirement. In practice, **over-generation** during the 7-hour "on" period (which produces 350 MWh) is also used to **re-charge** the BESS. The design constraint is therefore a **daily energy-balance equation**, not just slack-period bridging.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Barrage vs. Stream vs. Lagoon

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comparison Matrix for KTU Examination:**

| Criterion | Tidal Barrage | Tidal Lagoon | Tidal Stream |
|-----------|---------------|--------------|--------------|
| Energy Form | Potential (head) | Potential (head) | Kinetic (velocity) |
| Typical Capacity | 100–1000 MW | 50–500 MW | 5–50 MW per array |
| Capacity Factor | 22–28% | 25–35% | 30–45% |
| Capital Cost (₹/kW) | High (1.5–2.5 Cr) | Medium (1.0–1.5 Cr) | Low–Medium (0.8–1.2 Cr) |
| Environmental Impact | High (estuary disruption) | Moderate (offshore) | Low (submerged) |
| Maturity | Mature (1966 La Rance) | Demonstration stage | Commercial scale |
| Storage Pairing | PHS, BESS, H₂ | BESS, CAES | BESS, Supercapacitors |
| Best Suited For | Macro-tidal bays | Coastal embayments | Tidal channels |
| Indian Potential | Gulf of Kutch, Gulf of Cambay | Offshore Gujarat | Gulf of Kutch (Narara) |
