# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
**Define tidal energy. Distinguish between tidal range and tidal stream energy with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** **Tidal Energy:** Tidal energy is the renewable energy harnessed from the rise (flood) and fall (ebb) of sea levels caused by the gravitational pull of the Moon and Sun, and the centrifugal force of the Earth–Moon system.
- **(1 mark)** **Tidal Range Energy:** It exploits the **vertical head difference** (potential energy) between high and low tides. Example: **La Rance Tidal Barrage, France (240 MW)** uses a dam across the Rance estuary.
- **(1 mark)** **Tidal Stream Energy:** It exploits the **horizontal kinetic energy** of flowing tidal currents. Example: **MeyGen project, Pentland Firth, Scotland** uses submerged horizontal-axis turbines in tidal channels.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**List any three advantages and two limitations of tidal energy as a renewable resource.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Advantages (any 3 × 0.5 = 1.5 marks):**
  1. **Highly predictable** — accurate forecasts years in advance due to deterministic astronomy.
  2. **Zero carbon emissions** during operation (≈ 15 g CO₂/kWh lifecycle).
  3. **High energy density** of water (≈ 800× that of air) → small device footprints.
  4. Long plant lifetime (≈ 100 years for barrages; 25–30 years for turbines).
- **Limitations (any 2 × 0.75 = 1.5 marks):**
  1. **High capital cost** and **geographically constrained** to specific high-tidal sites.
  2. **Intermittent in short cycles** (tidal pulsing) — **requires energy storage** for firm power.
  3. Ecological impact on marine ecosystems and sediment transport.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Internal Choice Pattern:** Students must answer either Question A OR Question B in full. Each 14-mark question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

**(a) Derive the expression for the average power available from a tidal barrage in terms of basin area, tidal range, seawater density, and tidal period. State clearly all assumptions made.** (7 marks)

**(b) A tidal barrage is proposed across a bay of area $A = 30 \text{ km}^2$ with a mean tidal range of $R = 7 \text{ m}$. Compute (i) the potential energy available per tidal cycle, and (ii) the average tidal power in MW. Assume the barrage operates in ebb-generation mode with overall plant efficiency $\eta = 0.30$. Use $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, and tidal period $T = 12.42 \text{ hr}$.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. Basin has uniform surface area $A$ (constant with depth).
2. Tidal range $R$ is constant (averaged over spring–neap cycle).
3. No losses due to sluice gate leakage, friction, or turbine inefficiency during theoretical derivation.
4. Water behaves as an incompressible fluid.

**Derivation (6 marks):**

**Step 1:** Volume of mobilisable water in one cycle:
$$V = A \cdot R$$

**Step 2:** Mass of mobilisable water:
$$m = \rho_{sw} \cdot V = \rho_{sw} \cdot A \cdot R$$

**Step 3:** Height of centre of mass of the slab above low-tide level:
$$h_{cm} = \dfrac{R}{2}$$

**Step 4:** Gravitational potential energy per cycle:
$$E = m \cdot g \cdot h_{cm} = \rho_{sw} \cdot A \cdot R \cdot g \cdot \dfrac{R}{2}$$

**Step 5:** Simplified energy expression:
$$E = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot A \cdot R^2$$

**Step 6:** Average power over a tidal period $T$:
$$P_{avg} = \dfrac{E}{T} = \dfrac{\rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot A \cdot R^2}{2 \cdot T}$$

This is **Baye's formula** for tidal power.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:** $A = 30 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^2$, $R = 7 \text{ m}$, $\eta = 0.30$, $T = 12.42 \times 3600 = 44{,}712 \text{ s}$.

**Part (b)(i) — Potential Energy per Cycle (3.5 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot A \cdot R^2
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= 0.5 \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times (30 \times 10^6) \times (7)^2
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= 0.5 \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times 30 \times 10^6 \times 49
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
E &\approx 7.39 \times 10^{12} \text{ J} \approx 7.39 \text{ TJ}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (b)(ii) — Average Tidal Power (3.5 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{gross} &= \dfrac{E}{T} = \dfrac{7.39 \times 10^{12}}{44{,}712}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{gross} &\approx 1.653 \times 10^8 \text{ W} = 165.3 \text{ MW}
\end{aligned}$$

Applying overall efficiency $\eta = 0.30$:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{net} &= \eta \times P_{gross} = 0.30 \times 165.3 = 49.6 \text{ MW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** Net average power ≈ **49.6 MW**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**(a) With the aid of a labelled schematic, describe the working principle of an ebb-generation tidal barrage. Discuss its advantages over flood-generation mode.** (7 marks)

**(b) A tidal stream turbine operates in a channel of cross-section $A = 800 \text{ m}^2$ with mean tidal velocity $v = 2.0 \text{ m/s}$. Calculate (i) the gross kinetic power, (ii) the Betz-limited power, and (iii) the annual energy output in MWh assuming a capacity factor of 35%.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Descriptive (7 marks)

**Working Principle (5 marks):**
1. **Flood Phase (1 mark):** Sluice gates open at low tide; rising tide water flows freely into the impoundment basin (no generation). Basin fills to high-tide level.
2. **Hold Phase (1 mark):** Sluice gates close at high tide. Basin is now a "**stored head**" reservoir of height $R$ above the downstream sea level.
3. **Ebb Phase (2 marks):** Once the downstream sea level falls below the impounded level by a sufficient head (typically 1–2 m), the **low-head bulb turbines** are opened. Water flows from basin to sea through turbines, generating electricity for ≈ 4–6 hours.
4. **Sluicing (1 mark):** At very low tide, any remaining water is released through the **sluice gates** to reset for the next cycle.

**Advantages of Ebb-Generation (2 marks):**
- **Maximum available head** at peak generation, giving higher turbine efficiency.
- **Lower environmental impact** on fish migration (turbines operate only one direction).
- Higher energy yield per cycle compared to flood-only mode.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:** $A = 800 \text{ m}^2$, $v = 2.0 \text{ m/s}$, $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $C_{P,Betz} = 16/27 \approx 0.593$, $CF = 0.35$, $H = 8760 \text{ hr/yr}$.

**Part (b)(i) — Gross Kinetic Power (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{gross} &= \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho_{sw} \cdot A \cdot v^3
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{gross} &= 0.5 \times 1025 \times 800 \times (2.0)^3
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{gross} &= 0.5 \times 1025 \times 800 \times 8 = 3.28 \times 10^6 \text{ W} = 3.28 \text{ MW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (b)(ii) — Betz-Limited Power (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{Betz} &= C_{P,max} \times P_{gross} = 0.593 \times 3.28
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{Betz} &= 1.945 \text{ MW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (b)(iii) — Annual Energy Output (3 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{annual} &= P_{Betz} \times CF \times 8760
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{annual} &= 1.945 \text{ MW} \times 0.35 \times 8760 \text{ hr}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{annual} &\approx 5{,}962 \text{ MWh/year}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** Annual energy output ≈ **5,962 MWh/year**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to square the tidal range** — Students often write $E = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho g A R$ instead of $E = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho g A R^2$. This is a **2-mark loss per occurrence**.
> 2. **Using freshwater density** ($1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$) instead of **seawater density** ($1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$). Always use $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ for tidal problems.
> 3. **Wrong tidal period** — Use $T = 12.42 \text{ hr}$, NOT 12 hr or 24 hr. The 12.42 hr is the **lunar semidiurnal period** and accounts for Earth's rotation relative to the Moon.
> 4. **Confusing Betz limit with 100%** — Maximum $C_P$ is $16/27 \approx 0.593$, never 1.0. If the problem says "ideal turbine," you still apply Betz unless explicitly stated as "idealised fluid power."
> 5. **Unit conversion errors** — $1 \text{ km}^2 = 10^6 \text{ m}^2$, NOT $10^3 \text{ m}^2$. Always show unit conversions in your working.
> 6. **Ignoring efficiency** — If $\eta$ is given, **multiply the final answer by $\eta$**. A common omission costs 1–2 marks.
> 7. **Mixing barrage (potential) and stream (kinetic) formulas** — Barrage power uses $R^2$ dependence; stream power uses $v^3$ dependence. Know which is which.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Bulleted Summary — Read This 5 Minutes Before the Exam.**

- ✅ **Tidal energy is driven by** the gravitational pull of the **Moon** (≈ 67% contribution) and **Sun** (≈ 33%), modulated by the Earth's rotation.
- ✅ **Spring tides** occur at New Moon and Full Moon (Sun–Moon–Earth aligned) → **maximum tidal range** (≈ 2× mean).
- ✅ **Neap tides** occur at First and Last Quarter (Sun–Moon perpendicular) → **minimum tidal range** (≈ 30% of spring).
- ✅ **Semidiurnal period** = **12.42 hours** (not 12 hr). This is the **golden number** for all KTU problems.
- ✅ **Baye's formula** for tidal barrage average power: $P = \dfrac{\rho_{sw} g A R^2}{2 T}$.
- ✅ **Tidal stream kinetic power** equation: $P = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_{sw} A v^3 C_P$.
- ✅ **Betz Limit** for tidal turbines: $C_{P,max} = 16/27 \approx 0.593$ (same as wind, because the underlying fluid mechanics is identical).
- ✅ **Seawater density** $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ — **always use this, not 1000**.
- ✅ **Three Tidal Energy Converter (TEC) types:** Tidal Barrage, Tidal Lagoon, Tidal Stream.
- ✅ **Generation modes:** Ebb (most common, highest efficiency), Flood, Bidirectional.
- ✅ **Tidal + ESS Synergy:** Tidal power is **dispatchable** only when paired with storage (PHS, BESS, CAES, or H₂).
- ✅ **BESS sizing formula** for slack-tide bridging: $E_{BESS} = \dfrac{P \cdot t_{slack}}{\eta_{BESS} \cdot DoD}$.
- ✅ **Macro-tidal threshold:** $R \geq 4 \text{ m}$ for commercial viability; $R > 5 \text{ m}$ preferred.
- ✅ **Top global sites:** La Rance (France), Sihwa Lake (South Korea, 254 MW), MeyGen (Scotland), Swansea Bay (UK planned).
- ✅ **Indian tidal potential sites:** Gulf of Kutch (Gujarat), Gulf of Cambay (Gujarat), and the planned Vizhinjam (Kerala) hybrid system.
- ✅ **Capacity factor** of tidal barrage ≈ 22–28%; tidal stream ≈ 30–45% (higher due to continuous flow).
- ✅ **Plant lifetime:** Barrage ≈ 100 years; Stream turbine ≈ 25–30 years; Lagoon ≈ 60–80 years.
- ✅ **Environmental concerns:** Marine ecosystem disruption, sediment transport changes, fish migration, brine dispersion — all addressed in EIA studies.
- ✅ **KTU numerical pattern:** Always show 6–7 steps; never write a one-line solution. Partial marks depend on **stepwise working**.
- ✅ **Two must-remember formulas** (in order of frequency in KTU papers):
  1. $E = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_{sw} g A R^2$
  2. $P_{stream} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_{sw} A v^3 C_P$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET521 - ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hydroelectric

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hydroelectric Energy Storage (HES)** is a **gravitational potential energy** based storage technology that converts the hydraulic head of stored water into electricity via hydraulic turbines coupled to synchronous generators, and (in its reversible form) regenerates the stored potential energy by pumping water from a lower reservoir back to an upper reservoir during periods of surplus grid power.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** classification for the course *PEEET521 – Energy Storage Systems*, hydroelectric systems are categorized under **Mechanical / Gravitational Energy Storage Systems**, and encompass three principal sub-topologies:

1. **Conventional (Impoundment) Hydroelectric** – continuous-run-of-river or dammed installations.
2. **Run-of-River (RoR) Hydroelectric** – minimal reservoir, river-flow dependent.
3. **Pumped Hydro Storage System (PHSS / PHS)** – the dominant **grid-scale energy storage** modality worldwide, responsible for over **95%** of all globally installed bulk electrical energy storage capacity.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Picture

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Water Battery"**
> Imagine two buckets stacked vertically. The **upper bucket** is the *reservoir*, the **hose connecting them** is the *penstock*, and the **little spinning wheel at the bottom** is the *turbine*. When you open the valve, water rushes down, spins the wheel, and lights up an LED. To "recharge," you take the water from the bottom bucket, carry it back to the top using a pump powered by surplus electricity from the grid. **The vertical distance between the two buckets is called HEAD (H), and the volume of water you move per second is called FLOW (Q).** More height + more water = more stored & retrievable energy. That is essentially a **Pumped Hydro Storage System (PHSS)** — a giant, rechargeable water battery.

## 1.3 Fundamental Physical Quantities & Constants

The governing constants used throughout the derivation of hydroelectric performance are:

- **Density of water:** $\rho_w = \mathbf{1000 \; kg/m^{3}}$
- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = \mathbf{9.81 \; m/s^{2}}$
- **Gravitational acceleration constant (KTU shorthand):** $g = \mathbf{9.8 \; m/s^{2}}$ (when approximation permitted)

These two constants combine into the **specific weight of water**, $\gamma = \rho_w g \approx \mathbf{9810 \; N/m^{3}}$, which appears in virtually every hydro power and energy equation.

## 1.4 Why Hydroelectric Storage Matters in the Modern Grid

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **KTU 2024 Module-3** emphasis on hydroelectric storage is driven by its role as the **backbone of large-scale renewable integration**. With the rapid expansion of intermittent solar PV and wind generation, PHSS provides:
> - **Time-shifting** of off-peak energy to peak demand hours.
> - **Frequency regulation** and **spinning reserve** capability.
> - **Black-start** capacity following grid collapse.
> - **Capacity factors** between **70%–85%**, far exceeding most other storage technologies.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Architecture of a Hydroelectric Storage Plant

A complete PHSS installation comprises the following functional subsystems, each of which contributes a finite efficiency to the overall round-trip performance:

### 2.1.1 Upper Reservoir (Head Pond)
- Stores hydraulic potential energy in the form of an elevated water column.
- Capacity sized in terms of **active storage volume**, $V_a$, measured in **m³** or **million m³ (Mm³)**.
- Operates between a **maximum head**, $H_{max}$, and a **minimum drawdown head**, $H_{min}$.

### 2.1.2 Water Conduit — Penstock & Surge Tank
- **Penstock:** high-pressure steel (or reinforced concrete) pipeline conveying water from the reservoir to the turbine.
- **Surge tank:** vertical chamber placed upstream of the turbine to absorb hydraulic transients ("water hammer") during rapid load rejection.
- Head loss in penstock: $h_{loss} \propto Q^{2}$ (Darcy-Weisbach relation).

### 2.1.3 Powerhouse & Turbo-Generator Assembly
- Houses the **hydraulic turbine**, **synchronous generator**, **governor**, and **excitation system**.
- Reversible **pump-turbine (Francis-type)** units are standard for PHSS.

### 2.1.4 Tailrace & Lower Reservoir
- Receives discharged water from the turbine.
- In closed-loop PHSS, this is a **separate lower reservoir**; in open-loop systems, it is the natural river body.

## 2.2 Operating Modes of Pumped Hydro Storage

| Mode | Direction of Power Flow | Grid Condition | KTU Notation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Generation (Turbining)** | Hydraulic → Mechanical → Electrical | Peak demand, high LMP | $\eta_{gen}$ |
| **Pumping (Charging)** | Electrical → Mechanical → Hydraulic | Off-peak, surplus renewables | $\eta_{pump}$ |
| **Standby / Spinning Reserve | — | — | Available for $< 10$ min dispatch |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Round-Trip Efficiency (RTE)** of a PHSS is defined as:
>
> $$\eta_{RTE} = \eta_{pump} \times \eta_{gen}$$
>
> Typical modern reversible PHSS units achieve $\eta_{RTE} \approx \mathbf{75\% – 82\%}$.

## 2.3 Hydraulic Turbine Classification (KTU High-Yield)

| Turbine Type | Head Range (m) | Flow Regime | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pelton Wheel** (Impulse) | High ($> 300$ m) | Low flow, high head | Mountain PHSS |
| **Francis Turbine** (Reaction) | Medium ($30$ – $300$ m) | Moderate flow & head | **Most PHSS installations** |
| **Kaplan / Propeller** (Reaction) | Low ($< 30$ m) | High flow, low head | Run-of-river, tidal |
| **Deriaz / Bulb** | Low to medium | Variable | Tidal / low-head PHSS |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Hydroelectric Energy Storage

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All quantities below are KTU board-exam ready. Units must accompany every numerical answer.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes / Assumptions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Theoretical Hydraulic Power | $P_{hyd} = \rho \, g \, Q \, H$ | W (Watts) | $Q$: volumetric flow rate; $H$: net head |
| 2 | Useful Electrical Output | $P_{elec} = \rho \, g \, Q \, H \, \eta_{t} \eta_{g}$ | W | $\eta_t$: turbine eff.; $\eta_g$: generator eff. |
| 3 | Specific Weight Form | $P_{hyd} = \gamma \, Q \, H$ | W | $\gamma = \rho g$ |
| 4 | Energy Stored (PHSS) | $E = \rho \, g \, V \, H$ | J | $V$: usable water volume |
| 5 | Specific Energy | $e = g \, H$ | J/kg | Energy per unit mass of water |
| 6 | Energy Density (volumetric) | $E_v = \rho \, g \, H$ | J/m³ | For fixed reservoir |
| 7 | Discharge Duration | $t = V / Q$ | s | Time to empty reservoir at flow $Q$ |
| 8 | Round-Trip Efficiency | $\eta_{RTE} = \eta_{pump} \cdot \eta_{gen}$ | — (decimal) | Often quoted as 75–82% |
| 9 | Penstock Head Loss (Darcy) | $h_f = f \frac{L}{D} \frac{V^{2}}{2g}$ | m | $f$: friction factor |
| 10 | Hydraulic Trans. Velocity | $a = \sqrt{\frac{K/\rho}{1 + (K/E)(D/t)}}$ | m/s | Water hammer wave speed |
| 11 | Net Head | $H_{net} = H_{gross} - h_{losses}$ | m | Subtracts penstock + trash-rack losses |
| 12 | Capacity Factor | $CF = \dfrac{E_{actual}}{P_{rated} \cdot T}$ | — (decimal) | PHSS typically 0.05–0.15 (cycling duty) |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility & Real-World Significance

- **Grid-scale storage:** PHSS constitutes **> 95%** of the world's installed bulk storage capacity (~$160$ GW globally as of 2024).
- **Long-duration capability:** Typical PHSS supports **6 – 20 hours** of full-load discharge, far exceeding Li-ion (2–4 h).
- **Lifetime:** **50 – 100 years** for civil works; **30 – 50 years** for electromechanical equipment.
- **Land footprint:** High, but mitigated by **offshore / underground PHSS** (e.g., proposed seawater PHSS in Hawaii, Okinawa).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Theoretical Hydraulic Power Available from a Head & Flow

### 3.1.1 Statement of the Physical Problem
A volume $V$ of water of mass $m = \rho V$ descends through a vertical height $H$. Determine the theoretical mechanical power available at the base of the column when water flows at a steady rate $Q$ (m³/s).

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Mass flow rate.** The mass of water passing a cross-section per second is given by the density multiplied by the volumetric flow rate:

$$
\dot{m} = \rho \, Q
$$

where $\rho$ is in **kg/m³** and $Q$ is in **m³/s**, giving $\dot{m}$ in **kg/s**.

**Step 2 — Gravitational potential energy per unit mass.** The potential energy per unit mass of water raised through a height $H$ is, by the definition of gravitational potential:

$$
\Delta PE_{per \, mass} = g \, H
$$

This has units of **J/kg** (specific energy).

**Step 3 — Total potential power released per second.** Multiplying the per-unit-mass energy by the mass flow rate yields the *power* (energy per unit time) released:

$$
P_{hyd} = \dot{m} \cdot g \cdot H
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the mass flow rate expression.** Replacing $\dot{m} = \rho Q$:

$$
P_{hyd} = (\rho Q) \, g \, H
$$

**Step 5 — Final compact form.** Rearranging the constants:

$$
\boxed{\,P_{hyd} = \rho \, g \, Q \, H\,}
$$

This is the canonical **KTU hydropower equation**, with units $\frac{kg}{m^{3}} \cdot \frac{m}{s^{2}} \cdot \frac{m^{3}}{s} \cdot m = W$.

### 3.1.3 Accounting for Conversion Losses

The hydraulic power must be multiplied successively by the efficiencies of the turbine ($\eta_t$) and the synchronous generator ($\eta_g$) to obtain the **net electrical output**:

$$
P_{elec} = \rho \, g \, Q \, H \cdot \eta_t \cdot \eta_g
$$

Typical numerical values used in **KTU 2024** problems:

- $\eta_t \approx 0.85$ – $0.92$ for Francis turbines
- $\eta_g \approx 0.95$ – $0.98$ for synchronous generators
- $\eta_{pump} \approx 0.88$ – $0.92$ for reversible pump-turbines

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Energy Stored in an Upper Reservoir (PHSS)

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement
An upper reservoir of usable volume $V$ (m³) is located at a mean head $H$ above a lower reservoir. Derive an expression for the **total energy that can be extracted** during a discharge cycle, assuming a constant head approximation.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Mass of usable water.** The mass of stored water available for discharge is:

$$
m = \rho \, V
$$

**Step 2 — Gravitational potential energy.** By definition of gravitational PE:

$$
E = m \, g \, H
$$

**Step 3 — Substitution of mass.**

$$
E = (\rho V) \, g \, H
$$

**Step 4 — Final stored-energy expression.**

$$
\boxed{\,E = \rho \, g \, V \, H\,} \quad \text{(Joules)}
$$

**Step 5 — Useful energy at grid terminals** (after both conversion & head losses):

$$
E_{useful} = \rho \, g \, V \, H \cdot \eta_t \eta_g
$$

### 3.2.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> **Problem:** A pumped hydro storage facility has an upper reservoir of usable volume $V = 5 \times 10^{6}$ m³ at an average head of $H = 200$ m above the lower reservoir. The turbine-generator efficiency is $\eta_{tg} = 0.86$ and the pump efficiency is $\eta_{pump} = 0.90$. Calculate (a) the stored hydraulic energy, (b) the electrical energy recoverable, and (c) the round-trip efficiency.

**Solution:**

**(a) Stored hydraulic energy:**

$$
E_{hyd} = \rho g V H = (1000)(9.81)(5 \times 10^{6})(200)
$$

$$
E_{hyd} = 9.81 \times 10^{12} \; J
$$

Converting to **megawatt-hours** ($1 \; MWh = 3.6 \times 10^{9} \; J$):

$$
E_{hyd} = \frac{9.81 \times 10^{12}}{3.6 \times 10^{9}} \approx 2725 \; MWh
$$

**(b) Recoverable electrical energy:**

$$
E_{elec} = E_{hyd} \cdot \eta_{tg} = 2725 \times 0.86 \approx 2343.5 \; MWh
$$

**(c) Round-trip efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{RTE} = \eta_{pump} \times \eta_{gen} = 0.90 \times 0.86 \approx 0.774 = 77.4\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always carry units through the calculation and present the final answer with proper SI prefixes (kWh, MWh, GWh). Marks are frequently deducted for missing or incorrect unit conversions.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Deriving Maximum Discharge Duration

### 3.3.1 Problem
For a given reservoir volume $V$ and rated turbine flow $Q$, derive the **maximum continuous discharge duration** $t_{max}$.

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Definition of volumetric flow rate.** By definition:

$$
Q = \frac{V}{t}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for time.**

$$
t = \frac{V}{Q}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $V = E / (\rho g H)$ to express in terms of energy:**

$$
t = \frac{E}{\rho \, g \, H \, Q}
$$

**Step 4 — Final form in terms of rated power $P_r$:**

$$
t_{max} = \frac{E_{usable}}{P_r}
$$

This is the **storage duration** commonly advertised for PHSS plants.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Net Head Calculation

### 3.4.1 Problem
The "gross head" measured between reservoir water surfaces is reduced by frictional and minor losses before reaching the turbine. Derive the **net head**.

### 3.4.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Gross head definition.**

$$
H_{gross} = Z_{upper} - Z_{lower}
$$

**Step 2 — Major (frictional) head loss in the penstock** (Darcy-Weisbach):

$$
h_f = f \, \frac{L}{D} \, \frac{V^{2}}{2g}
$$

where $f$ is the Darcy friction factor, $L$ the penstock length, $D$ the diameter, and $V$ the flow velocity.

**Step 3 — Minor losses** (entrance, trash-rack, bends):

$$
h_m = \sum K_i \frac{V^{2}}{2g}
$$

**Step 4 — Net head.**

$$
\boxed{\,H_{net} = H_{gross} - h_f - h_m\,}
$$

This value of $H_{net}$ must be used in all power equations for accurate KTU numerical answers.

---

## 4.3 Component-to-Function Mapping Matrix (KTU Revision Aid)

| Component | Primary Function | Energy Form Handled | Typical KTU Exam Focus |

| Upper Reservoir | Energy storage | Hydraulic potential | Volume sizing, $H_{max}/H_{min}$ |
| Penstock | Energy transport | Pressure head | Head loss, water hammer |
| Surge Tank | Transient suppression | Pressure surge | Necessity & location |
| Pump-Turbine | Energy conversion | Hydraulic ↔ Mechanical | Type selection, $\eta_t$ |
| Generator-Motor | Energy conversion | Mechanical ↔ Electrical | Synchronous operation, $\eta_g$ |
| Transformer | Voltage adaptation | Electrical | Step-up/down losses |
| Lower Reservoir | Energy return & balance | Hydraulic potential | Open vs closed loop |
| Governor | Frequency/load control | Mechanical control | Droop, primary response |

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: Define Pumped Hydro Storage System (PHSS). List any two advantages and one disadvantage.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** A Pumped Hydro Storage System (PHSS) is a type of **gravitational / mechanical energy storage** in which water is pumped from a lower reservoir to an upper reservoir during off-peak hours using surplus electricity, and is later released through turbines to generate electricity during peak demand hours.
>
> **Advantages (1 mark each, any two):**
> 1. Very large energy storage capacity (GWh-scale) with long discharge durations (6–20 hours).
> 2. Long operational lifetime (50–100 years) for civil works.
> 3. Provides ancillary services such as frequency regulation, voltage support, and black-start capability.
>
> **Disadvantage (1 mark):**
> 1. Requires specific geographical features (two reservoirs at different elevations), leading to high initial capital cost and environmental impact.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between impulse and reaction hydraulic turbines with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Impulse Turbine | Reaction Turbine |
|---|---|---|
| **Operating principle** | Kinetic energy of a jet drives the runner; pressure is atmospheric | Pressure drop occurs across the runner blades immersed in flow |
| **Pressure at inlet** | Atmospheric | Greater than atmospheric |
| **Examples** | Pelton wheel, Turgo | Francis, Kaplan, Propeller |
| **Head suitability** | High head ($> 300$ m) | Low to medium head |
| **KTU exam one-liner** | "Energy conversion is via kinetic energy only" | "Energy conversion is via pressure + kinetic energy" |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, With Internal Choice)

### Question A — Choice 1 [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **theoretical hydraulic power** available from a hydro plant with head $H$ and volumetric flow rate $Q$. Explain the role of **net head** and how it differs from gross head. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pumped hydro storage plant has the following specifications:
- Upper reservoir usable volume: $V = 2 \times 10^{6}$ m³
- Mean net head: $H = 150$ m
- Pump efficiency: $\eta_{pump} = 0.88$
- Turbine efficiency: $\eta_{turbine} = 0.90$
- Generator efficiency: $\eta_{gen} = 0.96$

Calculate:
1. The **total hydraulic energy stored** in the upper reservoir.
2. The **electrical energy input** required during pumping.
3. The **electrical energy output** available during generation.
4. The **overall round-trip efficiency**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Mass flow rate.**

$$
\dot{m} = \rho Q
$$

**Step 2 — Gravitational PE per unit mass.**

$$
\Delta PE / m = g H
$$

**Step 3 — Hydraulic power.**

$$
\boxed{P_{hyd} = \rho \, g \, Q \, H}
$$

**Step 4 — Net head discussion.**
The **gross head** $H_{gross} = Z_{upper} - Z_{lower}$ is the geometric elevation difference between the two reservoir surfaces. The **net head** $H_{net}$ subtracts all hydraulic losses:

$$
H_{net} = H_{gross} - h_{f,penstock} - h_{f,minor} - h_{f,trash-rack}
$$

These losses are typically **2%–8%** of gross head in well-designed penstocks. Using $H_{net}$ (not $H_{gross}$) in the power equation gives the actual available hydraulic power. **(Full 7 marks awarded for derivation + clear identification of each loss term.)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**

- $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- $V = 2 \times 10^{6}$ m³
- $H = 150$ m
- $\eta_{pump} = 0.88$
- $\eta_{turbine} = 0.90$
- $\eta_{gen} = 0.96$

**Step 1 — Total hydraulic energy stored.**

$$
E_{hyd} = \rho g V H = (1000)(9.81)(2 \times 10^{6})(150)
$$

$$
E_{hyd} = 2.943 \times 10^{12} \; J
$$

Convert to **MWh**:

$$
E_{hyd} = \frac{2.943 \times 10^{12}}{3.6 \times 10^{9}} \approx 817.5 \; MWh
$$

**Step 2 — Electrical input during pumping.**

$$
E_{input} = \frac{E_{hyd}}{\eta_{pump}} = \frac{817.5}{0.88} \approx 928.98 \; MWh
$$

**Step 3 — Electrical output during generation.**

$$
E_{output} = E_{hyd} \times \eta_{turbine} \times \eta_{gen} = 817.5 \times 0.90 \times 0.96
$$

$$
E_{output} \approx 706.32 \; MWh
$$

**Step 4 — Round-trip efficiency.**

$$
\eta_{RTE} = \eta_{pump} \times \eta_{turbine} \times \eta_{gen} = 0.88 \times 0.90 \times 0.96
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{RTE} \approx 0.7603 = 76.03\%}
$$

---

### Question B — Choice 2 [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **functional components** of a Pumped Hydro Storage System. Describe the **operating modes** of PHSS in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A run-of-river hydroelectric plant has a head of $25$ m and a flow rate of $80$ m³/s. The combined turbine-generator efficiency is $0.85$. Determine:
1. The **available hydraulic power**.
2. The **net electrical power output**.
3. If the plant operates for **12 hours per day**, calculate the **daily energy generation** in kWh. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Block Diagram (3 marks)** — Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the complete Mermaid architecture. The student should sketch:

> Upper Reservoir → Intake → Penstock → Surge Tank → Pump-Turbine ↔ Generator-Motor → Transformer → Grid → Lower Reservoir, with Governor & SCADA control loops.

**Operating Modes Description (4 marks):**

1. **Charging (Pumping) Mode:** During off-peak hours, when grid LMP (Locational Marginal Price) is low, surplus electrical energy is drawn from the grid. The **synchronous machine operates as a motor**, driving the reversible pump-turbine in pump mode. Water is lifted from the lower reservoir to the upper reservoir, storing gravitational potential energy.
2. **Generation (Turbining) Mode:** During peak hours, water is released from the upper reservoir through the penstock. The reversible machine acts as a turbine coupled to a generator, producing electricity delivered to the grid at high LMP.
3. **Standby / Synchronous Condenser Mode:** The unit can be decoupled from the shaft and used purely for **reactive power (VAR) support** to regulate grid voltage.
4. **Spinning Reserve Mode:** The unit remains synchronized but at zero or partial load, ready to ramp up to full output within **seconds to minutes** in response to grid contingencies.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $H = 25$ m, $Q = 80$ m³/s, $\eta_{tg} = 0.85$

**Step 1 — Hydraulic power.**

$$
P_{hyd} = \rho g Q H = (1000)(9.81)(80)(25)
$$

$$
P_{hyd} = 19.62 \times 10^{6} \; W = 19.62 \; MW
$$

**Step 2 — Net electrical power output.**

$$
P_{elec} = P_{hyd} \times \eta_{tg} = 19.62 \times 0.85
$$

$$
P_{elec} = 16.677 \; MW
$$

**Step 3 — Daily energy generation.**

$$
E_{daily} = P_{elec} \times t = 16.677 \; MW \times 12 \; h
$$

$$
\boxed{E_{daily} \approx 200.12 \; MWh = 200{,}124 \; kWh}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the pump efficiency** in round-trip efficiency calculations (students often quote $\eta_{RTE} = \eta_{gen}$ alone, losing 1 mark).
> 2. **Using $H_{gross}$ instead of $H_{net}$** in numerical problems, leading to overestimation of power (–2 marks typical).
> 3. **Unit conversion errors** between Joules and kWh / MWh — a recurring 1-mark deduction.
> 4. **Confusing impulse vs reaction turbine** characteristics; remember: Pelton = impulse; Francis & Kaplan = reaction.
> 5. **Not stating assumptions** (e.g., constant head, steady-state flow, neglect of minor losses) — losing marks for "incomplete solution."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Hydroelectric Energy Storage**

- **Core Equation (must memorize):** $P_{hyd} = \rho \, g \, Q \, H$ — universally applied in **every** hydro numerical.
- **Stored Energy Equation:** $E = \rho \, g \, V \, H$ — applies for PHSS capacity sizing.
- **Two key constants:** $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³ (water), $g = 9.81$ m/s² (gravity).
- **Net Head Formula:** $H_{net} = H_{gross} - h_{f,major} - h_{f,minor}$.
- **Round-Trip Efficiency:** $\eta_{RTE} = \eta_{pump} \times \eta_{turbine} \times \eta_{gen} \approx 75$–$82\%$.
- **Turbine Selection Rule of Thumb:**
    - High head ($> 300$ m) → **Pelton** (impulse).
    - Medium head ($30$–$300$ m) → **Francis** (reaction) — **most common for PHSS**.
    - Low head ($< 30$ m) → **Kaplan / Propeller** (reaction).
- **PHSS Operating Modes:** Pumping, Generation, Synchronous Condenser, Spinning Reserve.
- **Global Significance:** PHSS = **> 95%** of worldwide bulk electrical energy storage.
- **Storage Duration:** Typically **6 – 20 hours**, far exceeding electrochemical storage.
- **Lifetime:** Civil works **50 – 100 years**; electromechanical equipment **30 – 50 years**.
- **Discharge Duration Formula:** $t = V / Q$ — frequently tested as a 3-mark direct question.
- **Energy Unit Conversion:** $1 \; MWh = 3.6 \times 10^{9}$ J (must be committed to memory).
- **Major vs Minor Head Loss:** Major = pipe friction ($h_f = f \frac{L}{D} \frac{V^2}{2g}$); Minor = fittings, bends, valves.
- **Environmental Considerations:** Land submergence, ecological disruption to riverine fauna (fish migration), sedimentation management.
- **Modern Variants:** Underground PHSS, Seawater PHSS, Variable-speed PHSS (for improved partial-load efficiency).

---

## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITIONS & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Solar Thermal Technologies (STT)

**Formal Definition:**
Solar Thermal Technologies (STT) refer to engineered systems that capture and convert incident solar radiation into *usable thermal energy* (heat) rather than directly into electricity. When the captured heat is subsequently used to drive a thermodynamic cycle (Rankine, Brayton, or Stirling), the system is termed **Concentrated Solar Power (CSP)** or **Solar Thermal Electricity (STE)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> The four canonical CSP architectures are: (1) Parabolic Trough Collector (PTC), (2) Linear Fresnel Reflector (LFR), (3) Solar Power Tower / Central Receiver (SPT), and (4) Parabolic Dish / Dish-Stirling (PD). KTU questions frequently compare these on *concentration ratio*, *operating temperature*, and *storage compatibility*.

**Key Sub-classifications of Solar Thermal:**

| Sub-system | Operational Mechanism |
|---|---|
| **Low-Temperature Collectors** (< 100 °C) | Flat-plate & evacuated tube for water heating, space heating |
| **Medium-Temperature Collectors** (100 – 400 °C) | Parabolic trough, Linear Fresnel for process heat |
| **High-Temperature Collectors** (> 400 °C) | Solar tower & Dish-Stirling for power generation |
| **Solar Ponds** | Salinity-gradient based thermal collection & storage |

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine the sun as a giant, free, but *intermittent* gas stove. A solar thermal system is essentially a focused "kitchen" — concentrating sunlight through curved mirrors (the *concentrator*) onto a small, dark receiver (the *pot*). The "pot" holds a working fluid (water, oil, or molten salt) which gets heated. If we keep this "pot" very well insulated and large enough, we can store the heat and cook dinner long after sunset — this is precisely what Thermal Energy Storage (TES) does.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Concentration Ratio ($C$)** — defined as the ratio of the *effective aperture area* of the concentrator to the *area of the solar receiver*. A higher $C$ produces a higher receiver temperature but demands stricter tracking accuracy.
> $$C = \frac{A_{\text{aperture}}}{A_{\text{receiver}}}$$

### 1.2 Photovoltaics (PV)

**Formal Definition:**
Photovoltaics (PV) is the direct conversion of photons (incident solar radiation) into electrical energy through the *photovoltaic effect*, first observed by Becquerel in 1839 and operationally demonstrated in 1954 at Bell Labs using crystalline silicon. The active element is a semiconductor p-n junction (most commonly silicon).

**Key PV System Components:**

- **Solar Cell** — Basic unit; typical open-circuit voltage $\approx 0.6$ V for crystalline Si.
- **Module / Panel** — Series/parallel combination of cells (typically 60 or 72 cells).
- **String** — Series-connected modules to achieve desired DC voltage.
- **Array** — Parallel connection of strings to achieve desired power rating.
- **Power Conditioning Unit (PCU)** — Inverter + MPPT + protections.
- **Balance of System (BOS)** — Cables, mounting structures, junction boxes, earthing.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of a PV cell as a *one-way valve* for electrons. Photons strike the silicon, knocking electrons loose. A built-in electric field at the p-n junction acts like a one-way ramp — it lets these "knocked" electrons flow out through the external circuit (producing current) but blocks them from flowing back. No moving parts, no fuel, no emissions — just light → electricity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Test Conditions (STC) for PV Rating:** Irradiance $G = 1000 \text{ W/m}^2$, Cell temperature $T_{\text{cell}} = 25\,^\circ\text{C}$, Air mass AM 1.5. Always quote a panel's Watt-peak (Wp) under STC.

### 1.3 Storage Role in Isolated (Off-Grid) Power Systems

**Formal Definition:**
An *isolated* or *islanded* power system is an electrical network that is **physically disconnected** from any utility grid and is responsible for self-balancing generation, storage, and load *in real time*. Such systems almost always pair Renewable Energy Sources (RES) — typically solar PV, wind, or micro-hydro — with Energy Storage Systems (ESS) and a conventional back-up (usually diesel genset) to form a *hybrid renewable system*.

**Core Functions of Storage in an Isolated System:**

1. **Energy Time-Shift** — Store daytime solar/wind energy for night-time use.
2. **Load Following** — Match fluctuating load demand.
3. **Frequency & Voltage Stabilization** — Provide inertia and reactive support.
4. **Diesel Off-Loading / Fuel Displacement** — Reduce diesel runtime and fuel cost.
5. **Black-Start Capability** — Restore the microgrid after a complete shutdown.
6. **Renewable Penetration Smoothing** — Buffer the *intermittency* and *variability* of RES.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine you live on a remote island with a single rainwater tank. The rain is unpredictable — it might pour for an hour and then stay dry for days. The rainwater tank (storage) lets you use water *whenever* you want, decoupling the *rate at which water falls* from the *rate at which you drink*. Similarly, in an off-grid system, the battery bank decouples the *intermittent generation* of solar/wind from the *instantaneous demand* of loads.

### 1.4 Storage Role in Grid-Connected Power Systems

**Formal Definition:**
A *grid-connected* (or *utility-interactive*) renewable power system is one in which Renewable Energy Sources (RES) are tied to the main utility grid via a Point of Common Coupling (PCC). The grid acts as an *infinite sink/source*, but as RES penetration rises, storage becomes critical to address technical, economic, and regulatory issues such as *reverse power flow*, *voltage rise*, *frequency deviation*, and *curtailment*.

**Core Functions of Storage in a Grid-Connected System:**

| Function | Description |
|---|---|
| **Peak Shaving** | Discharge during utility peak hours to flatten the duck curve |
| **Load Leveling** | Charge off-peak, discharge peak — improves asset utilization |
| **Frequency Regulation** | Fast response to Area Control Error (ACE) |
| **Voltage Support** | Reactive power injection at PCC |
| **Spinning / Non-Spinning Reserve** | Stand-by capacity for contingencies |
| **Renewable Curtailment Mitigation** | Store excess RE that would otherwise be curtailed |
| **Power Quality Improvement** | Harmonic filtering, flicker mitigation |
| **Transmission Deferral** | Reduce need for new T&D infrastructure |
| **Arbitrage** | Energy time-shift to exploit time-of-use (ToU) tariffs |

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of the national grid as a busy highway. Solar and wind power plants are like *delivery trucks* that arrive erratically — sometimes too many at noon (causing a traffic jam of electrons), and almost none at night. The grid-connected battery is like a *multi-storey parking garage* next to the highway: it absorbs the excess trucks at noon and releases them smoothly at evening rush hour, keeping traffic flowing. The grid operator (system coordinator) pays for this service — a real revenue stream called *Frequency Regulation Ancillary Service*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Conceptual Distinction:** In an *isolated* system, storage is **mandatory** (the system cannot function without it). In a *grid-connected* system, storage is *optional but increasingly necessary* — its role shifts from *enabler* to *optimizer* and *stabilizer*.

## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundations of Solar Thermal Technologies

**2.1.1 Parabolic Trough Collector (PTC)**

A PTC consists of a *parabolic-shaped* mirror that focuses parallel solar rays onto a *linear receiver tube* placed along the focal line. A Heat Transfer Fluid (HTF) — typically *synthetic oil* (Therminol VP-1), molten salt (Hitec/Hitec XL), or increasingly *direct steam generation* (DSG) — circulates through the receiver, absorbing concentrated heat.

- **Concentration Ratio:** $C \approx 15 - 80$
- **Operating Temperature:** $300 - 400\,^\circ\text{C}$
- **Tracking:** Single-axis (typically N-S aligned, E-W tracking)
- **Commercial Maturity:** Highest among CSP technologies (e.g., SEGS, Noor I-III, Solana)
- **Storage Compatibility:** Excellent — molten-salt two-tank TES is the standard

**2.1.2 Linear Fresnel Reflector (LFR)**

LFR uses a *series of nearly-flat* (or slightly curved) mirror strips, all tilted to focus sunlight onto a *fixed linear absorber* mounted above. The optical efficiency is lower than PTC, but the structural simplicity, wind-load tolerance, and use of *boiling water* in the receiver make it economically attractive.

- **Concentration Ratio:** $C \approx 10 - 40$
- **Operating Temperature:** $250 - 350\,^\circ\text{C}$
- **Tracking:** Single-axis with secondary fixed reflector

**2.1.3 Solar Power Tower (Central Receiver System, CRS)**

An array of *two-axis tracking* heliostats (large, flat or slightly curved mirrors) reflects sunlight onto a *single central receiver* mounted atop a tower. The receiver can heat *molten salt*, *air*, *particles*, or generate *superheated steam* directly.

- **Concentration Ratio:** $C \approx 300 - 1000$
- **Operating Temperature:** $500 - 1000\,^\circ\text{C}$
- **Thermal Efficiency:** Highest of all CSP (>40 % net annual in some plants)
- **Storage:** Two-tank molten salt (e.g., Gemasolar — 15 h storage, 24/7 dispatchable)

**2.1.4 Parabolic Dish-Stirling**

A *parabolic-shaped dish* concentrates sunlight onto a *point-focus receiver* coupled to a *Stirling engine* at the focal point. Highest efficiency of any solar technology (peak $\eta \approx 31.25\%$, RECS test).

- **Concentration Ratio:** $C \approx 1000 - 3000$
- **Operating Temperature:** $650 - 850\,^\circ\text{C}$
- **Storage:** Integrated *thermal battery* (e.g., latent heat in metallic phase-change materials)

**2.1.5 Why 'C' and 'T' Matter: The Thermodynamic Justification**

The *Carnot efficiency* upper-bounds any heat engine:
$$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{T_{\text{cold}}}{T_{\text{hot}}}$$

Therefore, to maximize electrical conversion, $T_{\text{hot}}$ must be as high as practicable. Higher $T_{\text{hot}}$ requires higher concentration ratio $C$ and a more accurate tracking system. This is *why* dish and tower systems have higher electrical efficiencies than troughs.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Conceptual Hook:** Higher $T_{\text{hot}}$ also requires *more advanced* thermal storage media — for $T \le 565\,^\circ\text{C}$, molten nitrate salts (60% NaNO₃ + 40% KNO₃) are the workhorse. For $T > 565\,^\circ\text{C}$, we must use *chloride salts*, *carbonates*, or *solid particles*.

### 2.2 Theoretical Foundations of Photovoltaics

**2.2.1 The Single-Diode Model**

The electrical equivalent circuit of a PV cell is the *single-diode model* (also called the *five-parameter model*):

The *photocurrent* $I_{\text{ph}}$ is generated proportionally to incident irradiance $G$:
$$I_{\text{ph}} = I_{\text{ph,STC}} \cdot \frac{G}{G_{\text{STC}}}$$

The *diode current* follows the Shockley equation:
$$I_{\text{d}} = I_{\text{o}} \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{q(V + I R_s)}{n k T}\right) - 1\right]$$

The terminal I-V relationship of the PV cell:
$$I = I_{\text{ph}} - I_{\text{o}} \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{q(V + I R_s)}{n k T}\right) - 1\right] - \frac{V + I R_s}{R_{\text{sh}}}$$

Where:
- $I$ = terminal current (A)
- $V$ = terminal voltage (V)
- $I_{\text{o}}$ = diode reverse saturation current (A)
- $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$ (electron charge)
- $k = 1.381 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J/K}$ (Boltzmann constant)
- $n$ = ideality factor (1 – 2 for Si)
- $R_s$ = series resistance ($\Omega$) — should be as small as possible
- $R_{\text{sh}}$ = shunt resistance ($\Omega$) — should be as large as possible

**2.2.2 Maximum Power Point (MPP) and MPPT**

The PV I-V curve has a *unique* point where the product $P = V \cdot I$ is maximized. The corresponding $(V_{\text{mpp}}, I_{\text{mpp}})$ is the *Maximum Power Point*. To extract maximum energy under varying irradiance and temperature, an *MPPT algorithm* is implemented in the inverter's DC-DC stage.

**Common MPPT Algorithms:**

| Algorithm | Principle | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| **P&O (Perturb & Observe)** | Perturb V, observe $\Delta P$ | Simple, no sensors | Oscillates around MPP |
| **Incremental Conductance** | dI/dV = -I/V at MPP | Faster tracking, less oscillation | Complex, noisy at low irradiance |
| **Fractional Open-Circuit Voltage** | $V_{\text{mpp}} \approx k_1 \cdot V_{\text{oc}}$ | Very simple | Periodic open-circuit required |
| **Fuzzy Logic / Neural Network** | AI-based, trained | Best for partial shading | Needs training data |

**2.2.3 Fill Factor (FF) and Conversion Efficiency ($\eta$)**

$$\text{FF} = \frac{V_{\text{mpp}} \cdot I_{\text{mpp}}}{V_{\text{oc}} \cdot I_{\text{sc}}}$$

$$\eta = \frac{V_{\text{oc}} \cdot I_{\text{sc}} \cdot \text{FF}}{G \cdot A}$$

For a high-quality crystalline Si cell: $V_{\text{oc}} \approx 0.65$ V, $I_{\text{sc}} \approx 40 \text{ mA/cm}^2$, $\text{FF} \approx 0.78$, $\eta \approx 18 - 22\%$.

**2.2.4 Effect of Irradiance and Temperature (KTU Favorite)**

- **Irradiance (G) ↑** → $I_{\text{ph}}$ rises linearly, $V_{\text{oc}}$ rises logarithmically → Power ↑
- **Temperature (T) ↑** → $I_{\text{ph}}$ rises marginally, $V_{\text{oc}}$ *falls* (≈ –2 mV/°C per cell for Si) → Power ↓

**Empirical Temperature Coefficients for Si:**
$$P_{\max}(T) = P_{\max,\text{STC}} \left[ 1 + \gamma_p (T_{\text{cell}} - 25) \right]$$

where $\gamma_p \approx -0.4 \,\%/\!^\circ\text{C}$ for crystalline silicon.

### 2.3 Theoretical Foundations: Storage in Isolated Power Systems

**2.3.1 System Architecture**

A typical *isolated hybrid renewable system* contains:

- **RES Generation:** PV array, wind turbine(s), micro-hydro
- **Conventional Back-up:** Diesel genset (or biomass gasifier)
- **ESS:** Battery bank (lead-acid, Li-ion, or flow); sometimes supercapacitors for short bursts
- **Power Electronics:** Bi-directional inverter, charge controller, MPPT
- **Control Hierarchy:**
  - *Primary control*: Local (droop-based, ms-scale)
  - *Secondary control*: Microgrid EMS (seconds-minutes, SoC balancing)
  - *Tertiary control*: Economic dispatch (minutes-hours)

**2.3.2 Battery Sizing for Off-Grid PV Systems**

The required battery capacity $C_{\text{bat}}$ (in Ah) for an off-grid system with autonomy $N$ days is:

$$C_{\text{bat}} = \frac{E_{\text{daily}} \cdot N}{\eta_{\text{batt}} \cdot \eta_{\text{inv}} \cdot V_{\text{bus}} \cdot \text{DoD}_{\max}}$$

Where:
- $E_{\text{daily}}$ = daily energy demand (Wh)
- $N$ = days of autonomy (typically 2 – 5 days for remote sites)
- $\eta_{\text{batt}}$ = battery round-trip efficiency (~ 0.85 for lead-acid, ~ 0.95 for Li-ion)
- $\eta_{\text{inv}}$ = inverter efficiency (~ 0.93 – 0.97)
- $V_{\text{bus}}$ = DC bus voltage (e.g., 48 V)
- $\text{DoD}_{\max}$ = maximum allowable depth of discharge (0.5 for lead-acid, 0.8 – 0.9 for Li-ion)

**2.3.3 Penetration Index**

A critical KTU concept — defines the *degree* to which RES (with storage) displaces conventional generation:

$$\text{Conventional Penetration Index (CPI)} = \frac{E_{\text{conv,gen}} - E_{\text{conv,gen,RES-on}}}{E_{\text{load}}}$$

$$\text{Renewable Penetration Index (RPI)} = \frac{E_{\text{RES-gen}}}{E_{\text{load}}}$$

$$\text{Renewable Fraction (RF)} = \frac{E_{\text{RES-gen}}}{E_{\text{RES-gen}} + E_{\text{conv,gen}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High RE Penetration Caveat:** When RPI > 1, the system becomes *vulnerable* to RE droughts (cloudy, windless days). Storage sizing must account for the *worst-case weather week*, not the average day — this is a frequent KTU 14-mark question.

### 2.4 Theoretical Foundations: Storage in Grid-Connected Systems

**2.4.1 The "Duck Curve" — KTU Must-Know**

As solar PV penetration increases on a grid, the *net load* (load – solar) curve deforms into a *duck shape*:

- Mid-day: Net load drops sharply (high PV)
- Sunset (~6 – 8 PM): Net load *rises rapidly* (PV fading, demand peaking)

This *ramp rate* (typically 1 – 1.5 GW per hour in California) threatens grid stability. **Storage directly addresses this by:**

1. Charging mid-day (absorbing excess PV) → flattening the duck's belly
2. Discharging 5 – 9 PM (covering the sunset ramp) → smoothing the duck's neck

**2.4.2 Grid Services Provided by Storage**

**Frequency Regulation (Primary Response):**

The rate of change of frequency is governed by the *swing equation*:
$$\frac{2 H}{f_0} \frac{df}{dt} = P_{\text{mech}} - P_{\text{elec}}$$

- $H$ = inertia constant (seconds)
- $f_0$ = nominal frequency (50 Hz India / 60 Hz US)
- $P_{\text{mech}}, P_{\text{elec}}$ = per-unit mechanical input and electrical load

When a generator trips or load surges, $P_{\text{elec}} > P_{\text{mech}}$, and $f$ drops. Battery energy storage can inject *fast frequency response* in $< 200$ ms — far faster than spinning reserves (1 – 10 s).

**Voltage Support (Reactive Power):**

The reactive power-voltage relationship at a bus:
$$Q = \frac{V}{X}(V_{\text{recv}} - V \cos\delta)$$

Modern battery inverters (e.g., SMA, Tesla Powerpack, Fluence) can provide $\pm 0.8$ to $\pm 1.0$ *power factor* range — meaning they can supply/absorb reactive power without active power, supporting the grid voltage.

**2.4.3 Levelized Cost of Storage (LCOS) — KTU Economic Tool**

$$\text{LCOS} = \frac{\sum_t \left( C_{\text{cap}} + C_{\text{O\&M}} + C_{\text{charging}} \right) / (1+r)^t}{\sum_t Q_{\text{discharge}} / (1+r)^t}$$

Where:
- $C_{\text{cap}}$ = annualized capital cost
- $C_{\text{O\&M}}$ = operation and maintenance cost
- $C_{\text{charging}}$ = cost of energy to charge the storage
- $r$ = discount rate
- $Q_{\text{discharge}}$ = annual energy discharged (kWh)

Typical 2024 LCOS values:
- Li-ion (4-hr): **$200 – 350 / MWh**
- Lead-acid (4-hr): **$300 – 500 / MWh**
- Pumped hydro (8-hr): **$120 – 200 / MWh**
- Vanadium redox flow (8-hr): **$250 – 400 / MWh**

### 2.5 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Equation | Physical Meaning | Units / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C = A_{\text{aperture}}/A_{\text{receiver}}$ | Concentration ratio of CSP | 10 (LFR) – 3000 (Dish) |
| 2 | $\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - T_c / T_h$ | Max theoretical heat-to-work efficiency | 0 – 1 |
| 3 | $I_{\text{ph}} = I_{\text{ph,STC}} \cdot (G/G_{\text{STC}})$ | Photocurrent vs irradiance | Linear in G |
| 4 | $I = I_{\text{ph}} - I_{\text{o}} \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{q(V+IR_s)}{nkT}\right) - 1\right] - (V+IR_s)/R_{\text{sh}}$ | Single-diode PV model | A, V |
| 5 | $\text{FF} = (V_{\text{mpp}} I_{\text{mpp}})/(V_{\text{oc}} I_{\text{sc}})$ | "Squareness" of I-V curve | 0.7 – 0.85 (Si) |
| 6 | $\eta = V_{\text{oc}} I_{\text{sc}} \text{FF} / (G \cdot A)$ | PV conversion efficiency | 0.18 – 0.24 (Si) |
| 7 | $P_{\max}(T) = P_{\max,\text{STC}} [1 + \gamma_p(T-25)]$ | Power derating with temperature | $\gamma_p \approx -0.004/\!^\circ\text{C}$ |
| 8 | $C_{\text{bat}} = E_{\text{daily}} N / (\eta_{\text{batt}} \eta_{\text{inv}} V_{\text{bus}} \text{DoD})$ | Battery capacity sizing | Ah |
| 9 | $\text{RF} = E_{\text{RES}} / (E_{\text{RES}} + E_{\text{conv}})$ | Renewable fraction | 0 – 1 |
| 10 | $(2H/f_0)(df/dt) = P_{\text{mech}} - P_{\text{elec}}$ | Swing equation for frequency | per unit |
| 11 | $\text{LCOS} = \sum C_t (1+r)^{-t} / \sum Q_t (1+r)^{-t}$ | Levelized Cost of Storage | \$/MWh |
| 12 | $E_{\text{TES}} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat storage capacity | J (or kWh) |
| 13 | $E_{\text{TES}} = m \cdot L$ | Latent heat (PCM) storage capacity | J |
| 14 | $\eta_{\text{storage}} = E_{\text{out}} / E_{\text{in}}$ | Round-trip efficiency | 0.60 (Pumped) – 0.95 (Li-ion) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Equation 4 is implemented in tools like *PVsyst*, *SAM (System Advisor Model)*, and *PSpice* to predict PV array output. Equation 8 governs *IEEE 1562* and *IEC 62548* design guidelines for off-grid PV. Equation 14 is the *figure of merit* that any new battery chemistry (solid-state, sodium-ion, iron-air) must beat to displace Li-ion.

## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: PV Maximum Power Point Condition

We want to maximize $P = V \cdot I$ with respect to $V$.

**Step 1 — Write power delivered to external load:**
$$P = V \cdot I$$

For a *simplified* PV model (neglecting $R_s$ and $R_{sh}$ for clarity — the *ideal model*):
$$I = I_{\text{ph}} - I_{\text{o}} \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{nkT}\right) - 1\right]$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**
$$P(V) = V \cdot I_{\text{ph}} - V \cdot I_{\text{o}} \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{nkT}\right) - 1\right]$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate $P$ with respect to $V$ and set to zero (necessary condition for extremum):**
$$\frac{dP}{dV} = I + V \frac{dI}{dV} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Compute $dI/dV$ from the ideal diode model:**
$$\frac{dI}{dV} = -I_{\text{o}} \cdot \frac{q}{nkT} \exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{nkT}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into the extremum condition:**
$$I_{\text{ph}} - I_{\text{o}} \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{qV_{\text{mpp}}}{nkT}\right) - 1\right] - V_{\text{mpp}} \cdot I_{\text{o}} \cdot \frac{q}{nkT} \exp\!\left(\frac{qV_{\text{mpp}}}{nkT}\right) = 0$$

**Step 6 — Define the thermal voltage:**
$$V_t = \frac{nkT}{q} \quad (\approx 25.85 \text{ mV at } 300 \text{ K})$$

**Step 7 — Rewrite in compact form (the implicit MPP condition):**
$$I_{\text{mpp}} = I_{\text{o}} \cdot \frac{V_{\text{mpp}}}{V_t} \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{\text{mpp}}}{V_t}\right)$$

This is the *implicit* transcendental equation solved iteratively (Newton-Raphson) to obtain $V_{\text{mpp}}$ for any given $G$ and $T$. Once $V_{\text{mpp}}$ is known, $I_{\text{mpp}}$ follows from the I-V relation, and the maximum power is $P_{\max} = V_{\text{mpp}} \cdot I_{\text{mpp}}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** Many Module 3 questions ask *"Why is MPPT necessary?"* — the answer is hidden in Step 1 – 7: the PV's internal characteristic is non-linear, and only a *specific* $(V, I)$ pair maximises extraction. Any operating point other than MPP wastes free solar energy.

### 3.2 Derivation: Solar Thermal Storage Capacity (Two-Tank Molten Salt)

**Step 1 — Mass of salt required in *hot* tank:**
The energy stored = mass × specific heat × temperature swing:
$$E_{\text{hot}} = m_{\text{salt}} \cdot c_{p,\text{salt}} \cdot (T_{\text{hot}} - T_{\text{cold}})$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the required salt mass:**
$$m_{\text{salt}} = \frac{E_{\text{hot}}}{c_{p,\text{salt}} \cdot (T_{\text{hot}} - T_{\text{cold}})}$$

**Step 3 — Plug in standard values for Solar-Two / Gemasolar systems:**
- $c_{p,\text{salt}} = 1.55 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$ (60% NaNO₃ + 40% KNO₃)
- $T_{\text{hot}} = 565\,^\circ\text{C}$
- $T_{\text{cold}} = 290\,^\circ\text{C}$
- $E_{\text{hot}} = 100 \text{ MWh}_{\text{th}} = 3.6 \times 10^{11} \text{ J}$

**Step 4 — Substitute and evaluate:**
$$\Delta T = 565 - 290 = 275 \text{ K}$$

$$m_{\text{salt}} = \frac{3.6 \times 10^{11} \text{ J}}{1550 \text{ J/(kg·K)} \cdot 275 \text{ K}}$$

$$m_{\text{salt}} = \frac{3.6 \times 10^{11}}{4.2625 \times 10^{5}} = 8.45 \times 10^{5} \text{ kg}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to more intuitive units:**
$$m_{\text{salt}} \approx 845 \text{ tonnes of molten salt per 100 MWh}_{\text{th}} \text{ storage}$$

**Step 6 — Tank volume (using salt density $\rho \approx 1900 \text{ kg/m}^3$):**
$$V_{\text{tank}} = \frac{m_{\text{salt}}}{\rho} = \frac{8.45 \times 10^{5}}{1900} \approx 445 \text{ m}^3$$

This is why CSP-TES plants are visually *enormous* — each tank is roughly the size of a small lake.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Real-World Hook:** The Gemasolar plant in Spain uses ~ 8,500 tonnes of salt to provide 15 hours of full-load storage, enabling *24/7 dispatchable* solar power. The molten salt also acts as the HTF, eliminating the need for separate oil-and-salt systems used in older plants like Solar-Two (1996).

### 3.3 Derivation: Battery Sizing for Off-Grid PV System (Worked Example)

**Given:**
- Daily energy demand: $E_{\text{daily}} = 10 \text{ kWh/day}$
- Autonomy: $N = 3$ days
- DC bus voltage: $V_{\text{bus}} = 48$ V
- Battery: Lead-acid with $\eta_{\text{batt}} = 0.85$, $\text{DoD}_{\max} = 0.50$
- Inverter: $\eta_{\text{inv}} = 0.93$

**Step 1 — Apply the sizing formula:**
$$C_{\text{bat}} = \frac{E_{\text{daily}} \cdot N}{\eta_{\text{batt}} \cdot \eta_{\text{inv}} \cdot V_{\text{bus}} \cdot \text{DoD}_{\max}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values:**
$$C_{\text{bat}} = \frac{10 \text{ kWh} \times 3}{0.85 \times 0.93 \times 48 \text{ V} \times 0.50}$$

**Step 3 — Numerator:**
$$10 \times 3 = 30 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 4 — Denominator (with unit conversion: kWh = 1000 Wh):**
$$0.85 \times 0.93 \times 48 \times 0.50 = 0.7905 \times 24 = 18.972 \text{ V·(unitless)} \text{ — convert:}$$

Better to write in Wh: denominator = $0.85 \times 0.93 \times 48 \times 0.50 = 18.97$ (treating kWh in denominator is consistent — keep units consistent in numerator & denominator):

$$C_{\text{bat}} = \frac{30}{0.85 \times 0.93 \times 48 \times 0.50} = \frac{30}{18.97} \approx 1.581 \text{ kAh}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to Ah:**
$$C_{\text{bat}} = 1.581 \text{ kAh} = 1581 \text{ Ah at 48 V}$$

**Step 6 — Practical battery configuration:**
Using 12 V, 200 Ah lead-acid batteries (C/20 rate):
- Series per string: $48/12 = 4$ batteries
- Ampere-hours per string: 200 Ah
- Number of parallel strings: $1581 / 200 \approx 7.9 \to 8$ strings

**Total batteries:** $4 \times 8 = 32$ batteries of 12 V, 200 Ah

**Step 7 — PV array sizing (for completeness):**
With a *peak-sun-hours* PSH = 5 h/day and system derating factor $f_d = 0.77$:
$$P_{\text{PV}} = \frac{E_{\text{daily}}}{\text{PSH} \cdot f_d} = \frac{10}{5 \times 0.77} \approx 2.6 \text{ kWp}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** A common student mistake is to use $\text{DoD} = 1.0$ (full discharge) which *destroys* lead-acid batteries in < 100 cycles. The DoD constraint is the *single most important* design decision affecting battery life.

### 3.4 Derivation: Grid-Connected PV + Storage Sizing for Peak Shaving

**Given:**
- Commercial building peak demand: $P_{\text{peak}} = 500$ kW
- Mid-day PV generation: $P_{\text{PV,peak}} = 300$ kW
- Utility ToU tariff: peak hours 6 PM – 10 PM, peak charge ₹12/kWh; off-peak ₹6/kWh
- Battery round-trip efficiency: $\eta_{\text{BESS}} = 0.90$

**Step 1 — Net peak demand *without* storage:**
$$P_{\text{net,peak}} = 500 - 300 = 200 \text{ kW (at midday, PV is on)}$$

But 6 PM peak has no PV (sunset), so the *real* utility peak is **500 kW** from 6–10 PM.

**Step 2 — Determine target shaved peak:**
Suppose we want to limit grid import during 6–10 PM to $P_{\text{target}} = 350$ kW:
$$P_{\text{BESS,discharge}} = 500 - 350 = 150 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — Required energy capacity (for 4-hour peak window):**
$$E_{\text{BESS}} = P_{\text{BESS}} \times t = 150 \text{ kW} \times 4 \text{ h} = 600 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 4 — Account for round-trip efficiency (extra charging needed):**
$$E_{\text{charge}} = \frac{E_{\text{discharge}}}{\eta_{\text{BESS}}} = \frac{600}{0.90} = 667 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 5 — Daily economic benefit (arbitrage + demand charge avoidance):**
Assume demand charge = ₹500/kW/month, energy saving:
- Energy arbitrage: $(12 - 6) \times 600 = 3600$ ₹/day
- Demand charge saving: $150 \times 500 = 75{,}000$ ₹/month
- Combined monthly benefit: ~ ₹75,000 + 30 × 3600 = **₹1,83,000/month**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Real-World Hook:** The same BESS, when coordinated for *frequency regulation*, can earn ₹1.5 – 2.5 Lakh/month in *Deviation Settlement Mechanism* (DSM) payouts in India, on top of energy arbitrage. This *value stacking* is what makes modern grid storage projects financially viable.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Solar Thermal (CSP) with Thermal Energy Storage

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[SUN<br/>Solar Irradiance] --> B1[Heliostat Field /<br/>Parabolic Mirrors]
    B1 --> C1[Solar Receiver<br/>on Tower Trough Dish]
    C1 --> D1[Hot HTF Loop<br/>Molten Salt or Oil]
    D1 --> E1[Hot Storage Tank<br/>T hot approx 565 C]
    E1 --> F1[Cold Storage Tank<br/>T cold approx 290 C]
    F1 --> D1
    E1 --> G1[Steam Generator<br/>Heat Exchanger]
    G1 --> H1[RANKINE CYCLE<br/>Turbine Generator]
    H1 --> I1[Step Up Transformer]
    I1 --> J1[GRID / Industrial Load]
    H1 --> K1[Condenser<br/>Cooling Tower]
    K1 --> G1
    E1 -. Backup path<br/>15 h storage .-> H1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The *solid arrows* are the normal operating path; the *dashed arrow* is the *storage discharge* path that lets the plant generate electricity even at night — this is the very essence of *dispatchable* solar power.

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Grid-Connected PV System with Battery Storage

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A2[SUN] --> B2[PV Array<br/>DC 600 to 1000 V]
    B2 --> C2[DC DC Converter<br/>with MPPT]
    C2 --> D2[DC BUS 600 V]
    D2 --> E2[Battery Management<br/>System BMS]
    E2 --> F2[Li ion Battery Bank<br/>100 to 500 kWh]
    D2 --> G2[Bi directional Inverter<br/>with Anti islanding]
    G2 --> H2[AC BUS 415 V 50 Hz]
    H2 --> I2[Step up Transformer<br/>11 kV 415 V]
    I2 --> J2[Point of Common<br/>Coupling PCC]
    J2 --> K2[UTILITY GRID<br/>33 kV / 11 kV]
    J2 --> L2[Local Loads<br/>Lighting HVAC]
    M2[Smart Meter] --> J2
    N2[SCADA EMS<br/>Energy Management] --> G2
    N2 --> E2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Specific Insight:** The Bi-directional Inverter is the *heart* of grid-tied storage. In *grid-tied mode* (normal), it exports PV and discharges battery to offset peak loads. In *islanded mode* (grid outage), it must detect loss of mains within < 100 ms (anti-islanding) and seamlessly switch to *Voltage Source Inverter* (VSI) mode to feed the local loads.

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Off-Grid (Isolated) Hybrid PV-Wind-Diesel System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A3[PV Array] --> D3[DC BUS]
    B3[Wind Turbine] --> C3[AC DC Rectifier]
    C3 --> D3
    D3 --> E3[MPPT Charge<br/>Controller]
    E3 --> F3[Battery Bank<br/>Lead acid or Li ion]
    D3 --> G3[Bi directional<br/>Inverter VSI Mode]
    G3 --> H3[AC Load Bus<br/>415 V 50 Hz]
    H3 --> I3[Critical Loads<br/>Hospital Telecom]
    H3 --> J3[Non Critical Loads<br/>Shed During Low SoC]
    K3[Diesel Generator<br/>Back up] --> H3
    L3[Microgrid EMS<br/>SCADA PLC] --> E3
    L3 --> G3
    L3 --> K3
    F3 --> M3[SoC Monitor]
    M3 --> L3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The Microgrid EMS (Energy Management System) is the *brain* — it decides, every 1 – 5 minutes, whether to (a) charge batteries from PV/Wind, (b) start the diesel genset if SoC < threshold, (c) shed non-critical loads, or (d) export to grid (if grid-tied). The control logic is typically rule-based or optimization-based (linear programming, mixed-integer LP).

### 4.4 Mermaid Concept Map: Functional Roles of Storage in Grid vs Isolated Systems

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ROOT[ENERGY STORAGE<br/>in POWER SYSTEMS]
    ROOT --> P1[Isolated Off Grid]
    ROOT --> P2[Grid Connected]
    P1 --> R1[Energy Time Shift<br/>Day to Night]
    P1 --> R2[Load Following]
    P1 --> R3[Black Start]
    P1 --> R4[Diesel Displacement<br/>Fuel Savings]
    P1 --> R5[Autonomy Buffer<br/>3 to 5 Days]
    P2 --> S1[Peak Shaving<br/>Duck Curve]
    P2 --> S2[Frequency Regulation<br/>Primary Response]
    P2 --> S3[Voltage Support<br/>Reactive Power]
    P2 --> S4[Curtailment<br/>Mitigation]
    P2 --> S5[Arbitrage<br/>ToU Tariffs]
    P2 --> S6[Spinning Reserve]
    P2 --> S7[Transmission Deferral]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Comparative Analysis (Exam-Ready):** In *isolated* systems, storage is **survival-critical** (R1, R5). In *grid-connected* systems, storage is **value-critical** (S1 – S7). This distinction is the *most important* conceptual takeaway from Module 3.
