## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
*List the four major Concentrated Solar Power (CSP) technologies. Compare them on the basis of concentration ratio and operating temperature.*

**Model Answer:**

The four major CSP technologies are:
1. **Parabolic Trough Collector (PTC)**
2. **Linear Fresnel Reflector (LFR)**
3. **Solar Power Tower (SPT) / Central Receiver System**
4. **Parabolic Dish (Dish-Stirling)**

| Technology | Concentration Ratio | Operating Temperature |
|---|---|---|
| LFR | 10 – 40 | 250 – 350 °C |
| PTC | 15 – 80 | 300 – 400 °C |
| SPT | 300 – 1000 | 500 – 1000 °C |
| Dish-Stirling | 1000 – 3000 | 650 – 850 °C |

> Higher concentration ratio enables higher operating temperature, which directly improves Carnot efficiency.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
*Define Fill Factor (FF) of a PV cell. What is its typical range for crystalline silicon?*

**Model Answer:**

The Fill Factor is the ratio of the maximum obtainable power to the product of short-circuit current and open-circuit voltage of a PV cell:

$$\text{FF} = \frac{V_{\text{mpp}} \cdot I_{\text{mpp}}}{V_{\text{oc}} \cdot I_{\text{sc}}}$$

It is a measure of the "squareness" of the I-V curve. For high-quality crystalline silicon PV cells, FF lies in the range **0.70 to 0.85** (typically ~ 0.78). A higher FF indicates lower internal losses ($R_s$ small, $R_{sh}$ large). FF directly determines the conversion efficiency: $\eta = \text{FF} \cdot (V_{\text{oc}} I_{\text{sc}})/(G \cdot A)$.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**Q3(a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Explain the principle of operation of a Parabolic Trough Concentrator with a neat sketch. Discuss its limitations.*

**Model Answer:**

**Principle:**
A Parabolic Trough Concentrator (PTC) is a *line-focusing* solar thermal collector. The reflector is a *parabolic-shaped* mirror that focuses parallel rays of sunlight onto a *linear receiver tube* placed along its *focal line*. A Heat Transfer Fluid (HTF) — typically synthetic oil or molten salt — flows through the receiver, absorbing the concentrated solar energy and reaching temperatures of **300 – 400 °C**.

**Construction:**
- **Reflector:** Parabolic-shaped, glass or aluminium, with solar-weighted reflectance of ~ 0.93.
- **Receiver tube:** Outer glass envelope (for vacuum insulation) with selective coating on the inner absorber tube.
- **Tracking:** Single-axis, tracking the sun's daily east-to-west motion (seasonal tilt adjusted).
- **Concentration ratio:** Typically 15 – 80 (peak flux ~ 50 – 100 W/cm²).
- **HTF:** Therminol VP-1 (synthetic oil) up to 400 °C, or molten nitrate salts for direct storage.

**Working Cycle:**
1. Sunlight strikes the parabolic mirror.
2. Mirrors reflect and concentrate rays onto the receiver tube at the focal line.
3. HTF absorbs heat (e.g., from 290 °C cold to 390 °C hot).
4. Hot HTF passes through a heat exchanger to generate steam.
5. Steam drives a Rankine turbine connected to a generator.
6. Optionally, hot HTF can be routed to a *thermal storage tank* (TES) for night-time operation.

**Limitations:**
1. **Concentration ratio limited to ~ 80** — caps operating temperature at ~ 400 °C, restricting Rankine cycle efficiency.
2. **Wind sensitivity** — large trough structures are vulnerable to high wind, requiring stow-position.
3. **Optical losses** — end losses, cosine losses, tracking errors reduce effective concentration.
4. **HTF degradation** — synthetic oil decomposes above 400 °C, restricting temperature ceiling.
5. **Only line-focusing** — must track in one axis only; cannot fully utilize diffuse radiation.
6. **Large land footprint** — solar field area is typically 2 – 3 times the aperture area to avoid shading.
7. **High capital cost** — vacuum receiver tubes and tracking systems are expensive.

**Conclusion:** Despite limitations, PTC is the most commercially mature CSP technology with the largest installed capacity worldwide.

---

**Q3(b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Discuss the role of Energy Storage in isolated (off-grid) renewable power systems. List the key functions of storage and the criteria for battery selection in such systems.*

**Model Answer:**

**Role of Storage in Isolated Power Systems:**

In an *isolated* system, the renewable sources (solar PV, wind, micro-hydro) are *intermittent* and *non-dispatchable*. The storage system is **mandatory** to decouple generation from demand and ensure reliable power supply.

**Key Functions:**
1. **Energy Time-Shift** — Store energy when generation exceeds demand (midday sun, windy nights), discharge when demand exceeds generation.
2. **Load Following** — Track variations in load at seconds-to-minutes timescale.
3. **Frequency and Voltage Regulation** — Battery inverters can provide synthetic inertia and reactive support.
4. **Black-Start Capability** — Energize the microgrid from dead state after a total shutdown.
5. **Diesel Displacement** — Reduce diesel genset runtime, saving fuel and lowering emissions.
6. **Autonomy Buffer** — Bridge days of poor RE availability (cloudy, low-wind) — typically 3 – 5 days.
7. **Renewable Smoothing** — Absorb ramps and short-term fluctuations in PV/wind output.
8. **Peak Demand Shaving** — Discharge during brief load peaks instead of starting the diesel genset.

**Criteria for Battery Selection:**
1. **Energy capacity (kWh)** — Must meet autonomy requirement.
2. **Power rating (kW)** — Must meet peak load and surge demands.
3. **Round-trip efficiency ($\eta$)** — Higher is better (Li-ion 95%, Lead-acid 80 – 85%).
4. **Depth of Discharge (DoD)** — Max usable fraction; affects cycle life.
5. **Cycle life** — Number of full charge-discharge cycles before capacity drops to 80% of nominal.
6. **Calendar life** — Aging even when not in use.
7. **Operating temperature range** — Must suit site climate.
8. **Capital cost (₹/kWh)** and **LCOS** (Levelized Cost of Storage).
9. **Safety** — Thermal runaway risk (Li-ion), acid spill (lead-acid).
10. **Maintenance** — Lead-acid needs water top-up; Li-ion is maintenance-free.
11. **Self-discharge rate** — Critical for systems sitting idle for long periods.

**Common Storage Choices:**
- **Lead-acid (flooded, VRLA, gel):** Low cost, short life (~ 500 – 1200 cycles), used in small off-grid systems.
- **Li-ion (LFP, NMC):** High efficiency, long life (~ 3000 – 6000 cycles), now dominant in new installations.
- **Flow batteries (Vanadium Redox):** Long duration (8 – 12 h), very long life, high upfront cost.
- **Supercapacitors:** Power-dense, used as *hybrid* storage alongside batteries for short bursts.

**Conclusion:** Storage is the *enabler* of isolated renewable systems. Battery selection is a multi-criteria optimization balancing cost, life, efficiency, and site-specific constraints.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q4(a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*With a neat single-diode equivalent circuit, derive the I-V characteristic equation of a solar cell. Explain the role of series and shunt resistances.*

**Model Answer:**

**Single-Diode Equivalent Circuit:**

A PV cell is modelled as a *current source* (photocurrent $I_{\text{ph}}$) in parallel with a *diode*, a *shunt resistance* $R_{\text{sh}}$, and a *series resistance* $R_s$.

**Derivation of I-V Equation:**

Applying **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)** at the top node:
$$I_{\text{ph}} = I_d + I_{\text{sh}} + I$$

Where:
- $I_d$ = current through the diode
- $I_{\text{sh}}$ = current through $R_{\text{sh}}$
- $I$ = terminal (load) current

The Shockley diode equation gives:
$$I_d = I_{\text{o}} \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{q V_d}{nkT}\right) - 1\right]$$

The voltage across the diode $V_d$ is related to terminal voltage $V$ and terminal current $I$ by:
$$V_d = V + I R_s$$

Substituting:
$$I_d = I_{\text{o}} \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{q(V + I R_s)}{nkT}\right) - 1\right]$$

The current through the shunt resistance is:
$$I_{\text{sh}} = \frac{V + I R_s}{R_{\text{sh}}}$$

Substituting into the KCL equation:
$$I_{\text{ph}} = I_{\text{o}} \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{q(V + I R_s)}{nkT}\right) - 1\right] + \frac{V + I R_s}{R_{\text{sh}}} + I$$

Rearranging for terminal current $I$:
$$I = I_{\text{ph}} - I_{\text{o}} \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{q(V + I R_s)}{nkT}\right) - 1\right] - \frac{V + I R_s}{R_{\text{sh}}}$$

This is the **single-diode model** (or *five-parameter model* since $I_{\text{ph}}, I_{\text{o}}, n, R_s, R_{\text{sh}}$ are the five parameters).

**Boundary Conditions:**
- **Short-circuit (V = 0):** $I_{sc} \approx I_{\text{ph}}$ (since the exponential term is small and $V/R_{\text{sh}} \approx 0$).
- **Open-circuit (I = 0):** Setting $I = 0$ and solving gives the open-circuit voltage $V_{oc}$.

**Role of Series Resistance $R_s$:**
- Represents the *ohmic losses* in the semiconductor bulk, metal contacts, and interconnections.
- $R_s$ should be as **small as possible** (ideally $< 1\,\Omega \cdot \text{cm}^2$ for a Si cell).
- High $R_s$ *reduces* the Fill Factor and lowers the slope of the I-V curve near $V_{oc}$.
- A small $R_s$ ensures minimal voltage drop under load.

**Role of Shunt Resistance $R_{\text{sh}}$:**
- Represents *leakage current paths* along the cell edges and through defects in the p-n junction.
- $R_{\text{sh}}$ should be as **large as possible** (typically $> 1000\,\Omega \cdot \text{cm}^2$).
- A small $R_{\text{sh}}$ indicates manufacturing defects, partial shading damage, or PID (potential-induced degradation).
- $R_{\text{sh}}$ affects the *slope* of the I-V curve near $I_{sc}$ — a small $R_{\text{sh}}$ makes the curve tilt leftward near short-circuit.

**Effect on Fill Factor:**
- $R_s$ ↑ → FF ↓ (more loss)
- $R_{\text{sh}}$ ↓ → FF ↓ (more leakage)

**Conclusion:** The single-diode model captures the *essential* electrical behavior of a PV cell using five physically meaningful parameters. $R_s$ and $R_{\text{sh}}$ act as the *parasitic losses* — the goal of cell manufacturing is to minimize $R_s$ and maximize $R_{\text{sh}}$.

---

**Q4(b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Explain the role of energy storage in grid-connected renewable power systems. Discuss any four grid services that a Battery Energy Storage System (BESS) can provide.*

**Model Answer:**

**Role of Storage in Grid-Connected Systems:**

In a *grid-connected* system, the utility grid provides a *stable voltage and frequency reference*, so storage is not strictly required for basic operation. However, as Renewable Energy Source (RES) penetration increases, storage becomes **critical** for:

1. **Addressing Duck-Curve Ramping** — Mid-day solar over-generation and evening peak ramps.
2. **Mitigating Curtailment** — Storing otherwise-wasted RE energy.
3. **Providing Ancillary Services** — Frequency, voltage, reserves, black-start.
4. **Economic Value-Stack** — Arbitrage, demand-charge reduction, capacity payments.
5. **Power Quality** — Harmonics, flicker, voltage sags.
6. **Deferring T&D Upgrades** — Storing energy at congested nodes.

**Four Key Grid Services from a BESS:**

**Service 1: Frequency Regulation (Primary Response)**
- **Problem:** Generator trip, load surge, or sudden RE fluctuation causes frequency deviation.
- **Solution:** BESS detects the *Rate of Change of Frequency (RoCoF)* and injects/absorbs active power in < 200 ms.
- **Mechanism:** Inverter operates in *droop mode* with $df/dt$ response.
- **Value:** Replaces costly spinning reserve; earns capacity and performance payments.

**Service 2: Peak Shaving (Duck-Curve Mitigation)**
- **Problem:** With high solar PV penetration, the *net load* curve becomes a "duck" with sharp evening ramp.
- **Solution:** BESS charges during mid-day PV surplus, discharges during 6 – 9 PM peak.
- **Value:** Avoids starting expensive peaker plants; reduces electricity cost for end-users.

**Service 3: Voltage Support (Reactive Power Compensation)**
- **Problem:** RE inverters (grid-following type) cannot inherently support voltage during faults.
- **Solution:** Modern BESS inverters (grid-forming) can provide $\pm 0.8$ to $\pm 1.0$ power factor (Q injection).
- **Mechanism:** Inverter's reactive current $I_q$ is controlled independently of active current $I_d$.
- **Value:** Maintains voltage within statutory limits (e.g., $\pm 5\%$ of nominal in India).

**Service 4: Spinning/Non-Spinning Reserve**
- **Problem:** System operator must keep margin of generation available for contingencies (N-1 criterion).
- **Solution:** A BESS, kept at ~ 50% SoC, can ramp to full discharge in < 1 s.
- **Value:** Cheaper than keeping a gas turbine on hot standby; only incurs *degradation cost*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Optional 5th Service (For Depth):** *Black-Start* — A BESS with grid-forming inverter can energize a de-energized network from a dead state, enabling the grid to be restored after a total blackout. This is a critical service for grid resilience.

**Value-Stacking Concept:**
A single BESS can provide *multiple* services sequentially or simultaneously (e.g., frequency regulation by day, peak shaving by evening). The combined revenue (called *value stacking*) is what makes BESS projects financially viable.

**Conclusion:** In a grid-connected system, storage transitions from being a *survival enabler* to a *value optimizer*. With the rise of grid-scale renewables, BESS is becoming an *indispensable* grid asset.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Module 3):**
> 1. **Confusing PTC with Solar Tower** — Many students write "PTC generates steam directly" — it does NOT; PTC uses HTF (oil/salt) which then boils water. Solar Tower can do either, depending on receiver design.
> 2. **Ignoring Temperature Coefficient** — A 25 °C temperature rise derates crystalline Si panel power by ~ 10%. Kerala's hot climate (35 – 40 °C) means *real-world output is significantly lower than nameplate Wp*.
> 3. **Forgetting the Inverter Efficiency in Battery Sizing** — A typical student calculation misses $\eta_{\text{inv}} = 0.93$, leading to undersized battery and system failure during cloudy days.
> 4. **Using Full DoD for Lead-Acid** — This is the *#1 cause* of off-grid PV system failure. Always restrict to DoD $\le 0.5$ for flooded lead-acid.
> 5. **Confusing 'Renewable Fraction' and 'Renewable Penetration'** — They are *not* the same. RF includes the *contribution of RE to total generation*. RPI is the *ratio of RE to load*. KTU often tests this distinction.
> 6. **Misspelling 'concentrator' as 'concentrate' or 'concentration'** — Subtle but loses marks.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> **Final Memory Anchors for Module 3:**

- [ ] **Four CSP Technologies:** LFR, PTC, SPT, Dish — ordered by *concentration ratio* and *temperature*.
- [ ] **Concentration Ratio ($C$):** $C = A_{\text{aperture}} / A_{\text{receiver}}$. Higher $C$ → Higher $T$ → Higher Carnot efficiency.
- [ ] **Carnot Efficiency:** $\eta = 1 - T_c/T_h$. Always use *absolute* (Kelvin) temperature.
- [ ] **PV Single-Diode Equation:** $I = I_{\text{ph}} - I_{\text{o}}[\exp(q(V+IR_s)/nkT) - 1] - (V+IR_s)/R_{\text{sh}}$. *Five parameters: $I_{\text{ph}}, I_{\text{o}}, n, R_s, R_{\text{sh}}$.*
- [ ] **Fill Factor:** $\text{FF} = V_{\text{mpp}} I_{\text{mpp}} / (V_{\text{oc}} I_{\text{sc}})$. Typical Si range: **0.70 – 0.85**.
- [ ] **Temperature Coefficient:** $\gamma_p \approx -0.4\%/\!^\circ\text{C}$ for crystalline Si. Power *decreases* as temperature *increases*.
- [ ] **MPPT Algorithms:** P&O, Incremental Conductance, Fractional $V_{\text{oc}}$, AI-based. MPPT maximizes $P = V \cdot I$.
- [ ] **Thermal Storage Equation:** $E_{\text{TES}} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ (sensible) or $E_{\text{TES}} = m \cdot L$ (latent, PCM).
- [ ] **Molten Salt (Hitec):** 60% NaNO₃ + 40% KNO₃, $c_p \approx 1.55 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$, range 290 – 565 °C.
- [ ] **Gemasolar:** 15-hour full-load storage, 24/7 dispatchable, ~ 8500 tonnes of salt.
- [ ] **Battery Sizing Formula:** $C_{\text{bat}} = E_{\text{daily}} N / (\eta_{\text{batt}} \eta_{\text{inv}} V_{\text{bus}} \text{DoD})$.
- [ ] **Lead-Acid DoD Limit:** 0.5 (flooded), 0.6 (VRLA), 0.8 (Li-ion LFP).
- [ ] **Renewable Fraction (RF):** $E_{\text{RES}} / (E_{\text{RES}} + E_{\text{conv}})$. *Not* the same as RPI.
- [ ] **Duck Curve:** Solar over-generation at noon, sharp ramp at sunset. Storage flattens both.
- [ ] **Swing Equation:** $(2H/f_0)(df/dt) = P_{\text{mech}} - P_{\text{elec}}$. Governs frequency dynamics.
- [ ] **BESS Round-Trip Efficiency:** Pumped 75 – 80%, Lead-acid 80 – 85%, Li-ion 90 – 95%, Flow 65 – 75%, Compressed Air 45 – 60%.
- [ ] **Anti-Islanding:** BESS must disconnect within < 100 ms of grid failure (IEEE 1547).
- [ ] **Grid-Forming vs Grid-Following Inverter:** Grid-following needs an *existing* grid reference; grid-forming can *create* one (essential for black-start).
- [ ] **Value Stacking:** A grid BESS earns from multiple services (arbitrage + frequency + capacity) — *simultaneous* revenue is what justifies the project.
- [ ] **STC Conditions:** $G = 1000$ W/m², $T_{\text{cell}} = 25\,^\circ\text{C}$, AM 1.5. Always quote Wp at STC.
- [ ] **Autonomy:** Number of consecutive cloudy/low-wind days a system can survive. Typical 3 – 5 days.
- [ ] **Hybrid Storage:** Combine batteries (energy-dense) with supercapacitors (power-dense) for optimal off-grid performance.
- [ ] **Levelized Cost of Storage (LCOS):** The *true* cost metric for comparing storage technologies — analogous to LCOE for generation.

> **Last-Minute Tip:** Before the exam, draw the *single-diode equivalent circuit* from memory and derive the I-V equation — this single question has appeared in **3 of the last 4 KTU university exams**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET521 - ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Electric Vehicles: Grid to Vehicle (G2V) and Vehicle to Grid (V2G)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Grid-to-Vehicle (G2V):** A unidirectional power flow operational mode in which an Electric Vehicle (EV) is connected to the electrical grid through a power electronics interface (typically an on-board or off-board AC–DC converter) and draws active power from the grid to charge the **Energy Storage System (ESS)** — usually a Li-ion battery pack — to a target State of Charge (SoC). Per KTU 2024 ESS module terminology, G2V is also termed *unidirectional smart charging*.

**Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G):** A bidirectional power flow paradigm in which the EV's onboard battery, while grid-connected through a bidirectional power converter, can inject active and/or reactive power back into the distribution grid. The EV acts as a **Distributed Energy Resource (DER)**, providing ancillary services such as peak shaving, load leveling, frequency regulation, voltage support, and spinning reserves.

The broader family of bidirectional EV-grid interactions is collectively called **V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything)**, which includes V2G, V2H (Vehicle-to-Home), V2B (Vehicle-to-Building/Load), and V2V (Vehicle-to-Vehicle).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Exam Ready):** *V2G is the controlled bidirectional flow of electrical energy between an EV's battery and the power grid, enabled by a bidirectional charger and a communication protocol (ISO 15118 / OCPP), allowing the EV to function as both a load (G2V) and a mobile storage asset (V2G).*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an EV as a **giant, mobile power bank** — the same kind you carry for your smartphone, only 1,000× larger. 

- **G2V mode** = You are *charging* the power bank from a wall socket. Energy flows **inward** only. The grid acts as the "source" and the battery acts as the "sink."
- **V2G mode** = You are *discharging* the power bank back into the house to run a fan during a power cut. Energy flows **outward**. The battery acts as the "source" and the grid acts as the "sink."

Picture this scenario: During daytime, **solar panels** on rooftops flood the grid with cheap power — your EV drinks this excess energy (G2V). In the evening, when the whole colony switches on ACs and lights (peak demand), your EV returns a portion of its stored energy to the grid (V2G), earning you money in the process through **demand-response programs**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Example:** In Denmark and the UK, Nissan Leaf fleets participate in V2G pilot programs where the EV battery is discharged to the grid during 6 PM – 9 PM peak demand, then recharged after midnight when tariffs drop. The same hardware — a CHAdeMO or CCS2 bidirectional charger — handles both directions.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

| Parameter | Standard Symbol | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| EV Battery Energy Capacity | $E_{bat}$ | 30 – 100 | kWh |
| Usable SoC Window | $SoC_{op}$ | 20 – 80 (default) | % |
| Charger Power Rating | $P_{ch}$ | 3.3 – 350 | kW |
| AC Grid Voltage (India) | $V_{LL}$ | 415 | V |
| Grid Frequency | $f$ | 50 | Hz |
| AC-to-DC Conversion Efficiency | $\eta_{ACDC}$ | 90 – 95 | % |
| DC-to-AC (Inversion) Efficiency | $\eta_{DCAC}$ | 92 – 97 | % |
| Round-Trip Efficiency | $\eta_{RT}$ | 80 – 88 | % |
| Standard Communication | — | **ISO 15118**, OCPP 2.0.1 | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Round-Trip Efficiency ($\eta_{RT}$)** is the single most cited metric in KTU exam questions: $\eta_{RT} = \eta_{ACDC} \times \eta_{DCAC} \approx 0.85$ (typical).

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architecture of a G2V/V2G System

A complete V2G-capable system consists of four tightly-coupled functional layers:

**Layer 1 — Physical/Power Layer:**
- AC grid (3-phase, 415 V, 50 Hz in India) → Isolation transformer → AC filter → Bidirectional AC–DC converter (PFC rectifier / inverter) → DC link capacitor → Bidirectional DC–DC converter → EV battery pack.
- For DC fast charging (Level 3), the AC–DC stage is off-board (e.g., a 50 kW CCS2 station).

**Layer 2 — Control Layer:**
- Battery Management System (BMS) regulates cell-level SoC, SoH, temperature.
- Vehicle Control Unit (VCU) controls the on-board charger.
- A bidirectional charger uses **dual-active-bridge (DAB)** topology for galvanic isolation and zero-voltage switching (ZVS).

**Layer 3 — Communication Layer:**
- **ISO 15118** (vehicle-to-charger high-level communication, supports Plug & Charge and bidirectional power transfer profiles).
- **OCPP 2.0.1** (charger-to-central management system, used by aggregators).
- **IEC 61850** (for substation/grid integration of aggregated EV fleets).

**Layer 4 — Market/Coordination Layer:**
- **Aggregator** — a software entity that pools thousands of EVs and bids their combined capacity into the wholesale electricity market (e.g., PJM, CAISO in USA; Indian Energy Exchange in India).

### 2.2 Operational Modes Explained

#### Mode 1 — Unidirectional G2V (Dumb Charging)
- Power flows only grid → EV.
- No communication. Charger begins at plug-in.
- Problem: causes *"evening peak on top of evening peak"* (the "duck curve" on steroids).

#### Mode 2 — Smart G2V (V1G / Controlled Charging)
- Power flows only grid → EV, but rate is **modulated** by the utility.
- Mitigates grid stress; no battery degradation penalty.
- Charger must support OCPP 1.6+.

#### Mode 3 — V2G (Full Bidirectional)
- Power flows in **both** directions.
- EV provides ancillary services.
- Requires ISO 15118-compliant hardware and bidirectional BMS firmware.

### 2.3 Why V2G? The Grid-Side "Why"

V2G addresses four fundamental grid problems:
1. **Peak Shaving** — discharging EVs at 7 PM reduces the system peak that typically coincides with solar drop-off.
2. **Frequency Regulation** — EVs respond in <1 second to grid frequency deviations (50 Hz nominal in India).
3. **Renewable Integration** — absorbs midday solar surplus, discharges at night → directly flattens the duck curve.
4. **Deferral of T&D Upgrades** — utilities avoid spending crores on new substations by using parked EVs as virtual storage.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Board-Exam Critical)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{bat} = \int P_{ch}(t)\, dt$ | Energy delivered to battery | kWh |
| 2 | $SoC(t) = SoC_0 + \dfrac{1}{E_{nom}} \int_{0}^{t} P_{ch}(\tau)\, d\tau$ | Instantaneous State of Charge | % or p.u. |
| 3 | $\eta_{ACDC} = \dfrac{P_{DC,out}}{P_{AC,in}}$ | Rectifier efficiency | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\eta_{DCAC} = \dfrac{P_{AC,out}}{P_{DC,in}}$ | Inverter efficiency | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\eta_{RT} = \eta_{ACDC} \times \eta_{DCAC} \times \eta_{batt}$ | Round-trip efficiency | dimensionless |
| 6 | $E_{delivered} = E_{charged} \times \eta_{RT}$ | Net energy returned to grid | kWh |
| 7 | $P_{ch,max} = \dfrac{V_{bat} \cdot I_{ch,max}}{1000}$ | Charger power limit | kW |
| 8 | $t_{ch} = \dfrac{E_{bat} \cdot \Delta SoC}{P_{ch} \cdot \eta_{ACDC}}$ | Charging time | h |
| 9 | $C_{rate} = \dfrac{P_{ch}}{E_{bat}}$ | C-rate of charging | 1/h |
| 10 | $N_{agg} = \dfrac{P_{ancillary}}{P_{ch,avg}}$ | Aggregator fleet size | dimensionless |
| 11 | $C_{payback} = \dfrac{C_{bidir}}{R_{V2G} \cdot E_{bat} \cdot 365}$ | Payback period (years) | years |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Table Note:** In the KTU Formula Sheet, the pipe symbol $\vert$ is replaced by `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown syntax. The State-of-Charge rate equation is fundamental and frequently asked in **Part B (14 marks)** derivations.

### 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Aggregator Business Model:** A startup like Nuvve (USA) or The Mobility House (Germany) pools 10,000 EVs × 50 kWh = 500 MWh of dispatchable storage — equivalent to a mid-size pumped hydro station.
- **Indian Context:** With 3 crore EVs expected by 2030 (NITI Aayog), even 10% V2G participation = 15 GWh of distributed storage.
- **Military/Disaster Relief:** V2H can power a hospital or command center during grid outages.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: State of Charge (SoC) Dynamics in G2V Mode

We model the battery as a controlled current sink receiving charging current $I_{ch}(t)$ at terminal voltage $V_{bat}(t)$.

**Starting Point — The fundamental charge conservation law:**

$$Q(t) = Q_0 + \int_{0}^{t} I_{ch}(\tau)\, d\tau$$

**Step 1:** Convert charge $Q$ to energy by multiplying by terminal voltage.

$$E_{bat}(t) = V_{bat}(t) \cdot Q(t)$$

**Step 2:** Assume a quasi-static operating point where $V_{bat}$ is approximately constant over the charging window (a common KTU simplification; advanced courses use a Thevenin equivalent).

$$E_{bat}(t) \approx V_{bat} \cdot \left(Q_0 + \int_{0}^{t} I_{ch}(\tau)\, d\tau\right)$$

**Step 3:** Define nominal battery capacity in energy units.

$$E_{nom} = V_{bat} \cdot Q_{nom}$$

**Step 4:** Non-dimensionalize to get State of Charge in per-unit form.

$$SoC(t) = \dfrac{E_{bat}(t)}{E_{nom}} = SoC_0 + \dfrac{1}{Q_{nom}} \int_{0}^{t} I_{ch}(\tau)\, d\tau$$

**Step 5:** Express in terms of power, since chargers are power-controlled devices.

$$SoC(t) = SoC_0 + \dfrac{1}{E_{nom}} \int_{0}^{t} P_{ch}(\tau)\, d\tau$$

**Step 6:** For a constant power charger, integrate analytically.

$$SoC(t) = SoC_0 + \dfrac{P_{ch} \cdot t}{E_{nom}}$$

This is the canonical "constant-power charging" line — linear in time, used as a first-order benchmark.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Insight:** The $SoC(t)$ linear ramp under constant power is a **Frequently Asked Question (FAQ)** in KTU Part B. Examiners expect you to show Steps 1–6 explicitly; do not jump from "charge conservation" straight to the final equation.

### 3.2 Derivation: Round-Trip Energy Accounting in V2G

**Scenario:** A 60 kWh EV battery is charged from 20% to 80% (ΔSoC = 60%) and later discharged back.

**Step 1:** Compute the energy drawn from the grid during the G2V phase.

$$E_{grid,in} = E_{bat} \cdot \Delta SoC = 60 \times 0.60 = 36 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 2:** Account for rectifier losses during the G2V phase.

$$E_{bat,stored} = E_{grid,in} \times \eta_{ACDC} = 36 \times 0.93 = 33.48 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 3:** Account for battery round-trip losses (Coulombic × voltage efficiency).

$$E_{bat,out} = E_{bat,stored} \times \eta_{batt} = 33.48 \times 0.97 = 32.48 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 4:** Account for inverter losses during the V2G phase.

$$E_{grid,out} = E_{bat,out} \times \eta_{DCAC} = 32.48 \times 0.95 = 30.86 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 5:** Compute overall round-trip efficiency.

$$\eta_{RT} = \dfrac{E_{grid,out}}{E_{grid,in}} = \dfrac{30.86}{36} = 0.857 \approx 85.7\%$$

**Step 6:** The "tax" paid to the universe.

$$E_{loss} = E_{grid,in} - E_{grid,out} = 36 - 30.86 = 5.14 \text{ kWh}$$

This 5.14 kWh loss appears as heat in the inductors, switching devices, and from battery internal resistance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap:** Students often write $\eta_{RT} = \eta_{ACDC} \times \eta_{DCAC}$ and forget the battery term $\eta_{batt} \approx 0.97$. Examiners deduct **2 marks** for this oversight.

### 3.3 Derivation: Bidirectional Power Flow Power Balance

For a single V2G station at PCC (Point of Common Coupling):

$$P_{grid}(t) = P_{ch}(t) - P_{V2G}(t) - P_{aux}(t)$$

where:
- $P_{grid}(t)$ = net power drawn from grid (negative if EV is supplying)
- $P_{ch}(t)$ = power flowing into EV (≥ 0 in G2V)
- $P_{V2G}(t)$ = power flowing out of EV (≥ 0 in V2G)
- $P_{aux}(t)$ = auxiliary load of charger electronics (cooling fans, control board ≈ 50–200 W)

**Aggregation over $N$ EVs in a fleet:**

$$P_{fleet}(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ P_{ch,i}(t) - P_{V2G,i}(t) - P_{aux,i}(t) \right]$$

For a 1,000-EV fleet with 50 kW average discharge during peak hours:

$$P_{fleet,peak} = 1000 \times 50\, \text{kW} = 50\, \text{MW}$$

This is dispatchable within seconds — equivalent to a small gas-turbine peaker plant at zero fuel cost.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: G2V vs V2G Side-by-Side

| Feature | G2V (Unidirectional) | V2G (Bidirectional) |
|---|---|---|
| Power direction | Grid → EV only | Bidirectional |
| Charger type | Standard / Smart | Bidirectional (DAB / PFC + Inverter) |
| Communication | OCPP 1.6 / none | ISO 15118 + OCPP 2.0.1 |
| BMS complexity | Simple | High (SoC, SoH, temp, cycle counting) |
| Grid service | Demand | Demand + Supply + Ancillary |
| Battery degradation | Lower | Higher (extra cycles) |
| Hardware cost | ₹25,000 – 75,000 | ₹2,00,000 – 5,00,000 |
| Round-trip η | N/A (only one-way) | 80 – 88% |
| Standard | IEC 61851 | IEC 61851-23 + ISO 15118-20 |
| KTU marks weightage | 3–7 | 7–14 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **3.4 Analytical Matrix** is board-exam gold. KTU examiners frequently ask students to *compare* G2V and V2G in a tabular form (carries 7 marks when asked in Part B).

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the complete power and signal flow in a bidirectional EV charging station capable of both G2V and V2G operations.     AGG["AGGREGATOR PLATFORM CLOUD"]:::comm
    METER["SMART METER + CT PT"]:::meter
    RELAY["CONTACTOR RELAY"]:::power
    GROUND["PROTECTION EARTHING + RCCB"]:::prot

    GRID --> XFMR
    XFMR --> FILTER

**Diagram Reading Guide:**

- **Blue (GRID):** Utility supply side — high-voltage AC, frequency, protection.
- **Orange (POWER):** Power electronics conversion stages that handle bidirectional flow.
- **Green (BATT):** The EV battery itself, the heart of the system.
- **Purple (CTRL):** Local controllers (BMS, VCU) that enforce safety and SoC limits.
- **Cyan (COMM):** External coordination via aggregators and ISO 15118.
- **Pink (METER):** Revenue-grade metering for billing.
- **Grey (PROT):** Earth fault, overcurrent, and residual current protection.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper Set A]**
**(3 Marks)** Define Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G) technology. List any two grid services that V2G can provide.

**Model Answer:**

> **Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G)** is a smart energy management concept in which battery-powered Electric Vehicles (EVs) communicate with the power grid to perform bidirectional energy exchange. Through a bidirectional charger, EVs can draw power from the grid during off-peak hours (G2V mode) and supply stored power back to the grid during peak hours or grid emergencies (V2G mode), thereby functioning as **Distributed Energy Storage Resources (DESR)**.
>
> **Two grid services provided by V2G:**
> 1. **Peak Shaving / Load Leveling:** Aggregated EVs discharge during high-demand hours, reducing the evening peak load on the grid.
> 2. **Frequency Regulation:** EVs respond in < 1 second to deviations in grid frequency (50 Hz nominal), injecting or absorbing small amounts of power to maintain stability.
>
> *(Other acceptable answers: Voltage support via reactive power, spinning reserves, renewable integration.)*
> **[3 marks]**

#### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
**(3 Marks)** Differentiate between G2V and V2G in terms of (i) power flow direction, (ii) communication protocol, and (iii) battery degradation.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | G2V (Grid to Vehicle) | V2G (Vehicle to Grid) |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Power flow | Unidirectional — Grid → EV | Bidirectional — Grid ↔ EV |
| (ii) Communication | OCPP 1.6 or simpler | ISO 15118 + OCPP 2.0.1 |
| (iii) Battery degradation | Lower (only charge cycles) | Higher (extra discharge cycles) |

> **[3 marks]**

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern Note:** Part B questions in the End Semester Examination carry 14 marks each with **internal choice** — students must answer EITHER Choice A OR Choice B in full. Each sub-part is typically 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A [KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper, Module 4, 14 Marks]**
**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a bidirectional EV charging system (V2G). Identify the role of each block.
**(b) [7 Marks)** A 50 kWh EV battery is to be charged from 20% to 90% SoC using a 22 kW AC Level-2 bidirectional charger. The rectifier efficiency is 92% and the battery Coulombic efficiency is 98%. Calculate: (i) the energy drawn from the grid, (ii) the charging time, and (iii) the net energy that can be returned to the grid assuming a round-trip inverter efficiency of 95%.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Block Diagram & Working:**

A V2G system consists of the following functional blocks:

1. **AC Grid (3-phase, 415 V, 50 Hz)** — Utility supply.
2. **Isolation Transformer** — Provides galvanic isolation, steps voltage, and limits fault current.
3. **EMI / LCL Filter** — Suppresses switching harmonics injected back into the grid.
4. **Bidirectional AC–DC Converter** — Operates as a **PWM rectifier** (G2V) or as a **grid-tie inverter** (V2G). Uses IGBT/SiC MOSFETs.
5. **DC Link Capacitor** — Smooths intermediate DC bus voltage (typically 400 – 800 V DC).
6. **Bidirectional DC–DC Converter (DAB)** — Provides voltage matching between DC bus (~400 V) and battery pack (~350 – 800 V) with high-frequency isolation.
7. **Battery Pack** — Stores energy; managed by the BMS.
8. **BMS (Battery Management System)** — Monitors cell voltage, current, temperature, and enforces SoC limits.
9. **VCU (Vehicle Control Unit) / Charger Controller** — Executes charge/discharge commands.
10. **Communication Module (ISO 15118 + OCPP)** — Receives dispatch signals from the aggregator.
11. **Smart Meter** — Bidirectional kWh recording for billing.

**Working:** In **G2V mode**, the AC–DC converter operates as a rectifier, drawing power from the grid; the DC–DC converter steps it down to charge the battery. In **V2G mode**, the DC–DC converter boosts battery voltage; the AC–DC converter inverts it into 50 Hz AC synchronized with the grid. The BMS prevents over-discharge below 20% SoC. **[7 marks]**

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $E_{bat} = 50$ kWh
- $SoC_i = 20\%$, $SoC_f = 90\%$
- $\Delta SoC = 0.70$
- $P_{ch} = 22$ kW
- $\eta_{ACDC} = 0.92$
- $\eta_{batt} = 0.98$
- $\eta_{DCAC} = 0.95$

**(i) Energy drawn from the grid:**

$$E_{bat,stored} = E_{bat} \times \Delta SoC = 50 \times 0.70 = 35 \text{ kWh}$$

$$E_{grid,in} = \dfrac{E_{bat,stored}}{\eta_{ACDC}} = \dfrac{35}{0.92} = 38.04 \text{ kWh}$$

**(ii) Charging time:**

$$t_{ch} = \dfrac{E_{grid,in}}{P_{ch}} = \dfrac{38.04}{22} = 1.729 \text{ h} \approx 1 \text{ h } 44 \text{ min}$$

**(iii) Net energy returned to grid:**

$$E_{bat,out} = E_{bat,stored} \times \eta_{batt} = 35 \times 0.98 = 34.3 \text{ kWh}$$

$$E_{grid,out} = E_{bat,out} \times \eta_{DCAC} = 34.3 \times 0.95 = 32.585 \text{ kWh}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{RT} = \dfrac{E_{grid,out}}{E_{grid,in}} = \dfrac{32.585}{38.04} = 0.8566 \approx 85.7\%}$$

**[7 marks]**

---

#### **Question B [KTU University Exam – Alternative Choice, 14 Marks]**
**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the four main operational modes of EV-grid interaction: G2V (dumb charging), smart G2V (V1G), V2G, and V2H. Compare their impact on grid stability.
**(b) [7 Marks]** An aggregator manages 2,000 EVs, each with a 60 kWh battery and 50 kW bidirectional charger. The SoC operating window is 20%–80%. During a frequency regulation event, the aggregator is asked to dispatch the fleet at full power for 15 minutes. Calculate: (i) the total fleet power capacity, (ii) the available energy headroom, and (iii) the SoC change over 15 minutes.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Four Operational Modes:**

1. **G2V (Dumb Charging):** No control; EV starts charging immediately at full rated power upon plug-in. Causes new evening peaks. No communication infrastructure needed.
2. **V1G (Smart / Controlled Unidirectional Charging):** Charging rate is modulated by the utility or aggregator via OCPP. EV is a flexible load but cannot inject power. Battery degradation impact is minimal.
3. **V2G (Full Bidirectional):** EV both charges and discharges; provides frequency regulation, peak shaving, and renewable integration. Requires ISO 15118 and bidirectional hardware. Higher battery cycling impact.
4. **V2H (Vehicle-to-Home):** The EV powers a single home via an off-grid islanded inverter during outages. Uses a dedicated bidirectional home charger (e.g., Wallbox Quasar). Power rating 5–22 kW.

**Impact on Grid Stability:**

| Mode | Peak Reduction | Frequency Support | Renewable Absorption | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| G2V | Negative (worsens) | None | None | Lowest |
| V1G | Moderate | None | Moderate | Low |
| V2G | High | Excellent | Excellent | High |
| V2H | Off-grid only | None | Low | Medium |

**[7 marks]**

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $N = 2000$ EVs
- $E_{bat,i} = 60$ kWh each
- $P_{ch,i} = 50$ kW each
- SoC window: 20% – 80%
- $t = 15$ min = 0.25 h

**(i) Total fleet power capacity:**

$$P_{fleet} = N \times P_{ch,i} = 2000 \times 50 = 100{,}000 \text{ kW} = \boxed{100 \text{ MW}}$$

**(ii) Available energy headroom (full cycle from 80% to 20%):**

$$E_{headroom} = N \times E_{bat,i} \times (0.80 - 0.20) = 2000 \times 60 \times 0.60 = \boxed{72{,}000 \text{ kWh} = 72 \text{ MWh}}$$

**(iii) SoC change over 15 minutes at full discharge:**

$$E_{discharged,total} = P_{fleet} \times t = 100{,}000 \times 0.25 = 25{,}000 \text{ kWh}$$

$$E_{discharged,per\,EV} = \dfrac{25{,}000}{2000} = 12.5 \text{ kWh}$$

$$\Delta SoC = \dfrac{E_{discharged,per\,EV}}{E_{bat,i}} = \dfrac{12.5}{60} = 0.2083 = \boxed{20.83\%}$$

This single 15-minute event moves the fleet SoC by ~20%, which fits neatly within the 60% operational window. **[7 marks]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing AC–DC with DC–DC efficiencies:** Many students apply the rectifier efficiency to the inverter stage. Remember: charging = $\eta_{ACDC}$; discharging = $\eta_{DCAC}$. They are NOT the same.
> 2. **Forgetting $\eta_{batt}$:** Battery Coulombic efficiency (~0.97–0.99) must be included in the round-trip calculation. Loss of 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Using energy instead of charge for SoC:** The SoC integral must be over current (charge) OR power/E_nom. Using voltage inconsistently loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Ignoring SoC limits:** Real EVs never discharge below 20% or charge above 80% in V2G operations. Numerical answers using 0%–100% are *physically unrealistic* and penalized.
> 5. **Missing the Aggregator concept:** A 14-mark V2G answer that doesn't mention aggregators, ISO 15118, or OCPP is incomplete. Examiners explicitly look for these keywords.
> 6. **Sign convention errors:** When computing net grid power, V2G discharge is **negative** (power *leaving* the grid). Mixing signs costs 2 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **G2V = unidirectional charging**; **V2G = bidirectional power flow**.
- V2G makes the EV a **Distributed Energy Resource (DER)**.
- Key formula: $SoC(t) = SoC_0 + \dfrac{1}{E_{nom}} \int P_{ch}(\tau)\, d\tau$.
- Round-trip efficiency: $\eta_{RT} = \eta_{ACDC} \times \eta_{batt} \times \eta_{DCAC} \approx 0.85$.
- **Communication standards:** ISO 15118 (vehicle ↔ charger), OCPP 2.0.1 (charger ↔ cloud), IEC 61850 (substation).
- **Aggregator** = software that pools thousands of EVs to bid into the electricity market.
- **Operational SoC window** in V2G is typically **20%–80%** to protect battery longevity.
- **Bidirectional converter topologies:** PFC + Inverter (AC–DC side), Dual-Active-Bridge (DC–DC side).
- **Grid services from V2G:** Peak shaving, frequency regulation, voltage support, renewable integration, deferral of T&D upgrades.
- **Battery degradation cost** is the biggest barrier to V2G economics; mitigated by smart SoC windowing and chemistry-aware dispatch.
- **Indian context:** CEA and BEE are framing V2G regulations; 2030 target of 3 crore EVs implies ~15 GWh of dispatchable storage at 10% V2G penetration.
- **Difference between V1G and V2G:** V1G = controlled *unidirectional* charging; V2G = *bidirectional* flow.
- **Hardware cost ratio:** Bidirectional charger is ~3–5× the cost of a unidirectional smart charger.
- **Standard connector for V2G:** CHAdeMO (Japan, supports V2X natively), CCS2 (Europe, ISO 15118-20), and GB/T (China).

> **Revision Priority:** For a 14-mark answer, memorize the four-block diagram (Grid → AC–DC → DC link → DC–DC → Battery), the SoC integral, the round-trip efficiency formula, and the analytical matrix from Section 3.4. Together they cover ~80% of expected marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Management and Control Hierarchy of Storage Systems. Aggregating Energy Storage Systems and Distributed Generation (Virtual Power Plant Energy Management with Storage Systems)
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET521 - ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Management and Control Hierarchy of Storage Systems. Aggregating Energy Storage Systems and Distributed Generation (Virtual Power Plant Energy Management with Storage Systems)

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Management and Control Hierarchy of Storage Systems

### Formal Academic Definition

The **Management and Control Hierarchy of Storage Systems** refers to the multi-layered, vertically integrated architecture of supervisory, coordination, and operational control functions that govern the charging, discharging, state-of-charge (SoC) balancing, protection, and grid-interaction of energy storage units. In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, this hierarchy is decomposed into three classical tiers defined by IEC 61850 and IEEE 1547 standards:

1. **Primary Control (Local / Device Level)** — Hardware-level, sub-second response (< 1 s) typically executed by the Power Conversion System (PCS) inner current/voltage control loop.
2. **Secondary Control (Supervisory / Microgrid Level)** — Minutes-scale coordination across multiple storage units, executed by the **Energy Management System (EMS)** or **Microgrid Central Controller (MGCC)**.
3. **Tertiary Control (Grid / Market Level)** — Hour-to-day scheduling interfacing with the utility dispatcher, aggregator, or market clearing entity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The control hierarchy is *not* optional — it is the **backbone of dispatchable storage integration**. Without explicit hierarchical decomposition, parallel-connected battery banks suffer from circulating currents, SoC drift, and DC-link voltage instability.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Hospital Triage System"

Imagine a large hospital:
- The **Primary Control** is the bedside nurse monitoring each patient's vitals every second — heart rate, oxygen level (analogous to the PCS tracking cell voltage, current, and temperature).
- The **Secondary Control** is the duty doctor coordinating across the ward every few minutes — deciding which patient needs more oxygen, who needs to rest (analogous to the EMS distributing power commands across multiple batteries).
- The **Tertiary Control** is the hospital superintendent handling weekly resource allocation — budgeting beds, scheduling surgeries (analogous to grid market bidding and day-ahead dispatch).

Just as no hospital operates with only nurses OR only superintendents, a storage system **cannot** function safely without all three layers working in harmony.

### Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **DC Bus Voltage Tolerance:** $\pm 10\%$ of nominal (per IEEE 1547)
- **Primary Response Time:** $< 100$ ms for grid-forming inverters
- **Secondary Time Constant:** 1 s to 5 min (droop + SoC balancing)
- **Tertiary Update Interval:** 15 min to 1 h (matches day-ahead market blocks)
- **State of Charge (SoC) Operating Window:** typically **20 %–80 %** for lithium-ion to maximize cycle life ($\geq 5000$ cycles)

---

## 1.2 Aggregating Energy Storage Systems and Distributed Generation

### Formal Definition

**Aggregation** is the process by which multiple heterogeneous Distributed Energy Resources (DERs) — rooftop PV, small wind turbines, diesel gensets, and especially **distributed Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS)** — are pooled under a single technical and commercial entity so that they can be dispatched, monitored, and remunerated **as if they were one large power plant**. This is regulated under **IEEE 1547.3** and the **IEC 62933** series.

> [!NOTE]
> The aggregation enables individual 5 kW residential batteries to collectively behave as a **50 MW virtual generator** capable of bidding into the day-ahead wholesale market.

### Intuitive Analogy — "Uber for Electricity"

Think of Uber — thousands of independent cars (your home batteries, rooftop solar) cannot individually serve airport transfers reliably. But once Uber aggregates them through a **digital platform** (the aggregator software), passengers (the grid operator) experience them as one **reliable transport fleet** (a virtual power plant). The aggregator handles dispatch signals, billing, and routing.

### Key Performance Indicators for Aggregation

| KPI | Typical Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Aggregation Efficiency** | $\eta_{agg} = 0.92$–$0.96$ | Round-trip losses across all DERs combined |
| **Ramp Rate** | $0.1$–$1$ MW/s | How fast the virtual plant can change output |
| **Capacity Factor Improvement** | $1.5\times$ to $3\times$ | Versus uncoordinated operation |
| **Forecast Error (24 h)** | $3\%$–$8\%$ | For aggregated vs individual DER |

---

## 1.3 Virtual Power Plant (VPP) Energy Management with Storage Systems

### Formal Definition

A **Virtual Power Plant (VPP)** is an Information and Communication Technology (ICT) framework that integrates disparate DERs, including distributed storage, through a unified **Energy Management System (EMS)** platform, enabling them to participate in **electricity markets** and provide **grid services** as a single dispatchable entity.

According to the KTU 2024 scheme module descriptor, the VPP architecture must specifically demonstrate:
- Bidirectional power flow management
- Real-time optimization of storage dispatch
- Market interface (day-ahead, intraday, ancillary)
- Cybersecurity and data integrity (per IEC 62351)

### Intuitive Analogy — "The Symphony Orchestra"

A VPP is like an orchestra. Each musician (solar panel, battery, EV charger, fuel cell) plays a different instrument. Without a conductor, the result is noise. The **VPP Energy Management System** is the conductor — reading the score (load forecast, market prices), cueing each section (dispatch command), and ensuring the entire symphony (grid) plays in harmony. The battery storage acts as the **drummer** — setting the tempo, smoothing transients, and keeping the entire ensemble synchronized.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Box:** A VPP is *not* a physical power plant. It is a **software-defined coordination layer** that adds intelligence, not hardware. The "power" it produces is the mathematical sum of all DER outputs minus all losses, validated at the point of common coupling (PCC).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Three-Tier Control Hierarchy — Detailed Architecture

### Tier 1: Primary Control (Device Level)

**Objective:** Guarantee instantaneous stability of the local power electronic interface.

**Components:**
- Inner **current control loop** (bandwidth $\approx 1$ kHz to $5$ kHz)
- **Phase-Locked Loop (PLL)** for grid synchronization
- **DC-link voltage regulator**
- Hardware protection (overcurrent, over-voltage, anti-islanding per IEEE 1547)

The primary controller implements the **droop law** for parallel operation of multiple inverters:

$$P_i = P_i^{ref} - k_{p,i} \cdot (f - f^{ref})$$

$$Q_i = Q_i^{ref} - k_{q,i} \cdot (V - V^{ref})$$

where $k_{p,i}$ and $k_{q,i}$ are the active and reactive droop coefficients of the $i$-th inverter.

**Why this matters in engineering:** Without droop, two inverters connected to the same AC bus will fight each other due to small impedance mismatches, causing circulating currents that destroy semiconductor switches.

---

### Tier 2: Secondary Control (Supervisory / Microgrid Level)

**Objective:** Restore frequency and voltage to nominal after primary action, balance SoC across heterogeneous storage units, and execute minute-scale optimization.

**Time horizon:** $1$ s to $5$ min.

**Key Algorithms:**
1. **SoC-based adaptive droop** — droop gains modified based on real-time SoC
2. **Consensus-based distributed control** — peer-to-peer negotiation
3. **Centralized MGCC dispatch** — single master controller

The **secondary control corrective term** $\Delta f$ is added to restore frequency:

$$f^{ref}_{secondary}(t) = f^{nominal} + \Delta f(t)$$

where

$$\Delta f(t) = K_{i} \int_{0}^{t} (f^{nominal} - f^{actual}(\tau)) \, d\tau$$

This integral action **eliminates steady-state frequency error** introduced by primary droop.

---

### Tier 3: Tertiary Control (Grid / Market Level)

**Objective:** Economic dispatch, market participation, and inter-area power exchange scheduling.

**Time horizon:** 15 min (intra-day) to 24 h (day-ahead).

**Optimization problem:**

$$\min_{P_{g,i}, P_{ess,j}} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{N_g} C_i(P_{g,i,t}) + \sum_{j=1}^{N_{ess}} C_j(P_{ess,j,t}) \right]$$

subject to:

- Power balance: $\sum P_{g,i} + \sum P_{ess,j} = P_{load}$
- Generator limits: $P_{g,i}^{min} \leq P_{g,i,t} \leq P_{g,i}^{max}$
- SoC dynamics: $SoC_{j,t+1} = SoC_{j,t} - \dfrac{\eta_{ess} P_{ess,j,t} \Delta t}{E_{j}^{rated}}$
- SoC bounds: $SoC_{j}^{min} \leq SoC_{j,t} \leq SoC_{j}^{max}$

---

## 2.2 Aggregation Mathematics

The **aggregated power** of $N$ DERs at the Point of Common Coupling (PCC) is:

$$P_{agg}(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ P_{pv,i}(t) + P_{wind,i}(t) - P_{load,i}(t) + \eta_{c} P_{c,i}(t) - \frac{P_{d,i}(t)}{\eta_{d}} \right]$$

where $\eta_c$ and $\eta_d$ are the storage charging and discharging efficiencies respectively.

The **aggregator revenue** $R_{agg}$ over horizon $T$:

$$R_{agg} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \lambda_t \cdot P_{agg}(t) \cdot \Delta t - \sum_{i=1}^{N} C_{degradation,i}(SoC_i(t))$$

---

## 2.3 VPP Energy Management Formulation

A VPP EMS solves a **Mixed-Integer Linear Program (MILP)** for discrete battery dispatch:

$$\min \sum_{t=1}^{T} \left[ c_{grid}(t) \cdot P_{import}(t) + c_{fuel}(t) \cdot P_{gen}(t) + c_{deg} \cdot \left( \vert P_{c}(t) \vert + \vert P_{d}(t) \vert \right) \right]$$

subject to:
- $P_{import}(t) - P_{export}(t) + P_{gen}(t) + P_{d}(t) - P_{c}(t) - P_{load}(t) = 0$
- $SoC(t+1) = SoC(t) + \dfrac{\eta_c P_c(t) \Delta t - P_d(t) \Delta t / \eta_d}{E_{rated}}$
- Binary variable $u(t) \in \{0,1\}$ prevents simultaneous charge/discharge

---

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbols | Units | Boundary / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_i = P_i^{ref} - k_p(f - f^{ref})$ | Primary droop | MW, Hz | $k_p$ in MW/Hz |
| 2 | $Q_i = Q_i^{ref} - k_q(V - V^{ref})$ | Reactive droop | MVAR, V | $k_q$ in MVAR/V |
| 3 | $\Delta f(t) = K_i \int (f^{nom} - f^{actual}) dt$ | Secondary integral | Hz | Restores nominal $f$ |
| 4 | $SoC(t+1) = SoC(t) - \dfrac{\eta P \Delta t}{E_{rated}}$ | SoC dynamics | % | $\Delta t$ in hours |
| 5 | $P_{agg}(t) = \sum_i \left[ P_{gen,i} - P_{load,i} + \eta_c P_{c,i} - P_{d,i}/\eta_d \right]$ | Aggregated PCC power | MW | Signed convention |
| 6 | $R_{agg} = \sum_t \lambda_t P_{agg}(t) \Delta t$ | Aggregator revenue | ₹, $ | $\lambda_t$ is LMP |
| 7 | $\eta_{agg} = \dfrac{E_{out,total}}{E_{in,total}}$ | Aggregation efficiency | % | Typically $0.92$–$0.96$ |
| 8 | $C_{cycle} = \dfrac{C_{battery}}{N_{cycles} \cdot DOD_{avg}}$ | Levelized storage cost | ₹/kWh | Degradation-aware |
| 9 | $P_{ramp} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\Delta t}$ | Ramp rate | MW/s | Critical for ancillary |
| 10 | $C_i(P) = a_i P^2 + b_i P + c_i$ | Quadratic generation cost | ₹/h | Economic dispatch |
| 11 | $\vert x \vert$ is represented as $\lvert x \rvert$ in $R_{agg}$ | Absolute power magnitude | MW | For penalty terms |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Absolute Value Convention:** Whenever you see $\lvert x \rvert$ in KTU answer scripts, write it as `\lvert x \rvert` in LaTeX and the vertical line as a single character to avoid markdown parsing errors.

---

## Real-World Engineering Utility

The control hierarchy described above is the **exact same architecture** used by:
- **Tesla Virtual Power Plant (South Australia)** — 50,000 home batteries aggregated to provide 250 MW of grid services.
- **SonnenCommunity (Germany)** — peer-to-peer energy sharing using secondary-tier consensus.
- **AGL Virtual Power Plant (Australia)** — combines rooftop PV, batteries, and smart HVAC into a single dispatchable resource.

In the KTU examination, students are expected to **map any real-world VPP deployment onto the three-tier model** to demonstrate comprehension.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: SoC-Adaptive Droop Coefficient

**Goal:** Derive the adaptive droop coefficient $k_p^{adaptive}$ that equalizes SoC across heterogeneous batteries.

**Step 1 — Standard droop law:**
$$f_i = f^{ref} - k_p P_i$$

Rearranging:

$$P_i = \frac{f^{ref} - f_i}{k_p}$$

**Step 2 — Introduce SoC weighting:**

When SoC is unbalanced, the battery with higher SoC should discharge more, and the battery with lower SoC should charge more. We modify the droop so that the *effective* power reference shifts:

$$P_i^{ref,mod} = P_i^{ref} + k_{soc} \cdot (SoC_i - SoC_{avg})$$

where $SoC_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} SoC_i$ and $k_{soc}$ is the SoC-balancing gain.

**Step 3 — Plug back into droop:**

$$P_i = P_i^{ref} + k_{soc}(SoC_i - SoC_{avg}) - k_p(f_i - f^{ref})$$

**Step 4 — Steady-state analysis:**

At steady state $f_i \to f^{ss}$ identical for all inverters. Therefore:

$$P_i^{ss} - P_j^{ss} = k_{soc}\left[ (SoC_i - SoC_{avg}) - (SoC_j - SoC_{avg}) \right]$$

$$\boxed{P_i^{ss} - P_j^{ss} = k_{soc}(SoC_i - SoC_j)}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**

If $SoC_i > SoC_j$, then $P_i^{ss} > P_j^{ss}$, meaning inverter $i$ discharges more than inverter $j$. This **automatically equalizes SoC over time**, without needing centralized communication.

**Conversion Logic Explanation:**
- The integral gain $k_{soc}$ acts like a "virtual spring" pulling all SoC values toward their average.
- The choice $k_{soc} > 0$ ensures a high-SoC unit exports more.
- Choosing $k_{soc} = 0$ recovers the conventional non-adaptive droop.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Aggregator Net Revenue Maximization

**Setup:** A VPP aggregates $N$ batteries each rated $E_i$ kWh. The aggregator bids into a day-ahead market with Locational Marginal Price (LMP) $\lambda_t$.

**Step 1 — Decision variables:**
- $P_i^{c}(t)$: charging power of battery $i$ at time $t$ (MW, non-negative)
- $P_i^{d}(t)$: discharging power of battery $i$ at time $t$ (MW, non-negative)

**Step 2 — Net revenue expression:**

$$R_{net} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \lambda_t \left[ P_i^{d}(t) - P_i^{c}(t) \right] \Delta t - \sum_{i=1}^{N} C_{deg,i}$$

where $C_{deg,i}$ is the cost of degradation.

**Step 3 — Linearize degradation cost** as proportional to throughput:

$$C_{deg,i} = c_{deg} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \left[ P_i^{c}(t) + P_i^{d}(t) \right] \Delta t$$

**Step 4 — Substituting:**

$$R_{net} = \sum_{t,i} \Delta t \left[ (\lambda_t - c_{deg}) P_i^d(t) - (\lambda_t + c_{deg}) P_i^c(t) \right]$$

**Step 5 — Optimal discharge condition:**

Discharge only if $\lambda_t > c_{deg}$:

$$P_i^{d*}(t) = \begin{cases} P_i^{max} & \text{if } \lambda_t > c_{deg} + \epsilon \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 6 — Optimal charge condition:**

Charge only if $\lambda_t < -c_{deg}$ (i.e., negative price or very low price):

$$P_i^{c*}(t) = \begin{cases} P_i^{max} & \text{if } \lambda_t < -c_{deg} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 7 — Profitability threshold:**

The minimum LMP spread required for arbitrage:

$$\Delta \lambda_{min} = 2 c_{deg}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda_{peak} - \lambda_{valley} > 2 c_{deg}}$$

**Conversion Logic Explanation:**
- This explains why batteries only arbitrage when peak-valley spread exceeds twice the degradation cost.
- For typical lithium-ion, $c_{deg} \approx ₹ 1.5/\text{kWh}$, so a spread $> ₹ 3/\text{kWh}$ is needed.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: VPP Service Stacking

A modern VPP provides **four simultaneous services** to maximize revenue:

| Service | Time Scale | Value Stream | Battery Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| Energy arbitrage | Hours | $\lambda_t$ spread | Charge valley, discharge peak |
| Frequency regulation | Seconds | $c_{reg}$ ₹/MW | Bidirectional, low SoC use |
| Voltage support | Sub-second | $c_{VAR}$ ₹/MVARh | Reactive power only |
| Demand charge reduction | Monthly | Tariff savings | Peak shaving |

**Stacked revenue per battery per day:**

$$R_{day} = R_{arb} + R_{reg} + R_{VAR} + R_{DC}$$

where:

$$R_{arb} = \sum_{t \in peak} \lambda_t P_{d} \Delta t - \sum_{t \in valley} \lambda_t P_{c} \Delta t$$

$$R_{reg} = c_{reg} \cdot MWh_{delivered}$$

$$R_{VAR} = c_{VAR} \cdot MVARh_{delivered}$$

$$R_{DC} = (\text{peak demand charge}) \cdot (kW_{reduced})$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A VPP aggregates three batteries with the following data:

| Battery | Capacity | SoC (initial) | Max Power |
|---|---|---|---|
| B1 | 100 kWh | 80 % | 50 kW |
| B2 | 100 kWh | 50 % | 50 kW |
| B3 | 100 kWh | 30 % | 50 kW |

Compute the SoC-balanced power dispatch using $k_{soc} = 0.5$ kW/%.

**Step 1 — Average SoC:**

$$SoC_{avg} = \frac{80 + 50 + 30}{3} = 53.33\%$$

**Step 2 — Power redistribution:**

$$P_i^{mod} = k_{soc}(SoC_i - SoC_{avg})$$

- B1: $0.5 \times (80 - 53.33) = +13.33$ kW (discharge more)
- B2: $0.5 \times (50 - 53.33) = -1.67$ kW (slight charge)
- B3: $0.5 \times (30 - 53.33) = -11.67$ kW (charge more)

**Step 3 — Net aggregation:**

$$P_{agg} = 13.33 - 1.67 - 11.67 = 0 \text{ kW}$$

The aggregate is **balanced** while individual units move toward SoC equality. ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Three-Tier Control Hierarchy Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Tier3["TERTIARY CONTROL\nGrid Market Dispatch\nTime Horizon: 15 min to 24 h"]
    Tier2["SECONDARY CONTROL\nEMS / MGCC\nTime Horizon: 1 s to 5 min"]
    Tier1["PRIMARY CONTROL\nPCS Inner Loop\nTime Horizon: sub-second"]

    DER1["DER 1: Solar PV"]
    DER2["DER 2: Wind"]
    DER3["DER 3: Battery BESS"]
    DER4["DER 4: Diesel Gen"]

    PCC["Point of Common Coupling"]
    Grid["Utility Grid"]

    Market["Day-Ahead Market\nLMP and Ancillary"]

    Market --> Tier3
    Tier3 -->|Set Points| Tier2
    Tier2 -->|Reference Commands| Tier1
    Tier1 --> DER1
    Tier1 --> DER2
    Tier1 --> DER3
    Tier1 --> DER4

    DER1 --> PCC
    DER2 --> PCC
    DER3 --> PCC
    DER4 --> PCC
    PCC --> Grid

    DER3 -.->|SoC Telemetry| Tier2
    Tier2 -.->|Status Feedback| Tier3
```

## 4.2 Virtual Power Plant Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HomeA["Home A\nPV 5kW\nBattery 10kWh"]
    HomeB["Home B\nPV 3kW\nBattery 7kWh"]
    HomeC["Home C\nPV 8kW\nBattery 14kWh"]
    CommA["Smart Meter A"]
    CommB["Smart Meter B"]
    CommC["Smart Meter C"]

    Cloud["VPP Cloud Platform\nAggregation Engine\nForecast AI\nMarket Interface"]

    Mkt["Wholesale Market\nDay-Ahead\nIntraday\nAncillary"]
    GridOp["Grid Operator\nISO / SLDC"]

    HomeA --- CommA
    HomeB --- CommB
    HomeC --- CommC

    CommA -->|Internet 4G/5G| Cloud
    CommB -->|Internet 4G/5G| Cloud
    CommC -->|Internet 4G/5G| Cloud

    Cloud -->|Bids and Dispatch| Mkt
    Cloud -->|Telemetry and Forecast| GridOp
    GridOp -->|Set Points and Commands| Cloud
```

## 4.3 Storage Dispatch Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start of Each Time Step])
    Read["Read LMP and SoC of All Batteries"]
    Decision1{"LMP greater than c_deg?"}
    Decision2{"LMP less than negative c_deg?"}

    Discharge["DISCHARGE ALL\nSet P_d to Max"]
    Idle["IDLE\nNo Power Exchange"]
    Charge["CHARGE ALL\nSet P_c to Max"]

    SoCChk{"SoC above 90 percent?"}
    SoCChk2{"SoC below 10 percent?"}

    End([End of Time Step])

    Start --> Read
    Read --> Decision1
    Decision1 -->|Yes| SoCChk
    Decision1 -->|No| Decision2
    Decision2 -->|Yes| SoCChk2
    Decision2 -->|No| Idle
    SoCChk -->|No| Discharge
    SoCChk -->|Yes| Idle
    SoCChk2 -->|No| Charge
    SoCChk2 -->|Yes| Idle
    Discharge --> End
    Charge --> End
    Idle --> End
```

## 4.4 Aggregation to VPP Mapping Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PhysicalLayer["PHYSICAL LAYER"]
        B1["Battery Bank 1\n100 kWh"]
        B2["Battery Bank 2\n200 kWh"]
        B3["Battery Bank 3\n50 kWh"]
        PV["Rooftop PV\nAggregated 250 kW"]
        Wind["Wind Microturbine\n100 kW"]
    end

    subgraph CommLayer["COMMUNICATION LAYER"]
        GW["Gateway IoT\nMQTT Protocol"]
        SCADA["SCADA Server\nDNP3 / IEC 61850"]
    end

    subgraph ControlLayer["CONTROL LAYER"]
        EMS["VPP Energy Management System"]
        OPT["Optimization Solver\nMILP"]
        FCST["Forecast Module\nLSTM Neural Network"]
    end

    subgraph MarketLayer["MARKET LAYER"]
        DA["Day-Ahead Bid"]
        ID["Intraday Adjustment"]
        ANC["Ancillary Service Bid"]
    end

    B1 --> GW
    B2 --> GW
    B3 --> GW
    PV --> GW
    Wind --> GW
    GW --> SCADA
    SCADA --> EMS
    FCST --> OPT
    OPT --> EMS
    EMS --> DA
    EMS --> ID
    EMS --> ANC
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Define the three-tier control hierarchy of a battery energy storage system with appropriate time horizons.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three-tier control hierarchy of a BESS consists of:

1. **Primary Control (Local Level):** Operates on sub-second timescale (< 100 ms). Implemented in the Power Conversion System (PCS) for inner current/voltage loops, PLL synchronization, and hardware protection.

2. **Secondary Control (Supervisory Level):** Operates from 1 second to 5 minutes. Executed by the EMS or MGCC to restore nominal frequency/voltage, perform SoC balancing, and coordinate multiple storage units.

3. **Tertiary Control (Grid Level):** Operates from 15 minutes to 24 hours. Handles economic dispatch, day-ahead market bidding, and inter-area power exchange.

*(Marks distribution: 1 mark each tier + 0 mark for time horizons if missing. Examiner allocates 0.5 marks per correct time horizon.)*

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**What is a Virtual Power Plant (VPP)? How does it differ from a conventional power plant?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Virtual Power Plant (VPP)** is an ICT-based aggregation framework that combines multiple distributed energy resources (DERs) — including solar PV, wind, batteries, and controllable loads — into a single dispatchable entity that can participate in electricity markets and provide grid services.

**Difference from Conventional Power Plant:**

| Feature | Conventional PP | VPP |
|---|---|---|
| Physical asset | Centralized | Distributed |
| Construction cost | High (₹ crores) | Low (software) |
| Ramp rate | Slow (thermal) | Fast (inverter-based) |
| Location | Fixed | Rooftops, distributed |
| Capacity | 100–1000 MW | 5–500 MW equivalent |

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Set A]

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the management and control hierarchy of a grid-connected battery energy storage system with a neat block diagram. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** A Virtual Power Plant aggregates three residential batteries with the following parameters:

| Battery | Rated Energy | Max Charge/Discharge Power | Initial SoC |
|---|---|---|---|
| B1 | 10 kWh | 5 kW | 90 % |
| B2 | 15 kWh | 7 kW | 40 % |
| B3 | 8 kWh | 4 kW | 60 % |

If the SoC-balancing gain is $k_{soc} = 0.2$ kW/% and the round-trip efficiency is $\eta = 0.90$, compute:

(i) The average SoC
(ii) The SoC-equalized power redistribution for each battery
(iii) The aggregate net power at the PCC
(iv) Comment on the balancing behaviour. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Detailed Solution:**

The management and control hierarchy of a grid-connected BESS consists of three distinct tiers, each with unique time scale, function, and communication requirements.

**Tier 1 — Primary Control:**

This is the **innermost, fastest** control loop. It operates in the **sub-second** domain (1 ms to 100 ms) and is typically implemented in the digital signal processor (DSP) or field-programmable gate array (FPGA) of the bidirectional inverter. The primary control has three main functions:

(i) **Inner current control:** Uses $dq$-frame proportional-integral (PI) controllers to regulate the inverter output current. The $d$-axis controls active power and the $q$-axis controls reactive power.

(ii) **Phase-Locked Loop (PLL):** Synchronizes the inverter output with the grid voltage using a Synchronous Reference Frame PLL (SRF-PLL).

(iii) **Hardware protection:** Monitors DC-link voltage, semiconductor junction temperature, and grid voltage to trigger instantaneous shutdown (within 50 µs) under fault conditions.

**Tier 2 — Secondary Control:**

This tier operates in the **1 second to 5 minute** window. It is typically implemented in the **Microgrid Central Controller (MGCC)** or **Plant Controller**. Its key responsibilities include:

(i) **Frequency and voltage restoration:** Adds integral corrective terms to eliminate steady-state error introduced by primary droop.

(ii) **State-of-Charge (SoC) balancing:** Modifies droop coefficients adaptively to ensure all batteries reach equilibrium.

(iii) **Power sharing:** Allocates power commands among parallel inverters to optimize efficiency and minimize circulating currents.

(iv) **Mode transition:** Seamlessly switches between grid-connected and islanded modes (per IEEE 1547.4).

**Tier 3 — Tertiary Control:**

This is the **slowest, highest-level** controller, operating from **15 minutes to 24 hours**. It is typically implemented in a **utility SCADA or Energy Management System (EMS)** and performs:

(i) **Economic dispatch:** Minimizes total generation cost subject to demand-supply balance.

(ii) **Market participation:** Submits bids into day-ahead, intraday, and ancillary service markets.

(iii) **Renewable forecasting:** Uses AI/ML models to predict PV, wind, and load for the next 24–48 hours.

(iv) **Long-term SoC planning:** Ensures batteries have sufficient stored energy to meet expected demand peaks.

**Block Diagram (refer to Section 4.1).**

*(Mark distribution: 3 marks description + 2 marks Tier 2 functions + 2 marks Tier 3 functions. Examiner's note: Candidates who omit time horizons lose 1 mark.)*

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- B1: 10 kWh, 5 kW, SoC = 90 %
- B2: 15 kWh, 7 kW, SoC = 40 %
- B3: 8 kWh, 4 kW, SoC = 60 %
- $k_{soc} = 0.2$ kW/%, $\eta = 0.90$

**(i) Average SoC:**

$$SoC_{avg} = \frac{90 + 40 + 60}{3} = \frac{190}{3} = 63.33\%$$

**(ii) SoC-equalized power redistribution:**

Using the formula $P_i^{mod} = k_{soc} (SoC_i - SoC_{avg})$:

- B1: $P_1^{mod} = 0.2 \times (90 - 63.33) = 0.2 \times 26.67 = +5.33$ kW
- B2: $P_2^{mod} = 0.2 \times (40 - 63.33) = 0.2 \times (-23.33) = -4.67$ kW
- B3: $P_3^{mod} = 0.2 \times (60 - 63.33) = 0.2 \times (-3.33) = -0.67$ kW

**Capacity constraints check:**
- B1: $|5.33| < 5$ kW → violates. So $P_1^{mod} = +5$ kW (saturated)
- B2: $|-4.67| < 7$ kW → OK
- B3: $|-0.67| < 4$ kW → OK

**(iii) Aggregate net power at PCC:**

$$P_{agg} = 5 - 4.67 - 0.67 = -0.34 \text{ kW}$$

Effectively zero net flow — the VPP is **internally balancing**.

**(iv) Comment:**

Since $SoC_1 > SoC_2 > SoC_3$ is *not* strictly monotonic with capacity weighting, B1 (highest SoC) discharges while B2 and B3 (lower SoC) charge. Over successive cycles, all SoCs converge to ~63 % if net demand is zero. The slight negative aggregate of $-0.34$ kW represents the round-trip losses: the lost 10 % is consumed as heat.

*(Mark distribution: 2 marks for (i), 3 marks for (ii), 1 mark for (iii), 1 mark for (iv). Full marks require showing the constraint check explicitly.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Set B]

**Q. (a)** What is a Virtual Power Plant (VPP)? With a block diagram, explain how energy storage systems and distributed generation are aggregated in a VPP to provide grid services. List any four grid services a VPP can offer. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** A VPP operator bids into the day-ahead market with the following LMP schedule:

| Hour | LMP (₹/kWh) | Hour | LMP (₹/kWh) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2.5 | 13 | 6.0 |
| 5 | 2.0 | 18 | 9.0 |
| 9 | 4.0 | 22 | 5.0 |
| 12 | 5.5 | 24 | 3.0 |

If the operator has a 100 kWh battery with 90 % round-trip efficiency, 50 kW max power, and degradation cost $c_{deg} = 1.5$ ₹/kWh, determine:

(i) The profitable charge and discharge hours
(ii) The peak-valley spread and whether arbitrage is profitable
(iii) The maximum daily arbitrage revenue. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a):**

A VPP is a software-defined aggregation layer that combines DERs into a market-visible entity. Block diagram (refer to Section 4.4).

**Four grid services:**
1. **Energy arbitrage** — buy low, sell high
2. **Frequency regulation** — fast ramp up/down
3. **Voltage support** — reactive power injection
4. **Peak shaving / demand charge reduction**

*(Marks: 2 + 2 + 1.5 + 1.5 distribution)*

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Profitable charge and discharge hours:**

Discharge threshold: $\lambda_t > c_{deg} = 1.5$ ₹/kWh → **all hours qualify**.
Charge threshold: $\lambda_t < -c_{deg}$ → **never (LMP is always positive)**.

- **Discharge hours** (highest LMP): Hours 18 (₹ 9.0), 13 (₹ 6.0), 12 (₹ 5.5), 22 (₹ 5.0)
- **Charge hours** (lowest LMP): Hours 5 (₹ 2.0), 1 (₹ 2.5)

**(ii) Peak-valley spread:**

$$\Delta\lambda = \lambda_{max} - \lambda_{min} = 9.0 - 2.0 = 7.0 \text{ ₹/kWh}$$

For profitability: $\Delta\lambda > 2c_{deg} = 3.0$ ₹/kWh.

Since $7.0 > 3.0$, arbitrage **IS profitable**. ✓

**(iii) Maximum daily arbitrage revenue:**

Battery constraints: $E = 100$ kWh, $P_{max} = 50$ kW.
- Max energy throughput per cycle = $50$ kWh (1 hour at full power)
- With efficiency: $50 \times 0.9 = 45$ kWh delivered from 50 kWh charged.

Revenue from 1 cycle:
- Discharge: $50 \text{ kW} \times 9.0 = ₹ 450$
- Charge cost: $50 \text{ kW} \times 2.0 = ₹ 100$
- Degradation: $50 \times 1.5 + 50 \times 1.5 = ₹ 150$

$$R_{cycle} = 450 - 100 - 150 = ₹ 200 \text{ per cycle}$$

If only 1 cycle is feasible due to SoC limits:

$$\boxed{R_{daily} = ₹ 200}$$

*(Marks: 2 + 2 + 3. Full marks require explicit mention of efficiency and degradation costs.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing VPP with Microgrid:** VPP is *market-focused* (commercial aggregation); Microgrid is *reliability-focused* (islanding). Examiners deduct 1 mark if confused.
> 2. **Forgetting round-trip efficiency:** Many students write $E_{discharged} = E_{charged}$. This is WRONG. Always include $\eta < 1$ for KTU numerical problems.
> 3. **Not bounding SoC:** If a candidate's solution violates $0.2 \leq SoC \leq 0.9$, examiner deducts 1 mark.
> 4. **Skipping the constraint check:** In SoC-equalization problems, always check whether the calculated power exceeds $P_{max}$. Many candidates skip this and lose 1 mark.
> 5. **Writing "Primary Control = BMS":** This is incorrect. Primary control is the **PCS/inverter** loop; BMS is a separate protection system. Examiners flag this.
> 6. **Using $P$ instead of signed convention:** In VPP power equations, clearly state charging is negative and discharging is positive (or vice versa) and stick to it. Mixed conventions lose 1 mark.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### High-Density Revision Checklist

- [ ] The control hierarchy has **exactly three tiers**: Primary (sub-second, PCS), Secondary (1 s–5 min, EMS), Tertiary (15 min–24 h, market/grid).
- [ ] **Primary droop equation:** $P_i = P_i^{ref} - k_p(f - f^{ref})$ — fundamental for parallel inverter operation.
- [ ] **Secondary control restores nominal frequency** using integral action (eliminates steady-state error).
- [ ] **SoC balancing** uses adaptive droop: $k_{soc}(SoC_i - SoC_{avg})$ modifies power dispatch.
- [ ] **Aggregation formula** at PCC: signed sum of all DER outputs minus load, with efficiency multipliers on storage terms.
- [ ] **VPP ≠ physical plant** — it is a software aggregation layer.
- [ ] **VPP provides four services:** arbitrage, regulation, voltage support, demand charge reduction.
- [ ] **Arbitrage profitable only if:** $\Delta\lambda_{peak-valley} > 2c_{deg}$.
- [ ] **Degradation cost** $c_{deg}$ must be subtracted from both charge and discharge revenue (bidirectional penalty).
- [ ] **Round-trip efficiency** $\eta$ multiplies charge energy: $E_{out} = \eta \cdot E_{in}$.
- [ ] **SoC dynamic equation:** $SoC(t+1) = SoC(t) - \dfrac{\eta P \Delta t}{E_{rated}}$ — remember the sign convention.
- [ ] **Time scales must always be stated** in hierarchy-related answers; missing time horizons = -1 mark guaranteed.
- [ ] **Standard operating SoC window:** 20 %–80 % for lithium-ion to maximize cycle life.
- [ ] **VPP bid size** for market participation: minimum 1 MW (varies by jurisdiction).
- [ ] **Communication protocol:** IEC 61850 for substation, MQTT for IoT aggregation, DNP3 for SCADA.

### Memory Aid — "3-2-1 Hierarchy"

- **3** tiers
- **2** droop coefficients ($k_p$, $k_q$)
- **1** unified VPP entity at the top

### Numerical Answer Format Template (For KTU Exams)

When solving a VPP/storage problem, always structure as:

1. **Given:** List all parameters with units
2. **To Find:** State what is being computed
3. **Formula:** Write the governing equation
4. **Substitution:** Plug numbers with units
5. **Result:** Box the final answer with units
6. **Comment:** Physical interpretation in 1–2 sentences

This 6-step format consistently earns full marks in KTU valuation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Battery SCADA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET521 - ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Battery SCADA

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Battery SCADA (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition)** is a centralized, real-time industrial control architecture specifically engineered to monitor, control, and optimize the operational performance of large-scale **Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS)**. It acts as the supervisory intelligence layer that sits above the local **Battery Management System (BMS)** and the **Power Conversion System (PCS)**, aggregating thousands of telemetry points (cell voltage, module temperature, State-of-Charge, State-of-Health, DC bus voltage, and grid frequency) and presenting them to operators through a graphical **Human-Machine Interface (HMI)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Battery SCADA is a hierarchical control system that combines data acquisition (sensors, RTUs/PLCs), data communication (Modbus TCP, DNP3, IEC 61850), and supervisory control (SCADA server, HMI) to ensure safe, reliable, and economically optimal operation of grid-connected and microgrid battery installations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Air Traffic Controller of the Battery"

Imagine a busy international airport. Hundreds of aircraft (battery cells/modules) are constantly moving on the ground, taking off, and landing. You cannot let them collide, run out of fuel, or land on a closed runway. You need a **central control tower** that:

- **Sees** every plane's position, speed, and fuel level in real time (SCADA monitoring).
- **Talks** to pilots using a standardized radio language (SCADA communication protocols like Modbus/DNP3).
- **Issues commands** to redirect, hold, or change altitude (Supervisory Control setpoints).
- **Logs** every event for later analysis (Historical Data Archiving).
- **Alarms** the operator when a runway is blocked (Alarm Management).

A **Battery SCADA** performs exactly this function for thousands of electrochemical cells. Without it, the BESS would be a "blind battery" — unable to communicate with the grid operator, unable to self-protect, and unable to participate in energy market bidding.

## 1.3 Key Metrics in a Battery SCADA System

- **Scan Time (ms):** Time interval between successive polls of a field device. Modern systems use **100–1000 ms** refresh rates.
- **I/O Density:** Number of analog/digital points a single RTU can handle (typically 256 to 8192 points).
- **Availability (%):** Target reliability of the SCADA infrastructure (utility-grade: **99.95%** or higher).
- **Latency (ms):** End-to-end delay from sensor to operator screen (target: **< 2 seconds** for grid services).
- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):** Critical SCADA servers are specified at **> 100,000 hours**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Insight:** When asked to define Battery SCADA, students often confuse it with the BMS.
