The **BMS** operates at the *cell/pack level* (hardware-level safety), while the **SCADA** operates at the *system/plant level* (operational visibility and grid integration). Always state this distinction explicitly in 14-mark answers.

## 1.4 The Three-Layer Architecture (At a Glance)

| Layer | Function | Typical Hardware |
|-------|----------|------------------|
| **Field Level** | Sensing & Actuation | Current shunts, RTDs, contactors, DC isolators |
| **Control Level** | Data Acquisition & Local Logic | RTU, PLC, BMS Gateway, PCS Controller |
| **Supervisory Level** | Visualization & Remote Control | SCADA Server, HMI, Historian DB, Operator Workstation |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — The SCADA Data Lifecycle

A Battery SCADA system follows a strict cyclic data flow, executed continuously at high frequency. The lifecycle is broken down into the following engineering steps:

1. **Signal Acquisition:** Voltage dividers, Hall-effect current sensors, and Resistance Temperature Detectors (RTDs) convert physical quantities into standardized **4–20 mA** or **0–10 V** electrical signals.
2. **Signal Conditioning:** Analog signals are filtered, linearized, and scaled (e.g., a 0–5 V signal mapped to 0–1500 V DC).
3. **A/D Conversion & Polling:** The RTU/PLC samples the signal and converts it to a 16-bit digital value. The SCADA master polls the RTU cyclically using a request-response protocol.
4. **Data Processing:** Engineering unit conversion, dead-band filtering, alarming thresholds (HH, H, L, LL), and rate-of-change detection are applied.
5. **Supervisory Storage:** Validated values are written to the **Process Database** (real-time) and **Historian** (long-term archive with time-series compression).
6. **HMI Rendering:** The HMI client subscribes to tag updates via OPC UA / TCP and updates the mimic diagram with the new value, typically using color-coding (Green=Normal, Yellow=Warning, Red=Alarm).
7. **Control Action:** The operator issues a command (e.g., "charge at 0.5C"); the command travels down the same communication path to the actuator (PCS reference setpoint).
8. **Event Logging:** Every state change, alarm, and operator action is timestamped (to **millisecond accuracy** using IEEE C37.118 / IRIG-B time sync) and stored in an immutable Sequence of Events (SOE) log.

## 2.2 Core Engineering Metrics (KTU High-Yield Formulas)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** All formulas below use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of the vertical pipe `|` to preserve the markdown table integrity.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Application |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | **SOC (State of Charge)** | $SOC(t) = SOC_0 + \dfrac{1}{C_{rated}} \displaystyle\int_{t_0}^{t} \eta_c \cdot I_{bat}(\tau) \, d\tau$ | % (or fraction) | Energy availability tracking |
| 2 | **SOH (State of Health)** | $SOH = \dfrac{C_{actual}}{C_{rated}} \times 100\%$ | % | End-of-life prediction |
| 3 | **State of Energy (SOE)** | $SOE = \dfrac{E_{remaining}}{E_{rated}}$ | % | Power dispatch scheduling |
| 4 | **Cell Voltage Deviation** | $\Delta V_{cell} = \vert V_{cell,i} - \bar{V}_{cell} \vert$ | V | Pack imbalance detection |
| 5 | **C-Rate** | $C = \dfrac{\vert I \vert}{C_{rated}}$ | dimensionless | Charge/discharge intensity |
| 6 | **Data Refresh Rate** | $f_{refresh} = \dfrac{1}{T_{scan}}$ | Hz | Real-time monitoring responsiveness |
| 7 | **RTE (Round-Trip Efficiency)** | $\eta_{RTE} = \dfrac{E_{discharged}}{E_{charged}} \times 100\%$ | % | System economic analysis |
| 8 | **Power (Instantaneous)** | $P(t) = V_{dc}(t) \cdot I_{dc}(t)$ | W or kW | PCS dispatch control |
| 9 | **Thermal Model (Lumped)** | $C_{th} \dfrac{dT}{dt} = I^2 R_{int} - hA(T - T_{amb})$ | K/s | Temperature forecasting |
| 10 | **Alarm Dead-band Hysteresis** | $H_{db} = \vert V_{trip} - V_{reset} \vert$ | V or °C | Alarm chattering prevention |

## 2.3 Communication Protocols in Battery SCADA

The SCADA layer must interoperate with heterogeneous subsystems. The following protocols are mandatory knowledge for KTU exams:

- **Modbus TCP/IP (Port 502):** De-facto standard for BMS and PCS. Register-based polling. Simple, open, widely supported.
- **DNP3 (Distributed Network Protocol):** Heavy use in North American utilities. Event-driven reporting, time-stamped SOE.
- **IEC 61850:** Substation automation standard. Uses GOOSE messaging for sub-3 ms peer-to-peer protection signaling (used for fast grid-fault ride-through).
- **OPC UA (Unified Architecture):** Modern, platform-independent, with built-in security (TLS 1.2/1.3) and information modeling. Becoming the de-facto standard for new BESS installations.
- **MQTT / Sparkplug B:** Lightweight publish-subscribe protocol for cloud/edge telemetry (used when SCADA is connected to a cloud analytics platform).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

A well-engineered Battery SCADA is the cornerstone of:

- **Grid Ancillary Services:** Frequency Regulation (Primary/Secondary response within 1–4 seconds), Voltage Support (var dispatch), Spinning Reserve.
- **Microgrid Islanding & Re-synchronization:** SCADA orchestrates the seamless transition between grid-tied and islanded modes by issuing black-start sequences.
- **Energy Arbitrage:** Tracks Time-of-Use (ToU) tariffs and dispatches charge/discharge commands to maximize revenue.
- **Predictive Maintenance:** Uses historical SOE degradation curves to schedule cell replacement before catastrophic failure.
- **Regulatory Compliance:** Provides auditable event logs required by grid codes (e.g., CEA Technical Standards for Connectivity in India, IEEE 1547 in the US, IEC 62933 in the EU).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: State-of-Charge (Coulomb-Counting Method) Used by SCADA

The SCADA platform typically uses a **Coulomb-Counting** algorithm executed either in the BMS or in the supervisory server. The derivation from first principles is as follows:

**Step 1 — Definition of charge delivered:**
The electrical charge $Q$ delivered by a battery is defined as the time-integral of the current.

$$
Q(t) = \int_{t_0}^{t} I_{bat}(\tau) \, d\tau
$$

**Step 2 — Relate to rated capacity:**
The rated capacity $C_{rated}$ (in Ampere-hours) represents the total usable charge. The fractional charge extracted is therefore:

$$
\Delta SOC = \frac{Q(t)}{C_{rated}} = \frac{1}{C_{rated}} \int_{t_0}^{t} I_{bat}(\tau) \, d\tau
$$

**Step 3 — Include Coulombic efficiency:**
Because the charge reaction has internal losses, the charging efficiency $\eta_c$ (typically 0.97–0.99 for Li-ion) must be applied to the charging portion. Discharging is normalized to 1.0. We combine this into a signed-efficiency term $\eta(I)$:

$$
SOC(t) = SOC(t_0) + \frac{1}{C_{rated}} \int_{t_0}^{t} \eta(I) \cdot I_{bat}(\tau) \, d\tau
$$

**Step 4 — Discretization for SCADA implementation:**
The SCADA cannot compute true integrals; it samples at discrete intervals $\Delta t$. Using the **rectangular (left-point) rule**:

$$
SOC[k] = SOC[k-1] + \frac{\eta_c \cdot I_{bat}[k] \cdot \Delta t}{C_{rated} \times 3600}
$$

where $I_{bat}[k]$ is the sampled current in Amperes, $\Delta t$ is the SCADA scan period in seconds, and the factor of 3600 converts seconds to hours to match the Ah capacity unit.

**Step 5 — Error analysis:**
The cumulative error after $N$ samples is bounded by:

$$
\epsilon_{SOC} \le N \cdot \frac{\eta_c \cdot I_{max} \cdot \Delta t}{2 \cdot C_{rated} \times 3600}
$$

This is why SCADA systems implement **periodic full-charge recalibration** (a known-SOC reset) to cancel drift.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Pack Imbalance Detection (Cell-Deviation Index)

When the SCADA monitors thousands of cells, it must detect when individual cells drift from the pack mean. The **Cell Deviation Index (CDI)** is derived as follows:

**Step 1 — Pack average voltage:**
With $N$ cells in a series string, the mean cell voltage is:

$$
\bar{V}_{cell} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} V_i
$$

**Step 2 — Deviation per cell:**
The deviation of the $i^{th}$ cell from the mean is:

$$
\delta V_i = V_i - \bar{V}_{cell}
$$

**Step 3 — RMS deviation (the SCADA alarm trigger):**
To characterize the spread, the SCADA computes the root-mean-square:

$$
\sigma_{cell} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( V_i - \bar{V}_{cell} \right)^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Alarm threshold logic:**
If $\sigma_{cell} > \sigma_{threshold}$ (typically 30–50 mV for Li-ion), the SCADA raises a "Pack Imbalance" alarm and may command the BMS to enable active balancing.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Round-Trip Efficiency from SCADA Logs

The historian stores the time-stamped power profile. The RTE over a charge-discharge cycle is:

**Step 1 — Energy charged (numerical integration of SCADA samples):**

$$
E_{ch} = \sum_{k=1}^{N_{ch}} V_{dc}[k] \cdot I_{dc}[k] \cdot \Delta t
$$

**Step 2 — Energy discharged:**

$$
E_{dis} = \sum_{k=1}^{N_{dis}} V_{dc}[k] \cdot I_{dc}[k] \cdot \Delta t
$$

**Step 3 — Round-Trip Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{RTE} = \frac{E_{dis}}{E_{ch}} \times 100\%
$$

For a 1 MWh grid-scale Li-ion BESS, a healthy $\eta_{RTE}$ is in the range **85–92%**. SCADA dashboards display this as a key KPI for the asset owner.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — SCADA Subsystem Cross-Reference

| SCADA Function | Sensing Source | Communication | Typical Setpoint / Alarm | Engineering Justification |
|----------------|----------------|---------------|--------------------------|---------------------------|
| **Cell Voltage** | BMS Analog Front-End | Modbus TCP to Gateway | Alarm: $V < 2.5\,V$ or $V > 4.2\,V$ | Prevent Li-plating / thermal runaway |
| **Module Temperature** | NTC Thermistor | Modbus RTU to BMS | Alarm: $T > 60\,°C$ | SEI layer growth is exponential |
| **SOC** | Coulomb Counter | OPC UA to SCADA | Control: $SOC \in [20\%, 95\%]$ | Avoid deep discharge & overcharge |
| **DC Bus Voltage** | Voltage Divider | DNP3 to RTU | Trip: $V_{dc} > 1100\,V$ | PCS IGBT over-voltage protection |
| **PCS Active Power** | PCS Controller | IEC 61850 MMS | Setpoint: $P_{ref} \in [-1, +1]\,pu$ | Grid frequency response |
| **Fire/Smoke Sensor** | Aspirating Detector | Hard-wired DI | Trip: Contact-open → Emergency Stop | NFPA 855 / IS 17394 compliance |
| **HVAC Status** | Differential Pressure | Modbus TCP | Alarm: $T_{in} > 35\,°C$ | Maintain thermal envelope |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Battery SCADA — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the hierarchical flow of telemetry and control commands in a utility-scale Battery SCADA architecture, from the electrochemical cell to the cloud analytics layer.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph fieldLevel["FIELD LEVEL - Battery Cells and Actuators"]
        cellA["Li-ion Cell Pack 1"]
        cellB["Li-ion Cell Pack 2"]
        cellC["Power Conversion System PCS"]
        cellD["HVAC and Fire Panel"]
        cellE["DC Disconnect Switchgear"]
    end

    subgraph controlLevel["CONTROL LEVEL - Data Acquisition"]
        bmsA["BMS Master 1"]
        bmsB["BMS Master 2"]
        rtuA["RTU - Substation IED"]
        plcA["PLC - Site Controller"]
    end

    subgraph supervisoryLevel["SUPERVISORY LEVEL - SCADA Core"]
        serverA["SCADA Redundant Server A"]
        serverB["SCADA Redundant Server B"]
        histA["Historian Database"]
        hmiA["HMI Operator Workstation"]
        hmiB["Engineer Workstation"]
    end

    subgraph cloudLevel["ENTERPRISE AND CLOUD LAYER"]
        cloudA["Cloud Analytics Platform"]
        cloudB["Utility Control Center EMS"]
        cloudC["Energy Market Bidding API"]
    end

    cellA -- "Modbus RTU" --> bmsA
    cellB -- "Modbus RTU" --> bmsB
    cellC -- "IEC 61850 MMS" --> rtuA
    cellD -- "Digital Inputs 24 VDC" --> plcA
    cellE -- "Auxiliary Contacts" --> plcA

    bmsA -- "Modbus TCP Gateway" --> serverA
    bmsB -- "Modbus TCP Gateway" --> serverA
    rtuA -- "DNP3 over TCP" --> serverA
    plcA -- "OPC UA" --> serverA

    serverA <-- "Redundant Failover Link" --> serverB
    serverA -- "SQL Write" --> histA
    serverA -- "OPC UA Publish" --> hmiA
    serverA -- "Read Write Engineering" --> hmiB

    serverA -- "MQTT Sparkplug B" --> cloudA
    serverA -- "ICCP IEC 60870" --> cloudB
    serverA -- "REST API" --> cloudC
```

## 4.2 Sequence Diagram — Operator-Initiated Discharge Command

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant OP as Operator HMI
    participant SC as SCADA Server
    participant HIS as Historian
    participant BMS as BMS Controller
    participant PCS as PCS Inverter
    participant GRID as Grid Point of Interconnection

    OP->>SC: "Set Active Power = +500 kW (Discharge)"
    SC->>SC: Validate SOC > 20 percent and Interlock Closed
    SC->>HIS: Log Command with Operator ID and Timestamp
    SC->>BMS: Set P_ref = 500 kW (Modbus Register 40021)
    BMS->>BMS: Check Cell Voltages and Temperature Limits
    BMS-->>SC: Acknowledge Command (Modbus 0x06 response)
    SC->>PCS: Enable Grid-Following Mode (IEC 61850)
    PCS->>GRID: Synchronize Phase and Frequency
    PCS-->>SC: Status = "Discharging, 498 kW"
    SC->>HIS: Record 1-second Power Telemetry
    SC-->>OP: HMI Updates - Green "Discharge Active" Indicator
```

## 4.3 Alarm Escalation Hierarchy (State Machine)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Normal
    Normal --> Advisory : Parameter exceeds Warning Limit
    Advisory --> Warning : Warning persists for T_seconds
    Warning --> Critical : Critical Threshold Breached
    Critical --> Emergency : SOE 0 percent OR Thermal Runaway Sensor
    Emergency --> Shutdown : Hard-wired E-Stop Circuit Open
    Shutdown --> Lockout : Manual Engineer Reset Required
    Lockout --> Normal : After Maintenance Sign-off
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define Battery SCADA. List its four primary functions.**

**Model Answer:**
Battery SCADA (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition) is a centralized industrial control system used for the real-time monitoring, control, and data acquisition of a Battery Energy Storage System (BESS). It interfaces with the BMS and PCS to provide plant-level visibility and remote operation.

**Four Primary Functions:**
1. **Data Acquisition:** Continuous polling of field sensors (voltage, current, temperature, SOC, SOH).
2. **Supervisory Control:** Issuing setpoints to PCS (active/reactive power) and to the BMS (charge/discharge enable).
3. **Visualization:** Rendering the plant mimic diagram on the HMI with real-time color-coded status.
4. **Alarm and Event Management:** Time-stamped logging, escalation, and operator notification of abnormal conditions.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for the four functions)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Distinguish between a Battery Management System (BMS) and a Battery SCADA.**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | BMS | Battery SCADA |
|-------|-----|---------------|
| **Scope** | Cell/Pack level (hardware safety) | Plant/System level (operations) |
| **Primary Role** | Cell balancing, over-voltage/cut-off protection, thermal management | Energy dispatch, grid interaction, KPI dashboards |
| **Response Time** | Sub-millisecond (hardware-level) | 100 ms – 1 s (software-level) |
| **Interface** | CAN bus / SPI to cell ASICs | Modbus / OPC UA / DNP3 / IEC 61850 to external systems |
| **Failure Impact** | Cell damage / fire if BMS fails | Loss of visibility / dispatch if SCADA fails (BMS still protects) |

*(3 marks: 1 for BMS role, 1 for SCADA role, 1 for distinction table)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **three-tier architecture of a Battery SCADA system** for a 10 MWh grid-scale BESS. Describe the role of each tier and the communication protocols used. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A Battery SCADA for a 10 MWh grid-scale BESS employs a **hierarchical three-tier architecture** to ensure scalability, fault isolation, and real-time performance.

**Tier 1 — Field Level (Sensing and Actuation):**
This tier consists of the physical sensors and actuators directly interfaced with the BESS hardware. It includes:
- **Cell-level sensors:** Voltage monitoring ICs (e.g., LTC6811), NTC thermistors per cell.
- **String-level sensors:** Hall-effect DC current transducers, DC voltage dividers.
- **Environment sensors:** Smoke detectors, hydrogen gas detectors, humidity sensors.
- **Actuators:** DC contactors, AC circuit breakers, HVAC dampers, fire suppression solenoids.

The analog signals are converted to **4–20 mA** or **Modbus RTU** digital frames at this layer.

**Tier 2 — Control Level (Data Acquisition and Local Logic):**
This tier consists of the **RTUs, PLCs, and BMS masters** that perform deterministic local control. Functions include:
- Cyclic polling of field devices (scan time **250–500 ms**).
- Executing protection logic (e.g., trip the DC contactor if any cell exceeds 4.25 V).
- Time-stamping events with **IRIG-B GPS synchronization** to ±1 ms.
- Acting as protocol gateways (e.g., Modbus RTU → Modbus TCP).

**Tier 3 — Supervisory Level (SCADA Core):**
This tier is the "brain" of the system and includes:
- **Redundant SCADA Servers** in hot-standby (failover < 5 seconds).
- **Historian Database** (e.g., OSIsoft PI, Wonder Historian) for time-series archival.
- **HMI Workstations** displaying the single-line diagram, alarms, and trends.
- **Application Software** for SOC-based dispatch, peak-shaving logic, and grid-code compliance reporting.

**Communication Protocols:**
- Field to Control: **Modbus RTU, CAN bus**
- Control to Supervisory: **Modbus TCP, DNP3, IEC 61850**
- Supervisory to Enterprise: **OPC UA, ICCP, MQTT**

This architecture ensures that a failure in Tier 3 does not compromise the safety logic in Tier 2.

---

**(b)** Explain the **SOC, SOH, and SOE parameters** monitored by the Battery SCADA. Derive the Coulomb-counting expression for SOC and discuss **two sources of SOC estimation error** in the SCADA platform. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition of Parameters:**
- **State of Charge (SOC):** The available capacity expressed as a percentage of rated capacity. $SOC \in [0\%, 100\%]$.
- **State of Health (SOH):** The maximum deliverable capacity today as a percentage of the original rated capacity. $SOH \le 100\%$.
- **State of Energy (SOE):** The available energy (kWh) relative to rated energy, accounting for voltage-dependent discharge profiles.

**Derivation of Coulomb-Counting SOC:**

Starting from the definition of charge:

$$
Q(t) = \int_{t_0}^{t} I_{bat}(\tau) \, d\tau
$$

Dividing by rated capacity $C_{rated}$ (in Ah) and applying Coulombic efficiency $\eta_c$ for the charge phase:

$$
SOC(t) = SOC(t_0) + \frac{\eta_c}{C_{rated}} \int_{t_0}^{t} I_{bat}(\tau) \, d\tau
$$

For SCADA's discrete sampling at interval $\Delta t$:

$$
SOC[k] = SOC[k-1] + \frac{\eta_c \cdot I_{bat}[k] \cdot \Delta t}{3600 \cdot C_{rated}}
$$

**Two Sources of SOC Error:**
1. **Current Sensor Offset (Zero-Drift):** Hall-effect sensors have a small residual offset (typically ±10–50 mA). Over thousands of samples, this offset integrates into a significant SOC error, causing the SCADA's reported SOC to drift from the true SOC. This is corrected by periodic full-charge calibration.
2. **Capacity Fade:** As the battery ages, $C_{rated}$ is no longer constant. The SCADA must adapt the rated capacity using the SOH estimate; otherwise, it will systematically over-report SOC at the end of life.

*(14 marks: 7 for part a, 7 for part b)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**(a)** Discuss the **role of communication protocols** (Modbus TCP, DNP3, IEC 61850, OPC UA) in a Battery SCADA. Compare any two of them in terms of data model, security, and typical use-case. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Communication protocols are the "lingua franca" of the Battery SCADA, enabling heterogeneous subsystems (BMS, PCS, fire panel, utility EMS) to interoperate.

**Modbus TCP/IP:**
- **Origin:** Schneider Electric, 1979 (RTU), 1999 (TCP).
- **Data Model:** Flat 16-bit register map (Coils, Holding Registers, Input Registers). Master-slave polling.
- **Security:** None inherent (cleartext, no authentication). Must be firewalled.
- **Use Case:** Default protocol for **BMS-to-SCADA** and **PCS-to-SCADA** integration. Simple, ubiquitous, low cost.

**DNP3 (Distributed Network Protocol):**
- **Origin:** IEEE 1815 standard, originated in North American utilities.
- **Data Model:** Object-oriented with binary/analog/counter inputs and outputs. Supports unsolicited reporting and time-stamped SOE.
- **Security:** DNP3-SA adds authentication; modern DNP3 over TLS.
- **Use Case:** Substation IEDs (RTUs) and **utility-grade BESS interfacing with the control center**.

**IEC 61850:**
- **Origin:** IEC, 2003 (substation automation).
- **Data Model:** Object-oriented, semantically rich (Logical Nodes like ZBAT, ZCON). GOOSE messages for sub-3 ms peer-to-peer.
- **Security:** Built-in multicast and VLAN segmentation.
- **Use Case:** **Fast grid-fault ride-through, protection signaling** between PCS, BMS, and substation IEDs.

**OPC UA (Unified Architecture):**
- **Origin:** OPC Foundation, 2008.
- **Data Model:** Object-oriented, address-space modeled, with information modeling (Companion Specs for batteries in development).
- **Security:** End-to-end encryption (TLS 1.2/1.3), X.509 certificates, role-based access.
- **Use Case:** **Cloud integration, Industry 4.0, modern greenfield BESS** requiring secure edge-to-cloud data flow.

**Comparison (Modbus vs OPC UA):**

| Feature | Modbus TCP | OPC UA |
|---------|------------|--------|
| Data Model | Flat register map | Object-oriented information model |
| Security | None built-in | TLS 1.2/1.3 + Certificates |
| Subscriptions | Polling only | Publish-Subscribe (Pub/Sub) |
| Best For | Legacy device integration | Modern, secure, cloud-connected BESS |

---

**(b)** Explain **alarm management, time-stamping, and Sequence-of-Events (SOE) recording** in a Battery SCADA. Why is **IRIG-B or IEEE 1588 time synchronization** essential? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Alarm Management:**
A Battery SCADA implements a **four-level alarm priority hierarchy** based on the severity and required operator response time:
1. **Advisory:** Informational (e.g., SOC reached 80%).
2. **Warning:** Action required (e.g., cell temperature 55 °C).
3. **Critical:** Immediate action (e.g., over-voltage 4.25 V).
4. **Emergency:** Automatic system trip (e.g., smoke detected).

Each alarm has **dead-band hysteresis** to prevent chattering and is **time-stamped** at the source (not the SCADA server) to ensure chronological accuracy.

**Sequence of Events (SOE) Recording:**
SOE is a high-resolution, time-ordered log of every binary state change (e.g., breaker open/close, alarm ack, operator command). The SOE log is **immutable** and used for post-incident root-cause analysis. Resolution is typically **1 ms or better**.

**Time Synchronization — Why It Is Essential:**

In a multi-vendor BESS, the BMS, PCS, RTU, and SCADA server each have an independent real-time clock. Without synchronization:
- The SOE log would be **non-monotonic** (events from different devices would appear out of order).
- **Wide-area grid events** (e.g., a frequency dip) cannot be correlated across the BESS, the substation PMU, and the utility EMS.

**IRIG-B (Inter-Range Instrumentation Group Time Code B):**
A standard 1 kHz amplitude-modulated time code distributed via coaxial cable or fiber from a **GPS-disciplined master clock**. Provides time accuracy of **±1 to ±100 microseconds**.

**IEEE 1588 (Precision Time Protocol - PTP):**
An Ethernet-based packet protocol achieving **sub-microsecond accuracy** using hardware timestamping. Increasingly preferred in modern BESS because it leverages the existing Ethernet infrastructure.

Without these protocols, post-fault forensic analysis is impossible, and grid code compliance (e.g., for primary frequency response) cannot be demonstrated to the regulator.

*(14 marks: 7 for part a, 7 for part b)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Conflating BMS with SCADA:** Many students incorrectly describe cell-level functions (balancing, cell protection) as "SCADA functions." The SCADA operates at the *system/plant* level. The examiner will deduct **2–3 marks** for this.
> 2. **Omitting Communication Protocols:** When asked to "explain the architecture," students often draw boxes and arrows but do not **name the protocols** (Modbus, DNP3, IEC 61850). This is considered incomplete and costs **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Skipping Units in Formulas:** Always write the unit alongside the formula. $SOC = \int I \, dt / C_{rated}$ without stating "in Ah" is incomplete.
> 4. **No Time-Sync Justification:** A 14-mark answer on SOE that does not mention **IRIG-B or PTP** is incomplete. Examiners expect this to be a key sub-section.
> 5. **Misusing Vertical Pipe `\|` in Markdown Tables:** This is a technical-writing red flag. Use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX expressions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Definition:** Battery SCADA is a supervisory control + data acquisition system for plant-level monitoring and dispatch of a BESS.
- ✅ **Hierarchy:** Three tiers — Field (sensors/actuators) → Control (RTU/PLC/BMS) → Supervisory (SCADA server/HMI/Historian).
- ✅ **BMS vs SCADA:** BMS = cell-level safety; SCADA = plant-level operations and grid interaction.
- ✅ **Key Telemetry:** Cell voltage, module temperature, SOC, SOH, SOE, DC bus voltage, PCS status, fire/HVAC status.
- ✅ **Key Setpoints:** P-ref (active power), Q-ref (reactive power), charge/discharge enable, grid-sync command.
- ✅ **Core Protocols:** Modbus TCP (BMS↔SCADA), DNP3 (utility), IEC 61850 (substation/fast GOOSE), OPC UA (secure modern), MQTT (cloud).
- ✅ **SOC Formula:** $SOC[k] = SOC[k-1] + \dfrac{\eta_c \cdot I \cdot \Delta t}{3600 \cdot C_{rated}}$.
- ✅ **SOH Formula:** $SOH = \dfrac{C_{actual}}{C_{rated}} \times 100\%$.
- ✅ **RTE Formula:** $\eta_{RTE} = \dfrac{E_{discharged}}{E_{charged}} \times 100\%$ (typical: 85–92% for Li-ion).
- ✅ **Alarm Hierarchy:** Advisory → Warning → Critical → Emergency → Shutdown → Lockout.
- ✅ **Time Sync:** IRIG-B (±1–100 μs) or IEEE 1588 PTP (sub-μs) is **mandatory** for SOE correlation.
- ✅ **Typical Scan Time:** 100 ms – 1 s for utility-grade BESS; < 3 ms for IEC 61850 GOOSE protection messaging.
- ✅ **Availability Target:** ≥ 99.95% for grid services; SCADA servers deployed in redundant hot-standby.
- ✅ **Cybersecurity:** Modern SCADA mandates TLS 1.2/1.3, role-based access, network segmentation, and IEC 62443 compliance.
- ✅ **KPIs Displayed:** SOC, SOH, instantaneous P & Q, daily energy throughput, alarm counts, RTE, cell ΔV, max temperature.
- ✅ **Standards:** IEEE 1547 (interconnection), IEC 62933 (BESS safety), NFPA 855 (installation), IEC 62443 (cybersecurity).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hybrid Energy Storage Systems: Configurations and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET521 - ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hybrid Energy Storage Systems: Configurations and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Hybrid Energy Storage System (HESS)** is a power-electronics-interfaced, multi-source energy storage architecture that intelligently combines two or more heterogeneous energy storage devices (ESDs) of complementary characteristics — typically pairing a **high energy-density** source with a **high power-density** source — to deliver superior system-level performance metrics (lifetime, efficiency, power quality, dynamic response) than any single storage technology can achieve in isolation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim Concept):**
> HESS exploits the **complementary nature** of dissimilar storage technologies. The high-energy device (e.g., Li-ion battery, fuel cell) handles bulk energy provisioning during steady-state or sustained load conditions, while the high-power device (e.g., supercapacitor/ultracapacitor, flywheel) handles transient peaks, regenerative braking pulses, and short-duration fluctuations.

The key performance metrics that govern HESS design are:

| Metric | Symbol | High-Energy Device | High-Power Device |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Energy Density | $Wh/kg$ | **High (100–250)** | Low (5–10) |
| Power Density | $W/kg$ | Low (150–500) | **High (1000–10000)** |
| Cycle Life | $cycles$ | 500–2000 | **>500,000** |
| Response Time | $s$ | Seconds | **Milliseconds** |
| Energy Efficiency | $\eta$ | 80–90% | 95%+ |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Marathon Runner and the Sprinter"

Imagine a delivery truck designed for a city where most routes are long and steady (highway driving) but frequently encounters stop-and-go traffic and steep flyover climbs (instantaneous acceleration).

- A **marathon runner** (battery) can carry a huge parcel for 8 hours straight — high *energy*, but slow to accelerate.
- A **sprinter** (supercapacitor) cannot carry the parcel far, but can sprint 100 m in 10 seconds — high *power*, instant response.

Putting the sprinter in the back of the marathon runner's truck, and dispatching them based on need, gives you a **hybrid crew** that handles both long routes and sudden bursts. That is exactly what an HESS does in electrical terms: the **battery carries the load**; the **supercapacitor absorbs spikes**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Counterpart:** A Toyota Prius or Tesla with regenerative braking is an HESS. The Li-ion battery provides steady cruising energy, while the supercapacitor (in some advanced models) or the high-power bus capacitors absorb the millisecond regenerative pulses from braking — protecting the battery from damaging high-C-rate discharge events.

### 1.3 Why HESS is Necessary — The Ragone Trade-off

No single electrochemical or mechanical storage technology simultaneously satisfies:
1. High energy density (long duration)
2. High power density (instantaneous response)
3. Long cycle life (durability)
4. Low capital cost
5. Wide operating temperature range

The **Ragone plot** is the standard engineering tool used to visualize the power density vs. energy density trade-off. A single device sits on a single curve; a *hybrid system* effectively shifts the operating envelope to a *region above* the individual curves by combining their strengths.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Takeaway:** Always cite the Ragone plot in HESS definition questions. Use the terms "power density–energy density decoupling" and "complementary characteristic matching."

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functional Decomposition of an HESS

An HESS comprises five functional layers. Understanding each is critical for KTU Module 4.

1. **Source Layer (Storage Devices):**
   - Primary: Battery (Li-ion, LFP, NMC, Lead-acid) or Fuel Cell
   - Secondary: Supercapacitor (EDLC), Flywheel, SMES, or CAES

2. **Power Electronics Interface Layer:**
   - DC-DC converters (Buck, Boost, Bidirectional)
   - DC-AC inverters (for AC-bus systems)
   - Filters (LCL, LC)

3. **DC-Bus / AC-Bus Coupling Layer:**
   - Common DC-link (most common)
   - Common AC-link (modular)
   - Multi-port converter (MPC) coupling

4. **Control & Energy Management Layer (EMS):**
   - Decides, instant-by-instant, how much power each device supplies/absorbs.

5. **Load / Application Layer:**
   - EV traction, PV/wind smoothing, microgrid, UPS, railway.

### 2.2 Why Bother Hybridizing? — Quantified Benefits

- **Battery Life Extension:** High-C-rate discharge is the dominant cause of Li-ion capacity fade. Offloading the high-frequency, high-power transients to a supercapacitor reduces the battery's effective C-rate by **3×–10×**, multiplying cycle life proportionally.
- **Efficiency Gain:** Supercapacitors operate at >95% round-trip efficiency; routing pulses through them avoids the I²R losses in the battery's internal resistance.
- **Cost Optimization:** Sizing is decoupled. You buy less battery (costly per kWh) and more supercap (cheap per kW-cycle).
- **Thermal Management:** Batteries generate more heat at high current; HESS reduces peak thermal load.

### 2.3 Classification of HESS Topologies

**A) By Coupling Architecture (Most Important for KTU):**

1. **Passive Hybrid Configuration:** Both ESDs connected directly to the DC bus, no power electronics between them and the load. Simple, cheap, but inflexible (load voltage = battery terminal voltage).

2. **Semi-Active (Semi-Passive) Topology:** One ESD (usually the high-power one — supercap) is connected via a DC-DC converter; the other (battery) is directly on the bus. Good compromise of cost and control.

3. **Fully Active Topology:** Both ESDs interfaced through their own bidirectional DC-DC converters, both feeding a common DC bus. Maximum control flexibility, highest cost.

4. **Multi-Port Converter (MPC) Topology:** A single integrated power converter with multiple bidirectional ports. Highest power density, complex control, emerging technology.

5. **AC-Coupled Topology:** ESDs are connected to a common AC bus through individual DC-AC inverters (common in grid-tied or large microgrid systems).

**B) By Energy Management Strategy (EMS):**

- **Rule-Based / Deterministic:** Hard thresholds, lookup tables (e.g., supercap takes over when P_load > threshold).
- **Frequency-Decoupling / Filter-Based:** Low-frequency component → battery; high-frequency component → supercap. Implemented via Low-Pass Filter (LPF) or Wavelet Transform.
- **Optimization-Based:** Dynamic Programming (DP), Model Predictive Control (MPC), Equivalent Consumption Minimization Strategy (ECMS).
- **AI-Based:** Fuzzy Logic, Neural Networks, Reinforcement Learning.

### 2.4 The KTU Formula Sheet — HESS Power Sharing & Sizing

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this table as the authoritative reference for numerical problems in the KTU University Exam.**

| Concept | Governing Equation | Variable Definitions | Engineering Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Power Balance | $P_{load}(t) = P_{bat}(t) + P_{sc}(t)$ | $P_{load}$: Total demand, $P_{bat}$: Battery power, $P_{sc}$: Supercap power | Mandatory energy conservation check |
| Supercap Energy Stored | $E_{sc} = \frac{1}{2} C \left( V_{max}^{2} - V_{min}^{2} \right)$ | $C$: Capacitance (F), $V_{max}, V_{min}$: Voltage limits | Sizes supercap for transient energy |
| Battery Energy Stored | $E_{bat} = V_{bat} \cdot Q_{bat} \cdot \eta_{bat}$ | $Q_{bat}$: Capacity (Ah), $\eta_{bat}$: Coulombic efficiency | Sizes battery for sustained energy |
| LPF Power Split (Frequency Decoupling) | $P_{bat} = P_{load} \cdot \frac{1}{1+s\tau}$  ;  $P_{sc} = P_{load} \cdot \frac{s\tau}{1+s\tau}$ | $\tau$: Time constant of LPF (s), $s$: Laplace operator | Defines dynamic power sharing |
| Ragone Optimality | $E_{specific} \cdot P_{specific} = const.$ (along a curve) | $E_{specific}$: Wh/kg, $P_{specific}$: W/kg | Trade-off visualization |
| HESS Round-Trip Efficiency | $\eta_{HESS} = \frac{E_{out,total}}{E_{in,total}} = w_{bat}\eta_{bat} + w_{sc}\eta_{sc}$ | $w$: Energy weighting factor | Overall system efficiency |
| Battery C-Rate Reduction | $C_{rate,eff} = \frac{P_{bat,avg}}{E_{bat,capacity}}$ | $C_{rate,eff}$: Effective C-rate (1/h) | Quantifies battery protection |
| Supercap Voltage Dynamics | $V_{sc}(t) = V_{sc,0} - \frac{1}{C} \int_{0}^{t} i_{sc}(\tau)d\tau$ | $i_{sc}$: Supercap current | Models state-of-charge evolution |
| SoC Constraints | $0.2 \leq SoC_{bat} \leq 0.95$  ;  $0.5 \leq V_{sc}/V_{sc,max} \leq 1.0$ | SoC: State of Charge | Operating envelope |
| Energy Management Minimization | $\min J = \int_{0}^{T} \left[ \alpha \cdot fuel(t) + \beta \cdot P_{bat}^{2}(t) \right] dt$ | $\alpha, \beta$: Cost weights, $fuel$: ECMS term | Optimization cost function |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Reminder on Units:** For supercapacitor energy, $E = \frac{1}{2}CV^{2}$, where $C$ is in Farads, $V$ is in Volts, and $E$ is in **Joules**. To convert to Wh, divide by **3600**. Examiner's frequently deduct marks for unit errors here.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **EV/HEV Traction:** HESS allows downsizing the battery pack while meeting acceleration and regen power demands.
- **Renewable Integration:** Wind gusts and solar cloud transients are smoothed by the supercap; the battery handles the average power.
- **Microgrid Frequency Regulation:** In islanded microgrids, the supercap provides synthetic inertia, while the battery handles sustained generation-load mismatch.
- **Railway Regenerative Braking:** Trams and locomotives recover huge braking pulses that would otherwise damage the catenary-fed network.
- **Data Center UPS:** Millisecond ride-through by supercap; sustained ride-through by battery.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Frequency-Domain Power Decoupling Using a Low-Pass Filter

This is the most heavily tested analytical concept in Module 4. The derivation establishes **why and how** a low-frequency LPF is used to split the load power between the battery and the supercapacitor.

**Given:** Total load power demand $P_{load}(t)$ has both a low-frequency (LF) steady component and a high-frequency (HF) transient component.

**Goal:** Express $P_{bat}(s)$ and $P_{sc}(s)$ in the Laplace domain such that the battery handles LF and the supercap handles HF.

**Step 1 — Define the first-order Low-Pass Filter transfer function:**

$$H_{LPF}(s) = \frac{1}{1 + s\tau}$$

where $\tau$ is the time constant (in seconds), chosen based on the battery's safe ramp rate (typically 1 s to 10 s).

**Step 2 — Apply the LPF to the load power signal to obtain the battery reference:**

$$P_{bat}(s) = H_{LPF}(s) \cdot P_{load}(s)$$

$$\boxed{P_{bat}(s) = \frac{P_{load}(s)}{1 + s\tau}}$$

**Step 3 — Derive the supercapacitor reference by enforcing the power balance $P_{load} = P_{bat} + P_{sc}$:**

$$P_{sc}(s) = P_{load}(s) - P_{bat}(s)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and simplify:**

$$P_{sc}(s) = P_{load}(s) - \frac{P_{load}(s)}{1 + s\tau}$$

$$P_{sc}(s) = P_{load}(s) \left[ 1 - \frac{1}{1 + s\tau} \right]$$

**Step 5 — Combine the bracket over a common denominator:**

$$P_{sc}(s) = P_{load}(s) \left[ \frac{(1 + s\tau) - 1}{1 + s\tau} \right]$$

$$P_{sc}(s) = P_{load}(s) \left[ \frac{s\tau}{1 + s\tau} \right]$$

$$\boxed{P_{sc}(s) = \frac{s\tau}{1 + s\tau} P_{load}(s)}$$

**Step 6 — Asymptotic Verification (Sanity Check):**

\begin{aligned}
\text{As } s \to 0 \ (\text{DC, low frequency}) : \quad & P_{bat} \to P_{load}, \quad P_{sc} \to 0 \\
\text{As } s \to \infty \ (\text{High frequency}) : \quad & P_{bat} \to 0, \quad P_{sc} \to P_{load}
\end{aligned}

This confirms that the LPF mathematically routes low-frequency power to the battery and high-frequency power to the supercapacitor — exactly the design intent. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** If the question specifies the battery's "maximum safe ramp rate" as $R_{bat}$ (in W/s), then the time constant is calculated as $\tau = P_{load,avg} / R_{bat}$. This is a frequent 2-mark sub-question.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Required Supercapacitor Sizing for a Pulse Load

**Given:** A load draws a rectangular pulse of power $P_{pulse}$ for duration $\Delta t$. The battery cannot ramp fast enough; it supplies only $P_{bat,avg}$. The supercap must supply the rest.

**Step 1 — Power deficit that the supercap must cover:**

$$P_{sc,pulse} = P_{pulse} - P_{bat,avg}$$

**Step 2 — Energy that must be drawn from the supercap during the pulse:**

$$E_{sc,req} = P_{sc,pulse} \cdot \Delta t$$

**Step 3 — Relate to the supercap energy equation $E = \frac{1}{2}C(V_{max}^{2} - V_{min}^{2})$:**

$$\frac{1}{2} C \left( V_{max}^{2} - V_{min}^{2} \right) = P_{sc,pulse} \cdot \Delta t$$

**Step 4 — Solve explicitly for the required capacitance:**

$$\boxed{C = \frac{2 \cdot P_{sc,pulse} \cdot \Delta t}{V_{max}^{2} - V_{min}^{2}}}$$

**Step 5 — Convert Joules to Wh for battery energy analogue (informational):**

$$E_{sc,Wh} = \frac{E_{sc,J}}{3600}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Hint:** When $V_{min}$ is not given, the standard assumption (and what examiners expect) is $V_{min} = \frac{1}{2} V_{max}$ (i.e., the supercap is allowed to discharge to 50% of rated voltage). Substituting this gives the commonly used shortcut:

$$C = \frac{2 \cdot P_{sc,pulse} \cdot \Delta t}{V_{max}^{2} - (0.5 V_{max})^{2}} = \frac{2 \cdot P_{sc,pulse} \cdot \Delta t}{0.75 \cdot V_{max}^{2}} = \frac{8 \cdot P_{sc,pulse} \cdot \Delta t}{3 \cdot V_{max}^{2}}$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Configuration Selection

| Configuration | Cost | Efficiency | Control Flexibility | Voltage Regulation | Lifetime Impact | Typical Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Passive | **Lowest** | Highest (~98%) | None | Poor | Limited | Low-power toys, simple UPS |
| Semi-Active (Cap Side) | Medium | ~94% | Moderate | Good | Good | EV micro-hybrids, small PV |
| Fully Active | High | ~90–93% | **Maximum** | **Excellent** | **Best** | EV/HEV, grid storage |
| Multi-Port Converter | Highest | ~92% | Very high | Excellent | Excellent | Aerospace, advanced EV |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Efficiency Decreases with More Converters:** Each power electronic stage introduces switching and conduction losses (typically 2–5% per stage). Two cascaded DC-DC stages (fully active) lose more than one stage (semi-active), but gain control. This is the fundamental engineering trade-off.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (For Practice)

> **Problem:** A DC microgrid load has a steady demand of 5 kW with a superimposed pulse of 15 kW lasting 2 seconds, repeating every 30 s. The battery can ramp at 2 kW/s. Design a HESS with a supercapacitor rated at $V_{max} = 48 \text{ V}$, $V_{min} = 24 \text{ V}$.

**Step 1 — Battery contribution during the pulse ramp:**

$$P_{bat,end} = P_{bat,0} + R_{bat} \cdot \Delta t = 5 \text{ kW} + (2 \text{ kW/s})(2 \text{ s}) = 9 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 — Average battery power during pulse (assuming linear ramp):**

$$P_{bat,avg} = \frac{5 \text{ kW} + 9 \text{ kW}}{2} = 7 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — Peak supercap power:**

$$P_{sc,peak} = 15 \text{ kW} - 7 \text{ kW} = 8 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — Energy required from supercap:**

$$E_{sc} = 8 \text{ kW} \times 2 \text{ s} = 16 \text{ kJ} = 16000 \text{ J}$$

**Step 5 — Required capacitance:**

$$C = \frac{2 \times 16000}{48^{2} - 24^{2}} = \frac{32000}{2304 - 576} = \frac{32000}{1728} \approx 18.52 \text{ F}$$

$$\boxed{C \approx 18.5 \text{ F at 48 V rating}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Always state the rated voltage and the *usable* energy fraction. A 48 V / 18.5 F supercap module is the design output.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level HESS Block Architecture (Mermaid Flow)

The following Mermaid diagram represents the top-level functional architecture of a fully active HESS, showing power flow and control signal paths.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Battery Pack High Energy Density] --> B[Bidirectional DC DC Converter 1]
    C[Supercapacitor Bank High Power Density] --> D[Bidirectional DC DC Converter 2]
    B --> E[Common DC Bus 400V to 800V]
    D --> E
    E --> F[DC AC Inverter]
    F --> G[AC Load or Grid]
    H[Energy Management Controller EMS] -->|Gate Signal PWM| B
    H -->|Gate Signal PWM| D
    H -->|PWM Reference| F
    I[Sensors Voltage Current SoC Temperature] --> H
```

**Interpretation:**
- The two storage devices feed a common DC bus through independent bidirectional converters.
- The EMS controller is the brain — it reads sensors and generates PWM gate signals to dictate power flow.
- The DC-AC inverter feeds the load/grid from the DC bus.

---

### 4.2 Comparative Topology Diagram — Passive vs. Semi-Active vs. Fully Active

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph PassiveHybrid
        P1[Battery] --- P2[Supercap]
        P2 --- P3[DC Bus Direct Connection]
    end

    subgraph SemiActiveHybrid
        S1[Battery] --- S2[DC Bus Direct]
        S3[Supercap] --- S4[Bidirectional DC DC]
        S4 --- S2
    end

    subgraph FullyActiveHybrid
        F1[Battery] --- F2[Bidirectional DC DC 1]
        F3[Supercap] --- F4[Bidirectional DC DC 2]
        F2 --- F5[DC Bus Common]
        F4 --- F5
    end
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- **PassiveHybrid:** No converters — direct connection. Simplest, but no voltage matching.
- **SemiActiveHybrid:** Battery is direct; supercap goes through one DC-DC. Compromise.
- **FullyActiveHybrid:** Both through converters. Maximum flexibility, higher cost.

---

### 4.3 Energy Management Strategy (EMS) Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Start EMS Loop] --> B[Read Sensors Pload Vbat Vsc SoC]
    B --> C{Apply LPF or Optimization}
    C --> D[Compute Pref bat]
    C --> E[Compute Pref sc]
    D --> F{Clamp to Battery Limits}
    F -->|Within Limits| G[Generate PWM Battery Converter]
    F -->|Exceeds Limit| H[Adjust Pref sc to Compensate]
    H --> G
    E --> I{Clamp to Supercap Limits}
    I -->|Within Limits| J[Generate PWM Supercap Converter]
    I -->|Exceeds Limit| K[Flag Saturation Log Event]
    K --> J
    G --> L[Update SoC Estimates]
    J --> L
    L --> M[Wait Sampling Period Ts]
    M --> B
```

**Operational Note:** This is a closed-loop real-time control executed at a sampling period $T_s$ typically between 1 ms (fast inner loop) and 100 ms (slow outer loop).

---

### 4.4 Application Mapping Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    HESS[Hybrid Energy Storage System] --> APP1[EV HEV Traction]
    HESS --> APP2[Renewable Integration PV Wind]
    HESS --> APP3[Microgrid Frequency Regulation]
    HESS --> APP4[Railway Regenerative Braking]
    HESS --> APP5[Data Center UPS]
    HESS --> APP6[Aerospace Satellite Power]

    APP1 --> DEV1[Li ion Battery plus Supercap]
    APP2 --> DEV2[Li ion Battery plus Supercap plus Hydrogen optional]
    APP3 --> DEV3[Battery plus Flywheel or SC]
    APP4 --> DEV4[SC plus Battery for line regulation]
    APP5 --> DEV5[SC for ride through plus Battery for sustained]
    APP6 --> DEV6[Fuel Cell plus Battery plus SC]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper)]**
*Define a Hybrid Energy Storage System (HESS). Why is hybridization necessary in modern energy storage applications?*

**Model Answer:**

A Hybrid Energy Storage System (HESS) is an integrated power system that combines two or more energy storage devices of complementary characteristics — typically a high energy-density source (e.g., Li-ion battery) and a high power-density source (e.g., supercapacitor) — interfaced through power electronic converters and controlled by an Energy Management System (EMS).

Hybridization is necessary because:
1. **No single storage technology simultaneously meets** high energy density, high power density, long cycle life, and low cost. The Ragone plot shows the inherent trade-off.
2. **Peak shaving and pulse handling:** Loads frequently demand short, high-power pulses (e.g., EV acceleration, regenerative braking) that damage batteries operated at high C-rates. The high-power device handles these transients, protecting the battery.
3. **Efficiency improvement:** Supercapacitors (95%+) handle frequent cycles more efficiently than batteries (80–90%).
4. **Battery life extension:** Smoothing the high-frequency power components can multiply the battery's effective cycle life by 3–10 times.
5. **Voltage regulation:** Power electronic decouplers allow stable DC bus voltage regardless of battery SoC.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common 1-Mark Loss:** Students frequently forget to mention the **Ragone plot** trade-off. The phrase *"complementary characteristic matching"* is a marker phrase examiners look for.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Paper)]**
*Compare passive, semi-active, and fully active HESS configurations with suitable diagrams.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Passive | Semi-Active | Fully Active |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| DC-DC Converters | **None** | One (on supercap) | **Two (on both)** |
| Cost | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| DC Bus Voltage | Fixed by battery | Regulated | **Fully regulated** |
| Control Flexibility | None | Partial | **Maximum** |
| Efficiency | Highest | High | Slightly lower |
| Application | Toys, simple UPS | EV micro-hybrid, PV | Full EV/HEV, grid |

**Passive Configuration:** Both the battery and supercapacitor are directly connected to the DC bus without any interfacing converter. Simple and cheap, but the bus voltage is rigidly tied to the battery terminal voltage.

**Semi-Active Configuration:** The supercapacitor is interfaced through a single bidirectional DC-DC converter, while the battery is connected directly to the bus. This allows the supercap to be controlled for transient support while the battery sets the bus voltage. This is the most common industrial compromise.

**Fully Active Configuration:** Both the battery and supercapacitor are interfaced through their own bidirectional DC-DC converters feeding a common DC bus. This allows independent control of both power flows and full voltage regulation, at the cost of extra converter losses and capital cost.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Regulation:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, divided as Part (a) 7 marks and Part (b) 7 marks. Either Q-A or Q-B must be answered (internal choice).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Sample Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the fully active HESS configuration for an electric vehicle application. Discuss the role of each power electronic interface and the function of the Energy Management System (EMS). (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

A fully active HESS for an EV consists of the following blocks (refer to Section 4.1 diagram):

1. **Battery Pack (e.g., 400 V, 50 kWh Li-ion):** Provides the bulk energy for driving range. Its State of Charge (SoC) is maintained between 20% and 95% for longevity.

2. **Bidirectional DC-DC Converter 1 (Battery Side):** A typically boost-type or buck-boost converter that steps the battery voltage (which varies with SoC from 320 V to 420 V) up or down to match the DC bus voltage (e.g., 650 V). It controls battery current (and thus power) bidirectionally to support both traction and regenerative charging.

3. **Supercapacitor Bank (e.g., 48 V, 100 F module x N in series-parallel):** Stores transient energy for acceleration boosts and absorbs regenerative braking pulses. Operates at high C-rates (>100C) without degradation.

4. **Bidirectional DC-DC Converter 2 (Supercap Side):** Typically a boost converter in discharge mode and a buck converter in charge mode. Modulates the supercap voltage (which swings from 24 V to 48 V during a cycle) to deliver a constant DC bus voltage.

5. **DC-AC Inverter (Traction Inverter):** Converts the DC bus to a variable-frequency, variable-voltage three-phase AC for the traction motor. Modern EVs use SiC or GaN inverters for >98% efficiency.

6. **Energy Management System (EMS):** The brain of the system. It:
   - Reads the driver's torque demand from the accelerator pedal.
   - Reads SoC, voltage, current, and temperature from both ESDs.
   - Runs the energy management algorithm (rule-based, LPF, or MPC).
   - Splits the power demand: low-frequency component → battery, high-frequency component → supercap.
   - Generates PWM gate signals for both DC-DC converters and the inverter.
   - Monitors constraints and triggers protection (over-voltage, over-current, thermal).

**(b)** A DC microgrid load has a steady demand of 4 kW, with a rectangular pulse of 12 kW lasting 1.5 seconds, repeating every 20 seconds. The battery can supply a constant 4 kW only. A supercapacitor module rated at $V_{max} = 60$ V and $V_{min} = 30$ V is to be used. Calculate: (i) the energy required from the supercap per pulse, (ii) the required capacitance, and (iii) the average supercap C-rate per pulse. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Energy required from supercap per pulse:**

The supercap must cover the entire pulse power minus what the battery supplies:

$$P_{sc,pulse} = 12 \text{ kW} - 4 \text{ kW} = 8 \text{ kW}$$

$$E_{sc} = P_{sc,pulse} \cdot \Delta t = 8 \text{ kW} \times 1.5 \text{ s} = 12 \text{ kJ} = 12000 \text{ J}$$

**(ii) Required capacitance:**

$$C = \frac{2 \cdot E_{sc}}{V_{max}^{2} - V_{min}^{2}} = \frac{2 \times 12000}{60^{2} - 30^{2}} = \frac{24000}{3600 - 900} = \frac{24000}{2700}$$

$$\boxed{C \approx 8.89 \text{ F at 60 V rating}}$$

**(iii) Average supercap C-rate per pulse:**

The "C-rate" of a capacitor refers to the inverse of the discharge time constant. The supercap discharges 12000 J in 1.5 s.

Total energy stored in the supercap (full discharge from $V_{max}$ to 0 V):

$$E_{total} = \frac{1}{2} C V_{max}^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \times 8.89 \times 60^{2} = 16000 \text{ J}$$

C-rate is defined as: $C_{rate} = 1 / t_{discharge}$. Using the *usable* energy basis:

$$C_{rate} = \frac{P_{sc,pulse}}{E_{usable}} = \frac{8000 \text{ W}}{12000 \text{ J}} = 0.667 \text{ per second}$$

This corresponds to roughly **40C equivalent** (since 1 hour = 3600 s, 0.667/s = 2400/h, divided by typical 60C full rating — alternatively, state as "supercap discharges in 1.5 s, equivalent to ~2400/h C-rate").

$$\boxed{C_{rate} \approx 0.67 \text{ s}^{-1} \ (\text{or equivalently } 2400/\text{h})}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Sample Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the frequency-decoupling method of power sharing in a HESS using a first-order low-pass filter. Derive the transfer functions for battery and supercapacitor power references. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Concept:** In many loads (EVs, wind generators, pulsed power tools), the demand has a low-frequency average component and a high-frequency transient component. The battery is best at supplying the average (because of its high energy density and slow response), while the supercapacitor is best at supplying the transients (because of its high power density and fast response). The LPF mathematically separates these two components.

**Derivation:** See Section 3.1 of these notes for the complete step-by-step derivation. The final equations are:

$$P_{bat}(s) = \frac{P_{load}(s)}{1 + s\tau}$$

$$P_{sc}(s) = \frac{s\tau}{1 + s\tau} P_{load}(s)$$

**Implementation Notes:**

- The time constant $\tau$ is chosen based on the battery's safe ramp rate. A common choice is $\tau = 1$ s to $5$ s for EV applications.
- The LPF can be implemented in software (in the EMS microcontroller) or in analog hardware.
- For more advanced frequency decoupling, **Wavelet Transform** can be used, which decomposes the signal into multiple frequency bands (each routed to a different storage device).

**(b)** Discuss three major applications of Hybrid Energy Storage Systems with specific examples. For each application, identify which two storage technologies are typically combined and explain the role of each. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Application 1: Electric Vehicle (EV) Traction**
- **Combination:** Li-ion battery + supercapacitor
- **Role of battery:** Provides sustained cruising energy (high energy density, 150–250 Wh/kg). Sized for driving range.
- **Role of supercapacitor:** Provides acceleration boost and absorbs regenerative braking pulses (high power density, 5000–10000 W/kg). Protects the battery from high-C-rate stress.
- **Example:** Formula E racing cars use HESS for lap-time boosting; the McLaren P1 hybrid uses a similar architecture.

**Application 2: Renewable Energy Integration (Solar PV / Wind)**
- **Combination:** Li-ion battery + supercapacitor (or battery + flywheel)
- **Role of battery:** Smooths the average solar/wind power mismatch over minutes to hours. Handles night-time and low-wind conditions.
- **Role of supercapacitor:** Smooths rapid cloud-shadow transients (PV) and wind gusts (kHz-level fluctuations). Improves power quality to grid standards.
- **Example:** Hawaii's Maui and Lanai islands use HESS to firm up solar PV penetration above 60% of grid demand.

**Application 3: Railway Regenerative Braking / Electric Locomotive**
- **Combination:** Onboard Li-ion battery + supercapacitor (trackside substation HESS)
- **Role of battery:** Stores recovered braking energy for sustained reuse (e.g., station-to-station).
- **Role of supercapacitor:** Absorbs the immediate, high-power braking pulse (typically 1–3 MW for 10–30 seconds).
- **Example:** The Sitras HES system by Siemens, deployed on light rail networks in Germany and Spain.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not confuse units:** Energy in Joules vs. Watt-hours is a frequent 1-mark deduction. Always convert explicitly: $1 \text{ Wh} = 3600 \text{ J}$.
> 2. **State the time constant clearly:** When using LPF-based decoupling, write the unit of $\tau$ (seconds) and justify its value (battery ramp rate).
> 3. **Do not skip the supercap sizing formula derivation:** Examiners expect the equation $C = 2E/(V_{max}^2 - V_{min}^2)$ to be written and explained, not just the final number.
> 4. **Label diagrams properly:** Block diagrams must show *all* power electronic interfaces, *all* sensors, and the *EMS control loop* with arrow directions.
> 5. **Application questions require real-world specificity:** Stating "EV" alone is too generic. Name a system (e.g., "Siemens Sitras HES") and a function (e.g., "regenerative braking pulse absorption").

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — HESS: Configurations and Applications**

- ✅ **Definition:** HESS combines high-energy + high-power storage to overcome single-device limitations; complementary characteristic matching.
- ✅ **Ragone Plot:** Visualizes the power vs. energy trade-off. Always reference it.
- ✅ **Configurations:** Passive, Semi-Active, Fully Active, Multi-Port, AC-Coupled. Know cost, efficiency, and flexibility trade-offs.
- ✅ **Power Balance:** $P_{load} = P_{bat} + P_{sc}$ — must always hold in any HESS.
- ✅ **LPF Decoupling:** $P_{bat} = P_{load}/(1+s\tau)$; $P_{sc} = (s\tau/(1+s\tau)) P_{load}$.
- ✅ **Supercap Energy:** $E_{sc} = \frac{1}{2} C (V_{max}^2 - V_{min}^2)$. Convert J to Wh by /3600.
- ✅ **Supercap Sizing:** $C = 2 \cdot E_{req} / (V_{max}^2 - V_{min}^2)$.
- ✅ **C-Rate Reduction:** Offloading transients to supercap reduces battery C-rate 3–10× → multiplies cycle life.
- ✅ **EMS Strategies:** Rule-based, LPF/Frequency-decoupling, Optimization (DP, MPC), AI (Fuzzy, NN).
- ✅ **Applications:** EV traction, PV/wind smoothing, microgrid frequency regulation, railway regen, data center UPS, aerospace.
- ✅ **Common Pairings:** Battery+Supercap (most common), Battery+Flywheel (grid), FuelCell+Battery+Supercap (aerospace).
- ✅ **Why Fully Active Costs More:** Two converter stages → 4–10% extra loss, but full control flexibility.
- ✅ **Semi-Active Default:** Industrial best compromise — battery on bus, supercap via DC-DC.
